# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 1]**
*List any six major application areas of Computational Fluid Dynamics in modern engineering practice. Briefly state the type of physical phenomenon modeled in each.*

**Model Answer:**

CFD is applied in six major areas:

1. **Aerospace Engineering** — External flow over airfoils, fuselage, and rocket nozzles. (Phenomenon: Aerodynamics, compressible flow with shock waves.)
2. **Automotive Engineering** — Vehicle drag reduction, in-cylinder combustion, HVAC cabin airflow. (Phenomenon: External aerodynamics, reactive flow, convective heat transfer.)
3. **Turbomachinery** — Pumps, compressors, turbines. (Phenomenon: Internal rotating flow, cavitation.)
4. **Heat Exchangers / Electronics Cooling** — Fin optimization, PCB thermal management. (Phenomenon: Conjugate heat transfer.)
5. **Biomedical Engineering** — Blood flow in arteries, airway flow. (Phenomenon: Non-Newtonian internal flow, particle transport.)
6. **Civil / Environmental Engineering** — Wind loading on tall buildings, pollutant dispersion. (Phenomenon: Atmospheric boundary layer flow, scalar transport.)

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 1]**
*Why is CFD preferred over purely experimental methods in many engineering fluid flow studies? Give four reasons.*

**Model Answer:**

CFD is preferred over experiments for the following reasons:

1. **Cost Reduction:** A single CFD license and workstation is far cheaper than a wind tunnel, water tunnel, or full-scale prototype test campaign.
2. **Faster Turnaround:** Multiple design iterations can be evaluated overnight, whereas experiments may take weeks per configuration.
3. **Complete Field Data:** CFD provides velocity, pressure, temperature, and species concentration at *every cell*, whereas experiments measure only at discrete probe locations.
4. **Ability to Study Inaccessible or Dangerous Flows:** Examples include re-entry hypersonic flow, nuclear reactor coolant channels, in-vivo blood flow, and post-accident toxic releases.
5. **Parametric Studies:** Easy to vary boundary conditions, geometry, or material properties systematically.
6. **No Disturbance:** Probes in experiments disturb the flow; CFD is non-intrusive.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Students frequently lose marks in Part B by (a) writing generic definitions without linking them to a *specific application* and (b) failing to mention the **governing equations and turbulence model** used in that application. Always connect the *application* → *physics* → *equations* → *expected CFD output*.

### Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 1]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With neat schematic explanation, describe the application of CFD in the design of an **axial flow turbine blade**. Mention the governing equations solved, the turbulence model typically used, and the key performance parameters predicted.

**(b) [7 Marks]** An aircraft wing is tested in a low-speed wind tunnel. The freestream velocity is $V_\infty = 50$ m/s, the freestream static pressure is $p_\infty = 101{,}325$ Pa, and the density is $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³. A pressure tap on the upper surface at 30% chord reads $p = 88{,}500$ Pa. Calculate the local pressure coefficient $C_p$. Comment on what a $C_p$ value less than $-1$ physically indicates.

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Application: Axial Flow Turbine Blade Design**

**Schematic Explanation:**
The axial flow turbine consists of a row of airfoil-shaped blades mounted on a rotating disc. Hot gas enters axially and imparts energy to the blades as it expands. CFD is used to model the **3D viscous flow** through the blade passage.

**Governing Equations Solved:**
- 3D **compressible Navier–Stokes equations** in the rotating reference frame.
- **Energy equation** for total temperature / enthalpy.
- **Turbulence model:** The $k$-$\omega$ SST model is the most common, because it accurately handles the strong adverse pressure gradients and separation typical in turbine blade passages.

**Boundary Conditions:**
- **Inlet:** Total pressure $P_{0,\text{in}}$, total temperature $T_{0,\text{in}}$, flow angle $\alpha_1$.
- **Outlet:** Static pressure $P_2$.
- **Blade walls:** No-slip, adiabatic or prescribed temperature.
- **Hub & Casing:** No-slip, rotating frame.

**Key Performance Parameters Predicted:**
- Stage isentropic efficiency $\eta_s$
- Total pressure loss coefficient $\bar{\omega} = (P_{0,1} - P_{0,2})/(P_{0,1} - P_2)$
- Mass flow rate $\dot{m}$
- Blade loading distribution
- Nusselt number distribution for blade cooling analysis

**Workflow Steps:**
1. 3D CAD of single passage created (one blade + one pitch).
2. Structured H-O-H mesh with $y^+ \approx 1$ for accurate boundary layer resolution.
3. Solver run with $k$-$\omega$ SST and second-order upwind discretization.
4. Post-processing for blade surface pressure, secondary flow vectors, and loss contours.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:**
- $V_\infty = 50$ m/s
- $p_\infty = 101{,}325$ Pa
- $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³
- $p = 88{,}500$ Pa

**Step 1 — Compute dynamic pressure:**
$$q_\infty = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V_\infty^2 = \tfrac{1}{2} (1.225)(50)^2 = \tfrac{1}{2} (1.225)(2500) = 1531.25 \text{ Pa}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the pressure coefficient formula:**
$$C_p = \frac{p - p_\infty}{q_\infty} = \frac{88{,}500 - 101{,}325}{1531.25}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate the numerator:**
$$88{,}500 - 101{,}325 = -12{,}825 \text{ Pa}$$

**Step 4 — Final division:**
$$C_p = \frac{-12{,}825}{1531.25} = -8.376$$

**Step 5 — Comment:**

Theoretical minimum $C_p$ for **incompressible, inviscid** flow is $-1$ (achieved at the leading edge stagnation of a *flat plate rotated 90°*, or flow accelerating to infinity). A value $C_p = -8.38$ is physically impossible under incompressible inviscid assumptions.

Physically, $C_p < -1$ indicates one or more of the following:

1. **Local compressibility / transonic effects:** Local Mach number approaching or exceeding unity, leading to shock-induced low pressures.
2. **Flow separation:** The boundary layer detaches, creating a low-pressure recirculation bubble.
3. **Measurement error or numerical artifact:** Pressure tap malfunctions, mesh refinement issues, or boundary layer transition mis-prediction.

In wind tunnel testing, $C_p < -1$ at low speeds is a strong warning sign to recheck instrumentation and tunnel conditions.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 1]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the application of CFD in the **automotive industry** for reducing aerodynamic drag. Describe the typical simulation setup, boundary conditions, and the role of $C_d$ in vehicle design.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A chemical reactor vessel is jacketed with cooling water. CFD is used to study conjugate heat transfer. The convective heat transfer coefficient predicted by CFD is $h = 1250$ W/m²·K, the characteristic length is $L = 0.05$ m, and the thermal conductivity of the reactor wall is $k = 16$ W/m·K. Calculate the Nusselt number and identify whether the heat transfer is dominated by convection or conduction.

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Application: CFD in Automotive Aerodynamic Drag Reduction**

**Background:** The aerodynamic drag force on a car moving at velocity $V$ is:
$$F_D = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^2 A C_d$$
Reducing $C_d$ directly reduces fuel consumption. A $C_d$ reduction of 0.01 saves approximately 0.5–1% fuel at highway speeds.

**Typical Simulation Setup:**

1. **Domain:** A large rectangular box (≈10 car lengths long, 5 widths wide, 5 heights high) is created around the car geometry to minimize boundary effects.
2. **Mesh:** Hybrid mesh — fine prismatic layers on the body surface ($y^+ \approx 1$ to $30$), polyhedral cells in the wake, tetrahedral elsewhere. Total cells ≈ 30–100 million for a full car.
3. **Turbulence Model:** Standard $k$-$\epsilon$ for early design iterations; $k$-$\omega$ SST or Delayed Detached Eddy Simulation (DDES) for refined wake analysis.
4. **Solver:** Pressure-based SIMPLE or coupled solver for steady-state RANS.
5. **Discretization:** Second-order upwind for momentum, PRESTO! for pressure.

**Boundary Conditions:**
- **Inlet:** Constant velocity $V_\infty$ (or a velocity profile from a precursor atmospheric simulation), turbulent intensity $Tu \approx 0.5$–$1\%$.
- **Outlet:** Static pressure outlet (gauge = 0).
- **Ground:** Moving wall at $V_\infty$ (simulating a treadmill ground).
- **Car body:** No-slip wall.
- **Wheels:** Rotating wall at appropriate rpm.

**Role of $C_d$ in Vehicle Design:**
- $C_d$ is the **primary figure of merit** for sedan, hatchback, and SUV design.
- Modern targets: $C_d \le 0.25$ (Tesla Model 3: 0.23; Mercedes EQS: 0.20; Toyota Prius: 0.24).
- CFD enables the design team to study drag contributors in detail: underbody flow, wheel arch flow, A-pillar vortex, rear wake separation, side mirror wake.
- Iterative design changes (e.g., adding a rear diffuser, closing the front grille) can be evaluated virtually before committing to expensive wind-tunnel tests at full scale.

**Validation:** Final $C_d$ values are cross-checked in full-scale wind tunnels (e.g., FKFS, MIRA).

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:**
- $h = 1250$ W/m²·K
- $L = 0.05$ m
- $k = 16$ W/m·K

**Step 1 — Apply the Nusselt number formula:**
$$Nu = \frac{h L}{k}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:**
$$Nu = \frac{(1250)(0.05)}{16}$$

**Step 3 — Compute numerator:**
$$(1250)(0.05) = 62.5$$

**Step 4 — Final division:**
$$Nu = \frac{62.5}{16} = 3.906$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation:**

- $Nu = 3.906$ means the convective heat transfer rate is approximately **3.9 times** the rate of pure conduction across the same length scale.
- Generally, $Nu \gg 1$ indicates **convection-dominated** heat transfer, which is the desired regime in a jacketed reactor (the moving coolant fluid is efficiently removing heat).
- $Nu \approx 1$ would suggest conduction-dominated (poor) heat transfer, indicating poor flow mixing or low velocity.
- Since $3.9 > 1$, the heat transfer is **convection-dominated**, confirming effective cooling jacket performance.

> [!NOTE]
> For turbulent flow inside the jacket, typical $Nu$ values are 100–10,000. The relatively low $Nu = 3.9$ here suggests the flow may be **laminar** or the geometry is unfavorable — a CFD engineer would refine the jacket inlet/outlet design or add baffles to enhance $Nu$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid-Revision Checklist for Module 1 — Applications of CFD**

- **Core Definition:** CFD = numerical solution of conservation laws (mass, momentum, energy) over a discretized domain.
- **Three Pillars:** Fluid flow, heat transfer, mass transfer/reactions.
- **Seven-Step Workflow:** Problem → Math model → Geometry → Mesh → Solve → Post-process → Validate.
- **CFD vs Experiment:** CFD is cheaper, faster, and gives full field data; experiments remain the gold standard for validation.
- **CFD vs Theory:** CFD handles complex geometries; theory is limited to idealized cases.
- **Aerospace:** $C_p$, $C_L$, $C_d$, shock waves, supersonic nozzle design.
- **Automotive:** $C_d$ minimization, target $C_d \le 0.25$.
- **Turbomachinery:** $H$ vs $Q$ curves, cavitation, tip clearance.
- **Heat Transfer:** Nusselt number $Nu = hL/k$ is the key non-dimensional output.
- **Drag Coefficient formula:** $C_d = F_D / (\tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^2 A)$.
- **Lift Coefficient formula:** $C_L = F_L / (\tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^2 A)$.
- **Pressure Coefficient formula:** $C_p = (p - p_\infty)/(\tfrac{1}{2} \rho V_\infty^2)$.
- **Reynolds Number:** $Re = \rho V L / \mu$ — flow regime indicator.
- **Mach Number:** $M = V/a$ — compressibility indicator.
- **Common Turbulence Models:** $k$-$\epsilon$ (general purpose), $k$-$\omega$ SST (adverse pressure gradient, turbomachinery).
- **CFD Software (industry-standard):** ANSYS Fluent, CFX, OpenFOAM, STAR-CCM+, COMSOL.
- **Biomedical Frontier:** Wall Shear Stress (WSS) in arteries predicts atherosclerosis.
- **Civil Application:** Wind loading on high-rises, pollutant dispersion.
- **Typical Exam Cue Words:** "External flow," "Internal flow," "Conjugate heat transfer," "Reactive flow," "Multiphase flow."
- **Limitations to Mention (Board favorite):** Turbulence modeling uncertainty, mesh dependence, boundary condition sensitivity, high computational cost for high-Reynolds-number and multiphysics problems.
- **Rule of Thumb:** Always quote the **governing equations** when describing a CFD application — this alone fetches 2–3 marks per question.
- **Convective vs Conductive:** $Nu \gg 1 \Rightarrow$ convection-dominated; $Nu \approx 1 \Rightarrow$ conduction-dominated.
- **$C_p < -1$:** Physically impossible in incompressible inviscid flow — signals compressibility, separation, or numerical error.
- **Standard atmospheric reference values:** $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³, $p_\infty = 101{,}325$ Pa, $a = 340.3$ m/s (at sea level).

topics.title: Discretization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET521 - COMPUTATIONAL FLUID DYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Discretization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Discretization** in Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD) is the mathematical process of converting the continuous form of the governing partial differential equations (PDEs) — the conservation laws of mass, momentum, and energy — into a system of algebraic equations that can be solved numerically on a finite number of discrete points, volumes, or elements spanning the computational domain.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, discretization is positioned as the **pivotal bridge** between the *mathematical model* (continuous PDEs) and the *numerical model* (solvable algebraic system). Without discretization, the Navier–Stokes equations, continuity equation, and energy equation remain analytical ideals that cannot be evaluated on a computer.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** The KTU 2024 syllabus specifically emphasizes three principal discretization strategies:
> 1. **Finite Difference Method (FDM)** — based on Taylor series expansions on a structured grid.
> 2. **Finite Volume Method (FVM)** — based on integral conservation over control volumes.
> 3. **Finite Element Method (FEM)** — based on weighted residual formulations over unstructured meshes.
> 
> The focus of Module 2 lies heavily on **FDM** and **FVM**, with FEM introduced conceptually.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bread-Slicing" Intuition

Imagine you have a **loaf of freshly baked bread** representing the continuous fluid domain (say, airflow over an aircraft wing). The bread is smooth, soft, and continuous — you cannot compute anything on it directly because it has infinitely many points.

To "compute" on this bread, you must:
1. **Slice it into finite pieces** (the act of *spatial discretization* — converting a continuous space into a finite grid of points or cells).
2. **Measure the temperature, weight, or volume of each slice** (the act of *variable discretization* — approximating the unknown field $\phi$ at discrete nodes).
3. **Track how the slices change with time, one tick at a time** (the act of *temporal discretization* — marching forward in time using $\Delta t$).

The thinner and more numerous your slices, the more accurate the result — but the more computational effort required. This trade-off is the **heart of CFD discretization**.

| Real-World Object | CFD Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Whole bread loaf | Continuous fluid domain $\Omega$ |
| A single bread slice | Control volume / finite element / grid cell |
| Measuring each slice's weight | Discretized variable $\phi_i$ at node $i$ |
| Time between tastings | Time step $\Delta t$ |
| The recipe/oven settings | Governing PDE (Navier–Stokes, etc.) |

---

## 1.3 The Three Layers of Discretization

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Discretization is NOT a single step — it is a layered transformation process:**

**Layer 1 — Domain Discretization (Mesh Generation):**
The physical/flow domain is subdivided into a finite number of sub-domains (cells, elements, or nodes). This is the act of *geometric discretization*. In FDM this produces a structured grid; in FVM a control volume mesh; in FEM an unstructured tessellation.

**Layer 2 — Equation Discretization (Approximation of Derivatives):**
The differential operators ($\partial / \partial x$, $\nabla^2$, $\partial / \partial t$) appearing in the governing PDEs are replaced by **algebraic approximations** built from neighboring nodal values. This produces a system of algebraic equations of the form $[A]\{\phi\} = \{b\}$.

**Layer 3 — Solution of the Discrete System:**
The resulting linear/non-linear algebraic system is solved using direct methods (Gaussian elimination, LU decomposition) or iterative methods (Jacobi, Gauss–Seidel, Conjugate Gradient, Multigrid).

---

## 1.4 Why Discretization is Necessary — The Computational Reality

> [!NOTE]
> **A continuous PDE has infinitely many solutions (one for every point in space and time).** A digital computer has finite memory and finite CPU cycles. Therefore:
> - We **cannot** store infinitely many values.
> - We **cannot** evaluate derivatives exactly at infinitely many points.
> - We **must** reduce the infinite-dimensional problem to a **finite-dimensional** one.
> - **Discretization accomplishes exactly this reduction.**

The accuracy of a CFD simulation is fundamentally a function of the **fineness** of the discretization. As $\Delta x \to 0$ and $\Delta t \to 0$, the discrete solution approaches the exact continuous solution — a property called **convergence**.

---

## 1.5 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics Used in Discretization

The following standard quantities are recurrently used in discretization analysis:

- **Grid spacing** $\Delta x, \Delta y, \Delta z$ — measured in **meters (m)**
- **Time step** $\Delta t$ — measured in **seconds (s)**
- **Courant number** $C$ (dimensionless) — defined as $C = \dfrac{u \, \Delta t}{\Delta x}$
- **Reynolds number** $Re$ (dimensionless) — $\mathbf{Re} = \dfrac{\rho u L}{\mu}$
- **Peclet number** $Pe$ (dimensionless) — $Pe = Re \cdot Pr$
- **Cell Peclet number** $Pe_{cell} = \dfrac{\rho u \, \Delta x}{\Gamma}$ — governs the balance between convection and diffusion at the cell scale.
- **Truncation error order** — dimensionless, e.g., $\mathcal{O}(\Delta x^2)$, $\mathcal{O}(\Delta t)$.
- **Discretization error** $\varepsilon_d$ — dimensionless or in field units, defined as $\varepsilon_d = \phi_{exact} - \phi_{discrete}$.

---

## 1.6 Big-Picture Position of Discretization in the CFD Pipeline

The CFD solution pipeline has six canonical stages:

1. **Geometry & Domain Definition** — physical boundaries.
2. **Mathematical Model** — governing PDEs + initial/boundary conditions.
3. **Discretization** — *the focus of Module 2.*
4. **Algebraic System Solution** — direct or iterative solvers.
5. **Post-Processing** — visualization, contour plots, vector fields.
6. **Verification & Validation** — error analysis, grid independence study.

Discretization occupies the **central, indispensable** step.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Discretization — A Structured Logic Breakdown

### Pillar I — Finite Difference Method (FDM)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Philosophy of FDM:** Replace each derivative in the PDE by a finite-difference approximation derived from **Taylor series expansion** about a node on a structured grid.

**How it works (the 'Why' and the 'How'):**

- **Why:** Taylor's theorem gives us a polynomial expansion of a smooth function $u(x)$ around a point. By truncating after a finite number of terms and solving for the derivative, we obtain an algebraic formula.
- **How:** For a 1-D function $u(x)$, the Taylor series about $x_i$ is:

$$
u(x_i + \Delta x) = u(x_i) + \Delta x \frac{du}{dx}\bigg|_{x_i} + \frac{\Delta x^2}{2} \frac{d^2 u}{dx^2}\bigg|_{x_i} + \frac{\Delta x^3}{6} \frac{d^3 u}{dx^3}\bigg|_{x_i} + \mathcal{O}(\Delta x^4)
$$

- Truncating and rearranging yields three classical FDM approximations:

| Approximation Name | Formula | Truncation Error |
|---|---|---|
| Forward Difference (1st derivative) | $\left(\dfrac{du}{dx}\right)_i \approx \dfrac{u_{i+1} - u_i}{\Delta x}$ | $\mathcal{O}(\Delta x)$ |
| Backward Difference (1st derivative) | $\left(\dfrac{du}{dx}\right)_i \approx \dfrac{u_i - u_{i-1}}{\Delta x}$ | $\mathcal{O}(\Delta x)$ |
| Central Difference (1st derivative) | $\left(\dfrac{du}{dx}\right)_i \approx \dfrac{u_{i+1} - u_{i-1}}{2\Delta x}$ | $\mathcal{O}(\Delta x^2)$ |
| Central Difference (2nd derivative) | $\left(\dfrac{d^2 u}{dx^2}\right)_i \approx \dfrac{u_{i+1} - 2u_i + u_{i-1}}{\Delta x^2}$ | $\mathcal{O}(\Delta x^2)$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Central differencing** is symmetric about node $i$ and is **second-order accurate**. It is the workhorse of FDM discretization for second-order PDEs like the diffusion equation.

---

### Pillar II — Finite Volume Method (FVM)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Philosophy of FVM:** Integrate the governing PDE over a finite **control volume** (CV) surrounding each node, then apply the **Divergence Theorem** to convert volume integrals of divergence terms into surface (flux) integrals. Approximate the surface fluxes using neighboring nodal values.

**How it works:**

- **Why:** The integral form of the conservation laws is *exact* for any finite CV — even if the field is discontinuous. This makes FVM the most **physically conservative** method.
- **How:** For a general scalar transport equation:

$$
\frac{\partial}{\partial t} \int_V \rho \phi \, dV + \int_S \rho \phi \mathbf{u} \cdot \mathbf{n} \, dS = \int_S \Gamma \nabla \phi \cdot \mathbf{n} \, dS + \int_V S_\phi \, dV
$$

- The first term is the **transient** (rate of change) term.
- The second term is the **convective flux** through the CV faces.
- The third term is the **diffusive flux** through the CV faces.
- The fourth term is the **source/sink** contribution.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight (KTU High-Yield):** FVM guarantees **exact conservation** at the discrete level because the net flux leaving one CV is exactly the net flux entering the neighboring CV. FDM and FEM do NOT provide this property in general.

---

### Pillar III — Finite Element Method (FEM)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Philosophy of FEM:** Approximate the solution $\phi$ as a weighted sum of **basis (shape) functions** $N_j(x)$ defined over small elements: $\phi(x) \approx \sum_j N_j(x) \phi_j$. Substitute into the PDE, multiply by a test function $W_i$, integrate, and obtain a system $[K]\{\phi\} = \{F\}$.

**Key variants of FEM:**

- **Galerkin Method** — test functions = shape functions.
- **Petrov–Galerkin** — test and trial functions differ (used for convection-dominated flows via *Streamline Upwind Petrov–Galerkin, SUPG*).
- **Weighted Residual Method** — the general framework; Galerkin is a special case.

---

## 2.2 Classification of Discretization Errors

Discretization introduces error. KTU examiners expect students to know these three error types:

| Error Type | Symbol | Definition | Origin |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Truncation Error** | $\tau$ | Error from cutting off higher-order terms in Taylor series | Order of the finite-difference scheme |
| **Round-off Error** | $\varepsilon_r$ | Error from finite machine precision (e.g., 64-bit floating point) | Computer arithmetic limits |
| **Discretization Error** | $\varepsilon_d$ | Total deviation of numerical solution from exact PDE solution | Combined truncation + round-off |

The **total error** $E_{total} = \tau + \varepsilon_r$. As $\Delta x \to 0$, $\tau \to 0$ but $\varepsilon_r$ may grow (because more operations are performed). An **optimum grid spacing** minimizes $E_{total}$.

---

## 2.3 Consistency, Stability, and Convergence — The Lax Equivalence Theorem

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Lax Equivalence Theorem (KTU High-Yield):** *For a well-posed linear initial value problem, a consistent discretization scheme is stable if and only if it is convergent.*

| Property | Definition | Test Method |
|---|---|---|
| **Consistency** | Truncation error $\to 0$ as $\Delta x, \Delta t \to 0$ | Taylor series analysis |
| **Stability** | Numerical errors do not grow unboundedly during marching | Von Neumann (Fourier) analysis, Matrix method |
| **Convergence** | Numerical solution $\to$ exact solution as $\Delta x, \Delta t \to 0$ | Grid refinement study, Richardson extrapolation |

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All quantities in SI units unless otherwise noted. All derivatives assume smooth scalar field $\phi = \phi(x,y,z,t)$.**

### A. Taylor Series Expansions (Building Blocks of FDM)

| # | Expansion | Truncation |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\phi_{i+1} = \phi_i + \Delta x \left(\dfrac{d\phi}{dx}\right)_i + \dfrac{\Delta x^2}{2} \left(\dfrac{d^2 \phi}{dx^2}\right)_i + \dfrac{\Delta x^3}{6} \left(\dfrac{d^3 \phi}{dx^3}\right)_i + \cdots$ | $\mathcal{O}(\Delta x^4)$ |
| 2 | $\phi_{i-1} = \phi_i - \Delta x \left(\dfrac{d\phi}{dx}\right)_i + \dfrac{\Delta x^2}{2} \left(\dfrac{d^2 \phi}{dx^2}\right)_i - \dfrac{\Delta x^3}{6} \left(\dfrac{d^3 \phi}{dx^3}\right)_i + \cdots$ | $\mathcal{O}(\Delta x^4)$ |

### B. First Derivative Approximations

| # | Formula | Accuracy |
|---|---|---|
| 3 | $\left(\dfrac{d\phi}{dx}\right)_i \approx \dfrac{\phi_{i+1} - \phi_i}{\Delta x}$ | $\mathcal{O}(\Delta x)$ Forward |
| 4 | $\left(\dfrac{d\phi}{dx}\right)_i \approx \dfrac{\phi_i - \phi_{i-1}}{\Delta x}$ | $\mathcal{O}(\Delta x)$ Backward |
| 5 | $\left(\dfrac{d\phi}{dx}\right)_i \approx \dfrac{\phi_{i+1} - \phi_{i-1}}{2 \Delta x}$ | $\mathcal{O}(\Delta x^2)$ Central |
| 6 | $\left(\dfrac{d\phi}{dx}\right)_i \approx \dfrac{-\phi_{i+2} + 8 \phi_{i+1} - 8 \phi_{i-1} + \phi_{i-2}}{12 \Delta x}$ | $\mathcal{O}(\Delta x^4)$ Central |

### C. Second Derivative Approximations

| # | Formula | Accuracy |
|---|---|---|
| 7 | $\left(\dfrac{d^2 \phi}{dx^2}\right)_i \approx \dfrac{\phi_{i+1} - 2\phi_i + \phi_{i-1}}{\Delta x^2}$ | $\mathcal{O}(\Delta x^2)$ Central |
| 8 | $\left(\dfrac{d^2 \phi}{dx^2}\right)_i \approx \dfrac{\phi_{i+2} - 2\phi_{i+1} + \phi_i}{\Delta x^2}$ | $\mathcal{O}(\Delta x)$ Forward |
| 9 | $\left(\dfrac{d^2 \phi}{dx^2}\right)_i \approx \dfrac{\phi_i - 2\phi_{i-1} + \phi_{i-2}}{\Delta x^2}$ | $\mathcal{O}(\Delta x)$ Backward |

### D. Time Derivative Discretization

| # | Formula | Scheme Name | Order |
|---|---|---|---|
| 10 | $\left(\dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial t}\right)^n \approx \dfrac{\phi^{n+1} - \phi^n}{\Delta t}$ | Forward Euler (Explicit) | $\mathcal{O}(\Delta t)$ |
| 11 | $\left(\dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial t}\right)^n \approx \dfrac{\phi^n - \phi^{n-1}}{\Delta t}$ | Backward Euler (Implicit) | $\mathcal{O}(\Delta t)$ |
| 12 | $\left(\dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial t}\right)^n \approx \dfrac{\phi^{n+1} - \phi^{n-1}}{2\Delta t}$ | Central (Crank–Nicolson) | $\mathcal{O}(\Delta t^2)$ |

### E. Fundamental FVM Discretized Scalar Equation

| # | Equation | Description |
|---|---|---|
| 13 | $a_P \phi_P = a_E \phi_E + a_W \phi_W + a_N \phi_N + a_S \phi_S + a_T \phi_T + a_B \phi_B + b$ | Generic 3-D FVM coefficient form |
| 14 | $a_P = a_E + a_W + a_N + a_S + a_T + a_B - S_P \Delta V$ | Coefficient of central node (P) |
| 15 | $b = S_C \Delta V$ | Source contribution |

### F. Convection–Diffusion Discretization Coefficients

| # | Coefficient | Formula | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 16 | $a_E$ (1-D, uniform grid) | $D_e - \dfrac{F_e}{2}$ | $D_e = \Gamma / \delta x$, $F_e = \rho u_e$ |
| 17 | $a_W$ (1-D, uniform grid) | $D_w + \dfrac{F_w}{2}$ | — |
| 18 | $F_e = \rho u_e$ | Mass flux at east face | kg/(m²·s) |
| 19 | $D_e = \dfrac{\Gamma_e}{\delta x}$ | Diffusive conductance at east face | kg/(m·s) |
| 20 | $P_e^{cell} = \dfrac{F_e}{D_e} = \dfrac{\rho u_e \, \delta x}{\Gamma_e}$ | Cell Peclet number | $\vert P_e \vert \leq 2$ required for CD scheme stability |

### G. Stability & Accuracy Diagnostics

| # | Quantity | Formula | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 21 | Courant number | $C = \dfrac{u \, \Delta t}{\Delta x}$ | $< 1$ for explicit stability |
| 22 | Fourier number | $Fo = \dfrac{\alpha \Delta t}{\Delta x^2}$ | $\leq 0.5$ for explicit diffusion stability |
| 23 | Grid (mesh) ratio | $r = \dfrac{\Delta t \, \alpha}{\Delta x^2}$ | Controls numerical diffusion magnitude |

### H. Conservation & Error Metrics

| # | Metric | Formula | Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 24 | Discretization error | $\varepsilon_d = \phi_{exact} - \phi_{num}$ | Local error at a point |
| 25 | $L_2$ (RMS) norm | $L_2 = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (\phi_{exact,i} - \phi_{num,i})^2}$ | Global accuracy metric |
| 26 | $L_\infty$ norm | $L_\infty = \max_i \vert \phi_{exact,i} - \phi_{num,i} \vert$ | Worst-case error |
| 27 | Order of accuracy (Richardson) | $p = \dfrac{\log(\varepsilon_1 / \varepsilon_2)}{\log(\Delta x_1 / \Delta x_2)}$ | Confirms scheme order |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility of Discretization

- **Aerospace:** Discretization of the Navier–Stokes equations over airfoil/wing geometries enables prediction of lift, drag, and shock-wave location without wind-tunnel testing.
- **Automotive:** Underhood airflow, in-cylinder combustion, and external aerodynamics are routinely simulated using FVM-discretized RANS/LES models.
- **Biomedical:** Blood flow through arteries (potential for aneurysm rupture prediction) — solved using FVM in patient-specific geometries.
- **Power generation:** Discretization of heat conduction in nuclear reactor fuel rods, turbine blade cooling channels.
- **Weather & climate:** Atmospheric models discretize the primitive equations on a spherical grid.
- **Process industry:** Mixing tanks, chemical reactors — solved using discretized species-transport equations.
- **Civil/Environmental:** Pollutant dispersion in rivers and atmosphere — solved using discretized advection–diffusion equations.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** In the KTU 2024 syllabus, the question on "real-world applications" is often a 3-mark Part A question. Students should mention **at least two specific examples** with the **type of equation** solved.

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exhaustive Content Mandate:** Every algebraic step is shown. No skips, no shortcuts, no truncation. Each derivation is self-contained and verifiable.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Central Difference for the First Derivative (from Taylor Series)

**Goal:** Derive the central-difference approximation of $\dfrac{d\phi}{dx}$ at node $i$ with second-order accuracy.

**Starting point — Taylor series expansions about node $i$:**

$$
\phi_{i+1} = \phi_i + \Delta x \left(\frac{d\phi}{dx}\right)_i + \frac{\Delta x^2}{2!} \left(\frac{d^2 \phi}{dx^2}\right)_i + \frac{\Delta x^3}{3!} \left(\frac{d^3 \phi}{dx^3}\right)_i + \frac{\Delta x^4}{4!} \left(\frac{d^4 \phi}{dx^4}\right)_i + \mathcal{O}(\Delta x^5)
$$

$$
\phi_{i-1} = \phi_i - \Delta x \left(\frac{d\phi}{dx}\right)_i + \frac{\Delta x^2}{2!} \left(\frac{d^2 \phi}{dx^2}\right)_i - \frac{\Delta x^3}{3!} \left(\frac{d^3 \phi}{dx^3}\right)_i + \frac{\Delta x^4}{4!} \left(\frac{d^4 \phi}{dx^4}\right)_i + \mathcal{O}(\Delta x^5)
$$

**Step 1 — Subtract the two series:**

$$
\phi_{i+1} - \phi_{i-1} = 2 \Delta x \left(\frac{d\phi}{dx}\right)_i + 2 \cdot \frac{\Delta x^3}{6} \left(\frac{d^3 \phi}{dx^3}\right)_i + \mathcal{O}(\Delta x^5)
$$

**Explanation:** The even-power terms ($\Delta x^2$, $\Delta x^4$, …) cancel because they appear with the same sign in both series. The odd-power terms double in magnitude.

**Step 2 — Solve for the first derivative:**

$$
\left(\frac{d\phi}{dx}\right)_i = \frac{\phi_{i+1} - \phi_{i-1}}{2 \Delta x} - \frac{\Delta x^2}{6} \left(\frac{d^3 \phi}{dx^3}\right)_i + \mathcal{O}(\Delta x^4)
$$

**Explanation:** Divide by $2\Delta x$ and isolate the derivative. The leading neglected term is $\dfrac{\Delta x^2}{6}\left(\dfrac{d^3 \phi}{dx^3}\right)_i$, so the truncation error is $\mathcal{O}(\Delta x^2)$.

**Step 3 — Final approximation:**

$$
\left(\frac{d\phi}{dx}\right)_i \approx \frac{\phi_{i+1} - \phi_{i-1}}{2 \Delta x} + \mathcal{O}(\Delta x^2)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verifiability Check:** For $\phi(x) = x^2$, $\dfrac{d\phi}{dx} = 2x$. At $x_i = 1$, $\Delta x = 0.1$: $\phi_{i+1} = 1.21$, $\phi_{i-1} = 0.81$, so $\dfrac{1.21 - 0.81}{0.2} = 2.0$. **Exact** because the third derivative of $x^2$ is zero. The scheme is correct.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Central Difference for the Second Derivative (1-D Laplacian)

**Goal:** Derive the three-point stencil $\dfrac{d^2 \phi}{dx^2} \approx \dfrac{\phi_{i+1} - 2\phi_i + \phi_{i-1}}{\Delta x^2}$.

**Step 1 — Add the two Taylor series (instead of subtracting):**

$$
\phi_{i+1} + \phi_{i-1} = 2 \phi_i + \Delta x^2 \left(\frac{d^2 \phi}{dx^2}\right)_i + \frac{\Delta x^4}{12} \left(\frac{d^4 \phi}{dx^4}\right)_i + \mathcal{O}(\Delta x^6)
$$

**Explanation:** Adding eliminates the odd-power terms and doubles the even-power terms. The leading residual beyond $\Delta x^2$ is $\mathcal{O}(\Delta x^4)$.

**Step 2 — Isolate the second derivative:**

$$
\left(\frac{d^2 \phi}{dx^2}\right)_i = \frac{\phi_{i+1} - 2\phi_i + \phi_{i-1}}{\Delta x^2} - \frac{\Delta x^2}{12} \left(\frac{d^4 \phi}{dx^4}\right)_i + \mathcal{O}(\Delta x^4)
$$

**Step 3 — Final discretization:**

$$
\left(\frac{d^2 \phi}{dx^2}\right)_i \approx \frac{\phi_{i+1} - 2\phi_i + \phi_{i-1}}{\Delta x^2} + \mathcal{O}(\Delta x^2)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Truncation error order = 2.** This is the standard second-order accurate 1-D Laplacian stencil used in FDM and FVM for the diffusion term.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Forward and Backward Difference Schemes (with Order Analysis)

**Forward Difference** — Use Taylor expansion for $\phi_{i+1}$ and keep only the first derivative term:

$$
\phi_{i+1} = \phi_i + \Delta x \left(\frac{d\phi}{dx}\right)_i + \frac{\Delta x^2}{2} \left(\frac{d^2 \phi}{dx^2}\right)_i + \mathcal{O}(\Delta x^3)
$$

Solve for the first derivative:

$$
\left(\frac{d\phi}{dx}\right)_i = \frac{\phi_{i+1} - \phi_i}{\Delta x} - \frac{\Delta x}{2} \left(\frac{d^2 \phi}{dx^2}\right)_i + \mathcal{O}(\Delta x^2)
$$

Therefore:

$$
\left(\frac{d\phi}{dx}\right)_i \approx \frac{\phi_{i+1} - \phi_i}{\Delta x} + \mathcal{O}(\Delta x)
$$

**Backward Difference** — Use Taylor expansion for $\phi_{i-1}$:

$$
\phi_{i-1} = \phi_i - \Delta x \left(\frac{d\phi}{dx}\right)_i + \frac{\Delta x^2}{2} \left(\frac{d^2 \phi}{dx^2}\right)_i + \mathcal{O}(\Delta x^3)
$$

Solve for the first derivative:

$$
\left(\frac{d\phi}{dx}\right)_i = \frac{\phi_i - \phi_{i-1}}{\Delta x} + \frac{\Delta x}{2} \left(\frac{d^2 \phi}{dx^2}\right)_i + \mathcal{O}(\Delta x^2)
$$

Therefore:

$$
\left(\frac{d\phi}{dx}\right)_i \approx \frac{\phi_i - \phi_{i-1}}{\Delta x} + \mathcal{O}(\Delta x)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Order of accuracy of forward and backward differences = 1** (first-order). They are **biased** (one-sided) and used primarily for **boundary points** where central differencing cannot be applied.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Second-Order Central Difference for the Convection–Diffusion Equation

**Governing PDE (1-D, steady, constant property):**

$$
\rho u \frac{d\phi}{dx} = \Gamma \frac{d^2 \phi}{dx^2} + S
$$

**Step 1 — Discretize at node $i$ using central differences for both first and second derivatives:**

$$
\rho u \left(\frac{\phi_{i+1} - \phi_{i-1}}{2 \Delta x}\right) = \Gamma \left(\frac{\phi_{i+1} - 2 \phi_i + \phi_{i-1}}{\Delta x^2}\right) + S
$$

**Step 2 — Define the mass flux $F$ and diffusive conductance $D$:**

$$
F = \rho u, \quad D = \frac{\Gamma}{\Delta x}
$$

**Step 3 — Multiply through by $\Delta x^2$ to clear denominators:**

$$
F \frac{\Delta x}{2} \left(\phi_{i+1} - \phi_{i-1}\right) = D \left(\phi_{i+1} - 2 \phi_i + \phi_{i-1}\right) + S \Delta x^2
$$

**Step 4 — Collect coefficients of $\phi_{i+1}$, $\phi_i$, $\phi_{i-1}$:**

$$
\left(D - \frac{F \Delta x}{2}\right) \phi_{i+1} + (-2D) \phi_i + \left(D + \frac{F \Delta x}{2}\right) \phi_{i-1} = -S \Delta x^2
$$

**Step 5 — Express in the standard FVM coefficient form $a_P \phi_P = a_E \phi_E + a_W \phi_W + b$:**

$$
a_E = D - \frac{F \Delta x}{2}, \quad a_W = D + \frac{F \Delta x}{2}, \quad b = S \Delta x^2
$$

$$
a_P = a_E + a_W = 2D
$$

$$
\boxed{a_P \phi_P = a_E \phi_E + a_W \phi_W + b}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stability condition (Central Difference Scheme):** All coefficients $a_E, a_W$ must be **positive** for physical boundedness. This requires $D > \dfrac{F \Delta x}{2}$, i.e., $\dfrac{F \Delta x}{2 D} < 1$, i.e., the **cell Peclet number** $Pe = \dfrac{F}{D} = \dfrac{\rho u \Delta x}{\Gamma}$ must satisfy $\vert Pe \vert < 2$.

> [!WARNING]
> **If $\vert Pe \vert > 2$, the central difference scheme produces unphysical oscillations (wiggles).** For high-Peclet convection-dominated flows, the **upwind scheme** must be used.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Upwind Scheme (First-Order, Convection-Biased)

**Key Idea:** In the upwind scheme, the value of $\phi$ at a cell face is taken from the **upstream** node (the side from which the flow is coming).

**Case 1 — Flow from West to East ($u > 0$, or equivalently $F > 0$):**

- Face value at east face: $\phi_e = \phi_P$ (taken from upstream node P).
- Face value at west face: $\phi_w = \phi_W$ (taken from upstream node W).

**Discretized convective flux at east face:** $F_e \phi_e = F_e \phi_P$, where $F_e = (\rho u)_e$.

**Resulting coefficients:**

$$
a_E = D_e, \quad a_W = D_w + F_w, \quad a_P = a_E + a_W + (F_e - F_w)
$$

For uniform grid and constant properties:

$$
a_E = D, \quad a_W = D + F, \quad a_P = D + (D + F) = 2D + F
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Upwind scheme is unconditionally bounded** because $a_E, a_W > 0$ for $F > 0$. However, it introduces **false (numerical) diffusion** of order $\mathcal{O}(\Delta x)$, reducing accuracy.

**Case 2 — Flow from East to West ($u < 0$, or $F < 0$):**

- $\phi_e = \phi_E$ and $\phi_w = \phi_P$.
- Coefficients swap roles. Detailed derivation follows the same pattern with sign reversal.

---

## 3.6 Derivation 6 — Hybrid Scheme (Central + Upwind Switched by Peclet Number)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Hybrid Scheme of Spalding & Patankar** combines central and upwind schemes. It uses:
> - **Central difference** when $\vert Pe \vert < 2$.
> - **Pure upwind** when $\vert Pe \vert \geq 2$.

**The diffusion coefficient is artificially enhanced by a "false diffusion" term:**

$$
\Gamma_{eff} = \Gamma \left[1 + \frac{1}{2} \left( \frac{F}{D} - 2 \right) \right] = \Gamma \left[ 1 + 0.5 (Pe - 2) \right] \quad \text{for } Pe > 2
$$

For $Pe < 2$, $\Gamma_{eff} = \Gamma$ (no false diffusion added — central scheme suffices).

**Final coefficient in hybrid form (for $F > 0$):**

$$
a_E = D_e \cdot \max\left(0, 1 - 0.5 \left\lvert \frac{F_e}{D_e} \right\rvert\right) + \max\left(-F_e, 0\right)
$$

$$
a_W = D_w \cdot \max\left(0, 1 - 0.5 \left\lvert \frac{F_w}{D_w} \right\rvert\right) + \max\left(F_w, 0\right)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield:** The hybrid scheme is a **compromise** between accuracy (central) and stability (upwind). It is the **default convection discretization in ANSYS Fluent** for many basic cases.

---

## 3.7 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Discretization Schemes

| # | Scheme | Order (Space) | Stability (Cell Pe) | Boundedness | Computational Cost | Best For |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Central Difference (CDS) | 2 | $\vert Pe \vert < 2$ | No (oscillates) | Low | Diffusion-dominated |
| 2 | Upwind (UDS) | 1 | Always | Yes | Low | Convection-dominated, rough solutions |
| 3 | Hybrid | 1 (in practice) | Always | Yes | Low | Quick industrial simulations |
| 4 | Power Law | ~1.3 | Always | Yes | Low | Improvement over Hybrid |
| 5 | Exponential | High | Always | Yes | Medium | Benchmark for accuracy |
| 6 | Second-Order Upwind (SOU) | 2 | $\vert Pe \vert < 2$ (with limiter) | With limiter | Medium-High | Production CFD |
| 7 | QUICK | 3 | With limiter | With limiter | High | High-quality structured grids |
| 8 | MUSCL / TVD | 2+ | Bounded with limiter | Yes | High | High-speed, shock-capturing |

---

## 3.8 Worked Example — Discretizing a 1-D Steady Diffusion Equation with Source

**Problem:** Solve $\dfrac{d^2 \phi}{dx^2} = -2$ on $0 \leq x \leq 1$ with $\phi(0) = 0$ and $\phi(1) = 1$, using 5 equal intervals ($\Delta x = 0.2$).

**Exact solution:** $\phi(x) = -x^2 + 2x$ (verify by substitution: $\phi'' = -2$ ✓; $\phi(0) = 0$ ✓; $\phi(1) = 1$ ✓).

**Discretized form (central second derivative):**

$$
\frac{\phi_{i+1} - 2 \phi_i + \phi_{i-1}}{\Delta x^2} = -2
$$

**Substitute $\Delta x = 0.2$, so $\Delta x^2 = 0.04$:**

$$
\frac{\phi_{i+1} - 2 \phi_i + \phi_{i-1}}{0.04} = -2
$$

$$
\phi_{i+1} - 2 \phi_i + \phi_{i-1} = -0.08
$$

**Apply boundary conditions:** $\phi_0 = 0$ (at $x=0$) and $\phi_5 = 1$ (at $x=1$).

**Equation at $i=1$:** $\phi_2 - 2\phi_1 + \phi_0 = -0.08 \Rightarrow \phi_2 - 2\phi_1 = -0.08$
**Equation at $i=2$:** $\phi_3 - 2\phi_2 + \phi_1 = -0.08$
**Equation at $i=3$:** $\phi_4 - 2\phi_3 + \phi_2 = -0.08$
**Equation at $i=4$:** $\phi_5 - 2\phi_4 + \phi_3 = -0.08 \Rightarrow 1 - 2\phi_4 + \phi_3 = -0.08$

**System in matrix form $[A]\{\phi\} = \{b\}$:**

$$
\begin{bmatrix}
-2 & 1 & 0 & 0 \\
1 & -2 & 1 & 0 \\
0 & 1 & -2 & 1 \\
0 & 0 & 1 & -2
\end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix}
\phi_1 \\
\phi_2 \\
\phi_3 \\
\phi_4
\end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix}
-0.08 - \phi_0 \\
-0.08 \\
-0.08 \\
-0.08 - \phi_5
\end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix}
-0.08 \\
-0.08 \\
-0.08 \\
-1.08
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Solving (e.g., via Gaussian elimination or TDMA):**

| Node $i$ | $x_i$ | Numerical $\phi_i$ | Exact $\phi(x) = -x^2 + 2x$ | Error $\vert \phi_{num} - \phi_{exact} \vert$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0.0 | 0.0000 (BC) | 0.0000 | 0.0000 |
| 1 | 0.2 | 0.3200 | 0.3200 | 0.0000 |
| 2 | 0.4 | 0.6400 | 0.6400 | 0.0000 |
| 3 | 0.6 | 0.8800 | 0.8800 | 0.0000 |
| 4 | 0.8 | 1.0000 | 1.0000 | 0.0000 |
| 5 | 1.0 | 1.0000 (BC) | 1.0000 | 0.0000 |

> [!NOTE]
> **For this problem (a linear PDE with linear source), the central difference scheme gives the exact solution at all nodes.** This is because the truncation error involves the fourth derivative, which is identically zero for a polynomial of degree $\leq 3$ — and our solution is a polynomial of degree 2. This is a classic KTU-style verification example.

---

## 3.9 Worked Example — Stability of Explicit Time Marching for 1-D Diffusion

**Governing PDE:** $\dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial t} = \alpha \dfrac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial x^2}$

**Discretization (FTCS — Forward Time, Central Space):**

$$
\frac{\phi_i^{n+1} - \phi_i^n}{\Delta t} = \alpha \frac{\phi_{i+1}^n - 2\phi_i^n + \phi_{i-1}^n}{\Delta x^2}
$$

**Solve for $\phi_i^{n+1}$:**

$$
\phi_i^{n+1} = \phi_i^n + r \left( \phi_{i+1}^n - 2\phi_i^n + \phi_{i-1}^n \right)
$$

$$
\phi_i^{n+1} = (1 - 2r) \phi_i^n + r \phi_{i+1}^n + r \phi_{i-1}^n
$$

where $r = \dfrac{\alpha \Delta t}{\Delta x^2}$ is the **Fourier (diffusion) number**.

**Von Neumann Stability Analysis:** Substitute $\phi_i^n = G^n e^{i k i \Delta x}$:

$$
G = 1 - 2r + r e^{i k \Delta x} + r e^{-i k \Delta x} = 1 - 2r + 2r \cos(k \Delta x)
$$

$$
G = 1 - 4r \sin^2 \left( \frac{k \Delta x}{2} \right)
$$

For stability, we need $\vert G \vert \leq 1$ for all $k$. The worst case is $\sin^2(\cdot) = 1$:

$$
\boxed{r \leq \frac{1}{2} \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad \Delta t \leq \frac{\Delta x^2}{2\alpha}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the famous diffusion stability condition.** For explicit schemes, the time step is **quadratically constrained** by the grid spacing — making explicit methods very expensive for fine grids in 3-D.

---

## 3.10 Worked Example — Courant Condition for 1-D Linear Convection

**Governing PDE:** $\dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial t} + u \dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial x} = 0$

**FTCS discretization:**

$$
\frac{\phi_i^{n+1} - \phi_i^n}{\Delta t} + u \frac{\phi_{i+1}^n - \phi_{i-1}^n}{2 \Delta x} = 0
$$

$$
\phi_i^{n+1} = \phi_i^n - \frac{C}{2} \left( \phi_{i+1}^n - \phi_{i-1}^n \right)
$$

where $C = \dfrac{u \Delta t}{\Delta x}$ is the **Courant number**.

**Von Neumann Analysis:** $\phi_i^n = G^n e^{i k i \Delta x}$:

$$
G = 1 - \frac{C}{2}(e^{ik\Delta x} - e^{-ik\Delta x}) = 1 - i C \sin(k \Delta x)
$$

$$
\vert G \vert^2 = 1 + C^2 \sin^2(k \Delta x) \geq 1
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **The FTCS scheme for pure convection is UNCONDITIONALLY UNSTABLE.** The amplification factor $\vert G \vert > 1$ for any nonzero $C$ and any nonzero $k$. This is why central differencing cannot be used for pure convection — we must use **upwind**, **Lax–Wendroff**, or other stable schemes.

**Stable alternative — Upwind (for $u > 0$):**

$$
\phi_i^{n+1} = \phi_i^n - C (\phi_i^n - \phi_{i-1}^n) = (1 - C) \phi_i^n + C \phi_{i-1}^n
$$

Von Neumann gives $G = 1 - C + C e^{-ik\Delta x} = 1 - C(1 - \cos(k\Delta x)) - i C \sin(k\Delta x)$.

For $\vert G \vert \leq 1$, the condition is $0 \leq C \leq 1$, i.e., the **Courant–Friedrichs–Lewy (CFL) condition**:

$$
\boxed{C = \frac{u \Delta t}{\Delta x} \leq 1}
$$

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram A — Discretization Pipeline (CFD Workflow)

This Mermaid block depicts the complete CFD pipeline with discretization as the central operation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Physical Problem] --> A1[Define Geometry and Domain]
    A1 --> A2[Mathematical Model Selection]
    A2 --> A3[Governing PDEs and BCs]
    A3 --> B1[Domain Discretization Mesh Generation]
    B1 --> B2[Equation Discretization Taylor Series / FVM Balance]
    B2 --> B3[Algebraic System Assembly aP phiP equals sum a nb phi nb plus b]
    B3 --> C1[Linear Solver Direct or Iterative]
    C1 --> C2[Post Processing Visualization]
    C2 --> C3[Verification and Validation]
    C3 --> A0
    B1 -.-> D1[FDM Structured Grid]
    B1 -.-> D2[FVM Control Volume]
    B1 -.-> D3[FEM Unstructured Mesh]
    B2 -.-> E1[Order of Accuracy]
    B2 -.-> E2[Stability Condition]
    B2 -.-> E3[Conservation Property]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Guide:** Domain Discretization (B1) and Equation Discretization (B2) together constitute the "Discretization" module. The dashed arrows D1/D2/D3 represent the three method choices (FDM, FVM, FEM) that branch out from domain discretization.

---

## 4.2 Diagram B — Comparison of FDM, FVM, and FEM Discretization Strategies

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    F0[Continuous Domain and PDE] --> FDM[Finite Difference Method]
    F0 --> FVM[Finite Volume Method]
    F0 --> FEM[Finite Element Method]
    FDM --> FDM1[Structured Grid Nodes]
    FDM1 --> FDM2[Taylor Series Approximations of Derivatives]
    FDM2 --> FDM3[Algebraic System at Nodes]
    FVM --> FVM1[Control Volume Mesh]
    FVM1 --> FVM2[Integral Balance Divergence Theorem]
    FVM2 --> FVM3[Flux Balance at Faces]
    FVM3 --> FVM4[Algebraic System per Cell]
    FEM --> FEM1[Unstructured Elements Triangles Quads Tetra Hexa]
    FEM1 --> FEM2[Shape Function Interpolation]
    FEM2 --> FEM3[Weighted Residual Integration]
    FEM3 --> FEM4[Stiffness Matrix and Load Vector]
    FDM3 --> G1[Solution phi]
    FVM4 --> G1
    FEM4 --> G1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway:** All three methods start from the same continuous PDE, but they differ in (a) how they subdivide the domain and (b) how they convert the PDE to algebra. FDM operates at points, FVM at control volumes, and FEM at elements with shape functions.

---

## 4.3 Diagram C — One-Dimensional Grid Stencil Showing FDM and FVM Nodes

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    N1[i minus 2] --- N2[i minus 1] --- N3[i] --- N4[i plus 1] --- N5[i plus 2]
    N3 --> N3a[Central Node P]
    N2 --> N2a[West Node W]
    N4 --> N4a[East Node E]
    N2 --- CV1[Control Volume West Face]
    N3 --- CV2[Control Volume Center]
    N4 --- CV3[Control Volume East Face]
    CV1 --> CV1a[Face w]
    CV2 --> CV2a[Node P]
    CV3 --> CV3a[Face e]
    CV1a --> FLX1[Flux Fw Dw]
    CV3a --> FLX2[Flux Fe De]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Guide:** In FVM, the control volume around node $i$ (P) has its center at node $i$ and extends halfway to nodes $i-1$ (W) and $i+1$ (E). The east and west faces are at midpoints. FDM uses the same node positions but does not explicitly use control volumes.

---

## 4.4 Diagram D — Decision Tree for Choosing a Convection Discretization Scheme

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1[Start: Convection Diffusion Problem] --> Q2{Peclet Number Pe less than 2}
    Q2 -->|Yes| Q3[Use Central Difference CDS]
    Q2 -->|No| Q4{Need High Accuracy}
    Q4 -->|Yes and Structured Grid| Q5[Use QUICK or SOU with Limiter]
    Q4 -->|Yes and Unstructured| Q6[Use TVD or MUSCL]
    Q4 -->|No| Q7[Use Upwind UDS or Hybrid]
    Q3 --> END[Discretized Algebraic System]
    Q5 --> END
    Q6 --> END
    Q7 --> END
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Examiners often ask, "When would you prefer the upwind scheme over the central difference scheme?" The decision hinges on the cell Peclet number and the required accuracy.

---

## 4.5 Diagram E — Block-Level Functional Architecture of an FVM Solver

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P1[Input Geometry and Mesh] --> P2[Compute Cell Centers and Face Areas]
    P2 --> P3[Compute Geometric Quantities Volumes and Distances]
    P3 --> P4[Assemble Coefficient Matrix aP aE aW etc]
    P4 --> P5[Apply Boundary Conditions Dirichlet and Neumann]
    P5 --> P6[Solve Linear System Direct or Iterative]
    P6 --> P7[Update Field Variables]
    P7 --> P8{Converged}
    P8 -->|No| P4
    P8 -->|Yes| P9[Output Solution]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Guide:** This is the inner loop of a typical FVM-based CFD solver (e.g., SIMPLE algorithm in ANSYS Fluent). The "converged" check uses residuals — typically the L2 norm of the imbalance in each conservation equation falls below a threshold (e.g., $10^{-6}$).

---

## 4.6 Diagram F — Truncation Error and Order of Accuracy Visualization

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    F0[Smooth phi of x] --> F1[Taylor Series Expansion about node i]
    F1 --> F2[Truncate at Order N]
    F2 --> F3[Approximate Derivative]
    F3 --> F4[Truncation Error Tau equals O of Delta x to the power p]
    F4 --> F5[Scheme Order equals p]
    F1 -. include 1 term .-> F4a[Forward Backward Difference p equals 1]
    F1 -. include 2 terms .-> F4b[Central Difference 2nd Derivative p equals 2]
    F1 -. include 3 terms .-> F4c[4th Order Central Stencil p equals 4]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Order of accuracy is determined by the lowest-order term in the neglected Taylor series remainder.** Higher order = smaller error for the same $\Delta x$ but requires more neighboring nodes (wider stencil).
