# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Structure (Module 2 — Discretization):**
> - **Part A (2 × 3 = 6 marks):** Short-answer conceptual questions.
> - **Part B (1 × 14 marks, internal choice):** One full question with two sub-parts (a) and (b), each worth 7 marks.

---

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define discretization. With a neat sketch, explain the concept of grid generation as a pre-requisite for discretization.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Discretization is the process of converting the continuous governing partial differential equations of fluid flow into a system of algebraic equations by replacing continuous derivatives with finite-difference approximations (FDM), integral balances over control volumes (FVM), or weighted residuals over elements (FEM).

**Grid Generation (2 marks):** Grid generation is the geometric act of subdividing the continuous flow domain $\Omega$ into a finite number of sub-domains — nodes (in FDM), control volumes (in FVM), or elements (in FEM). The grid must be:

- **Fine enough** in regions of high gradient (boundary layers, shocks) to resolve the flow features.
- **Coarse enough** in regions of mild variation to keep computational cost manageable.
- **Structured** (i-j-k indexable) for FDM; **unstructured** (irregular connectivity) for complex geometries, best suited for FVM/FEM.

A typical 1-D grid consists of nodes $x_0, x_1, \ldots, x_N$ with uniform spacing $\Delta x$ or non-uniform spacing governed by a stretching function. The grid is the **scaffolding** on which all subsequent discretization is performed.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between Finite Difference Method (FDM) and Finite Volume Method (FVM). Mention any two advantages of FVM over FDM.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | FDM | FVM |
|---|---|---|
| Domain representation | Point values on a structured grid | Average values over control volumes |
| Governing form used | Differential form (pointwise PDE) | Integral form (conservation form) |
| Conservation | Not guaranteed at discrete level | **Exactly conservative** by construction |
| Geometry handling | Limited to simple/structured geometries | Handles complex unstructured meshes |
| Boundary condition application | Requires careful treatment of normal derivatives | Applied naturally as fluxes at boundary faces |
| Approximation strategy | Taylor series expansion of derivatives | Flux balance at control volume faces |

**Two advantages of FVM over FDM (2 marks):**

1. **Exact conservation** — FVM ensures that the net flux leaving one control volume equals the net flux entering its neighbor. This property is satisfied identically at the discrete level, regardless of grid quality. FDM does not provide this guarantee.

2. **Geometric flexibility** — FVM works on arbitrary polyhedral cells (tetrahedra, prisms, hexahedra, polyhedra) and handles complex industrial geometries (engine cylinders, aircraft fuselage, blood vessels) that would be nearly impossible to mesh with a structured FDM grid.

> [!NOTE]
> **Bonus point (1 mark if asked):** FVM is the **industry standard** for commercial CFD codes (ANSYS Fluent, OpenFOAM, STAR-CD) because of the two advantages above.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Question with Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper PEMET521]**

**(a)** Starting from the Taylor series expansion, derive the second-order accurate central difference approximation for the second derivative $\dfrac{d^2 \phi}{dx^2}$ at node $i$ on a uniform grid with spacing $\Delta x$. Show clearly that the truncation error is $\mathcal{O}(\Delta x^2)$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Using the result of part (a), discretize the 1-D steady convection–diffusion equation $\rho u \dfrac{d\phi}{dx} = \Gamma \dfrac{d^2 \phi}{dx^2}$ on a uniform grid using the **central difference scheme** for both convection and diffusion. Show that the discretized equation can be written in the form $a_P \phi_P = a_E \phi_E + a_W \phi_W$, and derive explicit expressions for the coefficients $a_P$, $a_E$, and $a_W$. State the **stability condition** for the scheme in terms of the cell Peclet number. **(7 marks)**

---

### Solution to Question A

#### Part (a) — Derivation of Central Difference for Second Derivative (7 marks)

**Step 1 (2 marks) — Write the Taylor series expansions about node $i$:**

$$
\phi_{i+1} = \phi_i + \Delta x \left(\frac{d\phi}{dx}\right)_i + \frac{\Delta x^2}{2!} \left(\frac{d^2 \phi}{dx^2}\right)_i + \frac{\Delta x^3}{3!} \left(\frac{d^3 \phi}{dx^3}\right)_i + \frac{\Delta x^4}{4!} \left(\frac{d^4 \phi}{dx^4}\right)_i + \mathcal{O}(\Delta x^5)
$$

$$
\phi_{i-1} = \phi_i - \Delta x \left(\frac{d\phi}{dx}\right)_i + \frac{\Delta x^2}{2!} \left(\frac{d^2 \phi}{dx^2}\right)_i - \frac{\Delta x^3}{3!} \left(\frac{d^3 \phi}{dx^3}\right)_i + \frac{\Delta x^4}{4!} \left(\frac{d^4 \phi}{dx^4}\right)_i + \mathcal{O}(\Delta x^5)
$$

**Step 2 (2 marks) — Add the two expansions to eliminate the odd-power terms:**

$$
\phi_{i+1} + \phi_{i-1} = 2 \phi_i + \Delta x^2 \left(\frac{d^2 \phi}{dx^2}\right)_i + \frac{\Delta x^4}{12} \left(\frac{d^4 \phi}{dx^4}\right)_i + \mathcal{O}(\Delta x^6)
$$

**Explanation:** Odd-power terms cancel (their signs are opposite in the two series), even-power terms double.

**Step 3 (2 marks) — Solve for the second derivative:**

$$
\left(\frac{d^2 \phi}{dx^2}\right)_i = \frac{\phi_{i+1} - 2\phi_i + \phi_{i-1}}{\Delta x^2} - \frac{\Delta x^2}{12} \left(\frac{d^4 \phi}{dx^4}\right)_i + \mathcal{O}(\Delta x^4)
$$

**Step 4 (1 mark) — Final discretization and order of accuracy:**

$$
\boxed{\left(\frac{d^2 \phi}{dx^2}\right)_i \approx \frac{\phi_{i+1} - 2\phi_i + \phi_{i-1}}{\Delta x^2} + \mathcal{O}(\Delta x^2)}
$$

The leading neglected term is $\dfrac{\Delta x^2}{12}\left(\dfrac{d^4 \phi}{dx^4}\right)_i$, so the scheme is **second-order accurate** in space.

---

#### Part (b) — Discretization of 1-D Convection–Diffusion Equation (7 marks)

**Step 1 (1 mark) — Write the PDE:**

$$
\rho u \frac{d\phi}{dx} = \Gamma \frac{d^2 \phi}{dx^2}
$$

**Step 2 (2 marks) — Apply central difference approximations at node $i$:**

$$
\rho u \left( \frac{\phi_{i+1} - \phi_{i-1}}{2 \Delta x} \right) = \Gamma \left( \frac{\phi_{i+1} - 2 \phi_i + \phi_{i-1}}{\Delta x^2} \right)
$$

**Step 3 (1 mark) — Define F and D:**

$$
F = \rho u, \quad D = \frac{\Gamma}{\Delta x}
$$

Substituting and multiplying through by $\Delta x^2$:

$$
\frac{F \Delta x}{2} (\phi_{i+1} - \phi_{i-1}) = D (\phi_{i+1} - 2\phi_i + \phi_{i-1})
$$

**Step 4 (2 marks) — Collect coefficients:**

$$
\left(D - \frac{F \Delta x}{2}\right) \phi_{i+1} + (D + \frac{F \Delta x}{2}) \phi_{i-1} - 2 D \phi_i = 0
$$

Rewriting as $a_P \phi_P = a_E \phi_E + a_W \phi_W$:

$$
2 D \phi_i = \left(D - \frac{F \Delta x}{2}\right) \phi_{i+1} + \left(D + \frac{F \Delta x}{2}\right) \phi_{i-1}
$$

**Step 5 (1 mark) — Final coefficient expressions:**

$$
\boxed{a_P = 2D = \frac{2\Gamma}{\Delta x}, \quad a_E = D - \frac{F \Delta x}{2}, \quad a_W = D + \frac{F \Delta x}{2}}
$$

**Stability condition (from the boundedness requirement that both $a_E > 0$ and $a_W > 0$):**

For $a_E > 0$: $D > \dfrac{F \Delta x}{2}$ ⟹ $\dfrac{F \Delta x}{2 D} < 1$ ⟹ $\dfrac{F}{D} \cdot \Delta x < 2$.

Recall cell Peclet number: $Pe = \dfrac{F}{D} = \dfrac{\rho u \Delta x}{\Gamma}$. The condition becomes:

$$
\boxed{\vert Pe \vert = \left\lvert \frac{\rho u \Delta x}{\Gamma} \right\rvert < 2}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students frequently forget to take the **absolute value** of the Peclet number. The condition is on $\vert Pe \vert$, not $Pe$. A negative $u$ (flow in the $-x$ direction) swaps the roles of $a_E$ and $a_W$, and the condition $\vert Pe \vert < 2$ must still hold. Examiners deduct 1 mark for this oversight.

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) — **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper PEMET521]**

**(a)** Explain the **Finite Volume Method** (FVM) of discretization. Starting from the integral form of the general scalar transport equation, show how the FVM reduces it to the discrete balance $a_P \phi_P = \sum_{nb} a_{nb} \phi_{nb} + b$, defining all terms. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider the 1-D unsteady heat conduction equation $\dfrac{\partial T}{\partial t} = \alpha \dfrac{\partial^2 T}{\partial x^2}$ on $0 \leq x \leq L$ with $T(0,t) = T_0$ and $\dfrac{\partial T}{\partial x}\bigg|_{x=L} = 0$. Discretize this equation using an **explicit forward-time central-space (FTCS)** scheme. Derive the stability limit on the time step. What is the maximum allowable $\Delta t$ if $L = 1$ m, $\alpha = 10^{-5}$ m²/s, and $\Delta x = 0.01$ m? **(7 marks)**

---

### Solution to Question B

#### Part (a) — Finite Volume Method Derivation (7 marks)

**Step 1 (1 mark) — Integral form of the transport equation:**

The general scalar transport equation in integral form over a fixed control volume $V$ bounded by closed surface $S$ with outward unit normal $\mathbf{n}$:

$$
\int_V \frac{\partial (\rho \phi)}{\partial t} dV + \int_S \rho \phi (\mathbf{u} \cdot \mathbf{n}) dS = \int_S \Gamma (\nabla \phi \cdot \mathbf{n}) dS + \int_V S_\phi dV
$$

**Explanation of each term:**

- $\int_V \dfrac{\partial(\rho \phi)}{\partial t} dV$ — Rate of accumulation of $\phi$ inside the CV.
- $\int_S \rho \phi (\mathbf{u} \cdot \mathbf{n}) dS$ — Net convective flux of $\phi$ out of the CV through its boundary.
- $\int_S \Gamma (\nabla \phi \cdot \mathbf{n}) dS$ — Net diffusive flux of $\phi$ out of the CV through its boundary.
- $\int_V S_\phi dV$ — Net volumetric source of $\phi$ inside the CV.

**Step 2 (2 marks) — Discretization of each term:**

For a small CV around node $P$ with neighbors $E, W$ (in 1-D), the integrals become:

- **Transient term:** $\int_V \dfrac{\partial(\rho \phi)}{\partial t} dV \approx \rho_P \dfrac{\phi_P^{n+1} - \phi_P^n}{\Delta t} \Delta V$

- **Convective flux:** $\int_S \rho \phi (\mathbf{u} \cdot \mathbf{n}) dS = (\rho u \phi)_e A_e - (\rho u \phi)_w A_w = F_e \phi_e - F_w \phi_w$

- **Diffusive flux:** $\int_S \Gamma (\nabla \phi \cdot \mathbf{n}) dS = \Gamma_e A_e \dfrac{\phi_E - \phi_P}{\delta x_{PE}} - \Gamma_w A_w \dfrac{\phi_P - \phi_W}{\delta x_{WP}} = D_e (\phi_E - \phi_P) - D_w (\phi_P - \phi_W)$

- **Source term:** $\int_V S_\phi dV = (S_C + S_P \phi_P) \Delta V$ (linearized source)

**Step 3 (2 marks) — Substitute and rearrange:**

$$
\rho_P \Delta V \frac{\phi_P^{n+1} - \phi_P^n}{\Delta t} + F_e \phi_e - F_w \phi_w = D_e (\phi_E - \phi_P) - D_w (\phi_P - \phi_W) + (S_C + S_P \phi_P) \Delta V
$$

Rearranging to collect all $\phi_P$ on the LHS and neighbor values on the RHS:

$$
\phi_P \left[ \frac{\rho_P \Delta V}{\Delta t} + D_e + D_w - F_e + F_w - S_P \Delta V \right] = D_e \phi_E + D_w \phi_W + F_w \phi_w - F_e \phi_e + S_C \Delta V + \frac{\rho_P \Delta V}{\Delta t} \phi_P^n
$$

For **steady** problems, the transient term vanishes and we obtain:

$$
\boxed{a_P \phi_P = a_E \phi_E + a_W \phi_W + b}
$$

where:

$$
a_E = D_e - F_e, \quad a_W = D_w + F_w, \quad b = S_C \Delta V
$$

$$
a_P = a_E + a_W + (F_e - F_w) - S_P \Delta V
$$

**Step 4 (1 mark) — Geometric interpretation:**

FVM works by enforcing conservation **exactly** over each control volume. The net flux leaving one cell is exactly the net flux entering the neighboring cell, so the **global conservation** property is guaranteed to machine precision.

**Step 5 (1 mark) — Comparison highlight:**

Unlike FDM (which approximates derivatives at points) and FEM (which solves a weighted residual formulation), FVM directly enforces the **integral conservation statement**, making it the most physically intuitive and conservative of the three methods.

---

#### Part (b) — FTCS Discretization and Stability (7 marks)

**Step 1 (1 mark) — Write the PDE:**

$$
\frac{\partial T}{\partial t} = \alpha \frac{\partial^2 T}{\partial x^2}, \quad 0 \leq x \leq L
$$

**Step 2 (2 marks) — Discretize using FTCS:**

Forward-time, central-space approximation:

$$
\frac{T_i^{n+1} - T_i^n}{\Delta t} = \alpha \frac{T_{i+1}^n - 2 T_i^n + T_{i-1}^n}{\Delta x^2}
$$

Solving for $T_i^{n+1}$:

$$
T_i^{n+1} = T_i^n + \frac{\alpha \Delta t}{\Delta x^2} \left( T_{i+1}^n - 2 T_i^n + T_{i-1}^n \right)
$$

Define the **Fourier (mesh) number** $r = \dfrac{\alpha \Delta t}{\Delta x^2}$:

$$
T_i^{n+1} = (1 - 2r) T_i^n + r T_{i+1}^n + r T_{i-1}^n
$$

**Step 3 (2 marks) — Von Neumann stability analysis:**

Assume $T_i^n = G^n e^{ik i \Delta x}$. Substituting:

$$
G = (1 - 2r) + r e^{ik\Delta x} + r e^{-ik\Delta x} = 1 - 2r + 2r \cos(k\Delta x)
$$

Using the identity $1 - \cos(\theta) = 2 \sin^2(\theta / 2)$:

$$
G = 1 - 4r \sin^2\left(\frac{k \Delta x}{2}\right)
$$

The amplification factor is bounded by $\vert G \vert \leq 1$ for all $k$. The worst case is $\sin^2(\cdot) = 1$ (i.e., $k \Delta x = \pi$):

$$
G_{min} = 1 - 4r
$$

For stability: $-1 \leq 1 - 4r \leq 1$, giving $r \leq 0.5$ and $r \geq 0$. Since $r$ is positive (positive $\alpha$, positive $\Delta t$, positive $\Delta x^2$):

$$
\boxed{r = \frac{\alpha \Delta t}{\Delta x^2} \leq \frac{1}{2}}
$$

or equivalently:

$$
\boxed{\Delta t \leq \frac{\Delta x^2}{2 \alpha}}
$$

**Step 4 (2 marks) — Numerical evaluation:**

Given $L = 1$ m, $\alpha = 10^{-5}$ m²/s, $\Delta x = 0.01$ m:

$$
\Delta t_{max} = \frac{\Delta x^2}{2 \alpha} = \frac{(0.01)^2}{2 \times 10^{-5}} = \frac{10^{-4}}{2 \times 10^{-5}} = \frac{10^{-4}}{2 \times 10^{-5}} = 5 \text{ seconds}
$$

$$
\boxed{\Delta t_{max} = 5 \text{ s}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity check:** Number of grid points = $L/\Delta x + 1 = 100/1 + 1 = 101$ nodes. For a transient simulation, the total time to reach steady state is of order $L^2 / \alpha = 10^5$ s. With $\Delta t = 5$ s, we need $2 \times 10^4$ time steps. This is feasible for explicit method on a small 1-D grid but becomes prohibitive for 3-D fine grids.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls on Discretization Questions:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting the truncation error term** in the derivation (lose 1–2 marks). Always end Taylor series derivations with the explicit "$\mathcal{O}(\Delta x^n)$" term.
> 
> 2. **Sign errors in upwind discretization.** When $u < 0$, the upwind direction flips. Students often write the same coefficients regardless of flow direction. Carefully re-derive for each case.
> 
> 3. **Confusing cell Peclet number with global Peclet number.** Cell Peclet uses $\Delta x$ (local grid spacing); global Peclet uses $L$ (domain length). Examiners specifically test this distinction.
> 
> 4. **Missing the stability condition on a question that explicitly asks for it.** Even if the discretization is correct, omitting $r \leq 1/2$ or $\vert Pe \vert < 2$ results in a 1–2 mark deduction.
> 
> 5. **Not stating assumptions clearly** (e.g., uniform grid, constant $\rho, u, \Gamma$). Examiners look for these qualifiers.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Summary for Last-Minute Revision — KTU Module 2, Topic: Discretization**

### **Conceptual Foundations**

- [x] Discretization = conversion of continuous PDEs into solvable algebraic equations.
- [x] Three methods: **FDM** (point-based), **FVM** (volume-based), **FEM** (element-based).
- [x] FVM is the **industry standard** because of exact conservation and geometric flexibility.

### **Taylor Series (FDM Backbone)**

- [x] Forward difference: 1st order accurate, $\mathcal{O}(\Delta x)$.
- [x] Backward difference: 1st order accurate, $\mathcal{O}(\Delta x)$.
- [x] Central difference (1st derivative): 2nd order, $\mathcal{O}(\Delta x^2)$.
- [x] Central difference (2nd derivative): 2nd order, $\mathcal{O}(\Delta x^2)$.

### **FVM Generic Discrete Equation**

- [x] $a_P \phi_P = a_E \phi_E + a_W \phi_W + a_N \phi_N + a_S \phi_S + a_T \phi_T + a_B \phi_B + b$
- [x] Coefficient of P: $a_P = \sum a_{nb} - S_P \Delta V$
- [x] Source term: $b = S_C \Delta V$

### **Convection Discretization Schemes (in order of accuracy)**

- [x] **Upwind (UDS)** — 1st order, unconditionally stable, false diffusion.
- [x] **Central (CDS)** — 2nd order, stable only if $\vert Pe \vert < 2$.
- [x] **Hybrid** — switches between CDS and UDS at $\vert Pe \vert = 2$.
- [x] **Power Law** — smoother transition than Hybrid.
- [x] **QUICK** — 3rd order, requires limiter for boundedness.
- [x] **SOU / MUSCL / TVD** — high-order, production-grade, used in industry.

### **Stability Conditions (Must Memorize)**

- [x] **Diffusion (explicit):** $r = \dfrac{\alpha \Delta t}{\Delta x^2} \leq \dfrac{1}{2}$.
- [x] **Convection (explicit upwind):** $C = \dfrac{u \Delta t}{\Delta x} \leq 1$.
- [x] **Convection (CDS):** $\left\lvert Pe \right\rvert = \left\lvert \dfrac{\rho u \Delta x}{\Gamma} \right\rvert < 2$.

### **Error Concepts**

- [x] **Truncation error** = from dropped Taylor terms.
- [x] **Round-off error** = from finite machine precision.
- [x] **Discretization error** = sum of both.
- [x] **Grid independence** = when refining $\Delta x$ no longer changes the solution significantly.
- [x] **Richardson extrapolation** = $p = \log(\varepsilon_1 / \varepsilon_2) / \log(\Delta x_1 / \Delta x_2)$.

### **Lax Equivalence Theorem**

- [x] For a well-posed linear IVP: **Consistency + Stability ⟺ Convergence**.

### **Three Discretization Layers**

- [x] **Domain** — mesh generation.
- [x] **Equation** — derivative approximation.
- [x] **Solution** — algebraic system solve.

### **Practical Recommendations**

- [x] Use **CDS** when $\vert Pe \vert < 2$ (diffusion-dominated or fine grid).
- [x] Use **upwind / hybrid** for high $Pe$ (convection-dominated).
- [x] Refine the grid in **boundary layers, shocks, regions of high gradient**.
- [x] Always perform a **grid independence study** before trusting simulation results.
- [x] Report the **order of accuracy** of your chosen scheme in any CFD report.

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Module 2 Discretization Notes.** For related topics in KTU PEMET521 (grid generation, solvers, turbulence modeling, SIMPLE algorithm), refer to the subsequent KTU-Premier notes in this series.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Converting Derivatives to Their Finite Difference Forms
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET521 - COMPUTATIONAL FLUID DYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Converting Derivatives to Their Finite Difference Forms

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Finite Difference Approximation (FDA)** is a numerical technique used to approximate the derivatives of a function by replacing continuous differentials with discrete algebraic differences evaluated at neighboring grid points on a computational mesh.

In the context of **Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD)**, the governing Partial Differential Equations (PDEs) — namely the continuity equation, Navier–Stokes equations, and energy equation — contain continuous spatial and temporal derivatives. Since a digital computer can only store and process *discrete* values at *discrete* points (nodes of a grid), these continuous derivatives **must** be replaced by algebraic finite difference expressions before numerical solution is possible.

The three foundational finite difference approximations for the first derivative $\dfrac{\partial f}{\partial x}$ are:

1. **Forward Difference (FDS)** — Uses points $i$ and $i+1$
2. **Backward Difference (BDS)** — Uses points $i-1$ and $i$
3. **Central Difference (CDS)** — Uses points $i-1$ and $i+1$

where $i$ denotes the index of the reference node and $\Delta x$ is the uniform grid spacing.

---

## Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Plain-English Intuition (The Speedometer Analogy):**
> Imagine you are driving a car on a highway. You want to know your **instantaneous speed** at exactly 10:00 AM. Unfortunately, your speedometer only works at whole-minute intervals. So you check the odometer at 10:00 AM and 10:01 AM, divide the distance traveled by 60 seconds, and *approximate* the speed at 10:00 AM.

In this analogy:
- The **true derivative** $\dfrac{df}{dt}$ is your *exact* instantaneous speed (an unmeasurable ideal).
- The **finite difference** is your approximation using only the readings at 10:00 and 10:01.
- If you use the **next** reading (10:01), you are doing a *forward difference*.
- If you use the **previous** reading (09:59), you are doing a *backward difference*.
- If you take the **average** of 09:59 and 10:01, you are doing a *central difference* — which is why it is more accurate (you are "centering" the estimate on the actual time of interest).

This is precisely the philosophical foundation of finite difference calculus — we trade exactness for computability, and the *order of accuracy* tells us how much error we are willing to tolerate.

---

## Physical Constants and Standard Metrics in Bold

- **Grid spacing (Δx):** the uniform distance between adjacent nodes in meters (m).
- **Order of accuracy (p):** the power of $\Delta x$ in the leading truncation error term; higher $p$ means faster error decay as the grid is refined.
- **Round-off error:** floating-point arithmetic error proportional to **machine epsilon ($\varepsilon$)** (typically $\sim 10^{-16}$ for double precision).
- **Truncation error:** the analytical error introduced by discarding higher-order terms of the Taylor series.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Theoretical Pillar: Taylor Series Expansion

The entire framework of finite differences rests upon the **Taylor Series Expansion** of a smooth, differentiable function $f(x)$ about a reference node $x_i$:

$$f(x_i + \Delta x) = f(x_i) + \Delta x \cdot f'(x_i) + \frac{\Delta x^2}{2!} \cdot f''(x_i) + \frac{\Delta x^3}{3!} \cdot f'''(x_i) + \frac{\Delta x^4}{4!} \cdot f^{(4)}(x_i) + \cdots$$

$$f(x_i - \Delta x) = f(x_i) - \Delta x \cdot f'(x_i) + \frac{\Delta x^2}{2!} \cdot f''(x_i) - \frac{\Delta x^3}{3!} \cdot f'''(x_i) + \frac{\Delta x^4}{4!} \cdot f^{(4)}(x_i) - \cdots$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Taylor Series?** The Taylor series expresses a function at a *neighboring* point purely in terms of the derivatives at the *current* point. By algebraically combining these expansions for forward, backward, or symmetric neighbors, we can **isolate** the derivative we want and treat the rest as "approximation error."

---

## 2.2 Operational Logic — How the Three Schemes Arise

The construction of each scheme follows a strict 3-step recipe:

1. **Write** the Taylor series expansion(s) for the required neighbor(s) of the reference node.
2. **Solve algebraically** for the target derivative (e.g., $f'(x_i)$) by combining equations.
3. **Truncate** the higher-order terms and call them the *truncation error* — these define the **order of accuracy** of the scheme.

### Forward Difference (FDS)

- Uses the Taylor expansion *forward* of node $i$.
- Truncates terms of order $\Delta x^2$ and higher.
- **Order of accuracy:** $O(\Delta x)$ — First order.

### Backward Difference (BDS)

- Uses the Taylor expansion *backward* of node $i$.
- Truncates terms of order $\Delta x^2$ and higher.
- **Order of accuracy:** $O(\Delta x)$ — First order.

### Central Difference (CDS)

- Subtracts the *backward* expansion from the *forward* expansion.
- The even-order terms (with $\Delta x^2$, $\Delta x^4$, …) **cancel automatically**.
- The odd-order error terms remain, with the **leading error** being $O(\Delta x^2)$.
- **Order of accuracy:** $O(\Delta x^2)$ — Second order.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **minimum set of expressions** every KTU student must memorize for Module 2 derivations and exam problems. **All absolute-value bars are written as `\vert` to preserve table syntax.**

| Derivative | Finite Difference Approximation | Scheme Name | Order of Accuracy |
|:----------:|:--------------------------------|:-----------:|:-----------------:|
| $f'(x_i)$ | $\dfrac{f_{i+1} - f_i}{\Delta x}$ | Forward Difference | $O(\Delta x)$ |
| $f'(x_i)$ | $\dfrac{f_i - f_{i-1}}{\Delta x}$ | Backward Difference | $O(\Delta x)$ |
| $f'(x_i)$ | $\dfrac{f_{i+1} - f_{i-1}}{2\Delta x}$ | Central Difference | $O(\Delta x^2)$ |
| $f''(x_i)$ | $\dfrac{f_{i+1} - 2f_i + f_{i-1}}{\Delta x^2}$ | Central Difference (2nd derivative) | $O(\Delta x^2)$ |
| $f''(x_i)$ | $\dfrac{f_{i+2} - 2f_{i+1} + f_i}{\Delta x^2}$ | Forward Difference (2nd derivative) | $O(\Delta x)$ |
| $f'''(x_i)$ | $\dfrac{f_{i+2} - 2f_{i+1} + 2f_{i-1} - f_{i-2}}{2\Delta x^3}$ | Central Difference (3rd derivative) | $O(\Delta x^2)$ |
| $f^{(4)}(x_i)$ | $\dfrac{f_{i+2} - 4f_{i+1} + 6f_i - 4f_{i-1} + f_{i-2}}{\Delta x^4}$ | Central Difference (4th derivative) | $O(\Delta x^2)$ |

**Stencil Notation Table — Number of grid points required:**

| Scheme | Direction | Number of Nodes | Stencil Span |
|:------:|:---------:|:---------------:|:------------:|
| FDS | Right | 2 | $i, i+1$ |
| BDS | Left | 2 | $i-1, i$ |
| CDS | Symmetric | 2 (for 1st derivative) / 3 (for 2nd derivative) | $i-1, i, i+1$ |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> Finite difference forms are the **backbone of every finite-difference CFD solver**, finite-difference time-domain (FDTD) electromagnetic solvers, and finite-difference option pricing models in computational finance. They are also used in:
> - Solving the heat conduction equation for fin design in mechanical engineering.
> - Weather and climate prediction models (discretization of atmospheric PDEs).
> - Aerodynamic flow simulation over airfoils, nozzles, and engine inlets.
> - Acoustic wave propagation in noise-control engineering.
> - Structural analysis of beams and plates (combined with boundary conditions).

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Convention used throughout this section:**
> - $f_i \equiv f(x_i)$ — function value at node $i$
> - $f_{i+1} \equiv f(x_i + \Delta x)$ — function value at the forward neighbor
> - $f_{i-1} \equiv f(x_i - \Delta x)$ — function value at the backward neighbor
> - $\Delta x$ — uniform grid spacing
> - Higher derivatives are denoted $f', f'', f''', f^{(4)}$ evaluated at $x_i$

---

## 3.1 Derivation of the Forward Difference Approximation (FDS)

We begin by writing the Taylor series expansion of $f(x)$ in the **forward** direction from node $i$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{i+1} &= f_i + \Delta x \cdot f'_i + \frac{\Delta x^2}{2!} \cdot f''_i + \frac{\Delta x^3}{3!} \cdot f'''_i + \frac{\Delta x^4}{4!} \cdot f^{(4)}_i + \mathcal{O}(\Delta x^5)
\end{aligned}
$$

This is a single equation in which the unknown $f'_i$ appears. We isolate it.

**Step 1 — Subtract $f_i$ from both sides:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{i+1} - f_i = \Delta x \cdot f'_i + \frac{\Delta x^2}{2} \cdot f''_i + \frac{\Delta x^3}{6} \cdot f'''_i + \frac{\Delta x^4}{24} \cdot f^{(4)}_i + \mathcal{O}(\Delta x^5)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Divide throughout by $\Delta x$ to make $f'_i$ the leading term:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{f_{i+1} - f_i}{\Delta x} = f'_i + \frac{\Delta x}{2} \cdot f''_i + \frac{\Delta x^2}{6} \cdot f'''_i + \frac{\Delta x^3}{24} \cdot f^{(4)}_i + \mathcal{O}(\Delta x^4)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange to solve explicitly for $f'_i$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f'_i = \frac{f_{i+1} - f_i}{\Delta x} - \frac{\Delta x}{2} \cdot f''_i - \frac{\Delta x^2}{6} \cdot f'''_i - \mathcal{O}(\Delta x^3)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Truncate the higher-order terms (the truncation error):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f'_i \approx \frac{f_{i+1} - f_i}{\Delta x} + \mathcal{O}(\Delta x)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation of the truncation error:**

The leading neglected term is $\dfrac{\Delta x}{2} \cdot f''_i$. Since this term is proportional to $\Delta x^1$, the scheme is **first-order accurate**, denoted $O(\Delta x)$.

$$\boxed{\left(\frac{\partial f}{\partial x}\right)_i \approx \frac{f_{i+1} - f_i}{\Delta x} \quad \text{with} \quad \tau_{FDS} = \mathcal{O}(\Delta x)}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Backward Difference Approximation (BDS)

We now write the Taylor series expansion of $f(x)$ in the **backward** direction from node $i$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{i-1} = f_i - \Delta x \cdot f'_i + \frac{\Delta x^2}{2!} \cdot f''_i - \frac{\Delta x^3}{3!} \cdot f'''_i + \frac{\Delta x^4}{4!} \cdot f^{(4)}_i - \mathcal{O}(\Delta x^5)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1 — Subtract $f_{i-1}$ from $f_i$ (i.e., compute $f_i - f_{i-1}$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_i - f_{i-1} = f_i - \left[ f_i - \Delta x \cdot f'_i + \frac{\Delta x^2}{2} \cdot f''_i - \frac{\Delta x^3}{6} \cdot f'''_i + \cdots \right]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Simplify the right-hand side (telescoping of $f_i$ terms):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_i - f_{i-1} = \Delta x \cdot f'_i - \frac{\Delta x^2}{2} \cdot f''_i + \frac{\Delta x^3}{6} \cdot f'''_i - \frac{\Delta x^4}{24} \cdot f^{(4)}_i + \mathcal{O}(\Delta x^5)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Divide by $\Delta x$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{f_i - f_{i-1}}{\Delta x} = f'_i - \frac{\Delta x}{2} \cdot f''_i + \frac{\Delta x^2}{6} \cdot f'''_i - \frac{\Delta x^3}{24} \cdot f^{(4)}_i + \mathcal{O}(\Delta x^4)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $f'_i$ and truncate:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f'_i = \frac{f_i - f_{i-1}}{\Delta x} + \frac{\Delta x}{2} \cdot f''_i - \frac{\Delta x^2}{6} \cdot f'''_i - \mathcal{O}(\Delta x^3)
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
f'_i \approx \frac{f_i - f_{i-1}}{\Delta x} + \mathcal{O}(\Delta x)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** The leading error term is again $\dfrac{\Delta x}{2} \cdot f''_i$, so the BDS is also **first-order accurate**.

$$\boxed{\left(\frac{\partial f}{\partial x}\right)_i \approx \frac{f_i - f_{i-1}}{\Delta x} \quad \text{with} \quad \tau_{BDS} = \mathcal{O}(\Delta x)}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation of the Central Difference Approximation (CDS) — First Derivative

The CDS is obtained by **subtracting** the backward expansion from the forward expansion, causing the *even-power* terms to cancel and dramatically improving accuracy.

**Step 1 — Write both Taylor expansions:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{i+1} &= f_i + \Delta x \cdot f'_i + \frac{\Delta x^2}{2} \cdot f''_i + \frac{\Delta x^3}{6} \cdot f'''_i + \frac{\Delta x^4}{24} \cdot f^{(4)}_i + \mathcal{O}(\Delta x^5)
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{i-1} &= f_i - \Delta x \cdot f'_i + \frac{\Delta x^2}{2} \cdot f''_i - \frac{\Delta x^3}{6} \cdot f'''_i + \frac{\Delta x^4}{24} \cdot f^{(4)}_i - \mathcal{O}(\Delta x^5)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Subtract the second equation from the first (i.e., compute $f_{i+1} - f_{i-1}$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{i+1} - f_{i-1} = 2 \Delta x \cdot f'_i + 2 \cdot \frac{\Delta x^3}{6} \cdot f'''_i + 2 \cdot \frac{\Delta x^5}{120} \cdot f^{(5)}_i + \mathcal{O}(\Delta x^7)
\end{aligned}
$$

Note that the $f''_i$ and $f^{(4)}_i$ terms **cancel exactly** (because they have the same sign in both expansions).

**Step 3 — Simplify the coefficients:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{i+1} - f_{i-1} = 2 \Delta x \cdot f'_i + \frac{\Delta x^3}{3} \cdot f'''_i + \frac{\Delta x^5}{60} \cdot f^{(5)}_i + \mathcal{O}(\Delta x^7)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Divide by $2\Delta x$ to isolate $f'_i$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{f_{i+1} - f_{i-1}}{2\Delta x} = f'_i + \frac{\Delta x^2}{6} \cdot f'''_i + \frac{\Delta x^4}{120} \cdot f^{(5)}_i + \mathcal{O}(\Delta x^6)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $f'_i$ and truncate:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f'_i = \frac{f_{i+1} - f_{i-1}}{2\Delta x} - \frac{\Delta x^2}{6} \cdot f'''_i - \mathcal{O}(\Delta x^4)
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
f'_i \approx \frac{f_{i+1} - f_{i-1}}{2\Delta x} + \mathcal{O}(\Delta x^2)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Critical interpretation:** The leading error term is now proportional to $\Delta x^2$ (not $\Delta x^1$). The error is therefore **four times smaller** when $\Delta x$ is halved, instead of just two times smaller as in FDS/BDS.

$$\boxed{\left(\frac{\partial f}{\partial x}\right)_i \approx \frac{f_{i+1} - f_{i-1}}{2\Delta x} \quad \text{with} \quad \tau_{CDS} = \mathcal{O}(\Delta x^2)}$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation of the Central Difference Approximation — Second Derivative

The second derivative is critical because it appears in the **diffusion term** of the Navier–Stokes and heat conduction equations.

**Step 1 — Add the two Taylor expansions (instead of subtracting):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{i+1} + f_{i-1} = 2 f_i + \Delta x^2 \cdot f''_i + 2 \cdot \frac{\Delta x^4}{24} \cdot f^{(4)}_i + 2 \cdot \frac{\Delta x^6}{720} \cdot f^{(6)}_i + \mathcal{O}(\Delta x^8)
\end{aligned}
$$

Note: When adding, the *odd-power* terms (containing $f'_i$, $f'''_i$, …) cancel, while the *even-power* terms reinforce.

**Step 2 — Simplify the coefficients:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{i+1} + f_{i-1} = 2 f_i + \Delta x^2 \cdot f''_i + \frac{\Delta x^4}{12} \cdot f^{(4)}_i + \frac{\Delta x^6}{360} \cdot f^{(6)}_i + \mathcal{O}(\Delta x^8)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Subtract $2f_i$ from both sides:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{i+1} - 2 f_i + f_{i-1} = \Delta x^2 \cdot f''_i + \frac{\Delta x^4}{12} \cdot f^{(4)}_i + \frac{\Delta x^6}{360} \cdot f^{(6)}_i + \mathcal{O}(\Delta x^8)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Divide by $\Delta x^2$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{f_{i+1} - 2 f_i + f_{i-1}}{\Delta x^2} = f''_i + \frac{\Delta x^2}{12} \cdot f^{(4)}_i + \frac{\Delta x^4}{360} \cdot f^{(6)}_i + \mathcal{O}(\Delta x^6)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $f''_i$ and truncate:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f''_i = \frac{f_{i+1} - 2 f_i + f_{i-1}}{\Delta x^2} - \frac{\Delta x^2}{12} \cdot f^{(4)}_i - \mathcal{O}(\Delta x^4)
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
f''_i \approx \frac{f_{i+1} - 2 f_i + f_{i-1}}{\Delta x^2} + \mathcal{O}(\Delta x^2)
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\boxed{\left(\frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x^2}\right)_i \approx \frac{f_{i+1} - 2 f_i + f_{i-1}}{\Delta x^2} \quad \text{with} \quad \tau = \mathcal{O}(\Delta x^2)}$$

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — Validation

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** Approximate $f'(2.0)$ for $f(x) = x^3$ using FDS, BDS, and CDS with $\Delta x = 0.1$. Compare with the exact derivative $f'(x) = 3x^2 \Rightarrow f'(2) = 12.0$.

**Function values:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f(1.9) &= 1.9^3 = 6.859 \\
f(2.0) &= 2.0^3 = 8.000 \\
f(2.1) &= 2.1^3 = 9.261
\end{aligned}
$$

**Forward Difference Approximation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f'_{FDS}(2.0) &= \frac{f(2.1) - f(2.0)}{0.1} = \frac{9.261 - 8.000}{0.1} = 12.610
\end{aligned}
$$

**Backward Difference Approximation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f'_{BDS}(2.0) &= \frac{f(2.0) - f(1.9)}{0.1} = \frac{8.000 - 6.859}{0.1} = 11.410
\end{aligned}
$$

**Central Difference Approximation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f'_{CDS}(2.0) &= \frac{f(2.1) - f(1.9)}{2(0.1)} = \frac{9.261 - 6.859}{0.2} = 12.010
\end{aligned}
$$

**Error Analysis:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Error}_{FDS} &= \vert 12.610 - 12.000 \vert = 0.610 \\
\text{Error}_{BDS} &= \vert 11.410 - 12.000 \vert = -0.590 \\
\text{Error}_{CDS} &= \vert 12.010 - 12.000 \vert = 0.010
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Observation:** The CDS error (0.010) is approximately **60× smaller** than the FDS/BDS errors. This is the empirical confirmation of the theoretical $O(\Delta x^2)$ accuracy. If we had used $\Delta x = 0.05$, the CDS error would shrink by a factor of **four**, while the FDS/BDS errors would only shrink by a factor of **two**.

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> Since this topic is fundamentally mathematical (no physical hardware or process flow), the diagrams below present the **stencil geometry** of each finite-difference scheme — that is, *which neighboring grid points* participate in the approximation. The second diagram illustrates the **functional architecture** of the entire finite-difference conversion workflow.

---

## 4.1 Stencil Diagram — Comparison of Three Schemes

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["i-2"] --- nodeB["i-1"] --- nodeC["i (reference)"] --- nodeD["i+1"] --- nodeE["i+2"]
    style nodeA fill:#FFE4E1,stroke:#8B0000
    style nodeB fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B
    style nodeC fill:#FFD700,stroke:#8B6914
    style nodeD fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B
    style nodeE fill:#FFE4E1,stroke:#8B0000
    nodeF["BDS stencil: nodes i and i-1, looks LEFTWARD, accuracy O of Delta x"]
    nodeG["FDS stencil: nodes i and i+1, looks RIGHTWARD, accuracy O of Delta x"]
    nodeH["CDS stencil: nodes i-1 and i+1, looks SYMMETRIC, accuracy O of Delta x squared"]
    nodeI["Inactive neighbors: only used by higher-order schemes"]
```

---

## 4.2 Functional Architecture — Converting a Derivative to a Finite Difference

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stepA["Start: Identify the continuous derivative df by dx at node i"] --> stepB["Step 1: Locate the reference node i on the 1D computational grid with uniform spacing Delta x"]
    stepB --> stepC{"Step 2: Choose scheme based on context and boundary conditions"}
    stepC -->|Open boundary or startup of marching solution| stepD["Apply FORWARD DIFFERENCE: f prime i equals f sub i plus 1 minus f sub i, all over Delta x"]
    stepC -->|Closed boundary or termination of marching solution| stepE["Apply BACKWARD DIFFERENCE: f prime i equals f sub i minus f sub i minus 1, all over Delta x"]
    stepC -->|Interior node of the domain| stepF["Apply CENTRAL DIFFERENCE: f prime i equals f sub i plus 1 minus f sub i minus 1, all over two Delta x"]
    stepD --> stepG["Step 3: Compute truncation error tau equals O of Delta x by Taylor series analysis"]
    stepE --> stepG
    stepF --> stepH["Step 3: Compute truncation error tau equals O of Delta x squared by Taylor series analysis"]
    stepG --> stepI["Step 4: Substitute into the governing PDE e.g. Navier-Stokes, Heat, Continuity"]
    stepH --> stepI
    stepI --> stepJ["Step 5: Apply boundary conditions to close the algebraic system of equations"]
    stepJ --> stepK["End: Solve the resulting sparse linear or nonlinear system numerically"]
    style stepA fill:#E6F3FF,stroke:#003366
    style stepK fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400
    style stepC fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#8B6914
```

---

## 4.3 Conceptual Map — Order of Accuracy vs. Stencil Width

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    qA["First order FDS: 2 points, error O of Delta x"] --> qB["Second order CDS: 3 points, error O of Delta x squared"]
    qB --> qC["Fourth order CDS: 5 points, error O of Delta x to the fourth"]
    qC --> qD["Sixth order CDS: 7 points, error O of Delta x to the sixth"]
    qD --> qE["Higher accuracy requires more neighbors and higher computational cost per node"]
    style qA fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000
    style qB fill:#FFD700,stroke:#8B6914
    style qC fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400
    style qD fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B
    style qE fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#4B0082
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Trade-off takeaway:** Higher-order accuracy demands wider stencils (more neighboring nodes), which in turn increases the bandwidth of the resulting linear system, computational memory, and CPU time per iteration. The choice of scheme is therefore a **balance between accuracy, stability, and computational cost** — a recurring theme in CFD.

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern Note:** Part A questions are typically 2-mark conceptual items in the Continuous Internal Evaluation and 3-mark direct questions in the University End-Semester Examination (ESE). They expect crisp, definition-level answers with one supporting equation.

---

### **Question A1 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

**Define truncation error. What is the order of accuracy of the central difference approximation for the first derivative?**

**Model Answer:**

Truncation error is the analytical error introduced when an infinite Taylor series is replaced by a finite algebraic expression. It is the difference between the exact derivative and its finite-difference approximation, expressed as a function of the grid spacing $\Delta x$.

For the central difference scheme, the Taylor series analysis yields the leading error term as $-\dfrac{\Delta x^2}{6} f'''(x_i)$. Since this leading term is proportional to $\Delta x^2$, the central difference approximation for the first derivative is **second-order accurate**, i.e., $\mathcal{O}(\Delta x^2)$. **(3 marks)**

---

### **Question A2 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`**

**Distinguish between forward, backward, and central difference schemes for the first derivative. State the stencils used by each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Scheme | Stencil | Formula | Order |
|:------:|:-------:|:-------:|:-----:|
| Forward Difference (FDS) | $i, i+1$ | $\dfrac{f_{i+1} - f_i}{\Delta x}$ | $\mathcal{O}(\Delta x)$ |
| Backward Difference (BDS) | $i-1, i$ | $\dfrac{f_i - f_{i-1}}{\Delta x}$ | $\mathcal{O}(\Delta x)$ |
| Central Difference (CDS) | $i-1, i+1$ | $\dfrac{f_{i+1} - f_{i-1}}{2\Delta x}$ | $\mathcal{O}(\Delta x^2)$ |

The CDS achieves higher accuracy because it is **symmetric** about the reference node $i$, which causes the even-order Taylor series terms to cancel, leaving only odd-order terms with a leading $\Delta x^2$ dependence. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Internal Choice Pattern:** Every Part B question in the ESE offers an internal choice. You are required to attempt **either** Question A **or** Question B. Both are provided below with complete sub-parts and step-by-step model solutions.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]`**

**A (a)** Derive the forward difference approximation for the first derivative $\dfrac{\partial f}{\partial x}$ using the Taylor series expansion method. Clearly state the truncation error. **(7 marks)**

**A (b)** Derive the central difference approximation for the **second derivative** $\dfrac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x^2}$ using the Taylor series. State its order of accuracy. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for A(a):**

**Step 1** — Expand $f_{i+1}$ about node $i$ using the Taylor series:

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{i+1} = f_i + \Delta x \cdot f'_i + \frac{\Delta x^2}{2!} \cdot f''_i + \frac{\Delta x^3}{3!} \cdot f'''_i + \mathcal{O}(\Delta x^4)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2** — Subtract $f_i$ from both sides:

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{i+1} - f_i = \Delta x \cdot f'_i + \frac{\Delta x^2}{2} \cdot f''_i + \frac{\Delta x^3}{6} \cdot f'''_i + \mathcal{O}(\Delta x^4)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3** — Divide by $\Delta x$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{f_{i+1} - f_i}{\Delta x} = f'_i + \frac{\Delta x}{2} \cdot f''_i + \frac{\Delta x^2}{6} \cdot f'''_i + \mathcal{O}(\Delta x^3)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4** — Solve for $f'_i$ and truncate:

$$
\begin{aligned}
f'_i = \frac{f_{i+1} - f_i}{\Delta x} - \frac{\Delta x}{2} \cdot f''_i - \mathcal{O}(\Delta x^2)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Result:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\left(\frac{\partial f}{\partial x}\right)_i \approx \frac{f_{i+1} - f_i}{\Delta x} + \mathcal{O}(\Delta x)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Truncation error:** $\tau = -\dfrac{\Delta x}{2} f''_i$, hence the scheme is **first-order accurate**, denoted $\mathcal{O}(\Delta x)$. **(7 marks — 2 for setup, 2 for algebra, 2 for final result, 1 for truncation error statement)**

---

#### **Model Solution for A(b):**

**Step 1** — Write the forward and backward Taylor expansions about node $i$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{i+1} &= f_i + \Delta x \cdot f'_i + \frac{\Delta x^2}{2} \cdot f''_i + \frac{\Delta x^3}{6} \cdot f'''_i + \frac{\Delta x^4}{24} \cdot f^{(4)}_i + \mathcal{O}(\Delta x^5)
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{i-1} &= f_i - \Delta x \cdot f'_i + \frac{\Delta x^2}{2} \cdot f''_i - \frac{\Delta x^3}{6} \cdot f'''_i + \frac{\Delta x^4}{24} \cdot f^{(4)}_i - \mathcal{O}(\Delta x^5)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2** — Add the two equations (this cancels the odd-power terms):

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{i+1} + f_{i-1} = 2 f_i + \Delta x^2 \cdot f''_i + \frac{\Delta x^4}{12} \cdot f^{(4)}_i + \mathcal{O}(\Delta x^6)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3** — Rearrange:

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{i+1} - 2 f_i + f_{i-1} = \Delta x^2 \cdot f''_i + \frac{\Delta x^4}{12} \cdot f^{(4)}_i + \mathcal{O}(\Delta x^6)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4** — Divide by $\Delta x^2$ and solve:

$$
\begin{aligned}
f''_i = \frac{f_{i+1} - 2 f_i + f_{i-1}}{\Delta x^2} - \frac{\Delta x^2}{12} \cdot f^{(4)}_i - \mathcal{O}(\Delta x^4)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Result:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\left(\frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x^2}\right)_i \approx \frac{f_{i+1} - 2 f_i + f_{i-1}}{\Delta x^2} + \mathcal{O}(\Delta x^2)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Order of accuracy:** Second order, $\mathcal{O}(\Delta x^2)$, since the leading truncation error is $\dfrac{\Delta x^2}{12} f^{(4)}_i$. **(7 marks — 2 for setup, 2 for algebraic manipulation, 2 for final expression, 1 for order statement)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`**

**B (a)** Derive the backward difference approximation for the first derivative $\dfrac{\partial f}{\partial x}$ using the Taylor series. Compare its truncation error with that of the forward difference scheme. **(7 marks)**

**B (b)** Derive the central difference scheme for the first derivative. Show that it is **second-order accurate** and explain physically why it is more accurate than FDS and BDS. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for B(a):**

**Step 1** — Expand $f_{i-1}$ about node $i$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{i-1} = f_i - \Delta x \cdot f'_i + \frac{\Delta x^2}{2} \cdot f''_i - \frac{\Delta x^3}{6} \cdot f'''_i + \mathcal{O}(\Delta x^4)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2** — Subtract $f_{i-1}$ from $f_i$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_i - f_{i-1} = \Delta x \cdot f'_i - \frac{\Delta x^2}{2} \cdot f''_i + \frac{\Delta x^3}{6} \cdot f'''_i - \mathcal{O}(\Delta x^4)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3** — Divide by $\Delta x$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{f_i - f_{i-1}}{\Delta x} = f'_i - \frac{\Delta x}{2} \cdot f''_i + \frac{\Delta x^2}{6} \cdot f'''_i - \mathcal{O}(\Delta x^3)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4** — Solve and truncate:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\left(\frac{\partial f}{\partial x}\right)_i \approx \frac{f_i - f_{i-1}}{\Delta x} + \mathcal{O}(\Delta x)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comparison of truncation errors:**

| Scheme | Leading Truncation Error | Order |
|:------:|:------------------------:|:-----:|
| FDS | $-\dfrac{\Delta x}{2} f''_i$ | $\mathcal{O}(\Delta x)$ |
| BDS | $+\dfrac{\Delta x}{2} f''_i$ | $\mathcal{O}(\Delta x)$ |

Both FDS and BDS have the **same magnitude** of leading truncation error, but with **opposite signs** — the FDS *overestimates* the derivative while the BDS *underestimates* it. This is why the **average** of the two (which is the CDS) is so much more accurate. **(7 marks — 2 for setup, 2 for algebra, 2 for final result, 1 for comparison)**

---

#### **Model Solution for B(b):**

**Step 1** — Write both Taylor expansions:

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{i+1} = f_i + \Delta x f'_i + \frac{\Delta x^2}{2} f''_i + \frac{\Delta x^3}{6} f'''_i + \frac{\Delta x^4}{24} f^{(4)}_i + \mathcal{O}(\Delta x^5)
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{i-1} = f_i - \Delta x f'_i + \frac{\Delta x^2}{2} f''_i - \frac{\Delta x^3}{6} f'''_i + \frac{\Delta x^4}{24} f^{(4)}_i - \mathcal{O}(\Delta x^5)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2** — Subtract $f_{i-1}$ from $f_{i+1}$ (odd-power terms survive, even-power terms cancel):

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{i+1} - f_{i-1} = 2 \Delta x f'_i + \frac{\Delta x^3}{3} f'''_i + \mathcal{O}(\Delta x^5)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3** — Solve for $f'_i$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
f'_i = \frac{f_{i+1} - f_{i-1}}{2\Delta x} - \frac{\Delta x^2}{6} f'''_i - \mathcal{O}(\Delta x^4)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4** — Truncate:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\left(\frac{\partial f}{\partial x}\right)_i \approx \frac{f_{i+1} - f_{i-1}}{2\Delta x} + \mathcal{O}(\Delta x^2)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Proof of second-order accuracy:** The leading neglected term is $-\dfrac{\Delta x^2}{6} f'''_i$, which is proportional to $\Delta x^2$. Hence the scheme is **second-order accurate**, $\mathcal{O}(\Delta x^2)$. **(4 marks for derivation)**

**Physical reasoning for superior accuracy (3 marks):**

- The CDS is **symmetric** about the reference node — it samples the function at equal distances on either side of $i$. This geometric symmetry causes the **leading asymmetric error** (proportional to $f''_i$, the curvature) to **cancel out automatically**.
- The FDS and BDS, being one-sided, are biased — FDS overestimates and BDS underestimates the true derivative by the same amount. The CDS is mathematically equivalent to the **average** of FDS and BDS, and since the errors are equal in magnitude but opposite in sign, they cancel to give an error an **entire order of magnitude smaller** in $\Delta x$.
- Physically, the CDS captures the **local slope** of the function as a centered secant, which is a much better geometric representation of the tangent than a one-sided secant.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls (Validated Against KTU Board Valuation Patterns):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the truncation error term.** A derivation that ends with the algebraic expression but omits the $\mathcal{O}(\Delta x)$ or $\mathcal{O}(\Delta x^2)$ annotation will typically lose **1 mark** in ESE.
> 2. **Sign errors in the Taylor series.** Students frequently write $+ \dfrac{\Delta x^3}{6} f'''_i$ in the backward expansion — it must be $-$. Such sign errors propagate and lead to wrong CDS formulas.
> 3. **Confusing the second-derivative CDS with the first-derivative CDS.** The second-derivative CDS has a coefficient of $-2$ for the central term (giving $f_{i+1} - 2f_i + f_{i-1}$), not $+2$. Writing it incorrectly costs full marks on the sub-question.
> 4. **Omitting intermediate steps.** KTU examiners award marks for **method** as well as final answer. A student who jumps from the Taylor series directly to the final CDS formula without showing the addition/subtraction step loses the **process marks** (typically 2 out of 7).
> 5. **Stating the order incorrectly.** Students often write "second order" for FDS or BDS — this is wrong; FDS and BDS are **first-order accurate**. The order refers to the **power of $\Delta x$ in the leading truncation error**, not the order of the derivative.
> 6. **Forgetting that the truncation error coefficient is a function of the derivatives of $f$.** The term $-\dfrac{\Delta x}{2} f''_i$ is not a constant — it depends on the local second derivative of the function. Examiners often check this understanding.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 2: Converting Derivatives to Their Finite Difference Forms**
>
> - The three fundamental one-dimensional finite-difference schemes for the **first derivative** are Forward ($\mathcal{O}(\Delta x)$), Backward ($\mathcal{O}(\Delta x)$), and Central ($\mathcal{O}(\Delta x^2)$).
> - The **Taylor series expansion** is the universal tool used to derive and analyze every finite-difference scheme.
> - The **Forward Difference** uses the stencil $\{i, i+1\}$ and approximates $f'_i \approx \dfrac{f_{i+1} - f_i}{\Delta x}$ with truncation error $-\dfrac{\Delta x}{2} f''_i$.
> - The **Backward Difference** uses the stencil $\{i-1, i\}$ and approximates $f'_i \approx \dfrac{f_i - f_{i-1}}{\Delta x}$ with truncation error $+\dfrac{\Delta x}{2} f''_i$.
> - The **Central Difference** uses the stencil $\{i-1, i+1\}$ and approximates $f'_i \approx \dfrac{f_{i+1} - f_{i-1}}{2\Delta x}$ with truncation error $-\dfrac{\Delta x^2}{6} f'''_i$.
> - The **Central Difference for the second derivative** is $f''_i \approx \dfrac{f_{i+1} - 2f_i + f_{i-1}}{\Delta x^2}$ with truncation error $-\dfrac{\Delta x^2}{12} f^{(4)}_i$.
> - The **order of accuracy** is the power of $\Delta x$ in the leading truncation error — higher order = faster error decay with grid refinement.
> - **Central differences are more accurate** than one-sided differences because the symmetric stencil cancels the even-order Taylor series terms, leaving only higher-order residuals.
> - The **second derivative CDS is derived by ADDING** the forward and backward Taylor expansions (not subtracting) — this cancels the odd-order terms and isolates $f''_i$.
> - The **first derivative CDS is derived by SUBTRACTING** the backward expansion from the forward expansion — this cancels the even-order terms and isolates $f'_i$.
> - In **CFD applications**, central differences are used for **interior nodes**, while forward/backward differences are used at the **domain boundaries** where neighbors on one side are unavailable.
> - The **trade-off** for higher-order accuracy is a wider stencil, which increases the bandwidth of the resulting algebraic system and the computational cost per node.
> - **Always include** the truncation error term ($\mathcal{O}(\Delta x)$ or $\mathcal{O}(\Delta x^2)$) in the final answer of any derivation — KTU examiners explicitly check for it.
> - **Verify** finite-difference formulas by testing against a known function (e.g., $f(x) = x^3$) and comparing the numerical result with the exact analytical derivative.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Taylor's Series Approach and Polynomial Fitting Approach
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET521 - COMPUTATIONAL FLUID DYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Taylor's Series Approach and Polynomial Fitting Approach

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Taylor's Series Approach — Formal Definition

The **Taylor's Series Approach** is the classical and most fundamental method for deriving finite difference approximations of derivatives in Computational Fluid Dynamics. It is based on the principle that any smooth, continuously differentiable function $f(x)$ can be expanded about a point $x_i$ using the Taylor's Theorem, expressed as an infinite power series involving the function value and its successive derivatives at that point.

> [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Definition (PEMET521, Module 2):**
> The Taylor's Series approach expresses the value of a function at a neighboring grid point in terms of the function value and its derivatives at a reference point. By truncating the series and rearranging, finite difference approximations to the derivatives (forward, backward, and central differences) are obtained along with their **truncation error orders**.

Mathematically, the expansion of $f(x)$ about the point $x_i$ with step size $\Delta x$ is:

$$f(x_i + \Delta x) = f(x_i) + \Delta x \frac{df}{dx}\bigg|_{x_i} + \frac{(\Delta x)^2}{2!} \frac{d^2f}{dx^2}\bigg|_{x_i} + \frac{(\Delta x)^3}{3!} \frac{d^3f}{dx^3}\bigg|_{x_i} + \cdots$$

> [!NOTE]
> In the CFD grid notation, the spacing is denoted as $\Delta x$ and grid points are written as $x_{i+1} = x_i + \Delta x$, $x_{i-1} = x_i - \Delta x$, etc.

### 1.2 Polynomial Fitting Approach — Formal Definition

The **Polynomial Fitting Approach** (also called the **Method of Undetermined Coefficients** or **Lagrange Polynomial Fitting**) is an alternative to the Taylor's series method. Instead of expanding in a series, this method assumes that the function $f(x)$ can be locally approximated by a polynomial of a specified degree. The coefficients of the polynomial are determined by requiring that the polynomial passes exactly through the function values at the chosen grid points. Differentiating the resulting polynomial gives finite difference formulas.

> [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Definition:**
> In the polynomial fitting approach, a polynomial of degree $(n-1)$ is fitted through $n$ grid points. The derivative of the polynomial at a particular node provides the finite difference approximation. This method directly yields the coefficients of the finite difference formula without explicitly computing Taylor series terms.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Real-World Analogy — The "Ruler on a Curve" Analogy:**

Imagine you are trying to measure the *slope* of a curvy mountain road at a particular point, but you only have a tape measure and cannot use calculus directly.

- **Taylor's Series Approach** is like looking at the road map (which has elevation data) and using a *mathematical telescope* to see the nearby terrain by zooming in continuously. You write down the elevation at a slightly distant point as elevation here + a small correction. By controlling how much "zoom" (i.e., how far and how many terms), you estimate the slope.
- **Polynomial Fitting Approach** is like using a *flexible plastic ruler* (a low-order polynomial) and bending it so that it touches the curve at several known elevation checkpoints. Once the ruler is perfectly bent, its slope at the central point is your estimate of the road's slope.

Both methods aim to do the same thing — approximate the derivative — but from two different philosophical starting points: **series expansion** vs. **direct curve fitting**.

> [!NOTE] > **Engineering Relevance:**
> These two approaches are the *backbone* of deriving finite difference schemes used in solving the **Navier-Stokes equations**, **heat conduction problems**, and **wave propagation** in CFD. Every commercial solver (ANSYS Fluent, OpenFOAM, COMSOL) ultimately relies on these foundational derivations to discretize continuous PDEs into solvable algebraic systems.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Taylor's Series Approach — Operational Logic

The Taylor's series approach follows a structured, stepwise logic:

1. **Choose a Reference Node** $x_i$ at which the derivative is to be approximated.
2. **Expand the Function** at neighboring nodes ($x_{i+1}$, $x_{i-1}$, $x_{i+2}$, $x_{i-2}$, etc.) using the Taylor series.
3. **Truncate the Series** at a suitable order based on the desired accuracy.
4. **Solve Algebraically** for the required derivative term by combining the truncated expansions.
5. **Identify the Truncation Error** as the leading term that was neglected.

> [!NOTE]
> The truncation error order is the **single most important outcome** in KTU examinations. Students must be able to identify whether a derived scheme is $O(\Delta x)$, $O(\Delta x^2)$, $O(\Delta x^3)$, etc.

#### 2.1.1 Forward Difference Approximation (First Derivative)

Expanding $f(x_{i+1})$ about $x_i$:

$$f_{i+1} = f_i + \Delta x f'_i + \frac{\Delta x^2}{2} f''_i + \frac{\Delta x^3}{6} f'''_i + \frac{\Delta x^4}{24} f^{(4)}_i + \cdots$$

Solving for $f'_i$:

$$\left(\frac{df}{dx}\right)_i = \frac{f_{i+1} - f_i}{\Delta x} - \frac{\Delta x}{2} f''_i - \frac{\Delta x^2}{6} f'''_i - \cdots$$

The **Forward Difference Formula** is therefore:

$$\boxed{\left(\frac{df}{dx}\right)_i \approx \frac{f_{i+1} - f_i}{\Delta x} \quad \text{with truncation error } O(\Delta x)}$$

#### 2.1.2 Backward Difference Approximation (First Derivative)

Expanding $f(x_{i-1})$ about $x_i$:

$$f_{i-1} = f_i - \Delta x f'_i + \frac{\Delta x^2}{2} f''_i - \frac{\Delta x^3}{6} f'''_i + \cdots$$

Solving for $f'_i$:

$$\left(\frac{df}{dx}\right)_i = \frac{f_i - f_{i-1}}{\Delta x} + \frac{\Delta x}{2} f''_i - \frac{\Delta x^2}{6} f'''_i + \cdots$$

The **Backward Difference Formula** is:

$$\boxed{\left(\frac{df}{dx}\right)_i \approx \frac{f_i - f_{i-1}}{\Delta x} \quad \text{with truncation error } O(\Delta x)}$$

#### 2.1.3 Central Difference Approximation (First Derivative)

Adding the expansions of $f_{i+1}$ and $f_{i-1}$:

$$f_{i+1} + f_{i-1} = 2f_i + \Delta x^2 f''_i + \frac{\Delta x^4}{12} f^{(4)}_i + \cdots$$

For the first derivative, **subtracting** the two expansions:

$$f_{i+1} - f_{i-1} = 2\Delta x f'_i + \frac{\Delta x^3}{3} f'''_i + \cdots$$

Solving:

$$\left(\frac{df}{dx}\right)_i = \frac{f_{i+1} - f_{i-1}}{2\Delta x} - \frac{\Delta x^2}{6} f'''_i - \cdots$$

The **Central Difference Formula** is:

$$\boxed{\left(\frac{df}{dx}\right)_i \approx \frac{f_{i+1} - f_{i-1}}{2\Delta x} \quad \text{with truncation error } O(\Delta x^2)}$$

#### 2.1.4 Second Derivative Central Difference

From the *sum* of expansions $f_{i+1} + f_{i-1}$:

$$\left(\frac{d^2f}{dx^2}\right)_i = \frac{f_{i+1} - 2f_i + f_{i-1}}{\Delta x^2} - \frac{\Delta x^2}{12} f^{(4)}_i - \cdots$$

$$\boxed{\left(\frac{d^2f}{dx^2}\right)_i \approx \frac{f_{i+1} - 2f_i + f_{i-1}}{\Delta x^2} \quad \text{with truncation error } O(\Delta x^2)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT] > **Why These Matter in CFD:**
> The second derivative central difference formula is **the most frequently used formula in CFD**, especially for the diffusion term ($\nabla^2 \phi$) in the Navier-Stokes equations. KTU questions on the **heat equation**, **Poisson equation**, and **Laplacian discretization** require this formula.

### 2.2 Polynomial Fitting Approach — Operational Logic

The polynomial fitting approach uses a different but equivalent methodology:

1. **Assume a Polynomial Form** of degree $(n-1)$: $f(x) = a_0 + a_1 x + a_2 x^2 + \cdots + a_{n-1} x^{n-1}$.
2. **Apply at $n$ Grid Points** to generate $n$ simultaneous equations in the $n$ unknown coefficients $a_0, a_1, \ldots, a_{n-1}$.
3. **Solve the Linear System** to express the coefficients in terms of the function values $f_i, f_{i+1}, \ldots$.
4. **Differentiate the Polynomial** and evaluate at the required node to get the finite difference formula.

> [!NOTE]
> The polynomial fitting method is *systematic* and *algorithmic* — it can be extended easily to any number of points and any derivative order without manually tracking Taylor series signs.

#### 2.2.1 Example — 3-Point Central Difference via Polynomial Fitting

Assume a quadratic polynomial: $f(x) = a_0 + a_1 x + a_2 x^2$. Apply at three points $x_{i-1}, x_i, x_{i+1}$:

$$f_{i-1} = a_0 + a_1 x_{i-1} + a_2 x_{i-1}^2$$
$$f_i = a_0 + a_1 x_i + a_2 x_i^2$$
$$f_{i+1} = a_0 + a_1 x_{i+1} + a_2 x_{i+1}^2$$

Setting $x_i = 0$ (without loss of generality) and letting $x_{i+1} = \Delta x$, $x_{i-1} = -\Delta x$:

$$f_{i-1} = a_0 - a_1 \Delta x + a_2 \Delta x^2$$
$$f_i = a_0$$
$$f_{i+1} = a_0 + a_1 \Delta x + a_2 \Delta x^2$$

From $f_i$: $a_0 = f_i$.
From $f_{i+1} - f_{i-1}$: $2a_1 \Delta x = f_{i+1} - f_{i-1}$, so $a_1 = \frac{f_{i+1} - f_{i-1}}{2\Delta x}$.
From $f_{i+1} + f_{i-1}$: $2a_0 + 2a_2 \Delta x^2 = f_{i+1} + f_{i-1}$, so $a_2 = \frac{f_{i+1} - 2f_i + f_{i-1}}{2\Delta x^2}$.

Since $f'(x_i) = a_1$:

$$\boxed{\left(\frac{df}{dx}\right)_i = \frac{f_{i+1} - f_{i-1}}{2\Delta x}}$$

Since $f''(x_i) = 2a_2$:

$$\boxed{\left(\frac{d^2f}{dx^2}\right)_i = \frac{f_{i+1} - 2f_i + f_{i-1}}{\Delta x^2}}$$

> [!NOTE] > **Observation:** The results are **identical** to those obtained from the Taylor's series method, but the derivation is purely algebraic, with no calculus of error terms required. The error order is inferred from the fact that we are using a quadratic fit, which is exact up to $O(\Delta x^2)$.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The table below contains every formula a KTU student must memorize for the End Semester Examination (ESE) on this topic.

| **Derivative** | **Finite Difference Formula** | **Stencil Used** | **Truncation Error** |
|---|---|---|---|
| First derivative, forward | $\dfrac{f_{i+1} - f_i}{\Delta x}$ | $i, i+1$ | $O(\Delta x)$ |
| First derivative, backward | $\dfrac{f_i - f_{i-1}}{\Delta x}$ | $i-1, i$ | $O(\Delta x)$ |
| First derivative, central | $\dfrac{f_{i+1} - f_{i-1}}{2\Delta x}$ | $i-1, i, i+1$ | $O(\Delta x^2)$ |
| First derivative, 3-pt forward | $\dfrac{-3f_i + 4f_{i+1} - f_{i+2}}{2\Delta x}$ | $i, i+1, i+2$ | $O(\Delta x^2)$ |
| First derivative, 3-pt backward | $\dfrac{3f_i - 4f_{i-1} + f_{i-2}}{2\Delta x}$ | $i-2, i-1, i$ | $O(\Delta x^2)$ |
| Second derivative, central | $\dfrac{f_{i+1} - 2f_i + f_{i-1}}{\Delta x^2}$ | $i-1, i, i+1$ | $O(\Delta x^2)$ |
| Second derivative, forward | $\dfrac{f_i - 2f_{i+1} + f_{i+2}}{\Delta x^2}$ | $i, i+1, i+2$ | $O(\Delta x)$ |
| Second derivative, 4-pt central | $\dfrac{-f_{i+2} + 16f_{i+1} - 30f_i + 16f_{i-1} - f_{i-2}}{12 \Delta x^2}$ | 5 points | $O(\Delta x^4)$ |
| Third derivative, central | $\dfrac{f_{i+2} - 2f_{i+1} + 2f_{i-1} - f_{i-2}}{2\Delta x^3}$ | 4 points | $O(\Delta x^2)$ |
| Fourth derivative, central | $\dfrac{f_{i+2} - 4f_{i+1} + 6f_i - 4f_{i-1} + f_{i-2}}{\Delta x^4}$ | 5 points | $O(\Delta x^2)$ |

> [!NOTE]
> The 4-point central second derivative formula is **highly recommended for KTU derivations** because it tests the student's ability to handle larger linear systems from polynomial fitting. It is a favorite in ESE questions.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE] > **Industrial Applications:**
> 1. **Aerospace CFD (NACA Airfoils, Supersonic Nozzles):** Central difference schemes of $O(\Delta x^2)$ are the default for inviscid flux derivatives.
> 2. **Heat Exchanger Design:** The second derivative central formula discretizes the 1-D heat conduction equation $\frac{d^2T}{dx^2} = 0$.
> 3. **Combustion Modeling:** Finite difference formulas derived using these methods are used in the scalar transport equations.
> 4. **Weather & Climate Models:** The advection term $u \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial x}$ uses the schemes derived in this module.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Taylor's Series Approach: 3-Point Forward Difference for First Derivative ($O(\Delta x^2)$)

**Goal:** Derive the formula $\left(\frac{df}{dx}\right)_i = \frac{-3f_i + 4f_{i+1} - f_{i+2}}{2\Delta x}$ with truncation error $O(\Delta x^2)$.

**Step 1:** Write the Taylor expansion of $f_{i+1}$ about $x_i$:

$$f_{i+1} = f_i + \Delta x f'_i + \frac{\Delta x^2}{2} f''_i + \frac{\Delta x^3}{6} f'''_i + \frac{\Delta x^4}{24} f^{(4)}_i + O(\Delta x^5) \quad \text{...(1)}$$

**Step 2:** Write the Taylor expansion of $f_{i+2}$ about $x_i$ (using $2\Delta x$ as the step):

$$f_{i+2} = f_i + 2\Delta x f'_i + \frac{(2\Delta x)^2}{2} f''_i + \frac{(2\Delta x)^3}{6} f'''_i + \frac{(2\Delta x)^4}{24} f^{(4)}_i + O(\Delta x^5) \quad \text{...(2)}$$

**Step 3:** Simplify Equation (2):

$$f_{i+2} = f_i + 2\Delta x f'_i + 2\Delta x^2 f''_i + \frac{4\Delta x^3}{3} f'''_i + \frac{2\Delta x^4}{3} f^{(4)}_i + O(\Delta x^5) \quad \text{...(2')}$$

**Step 4:** Form the linear combination $a \cdot f_{i+1} + b \cdot f_{i+2}$ to eliminate $f''_i$ and $f'''_i$, retaining only $f'_i$ as the leading unknown.

We need:
- $a + b = 0$ (to eliminate $f_i$),
- $a \cdot 1 + b \cdot 2 = 1$ (coefficient of $f'_i$, multiplied by $\Delta x$),
- $a \cdot \frac{1}{2} + b \cdot 2 = 0$ (to eliminate $f''_i$).

**Step 5:** Solve the system. From $a + b = 0$, $b = -a$. Substituting into the second equation:

$$a + 2(-a) = 1 \implies -a = 1 \implies a = -1, \quad b = 1$$

Check the third equation: $-\frac{1}{2} + 2 = \frac{3}{2} \neq 0$. So the 3-point formula cannot eliminate $f''_i$ if we also eliminate $f'''_i$. We must **choose** to eliminate $f''_i$ to obtain $O(\Delta x^2)$ accuracy.

Setting the third condition:
$$a \cdot \frac{1}{2} + b \cdot 2 = 0 \implies a = -4b$$

From $a + b = 0$: $b = -a$, so $a - a/4 \cdot \cdot$ — let us restart. Conditions to eliminate $f_i$ and $f''_i$:

$$a + b = 0, \quad \frac{a}{2} + 2b = 0$$

From the first: $b = -a$. Substituting: $\frac{a}{2} - 2a = 0 \implies -\frac{3a}{2} = 0 \implies a = 0$. This eliminates both! Not useful.

The standard 3-point forward formula **eliminates $f_i$** (i.e., uses it as a reference) and **truncates at $O(\Delta x^2)$** by ignoring terms $\Delta x^2$ and higher. So:

$$4 \cdot (1) - (2'): \quad 4f_{i+1} - f_{i+2} = 3f_i + 2\Delta x f'_i - \frac{2\Delta x^3}{3} f'''_i + O(\Delta x^4)$$

**Step 6:** Solve for $f'_i$:

$$2\Delta x f'_i = 4f_{i+1} - f_{i+2} - 3f_i + \frac{2\Delta x^3}{3} f'''_i + O(\Delta x^4)$$

$$\left(\frac{df}{dx}\right)_i = \frac{-3f_i + 4f_{i+1} - f_{i+2}}{2\Delta x} + \frac{\Delta x^2}{3} f'''_i + O(\Delta x^3)$$

**Step 7:** Identify the truncation error:

$$\boxed{\left(\frac{df}{dx}\right)_i \approx \frac{-3f_i + 4f_{i+1} - f_{i+2}}{2\Delta x} \quad \text{with truncation error } O(\Delta x^2)}$$

> [!NOTE] > **KTU Examiner Tip:** When you derive a finite difference formula, always explicitly write the leading neglected term (e.g., $-\frac{\Delta x^2}{3} f'''_i$ in this case). Examiners award **2 bonus marks** for correct error order identification.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Polynomial Fitting Approach: 4-Point Central Difference for First Derivative ($O(\Delta x^2)$)

**Goal:** Derive the central first-derivative formula using 4 points: $x_{i-2}, x_{i-1}, x_{i+1}, x_{i+2}$ (note: $x_i$ is not used in the standard form, but the result is equivalent). We will use $x_{i-1}, x_i, x_{i+1}, x_{i+2}$ to find $f'_i$.

**Step 1:** Assume a cubic polynomial:

$$f(x) = a_0 + a_1 x + a_2 x^2 + a_3 x^3$$

**Step 2:** Apply at four grid points. Place $x_i$ at the origin, with $\Delta x$ as the uniform spacing. Thus: $x_{i-1} = -\Delta x$, $x_i = 0$, $x_{i+1} = \Delta x$, $x_{i+2} = 2\Delta x$.

$$f_{i-1} = a_0 - a_1 \Delta x + a_2 \Delta x^2 - a_3 \Delta x^3 \quad \text{...(I)}$$
$$f_i = a_0 \quad \text{...(II)}$$
$$f_{i+1} = a_0 + a_1 \Delta x + a_2 \Delta x^2 + a_3 \Delta x^3 \quad \text{...(III)}$$
$$f_{i+2} = a_0 + 2a_1 \Delta x + 4a_2 \Delta x^2 + 8a_3 \Delta x^3 \quad \text{...(IV)}$$

**Step 3:** Solve for $a_1$. Subtract (I) from (III):

$$f_{i+1} - f_{i-1} = 2a_1 \Delta x + 2a_3 \Delta x^3 \quad \text{...(V)}$$

**Step 4:** Form another combination to eliminate $a_3$. Subtract (I) from (IV):

$$f_{i+2} - f_{i-1} = 3a_1 \Delta x + 3a_2 \Delta x^2 + 9a_3 \Delta x^3 \quad \text{...(VI)}$$

Wait, that introduces $a_2$. Instead, form a combination using (III) and (IV). Subtract (III) from (IV):

$$f_{i+2} - f_{i+1} = a_1 \Delta x + 3a_2 \Delta x^2 + 7a_3 \Delta x^3 \quad \text{...(VII)}$$

To eliminate both $a_2$ and $a_3$, we form a more systematic linear combination. Let us solve the full $4 \times 4$ system for $a_1$ directly.

In matrix form (with $\Delta x$ absorbed):

$$\begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 & 1 & -1 \\ 1 & 0 & 0 & 0 \\ 1 & 1 & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 & 4 & 8 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} a_0 \\ a_1 \Delta x \\ a_2 \Delta x^2 \\ a_3 \Delta x^3 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} f_{i-1} \\ f_i \\ f_{i+1} \\ f_{i+2} \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 5:** Use Cramer's rule or row reduction for $a_1$. Apply row operations: $R_2 \to R_2 - R_3$, $R_1 \to R_1 + R_3$, $R_4 \to R_4 - R_3$:

$$R_1 + R_3: \quad 2a_0 + 0 + 2a_2 \Delta x^2 + 0 = f_{i-1} + f_{i+1}$$

$$R_2 - R_3: \quad 0 - a_1 \Delta x + 0 - 0 = f_i - f_{i+1}$$

$$R_4 - R_3: \quad 0 + a_1 \Delta x + 3a_2 \Delta x^2 + 7a_3 \Delta x^3 = f_{i+2} - f_{i+1}$$

From $R_2 - R_3$:

$$a_1 \Delta x = f_{i+1} - f_i$$

But this gives the forward difference, not what we want. The 4-point central formula uses a *different* point arrangement: we use $x_{i-2}, x_{i-1}, x_i, x_{i+1}$ (or symmetric), and $a_1$ corresponds to the slope at a *non-central* point. For central schemes using 4 points, we use $x_{i-2}, x_{i-1}, x_{i+1}, x_{i+2}$ — these are symmetric about $x_i = 0$, so $a_1 = f'_i$ directly.

**Re-setup with symmetric 4 points:** $x_{i-2} = -2\Delta x$, $x_{i-1} = -\Delta x$, $x_{i+1} = \Delta x$, $x_{i+2} = 2\Delta x$. Apply cubic polynomial:

$$f_{i-2} = a_0 - 2a_1 \Delta x + 4a_2 \Delta x^2 - 8a_3 \Delta x^3 \quad \text{...(I')}$$
$$f_{i-1} = a_0 - a_1 \Delta x + a_2 \Delta x^2 - a_3 \Delta x^3 \quad \text{...(II')}$$
$$f_{i+1} = a_0 + a_1 \Delta x + a_2 \Delta x^2 + a_3 \Delta x^3 \quad \text{...(III')}$$
$$f_{i+2} = a_0 + 2a_1 \Delta x + 4a_2 \Delta x^2 + 8a_3 \Delta x^3 \quad \text{...(IV')}$$

**Step 6:** Subtract (II') from (III'):

$$f_{i+1} - f_{i-1} = 2a_1 \Delta x + 2a_3 \Delta x^3 \quad \text{...(A)}$$

**Step 7:** Subtract (I') from (IV'):

$$f_{i+2} - f_{i-2} = 4a_1 \Delta x + 16a_3 \Delta x^3 \quad \text{...(B)}$$

**Step 8:** Form the combination $8(A) - (B)$ to eliminate $a_3$:

$$8(f_{i+1} - f_{i-1}) - (f_{i+2} - f_{i-2}) = 16 a_1 \Delta x - 4a_1 \Delta x + 0 = 12 a_1 \Delta x$$

$$12 a_1 \Delta x = -f_{i+2} + 8f_{i+1} - 8f_{i-1} + f_{i-2}$$

**Step 9:** Solve for $a_1 = f'_i$:

$$\left(\frac{df}{dx}\right)_i = a_1 = \frac{f_{i-2} - 8f_{i-1} + 8f_{i+1} - f_{i+2}}{12 \Delta x}$$

$$\boxed{\left(\frac{df}{dx}\right)_i \approx \frac{f_{i-2} - 8f_{i-1} + 8f_{i+1} - f_{i+2}}{12 \Delta x} \quad \text{with truncation error } O(\Delta x^4)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Insight:** This 4-point central formula is **$O(\Delta x^4)$** — it is one order more accurate than the simple 3-point central scheme! It is widely used in high-accuracy CFD codes.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Polynomial Fitting: Second Derivative, 5-Point Central ($O(\Delta x^4)$)

**Goal:** Derive $\left(\frac{d^2f}{dx^2}\right)_i$ using 5 symmetric points: $x_{i-2}, x_{i-1}, x_i, x_{i+1}, x_{i+2}$.

**Step 1:** Assume a quartic polynomial $f(x) = a_0 + a_1 x + a_2 x^2 + a_3 x^3 + a_4 x^4$.

**Step 2:** Apply at the 5 points (with $x_i = 0$):

$$f_{i-2} = a_0 - 2a_1 \Delta x + 4a_2 \Delta x^2 - 8a_3 \Delta x^3 + 16 a_4 \Delta x^4 \quad \text{...(P1)}$$
$$f_{i-1} = a_0 - a_1 \Delta x + a_2 \Delta x^2 - a_3 \Delta x^3 + a_4 \Delta x^4 \quad \text{...(P2)}$$
$$f_i = a_0 \quad \text{...(P3)}$$
$$f_{i+1} = a_0 + a_1 \Delta x + a_2 \Delta x^2 + a_3 \Delta x^3 + a_4 \Delta x^4 \quad \text{...(P4)}$$
$$f_{i+2} = a_0 + 2a_1 \Delta x + 4a_2 \Delta x^2 + 8a_3 \Delta x^3 + 16 a_4 \Delta x^4 \quad \text{...(P5)}$$

**Step 3:** Form the symmetric sum $P1 + P5$:

$$f_{i-2} + f_{i+2} = 2a_0 + 8a_2 \Delta x^2 + 32 a_4 \Delta x^4 \quad \text{...(S1)}$$

**Step 4:** Form the symmetric sum $P2 + P4$:

$$f_{i-1} + f_{i+1} = 2a_0 + 2a_2 \Delta x^2 + 2a_4 \Delta x^4 \quad \text{...(S2)}$$

**Step 5:** From $P3$: $a_0 = f_i$.

**Step 6:** Form the combination $16(S2) - (S1)$:

$$16(f_{i-1} + f_{i+1}) - (f_{i-2} + f_{i+2}) = 32a_0 + 32a_2 \Delta x^2 + 32 a_4 \Delta x^4 - 2a_0 - 8a_2 \Delta x^2 - 32 a_4 \Delta x^4$$
$$= 30 a_0 + 24 a_2 \Delta x^2$$

**Step 7:** Solve for $a_2$:

$$24 a_2 \Delta x^2 = 16(f_{i-1} + f_{i+1}) - (f_{i-2} + f_{i+2}) - 30 f_i$$
$$24 a_2 \Delta x^2 = -f_{i-2} + 16f_{i-1} - 30 f_i + 16 f_{i+1} - f_{i+2}$$

**Step 8:** Since $f''(x_i) = 2 a_2$:

$$\left(\frac{d^2f}{dx^2}\right)_i = 2 a_2 = \frac{-f_{i-2} + 16f_{i-1} - 30f_i + 16f_{i+1} - f_{i+2}}{12 \Delta x^2}$$

$$\boxed{\left(\frac{d^2f}{dx^2}\right)_i \approx \frac{-f_{i-2} + 16f_{i-1} - 30f_i + 16f_{i+1} - f_{i+2}}{12 \Delta x^2} \quad \text{with truncation error } O(\Delta x^4)}$$

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of the Two Approaches

> [!NOTE]
> The following comparison table is **highly examinable** under "Compare and contrast" type questions in KTU.

| **Aspect** | **Taylor's Series Approach** | **Polynomial Fitting Approach** |
|---|---|---|
| **Starting Point** | Series expansion of $f$ at a reference node | Assume a polynomial of degree $n-1$ |
| **Required Concept** | Differential calculus (derivatives of $f$) | Linear algebra (solving for coefficients) |
| **Truncation Error** | Explicitly visible in the series remainder | Implicit; inferred from polynomial degree |
| **Higher-Order Extension** | Becomes algebraically messy with more terms | Systematic: just solve a larger linear system |
| **Symmetry Exploitation** | Requires careful sign tracking | Naturally handled by the linear system |
| **Pedagogical Value** | Connects finite differences to calculus | Connects finite differences to numerical linear algebra |
| **Result for Same Stencil** | **Identical formulas** (mathematically equivalent) | **Identical formulas** (mathematically equivalent) |
| **Ease of Higher-Order Derivative** | Tedious to derive $\geq 4$th order | Easier; just differentiate the polynomial |
| **Boundary Conditions** | Naturally accommodates one-sided formulas | Can also produce one-sided formulas but symmetric schemes are easier |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Derivation Workflow Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Need Finite Difference Approximation] --> B{Choose Method}
    B -->|Taylor's Series| C1[Select Reference Node x_i]
    B -->|Polynomial Fitting| C2[Assume Polynomial Degree n-1]
    C1 --> D1[Expand f at Neighbor Nodes]
    D1 --> E1[Truncate at Desired Order]
    E1 --> F1[Solve Algebraically for Derivative]
    F1 --> G1[Identify Truncation Error from Dropped Terms]
    C2 --> D2[Apply Polynomial at n Grid Points]
    D2 --> E2[Solve n x n Linear System for Coefficients]
    E2 --> F2[Differentiate Polynomial Analytically]
    F2 --> G2[Evaluate at Required Node]
    G1 --> H[Final Finite Difference Formula]
    G2 --> H[Final Finite Difference Formula]
    H --> I[Validate: Both Methods Must Agree]
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Input["INPUT LAYER"]

        P3[Boundary Conditions]
    end

        Q1[Choose Stencil and Order]
        Q2[Taylor's Series Approach]
        Q3[Polynomial Fitting Approach]
    end

    subgraph Assembly["ALGEBRAIC ASSEMBLY LAYER"]
        R1[Tridiagonal or Banded Matrix]
        R2[Right Hand Side Vector]
    end

    subgraph Solver["SOLVER LAYER"]
        S1[Thomas Algorithm or LU Decomposition]
        S2[Iterative Solvers if Nonlinear]
    end

    subgraph Output["OUTPUT LAYER"]
        T1[Field Variable phi at All Nodes]
        T2[Convergence and Error Analysis]
    end

    P1 --> Q1
    P2 --> Q1
    P3 --> Q1
    Q1 --> Q2
    Q1 --> Q3
    Q2 --> R1
    Q3 --> R1
    P3 --> R2
    R1 --> S1
    R2 --> S1
    S1 --> S2
    S1 --> T1
    T1 --> T2
```

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["x_{i-2}"] --- B["x_{i-1}"]

    style C fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px

```

> [!NOTE] > **Reading the Stencil:**
> - The **central yellow node** is the reference $x_i$ where the derivative is computed.
> - The **light blue nodes** $x_{i-1}$ and $x_{i+1}$ are the *3-point central* stencil.
> - The **light teal nodes** $x_{i-2}$ and $x_{i+2}$ extend to the *5-point stencil* for $O(\Delta x^4)$ accuracy.

### 4.4 Mermaid Concept Map — How the Two Approaches Interact with CFD

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Governing PDE e.g. Navier-Stokes] --> B[Discretize Spatial Derivatives]
    B --> C{Choose Difference Scheme}
    C -->|First-order upwind| D1[Forward or Backward Difference]
    C -->|Second-order central| D2[Central Difference]
    C -->|Compact schemes| D3[Padé-type Higher-Order]
    D1 --> E[Taylor's Series or Polynomial Fitting]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    E --> F[Discretized Algebraic System]
    F --> G[Time Integration or Steady Solve]
    G --> H[Numerical Solution]
    H --> I[Compare with Experimental or Analytical]
    I --> J[Grid Refinement Study]
    J --> K[Validate Order of Accuracy]
```

> [!NOTE] > **The Role of this Module in CFD:**
> This module forms the **mathematical foundation** of finite difference methods (FDM). The Taylor and Polynomial Fitting approaches are the two conceptual "engines" that power every discretization in FDM. Module 2 is the **gateway** to Module 3 (Finite Volume Method) and Module 4 (Stability and Consistency).
