## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Truncation Error of the Forward-Difference Scheme

We approximate $u'(x_i)$ using the forward stencil:

$$\left( \frac{\partial u}{\partial x} \right)_i \approx \frac{u_{i+1} - u_i}{\Delta x}$$

**Step 1:** Taylor-expand $u_{i+1}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
u_{i+1} = u_i + \Delta x \, u'_i + \frac{\Delta x^2}{2!} u''_i + \frac{\Delta x^3}{3!} u'''_i + \frac{\Delta x^4}{4!} u^{(4)}_i + \cdots
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Subtract $u_i$ from both sides:

$$
\begin{aligned}
u_{i+1} - u_i = \Delta x \, u'_i + \frac{\Delta x^2}{2} u''_i + \frac{\Delta x^3}{6} u'''_i + \frac{\Delta x^4}{24} u^{(4)}_i + \cdots
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Divide by $\Delta x$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{u_{i+1} - u_i}{\Delta x} = u'_i + \frac{\Delta x}{2} u''_i + \frac{\Delta x^2}{6} u'''_i + \frac{\Delta x^3}{24} u^{(4)}_i + \cdots
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Identify the truncation error:

$$
\boxed{\tau = \frac{\Delta x}{2} u''_i + \frac{\Delta x^2}{6} u'''_i + \cdots = O(\Delta x)}
$$

**Comparison Note:** The forward-difference scheme is only **first-order accurate** because the leading error term scales as $\Delta x$, not $\Delta x^2$. For the same grid spacing, the central difference is **twice as accurate per node** in terms of error exponent.

---

## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation: Stability of the 1-D Heat Equation FTCS Scheme

Consider the 1-D heat (diffusion) equation:

$$u_t = \alpha \, u_{xx}$$

The Forward-Time Central-Space (FTCS) finite-difference approximation is:

$$\frac{u_i^{n+1} - u_i^n}{\Delta t} = \alpha \cdot \frac{u_{i+1}^n - 2 u_i^n + u_{i-1}^n}{\Delta x^2}$$

**Step 1:** Substitute a Fourier-mode error into the discrete equation:

$$\epsilon_i^n = G^n e^{I k i \Delta x}$$

Here $G$ is the amplification factor (a complex number in general) and $I = \sqrt{-1}$.

**Step 2:** Plug into the LHS:

$$
\begin{aligned}
u_i^{n+1} - u_i^n &= G^{n+1} e^{I k i \Delta x} - G^n e^{I k i \Delta x} \\
&= G^n (G - 1) e^{I k i \Delta x}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Plug into the RHS:

$$
\begin{aligned}
u_{i+1}^n - 2 u_i^n + u_{i-1}^n &= G^n e^{I k i \Delta x} \left[ e^{I k \Delta x} - 2 + e^{-I k \Delta x} \right] \\
&= G^n e^{I k i \Delta x} \cdot \left[ 2 \cos(k \Delta x) - 2 \right] \\
&= G^n e^{I k i \Delta x} \cdot \left[ -4 \sin^2\left( \frac{k \Delta x}{2} \right) \right]
\end{aligned}
$$

(We used the identity $\cos\theta - 1 = -2\sin^2(\theta/2)$.)

**Step 4:** Equate LHS and RHS (after dividing by $G^n e^{I k i \Delta x}$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{G - 1}{\Delta t} &= \alpha \cdot \frac{-4 \sin^2\left( \frac{k \Delta x}{2} \right)}{\Delta x^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5:** Solve for $G$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
G = 1 - \frac{4 \alpha \Delta t}{\Delta x^2} \sin^2\left( \frac{k \Delta x}{2} \right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6:** Apply the stability requirement $\vert G \vert \le 1$. Since $G$ is real, the most restrictive case is when $\sin^2(\cdot) = 1$ (i.e., $k \Delta x = \pi$):

$$
\boxed{G_{min} = 1 - \frac{4 \alpha \Delta t}{\Delta x^2} \ge -1}$$

**Step 7:** Solve for the time-step restriction:

$$
\begin{aligned}
1 - \frac{4 \alpha \Delta t}{\Delta x^2} &\ge -1 \\
\frac{4 \alpha \Delta t}{\Delta x^2} &\le 2 \\
\boxed{\frac{\alpha \Delta t}{\Delta x^2} &\le \frac{1}{2}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Interpretation:** For the explicit FTCS scheme applied to the heat equation, the dimensionless number $r = \alpha \Delta t / \Delta x^2$ (sometimes called the **Fourier number** or **diffusion number**) must be $\le 0.5$. If $r > 0.5$, perturbations of wavelength $2\Delta x$ grow unboundedly — the scheme blows up.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | Truncation Error | Round-Off Error | Discretization Error |
|---|---|---|---|
| Source | Approximation of calculus by algebra | Finite word length of computer | Combined effect |
| Direction of action | Reduces as $\Delta \to 0$ | **Increases** as $\Delta \to 0$ (more ops) | Has a minimum (U-curve) |
| Determinism | Deterministic | Stochastic | Mixed |
| Control | Use higher-order schemes, finer grid | Use double precision, careful summation | Optimize grid (Richardson extrapolation) |
| Typical magnitude | $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-8}$ | $\sim 10^{-15}$ per operation | $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-6}$ overall |

> [!NOTE]
> **The U-Curve Phenomenon (KTU Favourite Question):** As $\Delta x$ decreases, the truncation error decreases (good!), but the round-off error *increases* (bad!) because more arithmetic operations are performed. The total discretization error has a **minimum** at an optimal $\Delta x$. Going finer than this is *worse* — a famous pitfall in engineering CFD practice.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Flow: The Lifecycle of a CFD Error

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the interaction between the four error/stability concepts and the governing PDE solution pipeline. Each block has a strictly alphanumeric identifier and a clean uppercase text label (no markdown formatting).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([Continuous PDE]) --> T1[Step 1 Discretization]
    T1[Step 1 Discretization] --> T2[Discrete Algebraic Equations]
    T2[Discrete Algebraic Equations] --> T3[Step 2 Numerical Solver]
    T3[Step 2 Numerical Solver] --> T4[Step 3 Floating Point Arithmetic]
    T4[Step 3 Floating Point Arithmetic] --> end1([Numerical Solution uh])

    errA[Truncation Error Tau O of Delta x p] -. introduced at .-> T1
    errB[Round Off Error Epsilon ro] -. introduced at .-> T4

    T2 --> check{Scheme Properties Check}
    check -- tau tends to 0 --> cons[Consistent]
    check -- G less or equal 1 --> stab[Stable]
    cons AND stab --> lax[Lax Equivalence Theorem]
    lax --> conv[Convergence uh tends to u exact]
    conv --> end1

    style start fill:#1e3a5f,color:#ffffff
    style end1 fill:#5f1e1e,color:#ffffff
    style lax fill:#1e5f3a,color:#ffffff
    style conv fill:#5f4a1e,color:#ffffff
    style errA fill:#5f1e4a,color:#ffffff
    style errB fill:#5f1e4a,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.2 Decision Tree: How to Diagnose Numerical Issues in a CFD Code

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([CFD Solution Looks Wrong]) --> q1{Is the scheme consistent}
    q1 -- No, truncation error does not vanish --> branch1[Fix the Stencil Increase Order or Use Central Differences]
    q1 -- Yes --> q2{Is the scheme stable}
    q2 -- No, G greater than 1 for some k --> branch2[Reduce Time Step Apply CFL or Fourier Condition]
    q2 -- Yes --> q3{Is the grid fine enough}
    q3 -- No --> branch3[Refine Mesh Use Grid Independence Study]
    q3 -- Yes --> q4{Is round off dominating}
    q4 -- Yes, many iterations --> branch4[Use Double Precision or Compensated Summation]
    q4 -- No --> branch5[Check Boundary Conditions and Source Terms]

    style startA fill:#1e3a5f,color:#ffffff
    style branch1 fill:#5f1e1e,color:#ffffff
    style branch2 fill:#5f1e1e,color:#ffffff
    style branch3 fill:#5f4a1e,color:#ffffff
    style branch4 fill:#5f4a1e,color:#ffffff
    style branch5 fill:#1e5f3a,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.3 Error Magnitude Schematic (U-Curve)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Coarse Grid Delta x large] --> B[Optimal Delta x Star]
    B --> C[Very Fine Grid Delta x tiny]
    A2[Truncation Error High] -. dominates .-> A
    A3[Round Off Error Low] -. minimal .-> A
    B2[Truncation Error Moderate] -. declining .-> B
    B3[Round Off Error Moderate] -. rising .-> B
    C2[Truncation Error Very Low] -. negligible .-> C
    C3[Round Off Error Very High] -. dominates .-> C

    style A fill:#5f1e1e,color:#ffffff
    style B fill:#1e5f3a,color:#ffffff
    style C fill:#5f1e1e,color:#ffffff
    style B2 fill:#5f4a1e,color:#ffffff
    style B3 fill:#5f4a1e,color:#ffffff
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The optimal $\Delta x^*$ in the U-curve is where the slopes of the truncation-error and round-off-error lines are equal in magnitude but opposite in sign. For double-precision CFD, this is typically around $\Delta x \sim 10^{-7}$ to $10^{-8}$ m, far finer than engineering meshes need — meaning **round-off is rarely the bottleneck** in standard industrial CFD. It becomes critical in Direct Numerical Simulation (DNS) and Molecular Dynamics.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Define truncation error. Show that the central difference approximation of the first derivative has a truncation error of order $O(\Delta x^2)$.**

**Model Answer (Step-by-step):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Truncation error is the difference between the exact value of the derivative in the Taylor series and its discrete approximation, measured as the grid spacing goes to zero.

**Taylor expansions (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
u_{i+1} = u_i + \Delta x u'_i + \frac{\Delta x^2}{2} u''_i + \frac{\Delta x^3}{6} u'''_i + \cdots \\
u_{i-1} = u_i - \Delta x u'_i + \frac{\Delta x^2}{2} u''_i - \frac{\Delta x^3}{6} u'''_i + \cdots
\end{aligned}
$$

**Subtract and divide (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
u'_i = \frac{u_{i+1} - u_{i-1}}{2\Delta x} - \frac{\Delta x^2}{6} u'''_i - \cdots
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore $\tau = O(\Delta x^2)$, confirming second-order accuracy.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**State and explain the Lax Equivalence Theorem. Why is it restricted to linear PDEs?**

**Model Answer:**

**Statement (1.5 marks):** For a well-posed linear initial-value problem and a consistent finite-difference scheme, the scheme is convergent **if and only if** it is stable.

**Explanation (1 mark):** Consistency alone does not guarantee that numerical round-off errors remain bounded. Stability ensures bounded growth. Together, they imply convergence (the numerical solution approaches the true PDE solution as $\Delta x, \Delta t \to 0$).

**Restriction to linear PDEs (0.5 marks):** The proof relies on linearity (superposition principle) and bounded operator theory, which fail for nonlinear problems. For nonlinear PDEs (e.g., Navier–Stokes), stability and consistency must be verified by other means (energy methods, nonlinear von Neumann analysis).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

#### (a) Derive the truncation error of the second-order central difference approximation for $u''(x)$. Hence explain why this scheme is consistent. **(7 marks)**

**Step-by-step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (2 marks) — Taylor expansions:** Expand $u_{i+1}$ and $u_{i-1}$ about $x_i$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
u_{i+1} = u_i + \Delta x u'_i + \frac{\Delta x^2}{2} u''_i + \frac{\Delta x^3}{6} u'''_i + \frac{\Delta x^4}{24} u^{(4)}_i + \frac{\Delta x^5}{120} u^{(5)}_i + \cdots \\
u_{i-1} = u_i - \Delta x u'_i + \frac{\Delta x^2}{2} u''_i - \frac{\Delta x^3}{6} u'''_i + \frac{\Delta x^4}{24} u^{(4)}_i - \frac{\Delta x^5}{120} u^{(5)}_i + \cdots
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 (2 marks) — Add the two expansions:** The odd-order terms cancel and the even-order terms double:

$$
\begin{aligned}
u_{i+1} + u_{i-1} = 2 u_i + \Delta x^2 u''_i + \frac{\Delta x^4}{12} u^{(4)}_i + \frac{\Delta x^6}{360} u^{(6)}_i + \cdots
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 (1 mark) — Subtract $2u_i$ and divide by $\Delta x^2$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{u_{i+1} - 2u_i + u_{i-1}}{\Delta x^2} = u''_i + \frac{\Delta x^2}{12} u^{(4)}_i + \frac{\Delta x^4}{360} u^{(6)}_i + \cdots
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 (1 mark) — Isolate the truncation error:**

$$
\boxed{\tau = \frac{\Delta x^2}{12} u^{(4)}_i + O(\Delta x^4) = O(\Delta x^2)}
$$

**Step 5 (1 mark) — Consistency:** Since $\tau \to 0$ as $\Delta x \to 0$, the central difference scheme for the second derivative is **consistent** with the continuous operator $\partial^2 / \partial x^2$.

---

#### (b) For the 1-D wave equation $u_t + c u_x = 0$ discretized by the explicit Upwind scheme, derive the CFL stability condition. **(7 marks)**

**Step-by-step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (1 mark) — Discretization:** Assume $c > 0$. The Upwind scheme is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{u_i^{n+1} - u_i^n}{\Delta t} = -c \cdot \frac{u_i^n - u_{i-1}^n}{\Delta x}
\end{aligned}
$$

Rearranged:

$$
\begin{aligned}
u_i^{n+1} = u_i^n - C (u_i^n - u_{i-1}^n)
\end{aligned}
$$

where $C = c \Delta t / \Delta x$ is the **Courant number**.

**Step 2 (1.5 marks) — Von Neumann substitution:** Let $\epsilon_i^n = G^n e^{I k i \Delta x}$. Substituting:

$$
\begin{aligned}
G - 1 = -C (1 - e^{-I k \Delta x})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 (1.5 marks) — Compute the modulus:** Note that $1 - e^{-I\theta} = 1 - \cos\theta + I \sin\theta$, so:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\vert 1 - e^{-I k \Delta x} \vert^2 = (1 - \cos k\Delta x)^2 + \sin^2 k\Delta x = 2(1 - \cos k\Delta x) = 4 \sin^2 \frac{k \Delta x}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 (1.5 marks) — Amplification factor modulus:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\vert G \vert^2 = \left[ 1 - C(1 - \cos k\Delta x) \right]^2 + C^2 \sin^2 k\Delta x
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 (1.5 marks) — Stability condition:** Setting $\vert G \vert^2 \le 1$ and simplifying (using the worst case $k \Delta x = \pi$, i.e. $\sin^2 = 1$, $\cos = -1$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
G_{min} = 1 - 2C \ge -1 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \boxed{C = \frac{c \Delta t}{\Delta x} \le 1}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout #1:** Students frequently confuse the **CFL number** (named after Courant, Friedrichs, and Lewy) with the **mesh Fourier number** used in diffusion problems. Remember: **CFL is for hyperbolic PDEs (waves, convection)**, while the Fourier number is for **parabolic PDEs (heat, diffusion)**. Mixing them up costs 1–2 marks.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

#### (a) Explain discretization error, truncation error, and round-off error with a clear distinction. Show with a sketch how the total error varies with grid spacing (U-curve). **(7 marks)**

**Step-by-step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (2 marks) — Definitions:**
- **Discretization error:** $e_{disc} = u_{exact,discrete} - u_{exact,continuous}$. The total error of the discrete system.
- **Truncation error:** $\tau = \mathcal{L}(u) - \mathcal{L}_h(u)$. The error in replacing the continuous operator by a discrete stencil.
- **Round-off error:** $\epsilon_{ro}$ — the error from finite floating-point representation in computer arithmetic.

**Step 2 (1 mark) — Distinction table (mental):**

| Aspect | Truncation | Round-off |
|---|---|---|
| Direction with $\Delta$ | Decreases | Increases |
| Type | Deterministic | Stochastic |
| Magnitude | $O(\Delta^p)$ | $O(N \cdot \epsilon_{mach})$ |

**Step 3 (2 marks) — U-Curve explanation:** Total error = Truncation + Round-off.

- For large $\Delta x$ (coarse grid): Truncation dominates. Error $\sim C_1 \Delta^p$, decreases as $\Delta x$ decreases.
- For small $\Delta x$ (very fine grid): Round-off dominates. Error $\sim C_2 N \epsilon_{mach} \sim C_2 \epsilon_{mach} / \Delta x$, increases as $\Delta x$ decreases.
- There exists an **optimal $\Delta x^*$** where total error is minimum.

**Step 4 (2 marks) — Sketch (described):** A typical U-shape curve on a log-log plot with $\Delta x$ on the abscissa and total error on the ordinate. The left branch is dominated by round-off (rising), the right branch by truncation (falling), meeting at a minimum.

---

#### (b) A finite-difference scheme has truncation error $\tau = O(\Delta x^2)$ and is found to be stable by the von Neumann method. Discuss whether the scheme will converge. **(7 marks)**

**Step-by-step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (1.5 marks) — Check consistency:** Since $\tau = O(\Delta x^2)$ and $\tau \to 0$ as $\Delta x \to 0$, the scheme is **consistent** with the governing PDE.

**Step 2 (1.5 marks) — Note stability:** The von Neumann analysis confirms $\vert G \vert \le 1$ for all wavenumbers $k$, so the scheme is **stable**.

**Step 3 (2 marks) — Apply Lax Equivalence Theorem:** For a linear, well-posed initial-value problem:

$$
\text{Consistency} + \text{Stability} \;\Longleftrightarrow\; \text{Convergence}
$$

Both conditions are satisfied, so **the scheme will converge**.

**Step 4 (1 mark) — Rate of convergence:** The order of convergence equals the order of the truncation error, i.e., $p = 2$ (second-order). The numerical error will decrease as $\Delta x^2$ as the grid is refined (modulo round-off effects).

**Step 5 (1 mark) — Caveat:** The Lax theorem is strictly for **linear** PDEs. For nonlinear PDEs (e.g., Navier–Stokes), additional nonlinear stability analysis is required.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout #2:** A common student error is to state that **consistency implies stability** or **stability implies consistency**. These are *independent properties* of a scheme. The Lax theorem shows they *jointly* imply convergence for linear problems. Always write the theorem using the biconditional ($\Longleftrightarrow$) to score full marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Board Exam (PEMET521 — Module 2)**

- **Discretization Error** = $u_{exact,discrete} - u_{exact,continuous}$. It is the *umbrella* term that includes truncation and round-off.

- **Truncation Error** arises from Taylor series truncation. Central differences → $O(\Delta x^2)$; forward/backward → $O(\Delta x)$.

- **Round-Off Error** $\sim N \cdot \epsilon_{mach}$, where $N$ is the number of operations. In double precision, $\epsilon_{mach} \approx 2.22 \times 10^{-16}$.

- **Consistency**: $\tau \to 0$ as $\Delta x, \Delta t \to 0$. It is a *local, algebraic* property of the scheme, independent of boundary conditions.

- **Stability**: $\vert G \vert \le 1$ for all wavenumbers $k$ (von Neumann test). It is a *global* property depending on the time-step.

- **Convergence**: $u_h \to u_{exact}$ as $\Delta x, \Delta t \to 0$. It is what we *want* from a numerical scheme.

- **Lax Equivalence Theorem** (linear PDEs): $\text{Consistency} + \text{Stability} \iff \text{Convergence}$. This is the single most-tested theorem in this module.

- **CFL Condition for Wave Equation** (Upwind/Lax-Wendroff): $C = c \Delta t / \Delta x \le 1$. Violation → numerical explosion.

- **Fourier Number for Heat Equation (FTCS)**: $r = \alpha \Delta t / \Delta x^2 \le 0.5$. Violation → checkerboard oscillations.

- **The U-Curve**: Total error has a minimum at an optimal $\Delta x^*$. Refining beyond this is *counter-productive* (round-off dominates).

- **Implicit schemes** (Backward Euler, Crank–Nicolson) are **unconditionally stable** for the heat equation but require solving a tridiagonal system per time-step.

- **FTCS for the wave equation is ALWAYS unstable** — remember this special case! (Both convection and time discretizations are central, leading to $\vert G \vert > 1$.)

- **Order of accuracy** in space and time are quoted *separately*: a scheme can be $O(\Delta x^2, \Delta t)$.

- **Always write the truncation error as $O(\Delta x^p)$** and explicitly state the value of $p$ in exams.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Iterative Convergence: Condition for Convergence, Rate of Convergence, and Termination of Iteration
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET521 - COMPUTATIONAL FLUID DYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Iterative Convergence: Condition for Convergence, Rate of Convergence, and Termination of Iteration

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In **Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD)**, the governing partial differential equations (PDEs) of fluid flow (Navier–Stokes, continuity, energy) are discretized into large, sparse, often diagonally dominant systems of linear algebraic equations of the form:

$$A \phi = b$$

Because direct solvers (Gauss elimination, LU decomposition) become computationally prohibitive for grids exceeding ~10⁵ cells, **iterative methods** are preferred. These methods start from an initial guess $\phi^{(0)}$ and generate a sequence of approximate solutions $\phi^{(1)}, \phi^{(2)}, \dots, \phi^{(k)}$ through a fixed-point update rule:

$$\phi^{(k+1)} = T \, \phi^{(k)} + C$$

where $T$ is the **iteration matrix** and $C$ is a constant vector derived from the source term and boundary conditions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Iterative Convergence** is the study of *whether*, *how fast*, and *when to stop* this sequence of approximations. The three pillars of this study are:
> 1. **Condition for Convergence** — guarantees the sequence approaches the exact solution.
> 2. **Rate of Convergence** — quantifies the speed of approach.
> 3. **Termination of Iteration** — defines the practical stopping criterion.

### 1.2 The Three Sub-Topics Defined

| Sub-Topic | Core Question It Answers |
|---|---|
| **Condition for Convergence** | *Will* the iterations eventually yield the true solution? |
| **Rate of Convergence** | *How fast* will the iterations reach a prescribed accuracy? |
| **Termination of Iteration** | *When* should we stop iterating in practice? |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Mariner Steering to Port"

Imagine a mariner steering a ship toward a harbour. Every few seconds he glances at the compass, applies a correction, and re-checks his position.

- **Condition for Convergence** is the guarantee that the *steering rule* (turn 30 % of the way toward the harbour each time) will *eventually* dock the ship — i.e., the rudder corrections *shrink* over time.
- **Rate of Convergence** is *how quickly* the ship closes on the harbour — a ship that halves its distance every 5 minutes converges faster than one that does so every 20 minutes.
- **Termination of Iteration** is the moment the mariner decides, *"Good enough, drop the anchor."* He doesn't sail forever — he stops when the harbour wall is within, say, 1 metre (a *tolerance*).

The mariner's steering rule is our **iteration matrix $T$**, his position estimate is $\phi^{(k)}$, and the harbour is the exact solution $\phi$.

> [!NOTE]
> In CFD, *convergence* does **not** mean the numerical solution equals the continuous PDE solution (that is *consistency* + *stability*). It means the iterative *solver* has produced a stable discrete answer within a prescribed tolerance.

---

### 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in CFD

Pressure–velocity coupling (SIMPLE, SIMPLEC, PISO) and large eddy simulation (LES) systems are typically solved using iterative solvers (Jacobi, Gauss–Seidel, SOR, conjugate gradient, multigrid). Selecting a solver, relaxation factor $\omega$, and tolerance $\varepsilon$ are the practical engineering decisions that depend *entirely* on the convergence theory covered in this module.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Building the Iteration Framework

Starting from $A \phi = b$, the matrix $A$ is split as $A = M - N$ (with $M$ invertible and "easy to invert"). The iterative scheme is:

$$M \, \phi^{(k+1)} = N \, \phi^{(k)} + b \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \phi^{(k+1)} = \underbrace{M^{-1} N}_{T} \, \phi^{(k)} + \underbrace{M^{-1} b}_{C}$$

For example:
- **Jacobi**: $M = D$ (diagonal of $A$), $N = L + U$ → $T_J = D^{-1}(L+U)$.
- **Gauss–Seidel**: $M = D + L$, $N = U$ → $T_{GS} = (D+L)^{-1} U$.
- **SOR (Successive Over-Relaxation)**: $M = \frac{1}{\omega}D + L$, $N = \frac{1-\omega}{\omega}U$.

### 2.2 The Error Equation — The Heart of Convergence Analysis

Let $\phi$ be the exact solution. Define the **error vector** at iteration $k$ as:

$$e^{(k)} = \phi - \phi^{(k)}$$

Subtract the iteration equation $\phi^{(k+1)} = T\phi^{(k)} + C$ from the fixed-point identity $\phi = T\phi + C$:

$$e^{(k+1)} = T \, e^{(k)}$$

By induction:

$$e^{(k)} = T^{k} \, e^{(0)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Convergence ⇔ $e^{(k)} \to 0$ as $k \to \infty$ ⇔ $T^{k} \to 0$ matrix.**

### 2.3 Condition for Convergence — Spectral Radius Criterion

The matrix $T^{k} \to 0$ **if and only if every eigenvalue** $\lambda_i$ of $T$ satisfies $|\lambda_i| < 1$. Define the **spectral radius**:

$$\rho(T) = \max_{1 \le i \le n} \vert \lambda_i(T) \vert$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Necessary and Sufficient Condition for Convergence of a Linear Stationary Iterative Scheme:**
> $$\boxed{\;\rho(T) < 1\;}$$
> This is the central theorem of this module and is the most-asked concept in KTU ESE.

**Corollary 1 (Sufficient condition):** If *any* induced matrix norm satisfies $\|T\| < 1$, then convergence is guaranteed (sufficient but not necessary).

**Corollary 2 (Diagonal dominance):** If $A$ is strictly (or irreducibly) diagonally dominant, then both Jacobi and Gauss–Seidel converge.

**Corollary 3 (SPD matrices):** If $A$ is symmetric positive-definite (SPD), the SOR method converges for $0 < \omega < 2$.

### 2.4 Rate of Convergence — How Fast?

Decompose the initial error in the eigenbasis of $T$:

$$e^{(0)} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i v_i \quad\Longrightarrow\quad e^{(k)} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i \lambda_i^{k} v_i$$

The dominant (slowest-decaying) term is the one with $|\lambda_i|$ closest to $\rho(T)$. Hence, asymptotically:

$$\|e^{(k)}\| \approx C \, [\rho(T)]^{k}$$

The **average (linear) rate of convergence** in iterations per *decimal digit* of error reduction is:

$$R = -\log_{10}\rho(T)$$

The **asymptotic rate of convergence** in *natural* units (Napierian) is:

$$R_{\infty} = -\ln \rho(T)$$

**Number of iterations to reduce the error by a factor $1/\varepsilon$:**

$$k \;\ge\; \frac{\ln(\varepsilon_0 / \varepsilon_t)}{\ln\!\big(1/\rho(T)\big)} \;=\; \frac{\ln(\varepsilon_0/\varepsilon_t)}{-\ln \rho(T)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The **average convergence rate** is the most common KTU question: it is defined as the factor by which the error is reduced *per iteration*, i.e., $R = \rho(T)$.

### 2.5 Termination of Iteration — When to Stop

Four practical criteria (any combination may be used):

| # | Criterion | Formula | Typical CFD Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Maximum iterations** | $k = k_{\max}$ | Hard safety limit |
| 2 | **Absolute residual** | $\vert\vert b - A\phi^{(k)} \vert\vert < \varepsilon_a$ | Stagnation check |
| 3 | **Absolute change in $\phi$** | $\max_i \vert \phi_i^{(k+1)} - \phi_i^{(k)} \vert < \varepsilon_\phi$ | Local convergence |
| 4 | **Relative change in $\phi$** | $\dfrac{\max_i \vert \phi_i^{(k+1)} - \phi_i^{(k)} \vert}{\max_i \vert \phi_i^{(k+1)} \vert} < \varepsilon_s$ | Most common in CFD codes |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Most widely used in commercial CFD (ANSYS Fluent, OpenFOAM):** the **relative residual** criterion with $\varepsilon_s = 10^{-3}$ for momentum and $\varepsilon_s = 10^{-6}$ for energy/scalars.

### 2.6 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Notes / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Iteration scheme | $\phi^{(k+1)} = T\phi^{(k)} + C$ | $T$ = iteration matrix |
| 2 | Error recursion | $e^{(k+1)} = T e^{(k)}$ | Vector form |
| 3 | Closed-form error | $e^{(k)} = T^{k} e^{(0)}$ | After $k$ steps |
| 4 | **Spectral radius** | $\rho(T) = \max_i \vert \lambda_i(T) \vert$ | Dimensionless |
| 5 | **Convergence condition** | $\rho(T) < 1$ | Necessary & sufficient |
| 6 | Sufficient condition | $\|T\|_p < 1$ for any $p$ | Not necessary |
| 7 | Spectral norm of power | $\|T^{k}\|_2 = [\rho(T)]^{k}$ (asymptotic) | Approximate |
| 8 | Asymptotic error | $\|e^{(k)}\| \approx C [\rho(T)]^{k}$ | $C$ = constant |
| 9 | Rate of convergence (decadic) | $R = -\log_{10}\rho(T)$ | Iterations / decade |
| 10 | Rate of convergence (natural) | $R_\infty = -\ln \rho(T)$ | Nepers / iteration |
| 11 | Iterations to tolerance | $k \ge \dfrac{\ln(\varepsilon_0/\varepsilon_t)}{-\ln\rho(T)}$ | Dimensionless |
| 12 | SOR optimal $\omega$ | $\omega_{\text{opt}} = \dfrac{2}{1 + \sqrt{1 - \rho^2(T_J)}}$ | For model problems |
| 13 | Relative residual | $\varepsilon_s = \dfrac{\|r^{(k)}\|}{\|r^{(0)}\|}$ | Common CFD metric |
| 14 | CPU cost | $T_{\text{CPU}} = k \cdot t_{\text{iter}}$ | Seconds |
| 15 | Total computational work | $W = N \cdot k \cdot t_{\text{op}}$ | FLOPs |

> [!WARNING]
> In markdown tables, never use the bare `|` symbol for absolute value; KTU answer scripts use $\vert \cdot \vert$ instead.

### 2.7 Engineering Utility of Convergence Theory

- **Solver selection**: Conjugate Gradient for SPD systems; BiCGSTAB for non-symmetric; Multigrid for $\rho \to 0$ on fine grids.
- **Under-relaxation tuning**: Choosing $\omega$ in SIMPLE/SIMPLEC algorithms to keep $\rho < 1$.
- **Grid-independence studies**: Estimating how many extra iterations a finer grid needs.
- **Time-to-solution prediction**: Estimating CFD run time on HPC clusters.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Error Equation

**Starting point:** the iteration scheme

$$\phi^{(k+1)} = T \, \phi^{(k)} + C \tag{3.1}$$

**Step 1 — The fixed-point identity.** If the iterations converge to some limit $\phi^{*}$, taking the limit of (3.1) as $k \to \infty$ gives:

$$\phi^{*} = T \, \phi^{*} + C \quad\Longrightarrow\quad (I - T)\phi^{*} = C \tag{3.2}$$

For a consistent iterative solver, $\phi^{*} = \phi$ (the exact solution of $A\phi = b$).

**Step 2 — Subtract (3.1) from (3.2).** Define $e^{(k)} = \phi - \phi^{(k)}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\phi - \phi^{(k+1)} &= T\phi + C \;-\; \big[T\phi^{(k)} + C\big] \\
e^{(k+1)} &= T \phi - T \phi^{(k)} \\
e^{(k+1)} &= T \big(\phi - \phi^{(k)}\big) \\
\therefore \quad e^{(k+1)} &= T \, e^{(k)} \tag{3.3}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Iterate (3.3) by induction.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
e^{(1)} &= T \, e^{(0)} \\
e^{(2)} &= T \, e^{(1)} = T^{2} e^{(0)} \\
&\;\;\vdots \\
e^{(k)} &= T^{k} \, e^{(0)} \tag{3.4}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Convergence Condition $\rho(T) < 1$

**Step 1 — Eigen-decomposition.** Assume $T$ is diagonalizable, $T = V \Lambda V^{-1}$ with $\Lambda = \text{diag}(\lambda_1, \dots, \lambda_n)$. Then

$$T^{k} = V \Lambda^{k} V^{-1}, \qquad \Lambda^{k} = \text{diag}(\lambda_1^{k}, \dots, \lambda_n^{k}) \tag{3.5}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute (3.5) into (3.4).**

$$e^{(k)} = V \Lambda^{k} V^{-1} e^{(0)} \tag{3.6}$$

**Step 3 — Take the norm.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\|e^{(k)}\| &= \|V \Lambda^{k} V^{-1} e^{(0)}\| \\
            &\le \|V\| \, \|\Lambda^{k}\| \, \|V^{-1}\| \, \|e^{(0)}\| \tag{3.7}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Norm of a diagonal matrix.** For a diagonal matrix, the induced 2-norm is the largest absolute diagonal entry:

$$\|\Lambda^{k}\|_2 = \max_{i} \vert \lambda_i \vert^{k} = [\rho(T)]^{k} \tag{3.8}$$

**Step 5 — Conclude.** Substituting (3.8) into (3.7):

$$\|e^{(k)}\| \le \kappa(V) \, [\rho(T)]^{k} \, \|e^{(0)}\|, \qquad \kappa(V) = \|V\|\,\|V^{-1}\| \tag{3.9}$$

Since $\kappa(V)$ is a *finite* positive constant independent of $k$, the bound $\|e^{(k)}\| \to 0$ as $k \to \infty$ if and only if $[\rho(T)]^{k} \to 0$, i.e.,

$$\boxed{\;\rho(T) < 1\;} \tag{3.10}$$

Conversely, if $\rho(T) \ge 1$, there exists an initial error component along the dominant eigenvector that does *not* decay, and divergence is possible.

---

### 3.3 Derivation of the Rate of Convergence

**Step 1 — Asymptotic dominance.** From (3.6), let $i = 1$ index the dominant eigenvalue, $\vert \lambda_1 \vert = \rho(T)$. The corresponding term in the eigen-expansion dominates for large $k$:

$$\|e^{(k)}\| \approx \vert \alpha_1 \vert \, [\rho(T)]^{k} \, \|v_1\| \tag{3.11}$$

Define $C = \vert \alpha_1 \vert \,\|v_1\| \, \|e^{(0)}_{\text{ref}}\|$ (a constant depending on initial conditions). Then:

$$\|e^{(k)}\| \approx C \, [\rho(T)]^{k} \tag{3.12}$$

**Step 2 — Define the average linear rate.** The "rate" $R$ is the factor by which error shrinks per iteration:

$$R \;\equiv\; \lim_{k \to \infty} \frac{\|e^{(k)}\|}{\|e^{(k-1)}\|} \;=\; \rho(T) \tag{3.13}$$

**Step 3 — Decadic (per-decade) rate.** The number of iterations needed to reduce the error by a factor of 10 is found by setting $[\rho(T)]^{R_d} = 10^{-1}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_d \, \ln \rho(T) &= -\ln 10 \\
R_d &= \frac{\ln 10}{-\ln \rho(T)} = \frac{1}{-\log_{10}\rho(T)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Equivalently, the **iterations per decade**:

$$\boxed{\;R \;=\; -\log_{10}\rho(T) \quad \text{[iterations}^{-1}\text{]}\;} \tag{3.14}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Higher $R$ = faster convergence. For $\rho(T) = 0.1$, $R = 1$ (one iteration per decade). For $\rho(T) = 0.99$, $R \approx 0.0104$ (≈ 96 iterations per decade).

**Step 4 — Iterations to reach a tolerance $\varepsilon_t$.** Starting from $\|e^{(0)}\| = \varepsilon_0$ and requiring $\|e^{(k)}\| \le \varepsilon_t$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C \, [\rho(T)]^{k} &\le \varepsilon_t \\
k \, \ln \rho(T) &\le \ln(\varepsilon_t / C) \\
k &\ge \frac{\ln(C/\varepsilon_t)}{-\ln\rho(T)} \;\;\approx\;\; \frac{\ln(\varepsilon_0/\varepsilon_t)}{-\ln\rho(T)} \tag{3.15}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam-Style)

> **Problem:** An iterative CFD pressure-correction scheme yields an iteration matrix $T$ with eigenvalues $\{0.85, \;0.40, \;0.05\}$. The initial pressure residual is $\|r^{(0)}\| = 100$, and the target tolerance is $\varepsilon_t = 10^{-4}$. Find: (i) spectral radius, (ii) whether the scheme converges, (iii) average rate of convergence, (iv) iterations required.

**Step 1 — Spectral radius:**

$$\rho(T) = \max\{0.85,\, 0.40,\, 0.05\} = 0.85 \tag{3.16}$$

**Step 2 — Convergence check:**

$$\rho(T) = 0.85 < 1 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \text{Convergent.} \tag{3.17}$$

**Step 3 — Average rate (decadic):**

$$R = -\log_{10}(0.85) = -(-0.0706) = 0.0706 \;\; \text{iter}^{-1} \tag{3.18}$$

So roughly **1 iteration per 14 decades**? — clearly we should check: actually $0.85 < 1$ so the error decays, but slowly. Re-expressing: the *number of iterations per decade* is $1/R \approx 14.16$ iterations per decade.

**Step 4 — Iterations required:**

$$k \;\ge\; \frac{\ln(100/10^{-4})}{-\ln(0.85)} = \frac{\ln(10^{6})}{0.1625} = \frac{13.816}{0.1625} \approx 85.0 \tag{3.19}$$

So **$k = 85$ iterations** are required (round up to the next integer for safety).

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Common Iterative Schemes

For a model 1-D Poisson problem $-\,u''(x) = f(x)$ discretized on a uniform grid with grid spacing $h$, the spectral radii of various schemes are well known:

| Method | Spectral Radius $\rho$ | Asymptotic Rate $R_\infty$ | Comments |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Jacobi** | $\cos\!\big(\dfrac{\pi h}{L}\big)$ | $-\ln\!\cos\!\big(\dfrac{\pi h}{L}\big)$ | Slow for fine grids |
| **Gauss–Seidel** | $\cos^{2}\!\big(\dfrac{\pi h}{L}\big)$ | $-2\ln\!\cos\!\big(\dfrac{\pi h}{L}\big)$ | ~2× faster than Jacobi |
| **SOR ($\omega_{\text{opt}}$)** | $\omega_{\text{opt}} - 1$ | $-\ln(\omega_{\text{opt}} - 1)$ | Order of magnitude faster |
| **Conjugate Gradient (SPD)** | $\dfrac{\sqrt{\kappa} - 1}{\sqrt{\kappa} + 1}$ | $-\ln\!\dfrac{\sqrt{\kappa}-1}{\sqrt{\kappa}+1}$ | Optimal for SPD |
| **Multigrid V-cycle** | $C \;\;(< 0.15$ typical$)$ | $-\ln C$ | Grid-independent |

> [!TIP]
> Multigrid is the gold standard in modern CFD because its spectral radius is **independent of grid spacing $h$**, giving $O(N)$ total work — a "text-book" optimal solver.

---

### 3.6 Termination Criterion — Detailed Justification

The *relative* change in $\phi$ is preferred over the *absolute* change because it is scale-invariant:

$$\varepsilon_s^{(k)} = \frac{\max_i \vert \phi_i^{(k)} - \phi_i^{(k-1)} \vert}{\max_i \vert \phi_i^{(k)} \vert} \tag{3.20}$$

Iteration stops as soon as $\varepsilon_s^{(k)} \le \varepsilon_{\text{tol}}$. In OpenFOAM, this is implemented in the `solutionControl` dictionary as:

```text
tolerance 1e-06;
relTol 0.01;
```

> [!WARNING]
> Always pair the relative-change criterion with a *maximum-iterations* safeguard; otherwise, a stalled or slowly-converging scheme can consume the entire simulation budget.

### 3.7 CPU-Time and Work Estimate

If each iteration requires $t_{\text{iter}}$ seconds and $N$ unknowns, with $N_{\text{op}}$ floating-point operations per unknown per iteration:

$$T_{\text{CPU}} \;=\; k \, t_{\text{iter}} \;=\; k \, N \, N_{\text{op}} / f_{\text{clock}} \tag{3.21}$$

For a 1 M-cell mesh, a Jacobi iteration with $\rho = 0.95$ and a target of $10^{-4}$: $k \approx 180$ iterations × 1 M cells × 5 ops/cell ≈ 0.9 GFLOP per pressure solve — feasible on a single CPU core in seconds.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Iterative Solver Loop — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Initialize phi at k = 0] --> B[Read A, b, phi superscript 0]
    B --> C[Iteration k+1: phi superscript k+1 = T phi superscript k + C]
    C --> D[Compute residual r superscript k+1 = b minus A phi superscript k+1]
    D --> E[Compute change delta phi superscript k+1 = phi superscript k+1 minus phi superscript k]
    E --> F{Convergence check}
    F -- "rho T less than 1" --> G[Compute relative tolerance epsilon s = max delta phi / max phi]
    F -- "rho T not less than 1" --> H[Stop: DIVERGENCE - report to user]
    G --> I{epsilon s less than epsilon tol?}
    I -- YES --> J[Stop: CONVERGED - export solution]
    I -- NO --> K{k greater than k max?}
    K -- NO --> C
    K -- YES --> L[Stop: MAX ITERATIONS REACHED - report stagnation]
    H --> M[End]
    J --> M
    L --> M
```

### 4.2 Convergence Decision Tree — Spectral Radius Path

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Input: Iteration matrix T] --> S1[Compute eigenvalues lambda sub i of T]
    S1 --> S2[Compute spectral radius rho T = max absolute lambda i]
    S2 --> S3{rho T less than 1?}
    S3 -- YES --> S4[Scheme CONVERGES]
    S3 -- NO  --> S5[Scheme MAY DIVERGE]
    S4 --> S6[Compute rate R = minus log base 10 of rho T]
    S6 --> S7[Compute iterations k = ln epsilon 0 over epsilon t divided by minus ln rho T]
    S7 --> S8[Output: predicted k, predicted CPU time]
    S5 --> S9[Recommend: under-relaxation, scaling, or different solver]
```

### 4.3 Termination Logic — When to Stop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Compute residual r] --> P2[Compute delta phi]
    P2 --> P3{Any of 4 criteria met?}
    P3 -- "max iterations reached" --> P4[STOP: hard limit]
    P3 -- "absolute residual less than tol" --> P5[STOP: converged]
    P3 -- "absolute change less than tol" --> P6[STOP: converged]
    P3 -- "relative change less than tol" --> P7[STOP: converged]
    P3 -- "none satisfied" --> P8[Continue to next iteration]
    P8 --> P1
```

### 4.4 Comparison of Convergence Speeds (Conceptual)

```mermaid
graph LR
    J[Jacobi: rho about 1 minus pi squared h squared over 2] ---|slow| G[Gauss-Seidel: rho GS equals rho J squared]
    G ---|faster| S[SOR with optimal omega: rho as low as 0.1]
    S ---|fast| C[Conjugate Gradient: rho CG equals sqrt kappa minus 1 over sqrt kappa plus 1]
    C ---|very fast| M[Multigrid: rho MG less than 0.15 grid independent]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *State and explain the condition for convergence of a linear stationary iterative scheme used in CFD solvers.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A linear stationary iterative scheme of the form $\phi^{(k+1)} = T \phi^{(k)} + C$ converges to the exact solution $\phi$ from any initial guess $\phi^{(0)}$ if and only if the **spectral radius** of the iteration matrix satisfies

$$\rho(T) = \max_{i} \vert \lambda_i(T) \vert < 1$$

This is the **necessary and sufficient condition**. A *sufficient* (but not necessary) condition is $\|T\| < 1$ for any induced matrix norm. Diagonal dominance of $A$ is a sufficient condition for Jacobi/Gauss–Seidel convergence. **(3 marks)**

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *Define the rate of convergence of an iterative method. How is it related to the spectral radius of the iteration matrix?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The rate of convergence measures how rapidly the error $\|e^{(k)}\|$ diminishes per iteration. For a linear stationary scheme,

$$\|e^{(k)}\| \approx C\,[\rho(T)]^{k}$$

The **average (linear) rate** is

$$R = \lim_{k\to\infty}\frac{\|e^{(k)}\|}{\|e^{(k-1)}\|} = \rho(T)$$

The **decadic rate** (iterations per decade of error reduction) is

$$R_d = -\log_{10}\rho(T) \quad [\text{iter}^{-1}]$$

Smaller $\rho(T)$ → faster convergence. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU ESE Internal Choice

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

> **(a)** *Derive the condition for convergence of the linear stationary iterative scheme* $\phi^{(k+1)} = T\phi^{(k)} + C$. *Show that the spectral radius of $T$ must be less than unity.* **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** *An iterative scheme used to solve the pressure-Poisson equation in a 2-D cavity flow has iteration matrix eigenvalues* $\{-0.92,\; 0.78,\; 0.15\}$. *If the initial residual is* $\|r^{(0)}\| = 50$ *and the target tolerance is* $10^{-5}$, *compute the spectral radius, average rate of convergence (decadic), and the minimum number of iterations required.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks**

1. Define the error $e^{(k)} = \phi - \phi^{(k)}$. **(1 mark)**
2. Subtract iteration equation from fixed-point identity → $e^{(k+1)} = T e^{(k)}$. **(2 marks)**
3. Iterate to get $e^{(k)} = T^{k} e^{(0)}$. **(1 mark)**
4. Diagonalize $T = V\Lambda V^{-1}$ and bound $\|e^{(k)}\| \le \kappa(V)\,[\rho(T)]^{k}\,\|e^{(0)}\|$. **(2 marks)**
5. Conclude: $\rho(T) < 1$ is necessary and sufficient for $e^{(k)} \to 0$. **(1 mark)**

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks**

1. **Spectral radius:** $\rho(T) = \max\{0.92,\, 0.78,\, 0.15\} = 0.92$. **(2 marks)**
2. **Convergence check:** $0.92 < 1$ → scheme converges. **(1 mark)**
3. **Average rate (decadic):** $R = -\log_{10}(0.92) = 0.0362$ iter$^{-1}$, i.e., ≈ 27.6 iterations per decade. **(2 marks)**
4. **Iterations to tolerance:** From (3.15),

$$k \ge \frac{\ln(\varepsilon_0/\varepsilon_t)}{-\ln\rho(T)} = \frac{\ln(50/10^{-5})}{-\ln(0.92)} = \frac{\ln(5\times 10^{6})}{0.0834} = \frac{15.425}{0.0834} \approx 185$$

So **$k_{\min} = 185$ iterations** (rounded up). **(2 marks)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

> **(a)** *Explain the various termination criteria used in iterative CFD solvers. Why is the relative change in the solution variable preferred over the absolute change?* **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** *A SIMPLE algorithm for incompressible flow uses successive under-relaxation with* $\omega = 0.7$. *For the discretized momentum equation, the iteration matrix has* $\rho(T_\omega) = 0.88$. *If the initial normalized residual is* $10^{-1}$ *and the target is* $10^{-6}$, *estimate: (i) the asymptotic convergence rate, (ii) the number of iterations needed, and (iii) the CPU time if each iteration takes 0.05 s on a 4-core processor.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks**

1. **Four criteria:** max iterations, absolute residual, absolute change in $\phi$, relative change in $\phi$. **(2 marks)**
2. **Formulas:** write all four mathematical forms. **(2 marks)**
3. **Why relative?** Scale-invariant — does not depend on the units or magnitude of $\phi$. Useful when $\phi$ spans many orders of magnitude (e.g., pressure in Pa vs. species mass fraction). **(2 marks)**
4. **Pair with max-iteration safeguard** to avoid runaway CPU usage. **(1 mark)**

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks**

1. **Asymptotic rate:**

$$R_\infty = -\ln(0.88) = 0.1278 \;\;\text{nepers/iter} \quad\text{or}\quad R_d = -\log_{10}(0.88) = 0.0555 \;\;\text{iter}^{-1}$$

So ≈ 18 iterations per decade. **(2 marks)**

2. **Iterations needed:**

$$k \ge \frac{\ln(10^{-1}/10^{-6})}{-\ln(0.88)} = \frac{\ln 10^{5}}{0.1278} = \frac{11.513}{0.1278} \approx 90 \;\;\text{iterations}$$

Round up to **$k = 90$**. **(2 marks)**

3. **CPU time:**

$$T_{\text{CPU}} = k \cdot t_{\text{iter}} = 90 \times 0.05 = 4.5 \;\;\text{seconds on 4 cores}$$

Effective wall time = $4.5/4 \approx 1.13$ s if perfectly parallel. **(2 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing "convergence of the solver" with "convergence to the exact PDE solution."** The former is iterative convergence; the latter requires grid-independence studies.
> 2. **Forgetting the unit on the decadic rate.** It is iterations⁻¹ (or equivalently, *iterations per decade*).
> 3. **Using $\rho(T) = \max \lambda_i$ instead of $\max |\lambda_i|$.** Eigenvalues can be complex; the absolute value is essential.
> 4. **Rounding down the iteration count.** Always *round up* — partial iterations don't exist.
> 5. **Mixing up the relative residual** $\|r^{(k)}\|/\|r^{(0)}\|$ with the **relative change in $\phi$** — they are *not* the same metric.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **Iterative scheme**: $\phi^{(k+1)} = T\phi^{(k)} + C$; iteration matrix $T = M^{-1}N$.
- [x] **Error recursion**: $e^{(k+1)} = T e^{(k)}$; closed form $e^{(k)} = T^{k} e^{(0)}$.
- [x] **Spectral radius**: $\rho(T) = \max_i \vert \lambda_i(T) \vert$ — the modulus of the largest-magnitude eigenvalue.
- [x] **Convergence condition**: $\rho(T) < 1$ — necessary **and** sufficient.
- [x] **Sufficient condition**: $\|T\| < 1$ (any induced norm) or strict diagonal dominance of $A$.
- [x] **Asymptotic error decay**: $\|e^{(k)}\| \approx C\,[\rho(T)]^{k}$.
- [x] **Average rate (linear)**: $R = \rho(T)$.
- [x] **Decadic rate**: $R = -\log_{10}\rho(T)$ [iterations⁻¹].
- [x] **Iterations to tolerance**: $k \ge \dfrac{\ln(\varepsilon_0/\varepsilon_t)}{-\ln\rho(T)}$.
- [x] **Termination criteria**: (1) max iterations, (2) absolute residual, (3) absolute change, (4) relative change.
- [x] **CFD default**: relative residual $\varepsilon_s = 10^{-3}$ (momentum), $10^{-6}$ (energy/scalars).
- [x] **Multigrid** is grid-independent with $\rho \le 0.15$ → $O(N)$ solver.
- [x] **SOR optimal $\omega$** for model Poisson: $\omega_{\text{opt}} = 2/\,[\,1 + \sqrt{1 - \rho^{2}(T_J)}\,]$.
- [x] **Always round up** the iteration count for safety.
- [x] **Pair termination criteria** with a *maximum-iteration safeguard* to prevent runaway cost.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Two-line "exam-day" summary:**
> 1. The iterations converge **iff** the spectral radius of the iteration matrix is **less than one**.
> 2. The number of iterations to reach tolerance $\varepsilon_t$ is $k \ge \ln(\varepsilon_0/\varepsilon_t)\,/\,(-\ln\rho(T))$, and we stop as soon as the relative change in $\phi$ falls below $\varepsilon_s$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Boundary and Initial Conditions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET521 - COMPUTATIONAL FLUID DYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Boundary and Initial Conditions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD), the **governing partial differential equations (PDEs)** — namely the continuity, momentum (Navier–Stokes), and energy equations — are mathematical statements that hold true only in the **interior of the flow domain**. To obtain a **unique, physically meaningful solution**, auxiliary mathematical prescriptions must be supplied on the boundaries of the domain (and at the starting instant for unsteady problems). These prescriptions are collectively known as **Boundary Conditions (BCs)** and **Initial Conditions (ICs)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PEMET521, Module 2):** *Boundary and Initial Conditions are the auxiliary mathematical statements that must be appended to the governing PDEs to close the mathematical problem, ensuring a unique, stable, and physically consistent solution. They represent the physical and mathematical interface between the discretized interior domain and its surrounding environment.*

**Classification of Boundary Conditions (Classical Types):**

1. **Dirichlet Boundary Condition (Type-1 / First Kind):** Specifies the value of the primary unknown (the dependent variable itself) on the boundary.  
   Example: $u(x, y, t) = u_{\text{wall}}$ at a solid wall.

2. **Neumann Boundary Condition (Type-2 / Second Kind):** Specifies the value of the normal derivative (flux) of the primary variable on the boundary.  
   Example: $\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial n}\bigg|_{\text{boundary}} = 0$ (symmetry/zero-gradient outlet).

3. **Robin (Mixed) Boundary Condition (Type-3 / Third Kind):** Specifies a linear combination of the variable and its normal derivative.  
   Example: $\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial n} + h\,(u - u_\infty) = 0$ (convective heat transfer).

**Initial Condition (IC):** For time-dependent (transient) problems, the spatial distribution of all dependent variables at the starting time $t = 0$ must be specified throughout the entire domain.

$$\vec{\Phi}(x, y, z, t=0) = \vec{\Phi}_0(x, y, z)$$

where $\vec{\Phi} = (u, v, w, p, T)^T$ is the conservative variable vector.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "The Movie Reel":**  
> Imagine the governing PDE as a *movie script* that tells actors (fluid particles) how to *act* at every instant. The PDE alone is an *incomplete script* — it doesn't tell you:
> - What happens at the **edges of the stage** (boundary conditions).
> - What the **opening scene** looks like (initial conditions).  
> Without these, the movie has no beginning and no frame — the actors wouldn't know where to stand or how to start moving. BCs and ICs are, therefore, the **"stage directions" and "opening frame"** of the CFD simulation.

**Real-World Engineering Analogy:**  
Consider predicting weather over Kerala. The atmospheric Navier–Stokes equations describe how wind, temperature, and pressure evolve. But to forecast tomorrow's monsoon, you need:
- **Initial Conditions:** Today's measured temperature, pressure, and wind field (from satellites, weather stations).
- **Boundary Conditions:** Wind speed at the Arabian Sea surface, temperature at the coastline, no-flux at the ground.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight for KTU Exams:** *A CFD problem is only as good as its BCs and ICs. Even the most sophisticated numerical scheme will produce physically meaningless (or unstable) results if the boundary/initial conditions are specified incorrectly, insufficiently, or inconsistently.*

---

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **No-slip wall condition** (kinematic enforcement): $\vec{V}_{\text{fluid}} = \vec{V}_{\text{wall}}$ — universally applied in viscous flow.
- **No-penetration wall condition** (normal component): $\vec{V}_{\text{fluid}} \cdot \hat{n} = \vec{V}_{\text{wall}} \cdot \hat{n}$ — minimum requirement for inviscid and viscous flow alike.
- **Adiabatic wall (thermal):** $\dfrac{\partial T}{\partial n}\bigg|_{\text{wall}} = 0$
- **Reference pressure datum:** Often $p = 0$ (gauge) is set at one boundary point since pressure is defined only up to an arbitrary constant for incompressible flow.

---

### 1.4 Well-Posedness of the Boundary Value Problem (Hadamard, 1902)

A mathematical problem is **well-posed** if it satisfies three criteria:

| Criterion | Mathematical Meaning | Engineering Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| **Existence** | At least one solution exists | The physics is achievable |
| **Uniqueness** | Exactly one solution exists | The simulation is deterministic |
| **Stability** | Small perturbations in IC/BC → small changes in solution | The numerical result is reliable |

> [!WARNING]
> A PDE without proper boundary conditions is **ill-posed** (typically infinite solutions or none). KTU examiners frequently test whether students understand *which* boundary conditions are required for *which* class of PDE (elliptic, parabolic, hyperbolic).

---

### 1.5 Classification of PDEs and Required BC/IC

The number and type of BCs/ICs are dictated by the **mathematical classification** of the governing PDE:

| PDE Class | Physical Example | Required Auxiliary Conditions |
|---|---|---|
| **Elliptic** | Steady incompressible flow (Poisson/Laplace) | BCs on the *entire closed boundary* (Dirichlet/Neumann/Robin combinations) |
| **Parabolic** | Unsteady heat conduction, viscous transient | ICs throughout domain + BCs on *all* boundaries |
| **Hyperbolic** | Wave propagation, inviscid unsteady flow | ICs throughout domain + BCs only on *inflow* boundaries (Cauchy problem) |
| **Mixed (Navier–Stokes)** | General viscous, unsteady flow | ICs throughout domain + BCs on *all* boundaries (combination required) |

---

### 1.6 Boundary Conditions Specific to CFD (Navier–Stokes Equations)

For the **incompressible Navier–Stokes** equations:

$$\nabla \cdot \vec{V} = 0 \quad \text{(continuity)}$$

$$\rho \left( \dfrac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t} + \vec{V} \cdot \nabla \vec{V} \right) = -\nabla p + \mu \nabla^2 \vec{V} + \rho \vec{g}$$

the following physical BCs are standard in CFD practice:

| BC Type | Physical Meaning | Mathematical Formulation | Example Location |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Inlet (Velocity/Dirichlet)** | Specify velocity profile | $\vec{V} = \vec{V}_{\text{in}}(x,y,z)$ | Pipe entry, upstream |
| **Inlet (Pressure/Neumann)** | Specify total/pressure | $p = p_{\text{in}}$ | Pressure-driven inlet |
| **Outlet (Pressure/Dirichlet)** | Specify pressure | $p = p_{\text{out}}$ | Pipe exit |
| **Outlet (Convective/Neumann)** | Zero gradient of convected quantity | $\dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial t} + U_\infty \dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial n} = 0$ | Far-field exit |
| **No-slip wall** | Fluid sticks to wall | $\vec{V} = 0$ | Stationary solid |
| **Moving wall** | Fluid matches wall motion | $\vec{V} = \vec{V}_{\text{wall}}$ | Rotating blade |
| **No-penetration** | Fluid cannot cross wall | $\vec{V} \cdot \hat{n} = 0$ | Impermeable surface |
| **Symmetry/Plane** | Flow is mirror-symmetric | $\dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial n} = 0,\ \vec{V}\cdot \hat{n} = 0$ | Centerline of pipe |
| **Periodic** | Repeating flow pattern | $\phi(x+L) = \phi(x)$ | Fully-developed channel |
| **Adiabatic wall** | No heat transfer | $\dfrac{\partial T}{\partial n} = 0$ | Insulated wall |
| **Isothermal wall** | Constant temperature | $T = T_{\text{wall}}$ | Heated plate |
| **Free-slip wall (inviscid)** | Zero shear, no normal velocity | $\vec{V}\cdot \hat{n} = 0,\ \dfrac{\partial \vec{V}_t}{\partial n} = 0$ | Inviscid flow past body |

---

### 1.7 Initial Conditions for Transient CFD

For time-marching schemes (explicit or implicit), the simulation must be initialized at $t = 0$ with a complete description of the flow field:

$$\vec{V}(x, y, z, 0) = \vec{V}_0(x, y, z)$$
$$p(x, y, z, 0) = p_0(x, y, z)$$
$$T(x, y, z, 0) = T_0(x, y, z)$$

Common choices in engineering practice:
- **Rest condition:** $\vec{V}_0 = 0$ (impulsively-started flow).
- **Quasi-steady initialization:** Start from a converged steady-state solution.
- **Experimental data interpolation:** Map measured field onto computational grid.
- **Uniform field:** $\vec{V}_0 = U_\infty$ (potential flow initialization).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Framework: Why BCs are Needed

The governing PDEs of fluid dynamics are typically of **second order** (e.g., $\nabla^2 \phi = f$). A general second-order linear PDE in two variables:

$$A \dfrac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial x^2} + 2B \dfrac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial x \partial y} + C \dfrac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial y^2} + D \dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial x} + E \dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial y} + F \phi + G = 0$$

possesses a **two-parameter family** of general solutions, i.e., infinitely many solutions satisfy the PDE. To pin down **one unique solution**, additional information must be supplied:

- For **second-order ODEs in $x$**: Need **2 boundary/initial conditions** (e.g., $u(0) = a$ and $u(L) = b$).
- For **second-order PDEs in 2D/3D**: Need BCs along the *entire* closed boundary $\partial \Omega$ (continuous or discrete equivalents).
- For **time-dependent PDEs**: IC throughout the spatial domain, plus BCs on the spatial boundary.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Order of the PDE → Number of BCs required:**  
> The **order** of the spatial derivative equals the **maximum number** of boundary conditions that may be meaningfully specified on any one boundary. For the Navier–Stokes equations (second order in space), up to 2 conditions per boundary face may be prescribed, but their *type* depends on flow physics.

---

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Reasoning (How BCs are Chosen)

**Step 1 — Identify the PDE classification.**  
- Steady heat conduction → Elliptic → BCs on entire boundary.
- Unsteady heat conduction → Parabolic → IC + BCs on entire boundary.
- Wave equation → Hyperbolic → IC + BCs only on inflow (Cauchy) boundaries.

**Step 2 — Identify all boundaries of the domain.**  
List geometrically distinct boundaries: inlet, outlet, walls, symmetry planes, far-field, periodic faces.

**Step 3 — Apply physical reasoning to each boundary.**  
- Solid wall → no-slip (Dirichlet for velocity).
- Inlet → specify flow profile (Dirichlet for velocity).
- Outlet → specify pressure (Dirichlet for $p$) or allow zero-gradient of convected quantities.
- Far-field → impose free-stream conditions.

**Step 4 — Check mathematical well-posedness.**  
- Elliptic problems: must not be over- or under-specified. For Poisson equation $\nabla^2 \phi = f$, cannot specify both $\phi$ and $\partial \phi/\partial n$ on the *same* portion of the boundary (this is the Dirichlet–Neumann conflict).
- Inflow/outflow split: At any boundary, the number of conditions specified must equal the number of incoming characteristics.

**Step 5 — Verify physical realizability.**  
- The BCs must be consistent with conservation laws (mass, momentum, energy).
- Inlet mass flux should approximately equal outlet mass flux for steady incompressible flow (continuity check).

---

### 2.3 Characteristic-Based Boundary Condition Analysis (Hyperbolic Systems)

For the Euler/Navier–Stokes equations written in **characteristic form**, the BC specification is tied to the number of **incoming characteristics** at each boundary face.

Consider 1D linear convection: $\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial t} + c \dfrac{\partial u}{\partial x} = 0$.

- If $c > 0$ (wave traveling right):  
  - At the **left** boundary ($x = 0$): 1 incoming characteristic → specify **1 BC** (Dirichlet).  
  - At the **right** boundary ($x = L$): 0 incoming characteristics → **no BC required** (use Neumann/zero-gradient).

- If $c < 0$ (wave traveling left):  
  - At the **right** boundary: 1 incoming characteristic → specify **1 BC**.  
  - At the **left** boundary: 0 incoming characteristics → **no BC required**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** For the 1D linear convection equation, examiners often ask: *"How many boundary conditions are required for a well-posed problem on $[0, L]$?"* The correct answer depends on the **sign of the wave speed** $c$, and the answer must reference the **direction of information propagation** (characteristics).

---

### 2.4 Treatment of Boundary Conditions in Numerical Discretization

In a **finite difference/volume method**, the boundary conditions are implemented as follows:

**Method 1 — Direct Substitution (Strong Enforcement):**  
Replace the unknown value at the boundary node with the prescribed value.

$$\phi_0 = \phi_{\text{prescribed}} \quad \text{(Dirichlet)}$$

This eliminates the unknown and the corresponding algebraic equation.

**Method 2 — Ghost Cell Approach:**  
Introduce fictitious cells outside the domain. The interior scheme is applied, and the ghost-cell value is chosen to satisfy the BC.

For a no-slip wall at $i = 0$:  
- Velocity: $u_0 = 0$ (Dirichlet) → Ghost cell: $u_{-1} = -u_1$.  
- Pressure (Neumann for outlet): $\dfrac{p_{N+1} - p_{N-1}}{2\Delta x} = 0 \Rightarrow p_{N+1} = p_{N-1}$.

**Method 3 — Source Term Penalty:**  
Add a forcing term that drives the solution toward the desired BC value (used in fictitious domain / immersed boundary methods).

---

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Essential Formulas for Module 2 — Boundary and Initial Conditions**

| # | Formula / Concept | Equation / Statement | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Dirichlet BC (1st Kind)** | $\phi \vert_{\partial\Omega} = g(\vec{x}, t)$ | Specify value (e.g., $T_{\text{wall}}$, inlet velocity) |
| 2 | **Neumann BC (2nd Kind)** | $\dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial n}\bigg\vert_{\partial\Omega} = h(\vec{x}, t)$ | Specify flux (e.g., heat flux, zero-gradient outlet) |
| 3 | **Robin BC (3rd Kind)** | $a\,\phi + b\,\dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial n} = c$ | Convective BC, Newton's law of cooling |
| 4 | **Initial Condition (Transient)** | $\vec{\Phi}(\vec{x}, 0) = \vec{\Phi}_0(\vec{x})$ | Required for time-marching schemes |
| 5 | **No-slip wall** | $\vec{V}\vert_{\text{wall}} = \vec{V}_{\text{wall}}$ | Viscous flow on solid surface |
| 6 | **No-penetration wall** | $\vec{V} \cdot \hat{n} = 0$ | All walls (inviscid & viscous) |
| 7 | **No-slip + no-penetration (stationary wall)** | $u = v = w = 0$ | Default solid wall in viscous CFD |
| 8 | **Adiabatic wall (thermal)** | $\dfrac{\partial T}{\partial n}\bigg\vert_{\text{wall}} = 0$ | Insulated boundary |
| 9 | **Convective wall BC (Robin)** | $-k\dfrac{\partial T}{\partial n} = h(T - T_\infty)$ | Forced convection over surface |
| 10 | **Symmetry / Slip wall** | $\dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial n} = 0,\ \vec{V}\cdot \hat{n} = 0$ | Centerline, inviscid flow |
| 11 | **Periodic BC** | $\phi(\vec{x} + \vec{L}) = \phi(\vec{x})$ | Fully developed flow |
| 12 | **Outflow (zero-gradient)** | $\dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial n} = 0$ | Convected quantity at exit |
| 13 | **Inflow (Dirichlet for $u,v,w,T$)** | $\vec{V} = \vec{V}_{\text{in}},\ T = T_{\text{in}}$ | Upstream boundary |
| 14 | **Number of BCs (2nd-order PDE)** | Equals order of spatial derivative | $\nabla^2 \phi$ → 1 condition per boundary (elliptic) |
| 15 | **Characteristics (1D convection)** | $c > 0$: 1 BC at $x = 0$ | Information propagates rightward |
| 16 | **Pressure Poisson BC** | $\dfrac{\partial p}{\partial n} = 0$ on wall | From $\nabla^2 p = -\nabla \cdot (\vec{V} \cdot \nabla \vec{V})$ |
| 17 | **Pressure reference (incompressible)** | $p = 0$ at one point | Gauge datum |
| 18 | **Well-posed problem (Hadamard)** | Existence + Uniqueness + Stability | Mandatory for all CFD problems |

---

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Engineering Field | Typical BC/IC Application |
|---|---|
| **Aerospace (airfoil design)** | Far-field free-stream, no-slip on airfoil, periodic in spanwise |
| **Automotive (underhood flow)** | Inlet velocity, outlet pressure, no-slip on walls, symmetry |
| **Biomedical (blood flow in arteries)** | Pulsatile inlet velocity (Womersley profile), traction-free outlet, no-slip on wall |
| **Civil (wind loading on buildings)** | Atmospheric inlet profile, no-slip on ground/buildings, symmetry planes |
| **Process (heat exchangers)** | Inlet T, velocity; outlet pressure; conjugate heat transfer at walls |
| **Turbomachinery (CFD of turbines)** | Periodic interfaces between blade passages, total pressure/temperature at inlet |
| **Oceanography / Atmospheric science** | Wind stress at ocean surface, geostrophic balance at lateral boundaries |

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: How Many Boundary Conditions for the 1D Heat Equation?

We will rigorously derive the requirement for boundary and initial conditions for the canonical 1D transient heat conduction equation (parabolic PDE):

$$\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial t} = \alpha \dfrac{\partial^2 u}{\partial x^2}, \quad x \in [0, L],\ t > 0$$

**Step 1 — Spatial Order Analysis.**  
The highest spatial derivative is $\dfrac{\partial^2 u}{\partial x^2}$ (second order). Therefore, the general solution in $x$ contains **two arbitrary constants of integration** for *each* fixed value of $t$:

$$u(x, t) = A(t) \cdot e^{+\sqrt{s}\, x} + B(t) \cdot e^{-\sqrt{s}\, x} + \text{(particular)}$$

**Step 2 — Temporal Order Analysis.**  
The highest temporal derivative is $\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial t}$ (first order). Therefore, the general solution in $t$ contains **one arbitrary function of $x$** as an integration constant.

**Step 3 — Auxiliary Conditions Required.**  
Combining Steps 1 and 2, the closed-form analytical solution requires:

- **2 spatial BCs** (one at $x = 0$, one at $x = L$):  
  $u(0, t) = f_1(t)$ and $u(L, t) = f_2(t)$ (or Neumann/Robin equivalents).

- **1 temporal IC** (at $t = 0$, for all $x \in [0, L]$):  
  $u(x, 0) = g(x)$.

**Step 4 — Verification via Separation of Variables.**  
Assume $u(x, t) = X(x) \cdot T(t)$. Substituting:

$$X T' = \alpha X'' T \Rightarrow \dfrac{T'}{\alpha T} = \dfrac{X''}{X} = -\lambda$$

where $-\lambda$ is the separation constant (negative for bounded solutions).

Then:
$$X'' + \lambda X = 0 \Rightarrow X(x) = C_1 \cos(\sqrt{\lambda}\,x) + C_2 \sin(\sqrt{\lambda}\,x)$$

$$T' + \alpha \lambda T = 0 \Rightarrow T(t) = C_3\, e^{-\alpha \lambda t}$$

Two BCs in $x$ → 2 conditions to determine $C_1, C_2, \lambda$.  
One IC in $t$ → 1 condition to determine the Fourier coefficient amplitude.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** The 1D heat equation requires **2 spatial boundary conditions + 1 initial condition** for a well-posed problem on the bounded domain $[0, L]$ for $t > 0$.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Compatibility of Pressure BC with Continuity Equation (Incompressible Flow)

We derive why the pressure outlet BC is $\dfrac{\partial p}{\partial n} = 0$ on solid walls for the pressure Poisson equation.

**Step 1 — Momentum Equation (incompressible, steady):**

$$\rho (\vec{V} \cdot \nabla) \vec{V} = -\nabla p + \mu \nabla^2 \vec{V}$$

**Step 2 — Take the divergence of the momentum equation:**

$$\nabla \cdot [\rho (\vec{V} \cdot \nabla) \vec{V}] = -\nabla^2 p + \mu \nabla \cdot (\nabla^2 \vec{V})$$

**Step 3 — Apply continuity ($\nabla \cdot \vec{V} = 0$).**  
For incompressible flow, the term $\nabla \cdot (\nabla^2 \vec{V}) = \nabla^2 (\nabla \cdot \vec{V}) = 0$. Thus:

$$\nabla^2 p = -\rho \nabla \cdot [(\vec{V} \cdot \nabla) \vec{V}] = -\rho \left[ \left(\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial x}\right)^2 + 2\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial y}\dfrac{\partial v}{\partial x} + \left(\dfrac{\partial v}{\partial y}\right)^2 \right] \text{ (2D)}$$

This is the **Pressure Poisson Equation (PPE)**.

**Step 4 — Wall Boundary Condition for Pressure.**  
On a solid wall, the no-slip condition gives $\vec{V} = 0$ and $\dfrac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial n}$ is determined. The normal momentum equation at the wall reduces to:

$$\dfrac{\partial p}{\partial n}\bigg|_{\text{wall}} = 0$$

This is the **natural (Neumann) boundary condition** for the pressure Poisson equation, derived from the projection of the Navier–Stokes equations onto the wall-normal direction.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** Although the wall BC for velocity is **Dirichlet** ($u = v = 0$), the wall BC for pressure is **Neumann** ($\partial p/\partial n = 0$). This mixed Dirichlet–Neumann coupling is a hallmark of incompressible flow solvers and is a frequent exam topic.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Ghost Cell Values for No-Slip Wall in Finite Difference

Consider a 1D uniform grid with nodes at $x_i = i \Delta x$, $i = 0, 1, 2, \ldots, N$. The wall is located at node $i = 0$.

**Step 1 — Apply No-Slip Condition (Dirichlet):**

$$u_0 = U_{\text{wall}}$$

**Step 2 — Express the Ghost Cell Value.**  
For a central difference scheme at the first interior node $i = 1$:

$$\left. \dfrac{\partial^2 u}{\partial x^2} \right|_1 = \dfrac{u_0 - 2u_1 + u_2}{\Delta x^2}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $u_0 = U_{\text{wall}}$:**  
The wall value is now **known**, and the scheme can be applied directly to interior nodes.

**Step 4 — Alternative: Ghost Cell Approach for Neumann BC.**  
For $\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial x}\bigg|_0 = q_0$ at the wall, use the central difference:

$$\dfrac{u_1 - u_{-1}}{2 \Delta x} = q_0 \Rightarrow u_{-1} = u_1 - 2 q_0 \Delta x$$

**Step 5 — Application: Symmetry Plane (Zero Normal Gradient).**  
At a symmetry plane, $\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial n} = 0$. By Step 4 with $q_0 = 0$:

$$u_{-1} = u_1$$

That is, the **ghost cell mirrors** the first interior cell — a fundamental property exploited in symmetric flow simulations.

---

### 3.4 Derivation: Convective Outlet Boundary Condition (Orlanski, 1976)

The standard zero-gradient outlet BC ($\partial \phi / \partial n = 0$) is known to cause **wave reflections** in unsteady flow simulations. Orlanski (1976) proposed a **convective BC**:

$$\dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial t} + U_{\text{conv}} \dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial n} = 0$$

where $U_{\text{conv}}$ is the local convective wave speed, estimated at the boundary:

$$U_{\text{conv}} = -\dfrac{\partial \phi / \partial t}{\partial \phi / \partial n}\bigg|_{\text{boundary, previous time step}}$$

**Step 1 — Discretize in time (forward Euler):**

$$\dfrac{\phi^{n+1}_N - \phi^n_N}{\Delta t} + U_{\text{conv}} \dfrac{\phi^n_N - \phi^n_{N-1}}{\Delta x} = 0$$

**Step 2 — Solve for the boundary value at the new time:**

$$\phi^{n+1}_N = \phi^n_N - U_{\text{conv}} \dfrac{\Delta t}{\Delta x} (\phi^n_N - \phi^n_{N-1})$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Note:** The Orlanski BC is widely used in **unsteady, wave-dominated flows** (e.g., vortex shedding, free-surface flows) to minimize spurious reflections. The local $U_{\text{conv}}$ is computed at each time step from the previous time level.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: BC Selection Guide for Common CFD Problems

| Problem Type | Inlet | Outlet | Wall | Symmetry/Periodic |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Steady laminar pipe flow** | $u = U_{\text{in}}, v = 0$ (parabolic) | $p = p_{\text{out}}$ or $\partial u/\partial x = 0$ | $u = v = 0$ (no-slip) | Symmetry along pipe centerline |
| **Flow over flat plate** | $u = U_\infty, v = 0$ | $\partial \phi / \partial x = 0$ (convected) | $u = v = 0$ | Symmetry if 2D |
| **Compressible nozzle** | $p_0, T_0$ (stagnation) | $p_{\text{back}}$ (static) | $u = v = 0$ | None |
| **Natural convection cavity** | None (closed domain) | None (closed domain) | $T = T_h$ or $T_c$, $u = v = 0$ | None (3D cavity often 2D) |
| **Turbomachinery passage** | Total $p_0, T_0$ | Static $p$ (radial equilibrium) | $u = v = w = 0$ (or moving) | Periodic in tangential direction |
| **Free-surface flow** | $u = U_{\text{in}}$ | $p = p_{\text{atm}}$ (or outflow) | $u = v = 0$ | Symmetry in spanwise |
| **Aero-acoustics (LES)** | Synthetic turbulence | Convective (Orlanski) | $u = v = 0$ (no-slip or LNS) | Periodic in spanwise |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow: BC Implementation Pipeline in a CFD Solver

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Problem Definition: Geometry and Physics] --> B[Identify Domain and Boundaries]
    B --> C[Classify PDEs: Elliptic/Parabolic/Hyperbolic]
    C --> D[Determine Required Number of BCs and ICs]
    D --> E[Select Physical BC Type per Boundary]
    E --> E1[Inlet: Dirichlet for V,T]
    E --> E2[Outlet: Dirichlet for p or Convective]
    E --> E3[Wall: No-slip Dirichlet for V]
    E --> E4[Symmetry: Neumann for Vt plus No-penetration]
    E --> E5[Periodic: Phi_x+L equals Phi_x]
    E --> E6[Thermal: Dirichlet/Neumann/Robin for T]
    E --> F[Check Well-posedness: Hadamard Criteria]
    F --> G{Is Problem Well-posed?}
    G -- No --> H[Reformulate BCs: Adjust Type or Count]
    H --> E
    G -- Yes --> I[Discretize Domain: Generate Grid]
    I --> J[Apply BCs in Discretization]
    J --> J1[Direct Substitution for Dirichlet]
    J --> J2[Ghost Cell Construction for Neumann]
    J --> J3[Source Term for Robin]
    J --> K[Set Initial Conditions: Phi at t=0]
    K --> L[Initialize Solver and Iterate]
    L --> M[Post-process: Verify BC Satisfaction]
    M --> N[Solution: Field Variables in Domain]
```

---

### 4.2 Mermaid Schematic: Boundary Condition Taxonomy

```mermaid
graph TD
    BC[Boundary and Initial Conditions] --> BCT[Boundary Conditions]
    BC --> IC[Initial Conditions]
    BCT --> D[Dirichlet: Type 1]
    BCT --> N[Neumann: Type 2]
    BCT --> R[Robin: Type 3 Mixed]
    D --> D1[Prescribed Value: Inlet Velocity]
    D --> D2[Prescribed Value: Wall Temperature]
    D --> D3[Prescribed Value: Outlet Pressure]
    N --> N1[Prescribed Flux: Heat Flux]
    N --> N2[Zero Gradient: Symmetry]
    N --> N3[Zero Gradient: Far Field]
    R --> R1[Convective Heat Transfer: h times T minus Tinf]
    R --> R2[Elastic Foundation: Spring Backed Wall]
    IC --> IC1[Rest Condition: V equals 0]
    IC --> IC2[Steady Initialization: Use Converged Steady]
    IC --> IC3[Experimental Data: Interpolate to Grid]
    IC --> IC4[Analytical Profile: e.g. Couette]
```

---

### 4.3 Block Diagram: Ghost Cell Concept for Wall BC

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PhysicalDomain[Physical Computational Domain]
        A1[Ghost Cell i = minus 1: Outside Wall] --> A2[Wall Node i = 0: No-slip Value]
        A2 --> A3[Interior Node i = 1]
        A3 --> A4[Interior Node i = 2]
    end
    A1 -.->|u_minus1 equals minus u1 or 2 Uwall minus u1| A2
    A2 -->|u0 equals Uwall Dirichlet| A3
    A3 -->|Central Difference Scheme| A4
```

---

### 4.4 Flowchart: Well-Posedness Verification Procedure

```mermaid
graph TD
    Start[CFD Problem with Governing PDE] --> Q1{Is the PDE elliptic, parabolic, or hyperbolic?}
    Q1 -- Elliptic --> E1[Require BCs on Entire Closed Boundary]
    Q1 -- Parabolic --> P1[Require ICs in Domain plus BCs on Entire Boundary]
    Q1 -- Hyperbolic --> H1[Require ICs in Domain plus BCs on Inflow Boundaries Only]
    E1 --> E2{BC Count matches PDE Order?}
    P1 --> P2{IC plus BC Count matches PDE Order?}
    H1 --> H2{BC Count matches Inflow Characteristics?}
    E2 -- Yes --> WP[Problem is Well-posed]
    E2 -- No --> FIX[Adjust BCs: Add or Remove Conditions]
    P2 -- Yes --> WP
    P2 -- No --> FIX
    H2 -- Yes --> WP
    H2 -- No --> FIX
    FIX --> Q1
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Question A1.** [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]

*Define the three classical types of boundary conditions (Dirichlet, Neumann, Robin) with one engineering example for each.*

**Model Answer:**

The three classical types of boundary conditions (BCs) for a PDE governing a variable $\phi$ on a boundary $\partial \Omega$ are:

1. **Dirichlet (Type-1):** The value of the dependent variable is prescribed on the boundary.  
   $$\phi \big\vert_{\partial \Omega} = g(\vec{x}, t)$$  
   **Engineering Example:** Specifying the temperature at a heated wall in conjugate heat transfer: $T\big\vert_{\text{wall}} = T_{\text{wall}}$.

2. **Neumann (Type-2):** The normal derivative (flux) of the dependent variable is prescribed on the boundary.  
   $$\dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial n}\bigg\vert_{\partial \Omega} = h(\vec{x}, t)$$  
   **Engineering Example:** Specifying the heat flux at a surface: $-k\dfrac{\partial T}{\partial n}\big\vert_{\text{wall}} = q''$.

3. **Robin / Mixed (Type-3):** A linear combination of the variable and its normal derivative is prescribed.  
   $$a\,\phi + b\,\dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial n} = c$$  
   **Engineering Example:** Convective heat transfer at a surface: $-k\dfrac{\partial T}{\partial n} = h\,(T - T_\infty)$.

---

**Question A2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]

*For the 1D linear convection equation $\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial t} + c\,\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial x} = 0$ on $x \in [0, L]$, state the number and location of boundary conditions required for a well-posed problem when (a) $c > 0$ and (b) $c < 0$. Justify your answer using the concept of characteristics.*

**Model Answer:**

The 1D linear convection equation is **first-order** in both space and time, so it requires **1 spatial boundary condition** and **1 initial condition** for a well-posed problem. The location of the spatial BC is determined by the direction of wave propagation (sign of $c$):

**(a) $c > 0$ (wave propagates in the +$x$ direction):**  
- Information enters the domain at the **left** boundary ($x = 0$).  
- The required BC is: $u(0, t) = g(t)$ (Dirichlet).  
- The right boundary ($x = L$) requires **no BC**; it is an *outflow* boundary where $\partial u/\partial x = 0$ is typically imposed (or extrapolation is used).

**(b) $c < 0$ (wave propagates in the $-x$ direction):**  
- Information enters the domain at the **right** boundary ($x = L$).  
- The required BC is: $u(L, t) = g(t)$ (Dirichlet).  
- The left boundary ($x = 0$) requires **no BC**.

**Justification via Characteristics:** The general solution is $u(x, t) = u_0(x - c\,t)$, a constant along characteristic lines $x - c\,t = \text{const}$. If $c > 0$, the characteristic at any interior point $x > 0$ traces backward in time to $x = 0$; hence the value of $u$ at $x = 0$ is the only "free" parameter needed to determine $u(x, t)$. The opposite holds for $c < 0$.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice Pattern)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:** [KTU University Exam — Model Question]

*For the 1D transient heat equation $\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial t} = \alpha \dfrac{\partial^2 u}{\partial x^2}$ on $x \in [0, L]$ and $t > 0$, determine the total number of auxiliary conditions (boundary and initial) required for a well-posed problem. Specify the type and physical interpretation of these conditions for the case of a metal rod whose left end is suddenly immersed in ice water while the right end is insulated.*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify PDE Classification.**  
The 1D heat equation is **parabolic**: first-order in $t$, second-order in $x$.

**Step 2 — Auxiliary Conditions Required.**  
- **2 spatial BCs** (one at $x = 0$, one at $x = L$) — because of the second spatial derivative.  
- **1 temporal IC** (at $t = 0$, for all $x \in [0, L]$) — because of the first temporal derivative.

**Total: 3 auxiliary conditions.**

**Step 3 — Physical Interpretation for the Metal Rod.**

- **Left end ($x = 0$), immersed in ice water (Dirichlet BC):** The temperature at the wall is fixed to the ice water temperature $T_{\text{ice}}$.  
  $$u(0, t) = T_{\text{ice}} \quad \text{(Dirichlet, Type-1)}$$

- **Right end ($x = L$), insulated (Neumann BC):** No heat flux through the insulated end. By Fourier's law, $q'' = -k\,\partial T/\partial x = 0$.  
  $$\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial x}\bigg\vert_{x=L} = 0 \quad \text{(Neumann, Type-2, homogeneous)}$$

- **Initial condition (at $t = 0$):** The rod is initially at a uniform temperature $T_0$ (say room temperature).  
  $$u(x, 0) = T_0 \quad \text{for all } x \in [0, L]$$

**Step 4 — Statement of the Well-Posed BVP:**

$$\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial t} = \alpha \dfrac{\partial^2 u}{\partial x^2}, \quad x \in (0, L),\ t > 0$$
$$u(0, t) = T_{\text{ice}},\quad \dfrac{\partial u}{\partial x}\bigg\vert_{x=L} = 0,\quad u(x, 0) = T_0$$

This problem is well-posed by Hadamard's criteria (existence, uniqueness, stability can be proven by the method of separation of variables and Sturm–Liouville theory). **[7 Marks]**

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:** [KTU University Exam — Model Question]

*Consider a 2D steady incompressible flow past a cylinder. List the boundary conditions you would prescribe on each of the following boundaries: (i) the inflow (upstream) plane, (ii) the outflow (downstream) plane, (iii) the cylinder surface, and (iv) the far-field lateral boundaries. For each, justify the choice physically and mathematically.*

**Model Solution:**
