| Type | Mathematical Form | Implementation in the Matrix |
|---|---|---|
| **Dirichlet (fixed $T$)** | $T = T_{b}$ at boundary | Replace the row with $T_{boundary} = T_b$ |
| **Neumann (fixed flux)** | $-k \dfrac{\partial T}{\partial x} = q_{b}$ | Use ghost node or forward/backward difference |
| **Convective (Robin)** | $-k \dfrac{\partial T}{\partial x} = h(T_{\infty} - T_b)$ | Use energy balance at boundary node |

For a Neumann BC at $x=0$ with flux $q_0$, a **forward difference** is used:

$$T_{-1}^{\,n} = T_{1}^{\,n} - \frac{2 q_0 \, \Delta x}{k}$$

For a convective BC at $x=0$:

$$-k\frac{T_1^{\,n} - T_0^{\,n}}{\Delta x} = h(T_{\infty} - T_0^{\,n})$$

Solving: $T_0^{\,n} = \dfrac{T_1^{\,n} + (h\Delta x/k)\,T_{\infty}}{1 + (h\Delta x/k)}$

---

## 2.5 Stability Analysis Summary (von Neumann Method)

Substitute $T_i^{\,n} = G^{\,n} e^{\,i k_s x_i}$ (where $G$ = amplification factor, $k_s$ = wavenumber) into each scheme. The magnitude of $G$ must satisfy $\vert G \vert \le 1$ for stability.

| Scheme | Amplification Factor $G$ | Stability Condition | Order of Accuracy |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Explicit (FTCS)** | $G = 1 - 4Fo\sin^{2}\!\left(\tfrac{k_s \Delta x}{2}\right)$ | $Fo \le 0.5$ | $\mathcal{O}(\Delta t, \Delta x^{2})$ |
| **Implicit (BTCS)** | $G = \dfrac{1}{1 + 4Fo\sin^{2}\!\left(\tfrac{k_s \Delta x}{2}\right)}$ | **Unconditional** | $\mathcal{O}(\Delta t, \Delta x^{2})$ |
| **Crank–Nicolson** | $G = \dfrac{1 - 2Fo\sin^{2}\!\left(\tfrac{k_s \Delta x}{2}\right)}{1 + 2Fo\sin^{2}\!\left(\tfrac{k_s \Delta x}{2}\right)}$ | **Unconditional** | $\mathcal{O}(\Delta t^{2}, \Delta x^{2})$ |

---

## 2.6 📋 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Markdown Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Use `\vert` (not `|`) inside LaTeX math to avoid breaking the table syntax below.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Governing PDE** | $\dfrac{\partial T}{\partial t} = \alpha\dfrac{\partial^{2} T}{\partial x^{2}}$ | 1D heat diffusion equation |
| 2 | **Fourier number** | $Fo = \dfrac{\alpha\,\Delta t}{(\Delta x)^{2}}$ | Dimensionless time step |
| 3 | **Explicit update** | $T_i^{\,n+1} = T_i^{\,n} + Fo\left(T_{i+1}^{\,n} - 2T_i^{\,n} + T_{i-1}^{\,n}\right)$ | Direct calculation |
| 4 | **Implicit equation** | $-Fo\,T_{i-1}^{\,n+1} + (1+2Fo)T_i^{\,n+1} - Fo\,T_{i+1}^{\,n+1} = T_i^{\,n}$ | Tridiagonal system |
| 5 | **Crank–Nicolson** | $-\tfrac{Fo}{2}T_{i-1}^{\,n+1} + (1+Fo)T_i^{\,n+1} - \tfrac{Fo}{2}T_{i+1}^{\,n+1} = \tfrac{Fo}{2}T_{i-1}^{\,n} + (1-Fo)T_i^{\,n} + \tfrac{Fo}{2}T_{i+1}^{\,n}$ | Time-centered, 2nd order |
| 6 | **Stability: Explicit** | $Fo \le 0.5$ | Conditional |
| 7 | **Stability: Implicit / CN** | Unconditional | Always stable |
| 8 | **Thermal diffusivity** | $\alpha = \dfrac{k}{\rho c_{p}}$ | Material property |
| 9 | **Convective BC** | $T_0 = \dfrac{T_1 + \mathrm{Bi}\,T_{\infty}}{1 + \mathrm{Bi}}$ | $\mathrm{Bi} = h\Delta x / k$ |
| 10 | **Biot number** | $\mathrm{Bi} = \dfrac{hL}{k}$ | Surface vs internal resistance |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Electronics Cooling:** Predicting chip junction temperature during power-up/power-down cycles.
- **Metal Quenching:** Designing steel-hardening baths (rapid surface cooling).
- **Nuclear Reactor Safety:** Predicting cladding temperature transients during loss-of-coolant accidents.
- **Building Energy:** Modeling thermal mass behavior in walls for HVAC design.
- **Food Processing:** Pasteurization, sterilization — ensuring pathogens are killed without overcooking.

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners frequently test: (i) stability criterion derivation, (ii) TDMA setup, (iii) one full time-step calculation.

# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Explicit (FTCS) Scheme

### Step 1: Write the governing PDE
$$\frac{\partial T}{\partial t} = \alpha \frac{\partial^{2} T}{\partial x^{2}}$$

### Step 2: Taylor-expand $T(x_i, t_n + \Delta t)$ about $(x_i, t_n)$
$$T_i^{\,n+1} = T_i^{\,n} + \Delta t \left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial t}\right)_i^{\,n} + \frac{(\Delta t)^{2}}{2!}\left(\frac{\partial^{2} T}{\partial t^{2}}\right)_i^{\,n} + \frac{(\Delta t)^{3}}{3!}\left(\frac{\partial^{3} T}{\partial t^{3}}\right)_i^{\,n} + \cdots$$

Rearranging for the time derivative:

$$\left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial t}\right)_i^{\,n} = \frac{T_i^{\,n+1} - T_i^{\,n}}{\Delta t} - \frac{\Delta t}{2}\left(\frac{\partial^{2} T}{\partial t^{2}}\right)_i^{\,n} - \cdots$$

Therefore the **forward-difference approximation** has a **truncation error** of order $\mathcal{O}(\Delta t)$.

### Step 3: Taylor-expand for the spatial second derivative
$$T_{i+1}^{\,n} = T_i^{\,n} + \Delta x \left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial x}\right)_i^{\,n} + \frac{(\Delta x)^{2}}{2!}\left(\frac{\partial^{2} T}{\partial x^{2}}\right)_i^{\,n} + \frac{(\Delta x)^{3}}{3!}\left(\frac{\partial^{3} T}{\partial x^{3}}\right)_i^{\,n} + \frac{(\Delta x)^{4}}{4!}\left(\frac{\partial^{4} T}{\partial x^{4}}\right)_i^{\,n} + \cdots$$

$$T_{i-1}^{\,n} = T_i^{\,n} - \Delta x \left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial x}\right)_i^{\,n} + \frac{(\Delta x)^{2}}{2!}\left(\frac{\partial^{2} T}{\partial x^{2}}\right)_i^{\,n} - \frac{(\Delta x)^{3}}{3!}\left(\frac{\partial^{3} T}{\partial x^{3}}\right)_i^{\,n} + \frac{(\Delta x)^{4}}{4!}\left(\frac{\partial^{4} T}{\partial x^{4}}\right)_i^{\,n} - \cdots$$

### Step 4: Add the two series
Adding $T_{i+1}^{\,n} + T_{i-1}^{\,n}$, the odd-derivative terms cancel:

$$T_{i+1}^{\,n} + T_{i-1}^{\,n} = 2T_i^{\,n} + (\Delta x)^{2}\left(\frac{\partial^{2} T}{\partial x^{2}}\right)_i^{\,n} + \frac{(\Delta x)^{4}}{12}\left(\frac{\partial^{4} T}{\partial x^{4}}\right)_i^{\,n} + \cdots$$

### Step 5: Solve for the second derivative
$$\left(\frac{\partial^{2} T}{\partial x^{2}}\right)_i^{\,n} = \frac{T_{i+1}^{\,n} - 2T_i^{\,n} + T_{i-1}^{\,n}}{(\Delta x)^{2}} - \frac{(\Delta x)^{2}}{12}\left(\frac{\partial^{4} T}{\partial x^{4}}\right)_i^{\,n} - \cdots$$

This shows the **central difference** has a truncation error of order $\mathcal{O}[(\Delta x)^{2}]$.

### Step 6: Substitute into the PDE and solve for $T_i^{\,n+1}$
Substituting Steps 2 and 5 into the PDE and neglecting higher-order terms:

$$\frac{T_i^{\,n+1} - T_i^{\,n}}{\Delta t} = \alpha \cdot \frac{T_{i+1}^{\,n} - 2T_i^{\,n} + T_{i-1}^{\,n}}{(\Delta x)^{2}}$$

Multiplying by $\Delta t$ and adding $T_i^{\,n}$:

$$\boxed{\,T_i^{\,n+1} = T_i^{\,n} + Fo\left(T_{i+1}^{\,n} - 2T_i^{\,n} + T_{i-1}^{\,n}\right)\,}$$

with $Fo = \dfrac{\alpha \Delta t}{(\Delta x)^{2}}$. This is the **Explicit (FTCS)** update formula.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Fully Implicit (BTCS) Scheme

### Step 1: Backward-difference approximation in time
Taylor-expand $T(x_i, t_n)$ about $(x_i, t_{n+1})$:

$$T_i^{\,n} = T_i^{\,n+1} - \Delta t \left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial t}\right)_i^{\,n+1} + \frac{(\Delta t)^{2}}{2!}\left(\frac{\partial^{2} T}{\partial t^{2}}\right)_i^{\,n+1} - \cdots$$

Rearranging:

$$\left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial t}\right)_i^{\,n+1} = \frac{T_i^{\,n+1} - T_i^{\,n}}{\Delta t} + \frac{\Delta t}{2}\left(\frac{\partial^{2} T}{\partial t^{2}}\right)_i^{\,n+1} - \cdots$$

Truncation error is again $\mathcal{O}(\Delta t)$, but the scheme is **unconditionally stable**.

### Step 2: Central difference in space evaluated at $t_{n+1}$
$$\left(\frac{\partial^{2} T}{\partial x^{2}}\right)_i^{\,n+1} = \frac{T_{i+1}^{\,n+1} - 2T_i^{\,n+1} + T_{i-1}^{\,n+1}}{(\Delta x)^{2}} + \mathcal{O}[(\Delta x)^{2}]$$

### Step 3: Substitute into PDE at $t_{n+1}$
$$\frac{T_i^{\,n+1} - T_i^{\,n}}{\Delta t} = \alpha \, \frac{T_{i+1}^{\,n+1} - 2T_i^{\,n+1} + T_{i-1}^{\,n+1}}{(\Delta x)^{2}}$$

### Step 4: Multiply by $\Delta t$ and rearrange
$$T_i^{\,n+1} - T_i^{\,n} = Fo\left(T_{i+1}^{\,n+1} - 2T_i^{\,n+1} + T_{i-1}^{\,n+1}\right)$$

### Step 5: Collect unknowns on the LHS
$$-Fo\,T_{i-1}^{\,n+1} + (1+2Fo)T_i^{\,n+1} - Fo\,T_{i+1}^{\,n+1} = T_i^{\,n}$$

This is the **Implicit (BTCS)** scheme. The matrix form for $N$ interior nodes is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\begin{bmatrix}
1+2Fo & -Fo & 0 & \cdots & 0 \\
-Fo & 1+2Fo & -Fo & \cdots & 0 \\
0 & -Fo & 1+2Fo & \cdots & 0 \\
\vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\
0 & 0 & 0 & -Fo & 1+2Fo
\end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix} T_1 \\ T_2 \\ T_3 \\ \vdots \\ T_{N} \end{bmatrix}^{\!n+1}
=
\begin{bmatrix} T_1 \\ T_2 \\ T_3 \\ \vdots \\ T_{N} \end{bmatrix}^{\!n}
+ \text{BC terms}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation of the Crank–Nicolson (CN) Scheme

### Step 1: Recognize CN is the time-centered average
The CN scheme replaces the spatial derivative at a single time level with the **arithmetic mean** of the spatial derivatives at $t_n$ and $t_{n+1}$:

$$\frac{\partial^{2} T}{\partial x^{2}} \;\approx\; \frac{1}{2}\!\left[\left(\frac{\partial^{2} T}{\partial x^{2}}\right)^{\!n} + \left(\frac{\partial^{2} T}{\partial x^{2}}\right)^{\!n+1}\right]$$

### Step 2: Substitute the central-difference approximations
$$\frac{T_i^{\,n+1} - T_i^{\,n}}{\Delta t} = \frac{\alpha}{2(\Delta x)^{2}}\Big[\left(T_{i+1}^{\,n} - 2T_i^{\,n} + T_{i-1}^{\,n}\right) + \left(T_{i+1}^{\,n+1} - 2T_i^{\,n+1} + T_{i-1}^{\,n+1}\right)\Big]$$

### Step 3: Multiply by $\Delta t$ and collect unknowns at $t_{n+1}$ on LHS
$$T_i^{\,n+1} - T_i^{\,n} = \frac{Fo}{2}\Big[T_{i+1}^{\,n} - 2T_i^{\,n} + T_{i-1}^{\,n} + T_{i+1}^{\,n+1} - 2T_i^{\,n+1} + T_{i-1}^{\,n+1}\Big]$$

### Step 4: Rearrange
$$T_i^{\,n+1} - \frac{Fo}{2}\left[T_{i+1}^{\,n+1} - 2T_i^{\,n+1} + T_{i-1}^{\,n+1}\right] = T_i^{\,n} + \frac{Fo}{2}\left[T_{i+1}^{\,n} - 2T_i^{\,n} + T_{i-1}^{\,n}\right]$$

### Step 5: Distribute and collect coefficients

$$-\frac{Fo}{2}T_{i-1}^{\,n+1} + (1+Fo)T_i^{\,n+1} - \frac{Fo}{2}T_{i+1}^{\,n+1} = \frac{Fo}{2}T_{i-1}^{\,n} + (1-Fo)T_i^{\,n} + \frac{Fo}{2}T_{i+1}^{\,n}$$

This is the **Crank–Nicolson** scheme — the tridiagonal LHS coefficients are $\left[-\tfrac{Fo}{2},\ (1+Fo),\ -\tfrac{Fo}{2}\right]$.

---

## 3.4 Stability Analysis — Explicit Scheme (von Neumann Method)

### Step 1: Assume a Fourier-mode trial solution
$$T_i^{\,n} = G^{\,n} e^{\,i\,k_s\,x_i} = G^{\,n} e^{\,i\,k_s\,i\Delta x}$$

where $i = \sqrt{-1}$ and $k_s$ is the (real) wavenumber.

### Step 2: Substitute into the explicit update formula

$$
\begin{aligned}
G^{\,n+1} e^{\,i k_s i \Delta x} &= G^{\,n} e^{\,i k_s i \Delta x} + Fo\left[G^{\,n} e^{\,i k_s (i+1) \Delta x} - 2G^{\,n} e^{\,i k_s i \Delta x} + G^{\,n} e^{\,i k_s (i-1) \Delta x}\right] \\
G &= 1 + Fo\left[e^{\,i k_s \Delta x} - 2 + e^{-i k_s \Delta x}\right] \\
G &= 1 + Fo\left[2\cos(k_s \Delta x) - 2\right] \\
G &= 1 - 2Fo\left[1 - \cos(k_s \Delta x)\right] \\
G &= 1 - 4Fo\sin^{2}\!\left(\frac{k_s \Delta x}{2}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Apply stability condition $\vert G \vert \le 1$
The maximum value of $\sin^{2}(\cdot)$ is $1$, so the most restrictive case is $k_s \Delta x = \pi$:

$$G_{\min} = 1 - 4Fo \quad \text{and} \quad G_{\max} = 1$$

For stability, we need $-1 \le G_{\min} \le 1$:

$$-1 \le 1 - 4Fo \quad\Rightarrow\quad Fo \le \frac{1}{2}$$

**Hence the explicit scheme is stable iff $Fo \le 0.5$.** $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.5 Numerical Example — Worked-Out Step by Step

> **Problem:** A 1D steel slab of thickness $L = 0.04$ m, initially at $T(x, 0) = 100^{\circ}\text{C}$, is suddenly immersed in water at $T_{\infty} = 0^{\circ}\text{C}$ with convection coefficient $h = 500$ W/(m²·K). Given $k = 50$ W/(m·K), $\rho = 7800$ kg/m³, $c_p = 500$ J/(kg·K). Use the **Explicit scheme** with $\Delta x = 0.01$ m, $\Delta t = 5$ s. Find $T$ after 1 time step at the center of the slab. (Take 5 grid nodes: $i = 0, 1, 2, 3, 4$.)

### Step 1: Compute material properties
$$\alpha = \frac{k}{\rho c_p} = \frac{50}{7800 \times 500} = \frac{50}{3.9 \times 10^{6}} = 1.282 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{m}^{2}/\text{s}$$

### Step 2: Compute Fourier number
$$Fo = \frac{\alpha \Delta t}{(\Delta x)^{2}} = \frac{1.282 \times 10^{-5} \times 5}{(0.01)^{2}} = \frac{6.41 \times 10^{-5}}{1 \times 10^{-4}} = 0.641$$

> [!WARNING]
> **$Fo = 0.641 > 0.5$ — Explicit scheme is UNSTABLE!** This choice violates the stability criterion. Reduce $\Delta t$ to $3$ s to get $Fo = 0.385 < 0.5$. We will use $\Delta t = 3$ s.

Recompute with $\Delta t = 3$ s:
$$Fo = \frac{1.282 \times 10^{-5} \times 3}{1 \times 10^{-4}} = 0.3846 \approx 0.385$$

### Step 3: Compute Biot number and convective BC at $i=0$
$$\mathrm{Bi} = \frac{h \Delta x}{k} = \frac{500 \times 0.01}{50} = 0.1$$

The convective BC at $i=0$ gives the surface temperature using energy balance on the half-cell:

$$T_0^{\,n} = \frac{T_1^{\,n} + \mathrm{Bi} \cdot T_{\infty}}{1 + \mathrm{Bi}} = \frac{100 + 0.1 \times 0}{1 + 0.1} = \frac{100}{1.1} = 90.91^{\circ}\text{C}$$

By symmetry at $i=4$ (since the other face has the same BC):
$$T_4^{\,n} = T_0^{\,n} = 90.91^{\circ}\text{C}$$

### Step 4: Apply the explicit update at interior nodes ($i = 1, 2, 3$)
$$T_i^{\,n+1} = T_i^{\,n} + Fo\left(T_{i+1}^{\,n} - 2T_i^{\,n} + T_{i-1}^{\,n}\right)$$

**At $i = 1$:**
$$T_1^{\,1} = 100 + 0.385 \times (100 - 2 \times 100 + 90.91) = 100 + 0.385 \times (-9.09) = 100 - 3.50 = 96.50^{\circ}\text{C}$$

**At $i = 2$ (center):**
$$T_2^{\,1} = 100 + 0.385 \times (100 - 2 \times 100 + 100) = 100 + 0.385 \times 0 = 100^{\circ}\text{C}$$

**At $i = 3$ (by symmetry, equals $T_1$):**
$$T_3^{\,1} = 96.50^{\circ}\text{C}$$

### Step 5: Recompute the boundary $T_0$ for the next step
$$T_0^{\,1} = \frac{T_1^{\,1} + 0}{1.1} = \frac{96.50}{1.1} = 87.73^{\circ}\text{C}$$

### Step 6: Final Result Table after first time step
| Node $i$ | Position $x$ (m) | $T^{\,0}$ (°C) | $T^{\,1}$ (°C) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0.00 | 90.91 | 87.73 |
| 1 | 0.01 | 100 | 96.50 |
| 2 | 0.02 | 100 | 100.00 |
| 3 | 0.03 | 100 | 96.50 |
| 4 | 0.04 | 90.91 | 87.73 |

✅ Center stays at $100^{\circ}\text{C}$ after 1 step (as expected for symmetric initial condition), and surface temperatures drop rapidly due to convection.

## 4.1 Computational Grid (1D Spatial Discretization)

The following Mermaid diagram shows the standard 1D staggered grid used to discretize the transient heat equation, including interior nodes, boundary nodes, and a "ghost node" used to handle Neumann/convective boundary conditions.

```mermaid
flowchart LR

    classDef interior fill:#06d6a0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef ghost fill:#ef476f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef time fill:#118ab2,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff

    BC0[Boundary Node i=0<br/>T0 prescribed or convective BC]:::boundary
    N1[Interior Node i=1<br/>Unknown T1]:::interior
    N2[Interior Node i=2<br/>Unknown T2]:::interior
    N3[Interior Node i=3<br/>Unknown T3]:::interior
    BCN[Boundary Node i=N<br/>TN prescribed or convective BC]:::boundary
    GHOST[Ghost Node i=-1<br/>Used for Neumann BC]:::ghost
    TIME[Time Level tn and tn+1]:::time

    BC0 --> N1
    N1 --> N2
    N2 --> N3
    N3 --> BCN
    GHOST -. Ghost approximation .-> BC0
    TIME --- BC0
    TIME --- N1
    TIME --- N2
    TIME --- N3
    TIME --- BCN
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Solver Flow

This Mermaid diagram describes the **computational flow** for solving the 1D transient heat conduction problem using any of the three schemes. Replace "FTCS" with the appropriate scheme at the "Update Strategy" block.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    classDef startend fill:#073b4c,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef input fill:#118ab2,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff

    classDef decision fill:#ffd166,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef solver fill:#ef476f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff

    A([Start of CFD Solver]):::startend
    B[Read Geometry, Material Properties, Initial and Boundary Conditions]:::input
    C[Discretize Domain into N nodes with spacing dx]:::process
    D[Choose Time Step dt and compute Fourier Number Fo]:::process
    E{Is Fo less than or equal to 0.5?}:::decision
    F[Use Explicit FTCS Scheme<br/>Direct pointwise update]:::solver
    G[Assemble Tridiagonal Matrix A]:::solver
    H{Fully Implicit or Crank Nicolson?}:::decision
    I[Use Fully Implicit BTCS<br/>TDMA solver]:::solver
    J[Use Crank Nicolson CN<br/>TDMA solver]:::solver
    K[Update Temperature Field at tn+1]:::process
    L{Has Time Reached Final Time?}:::decision
    M[Postprocess: Plot T vs x at selected times]:::process
    N([End]):::startend

    A --> B
    B --> C
    C --> D
    D --> E
    E -- Yes --> F
    E -- No --> G
    G --> H
    H -- Fully Implicit --> I
    H -- Crank Nicolson --> J
    F --> K
    I --> K
    J --> K
    K --> L
    L -- No --> E
    L -- Yes --> M
    M --> N
```

```mermaid
        I1[T at i minus 1, level n plus 1]:::implicitNode
        I2[T at i, level n plus 1]:::implicitNode
        I3[T at i plus 1, level n plus 1]:::implicitNode
        IRES[Result: T at i, level n plus 1<br/>Solved together as system]:::implicitNode
        I1 --> IRES
        I2 --> IRES
        I3 --> IRES
    end

    subgraph CRANK["Crank Nicolson CN Scheme"]
        C1A[T at i minus 1, level n]:::cnNode
        C2A[T at i, level n]:::cnNode
        C3A[T at i plus 1, level n]:::cnNode
        C1B[T at i minus 1, level n plus 1]:::cnNode
        C2B[T at i, level n plus 1]:::cnNode
        C3B[T at i plus 1, level n plus 1]:::cnNode
        CRES[Result: T at i, level n plus 1<br/>Tridiagonal system, second order]:::cnNode
        C1A --> CRES
        C2A --> CRES
        C3A --> CRES
        C1B --> CRES
        C2B --> CRES
        C3B --> CRES
    end

    LABEL[Key Insight: Explicit uses only KNOWN values; Implicit and CN use UNKNOWN values at n+1 solved simultaneously]:::labelNode
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
**State the stability criterion for the explicit (FTCS) finite-difference scheme used in solving the 1D transient heat conduction equation. What is its physical significance?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The stability criterion for the explicit scheme is:
$$Fo = \frac{\alpha \, \Delta t}{(\Delta x)^{2}} \;\le\; 0.5$$

**Physical significance:**
- The Fourier number $Fo$ represents the ratio of the heat conducted in time $\Delta t$ to the heat stored in a grid cell of size $\Delta x$.
- If $Fo > 0.5$, the numerical scheme allows heat to "leap" across grid cells faster than physically possible, causing unphysical oscillations and divergence.
- This places an upper bound on $\Delta t$ for any chosen $\Delta x$, e.g., $\Delta t_{\max} = \dfrac{(\Delta x)^{2}}{2\alpha}$.
- For small $\Delta x$ (fine mesh), the allowable $\Delta t$ becomes very small → many time steps → higher computational cost.

**Mark split:** 1 mark — formula, 1 mark — explanation of $Fo$, 1 mark — physical significance and $\Delta t$ bound.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between the Explicit and Crank–Nicolson schemes in terms of (i) stability, (ii) order of accuracy in time, and (iii) computational effort per time step.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Explicit (FTCS) | Crank–Nicolson |
|---|---|---|
| (i) **Stability** | Conditional: $Fo \le 0.5$ | **Unconditional** for all $\Delta t$ |
| (ii) **Time accuracy** | First order: $\mathcal{O}(\Delta t)$ | Second order: $\mathcal{O}(\Delta t^{2})$ |
| (iii) **Effort per step** | Trivial — direct calculation | Requires solving a tridiagonal system (TDMA) — $\mathcal{O}(N)$ operations |

**Mark split:** 1 mark each for the three differentiating features.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module 3 Internal Choice

> **Module 3 Internal Choice (KTU Pattern):** Students answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### 📝 Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]**

**A 1D steel rod of length $L = 0.1$ m, initially at uniform temperature $T(x, 0) = 50^{\circ}\text{C}$, has its ends suddenly cooled to $T = 0^{\circ}\text{C}$ and maintained there. Given thermal diffusivity $\alpha = 2 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s. Use the Explicit scheme with $\Delta x = 0.02$ m and $\Delta t = 20$ s.**

#### Part (a) — Stability Check and First Two Time Steps (7 marks)

**(i)** Check the stability of the chosen $\Delta t$. If unstable, suggest a remedy. (2 marks)
**(ii)** Using 6 grid nodes ($i = 0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5$), compute the temperature at interior nodes after the first time step. (3 marks)
**(iii)** Compute temperatures after the second time step. (2 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Compute Fourier number**
$$Fo = \frac{\alpha \Delta t}{(\Delta x)^{2}} = \frac{2 \times 10^{-5} \times 20}{(0.02)^{2}} = \frac{4 \times 10^{-4}}{4 \times 10^{-4}} = 1.0$$

**(i) Stability check:** $Fo = 1.0 > 0.5$ — **UNSTABLE** ❌
**Remedy:** Reduce $\Delta t$ such that $Fo \le 0.5$:
$$\Delta t_{\max} = \frac{0.5 \times (\Delta x)^{2}}{\alpha} = \frac{0.5 \times 4 \times 10^{-4}}{2 \times 10^{-5}} = 10\ \text{s}$$

We will redo the calculation with $\Delta t = 10$ s → $Fo = 0.5$.

**Step 2: Apply boundary conditions**
$T_0 = T_5 = 0^{\circ}\text{C}$ for all time. Initial condition: $T_1^{0} = T_2^{0} = T_3^{0} = T_4^{0} = 50^{\circ}\text{C}$.

**Step 3: First time step using $Fo = 0.5$ and explicit formula**
$$T_i^{\,1} = T_i^{\,0} + 0.5\left(T_{i+1}^{\,0} - 2T_i^{\,0} + T_{i-1}^{\,0}\right)$$

**At $i = 1$:**
$$T_1^{\,1} = 50 + 0.5(50 - 100 + 0) = 50 + 0.5(-50) = 50 - 25 = 25^{\circ}\text{C}$$

**At $i = 2$:**
$$T_2^{\,1} = 50 + 0.5(50 - 100 + 50) = 50 + 0.5(0) = 50^{\circ}\text{C}$$

**At $i = 3$ (by symmetry equals $T_2$):**
$$T_3^{\,1} = 50^{\circ}\text{C}$$

**At $i = 4$ (by symmetry equals $T_1$):**
$$T_4^{\,1} = 25^{\circ}\text{C}$$

**Step 4: Second time step**
**At $i = 1$:**
$$T_1^{\,2} = 25 + 0.5(50 - 50 + 0) = 25 + 0 = 25^{\circ}\text{C}$$

**At $i = 2$:**
$$T_2^{\,2} = 50 + 0.5(50 - 100 + 25) = 50 + 0.5(-25) = 50 - 12.5 = 37.5^{\circ}\text{C}$$

**At $i = 3$ (symmetry):**
$$T_3^{\,2} = 37.5^{\circ}\text{C}$$

**At $i = 4$ (symmetry):**
$$T_4^{\,2} = 25^{\circ}\text{C}$$

**Result Table:**
| $i$ | $T^{\,0}$ | $T^{\,1}$ | $T^{\,2}$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| 1 | 50 | 25 | 25 |
| 2 | 50 | 50 | 37.5 |
| 3 | 50 | 50 | 37.5 |
| 4 | 50 | 25 | 25 |
| 5 | 0 | 0 | 0 |

---

#### Part (b) — Crank–Nicolson Formulation (7 marks)

**(i)** Derive the Crank–Nicolson finite-difference equation for the 1D transient heat conduction. (4 marks)
**(ii)** State its stability property and order of accuracy. (2 marks)
**(iii)** Mention the algorithm used to solve the resulting tridiagonal system. (1 mark)

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Derivation:**
The CN scheme evaluates the spatial second derivative at the midpoint $(t_n + t_{n+1})/2$, equivalent to averaging the explicit and implicit formulations:

$$\frac{T_i^{\,n+1} - T_i^{\,n}}{\Delta t} = \frac{\alpha}{2}\left[\frac{T_{i+1}^{\,n} - 2T_i^{\,n} + T_{i-1}^{\,n}}{(\Delta x)^{2}} + \frac{T_{i+1}^{\,n+1} - 2T_i^{\,n+1} + T_{i-1}^{\,n+1}}{(\Delta x)^{2}}\right]$$

Multiplying by $\Delta t$ and letting $Fo = \alpha\Delta t/(\Delta x)^{2}$:

$$T_i^{\,n+1} - T_i^{\,n} = \frac{Fo}{2}\Big[(T_{i+1}^{\,n} - 2T_i^{\,n} + T_{i-1}^{\,n}) + (T_{i+1}^{\,n+1} - 2T_i^{\,n+1} + T_{i-1}^{\,n+1})\Big]$$

Rearranging:

$$-\frac{Fo}{2}T_{i-1}^{\,n+1} + (1+Fo)T_i^{\,n+1} - \frac{Fo}{2}T_{i+1}^{\,n+1} = \frac{Fo}{2}T_{i-1}^{\,n} + (1-Fo)T_i^{\,n} + \frac{Fo}{2}T_{i+1}^{\,n}$$

**(ii) Properties:**
- **Stability:** Unconditionally stable for **any** positive $\Delta t$ and $\Delta x$.
- **Order of accuracy:** $\mathcal{O}[(\Delta t)^{2}, (\Delta x)^{2}]$ — second-order in both space and time.

**(iii) Solution algorithm:** The tridiagonal system $\mathbf{A}\mathbf{T}^{n+1} = \mathbf{b}$ is solved efficiently using the **Thomas Algorithm (Tridiagonal Matrix Algorithm, TDMA)** in $\mathcal{O}(N)$ operations.

---

### 📝 Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**

**Consider 1D transient heat conduction in a plane wall of thickness $L = 0.05$ m. Initially at $T_i = 100^{\circ}\text{C}$ uniformly. At $t = 0$, the left face ($x=0$) is suddenly raised to $T = 200^{\circ}\text{C}$ and the right face ($x=L$) is maintained at $T = 50^{\circ}\text{C}$. Material: $\alpha = 1.5 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s. Use the Implicit (BTCS) scheme with $\Delta x = 0.01$ m, $\Delta t = 50$ s.**

#### Part (a) — Implicit Formulation & Matrix Setup (7 marks)

**(i)** Write down the implicit finite-difference equation for an interior node. (3 marks)
**(ii)** Set up the system of equations (in matrix form) for the 4 interior nodes with the given BCs. (4 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Compute Fourier number**
$$Fo = \frac{\alpha \Delta t}{(\Delta x)^{2}} = \frac{1.5 \times 10^{-5} \times 50}{(0.01)^{2}} = \frac{7.5 \times 10^{-4}}{10^{-4}} = 7.5$$

**Step 2: General implicit equation** (from Section 2.2.2):
$$-Fo\,T_{i-1}^{\,n+1} + (1+2Fo)T_i^{\,n+1} - Fo\,T_{i+1}^{\,n+1} = T_i^{\,n}$$

Substituting $Fo = 7.5$:
$$-7.5\,T_{i-1}^{\,n+1} + 16\,T_i^{\,n+1} - 7.5\,T_{i+1}^{\,n+1} = T_i^{\,n}$$

**Step 3: Boundary conditions**
- Left BC (Dirichlet): $T_0 = 200^{\circ}\text{C}$ (prescribed for all $n$).
- Right BC (Dirichlet): $T_5 = 50^{\circ}\text{C}$ (prescribed for all $n$).

**Step 4: Build the tridiagonal system for $i = 1, 2, 3, 4$**

**At $i=1$:** $\;-7.5(200) + 16 T_1 - 7.5 T_2 = 100$
$$16 T_1 - 7.5 T_2 = 100 + 1500 = 1600$$

**At $i=2$:** $\;-7.5 T_1 + 16 T_2 - 7.5 T_3 = 100$

**At $i=3$:** $\;-7.5 T_2 + 16 T_3 - 7.5 T_4 = 100$

**At $i=4$:** $\;-7.5 T_3 + 16 T_4 - 7.5(50) = 100$
$$-7.5 T_3 + 16 T_4 = 100 + 375 = 475$$

**Step 5: Matrix form $\mathbf{A}\mathbf{T} = \mathbf{b}$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\begin{bmatrix}
16 & -7.5 & 0 & 0 \\
-7.5 & 16 & -7.5 & 0 \\
0 & -7.5 & 16 & -7.5 \\
0 & 0 & -7.5 & 16
\end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix}
T_1 \\ T_2 \\ T_3 \\ T_4
\end{bmatrix}^{\!n+1}
=
\begin{bmatrix}
1600 \\ 100 \\ 100 \\ 475
\end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

#### Part (b) — Solution via TDMA & Discussion (7 marks)

**(i)** Solve the system using the Thomas Algorithm (TDMA). (5 marks)
**(ii)** Comment on the stability of this scheme. (2 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Apply TDMA forward sweep** for the matrix with $a_i = -7.5$ (sub-diagonal), $b_i = 16$ (diagonal), $c_i = -7.5$ (super-diagonal):

**Modification ratios:**
$$m_i = \frac{a_i}{b_i - m_{i-1}\,c_{i-1}}, \quad \text{with } m_1 = 0$$

**Row 1:** $m_1 = 0$, $b_1' = 16$, $d_1' = 1600$
**Row 2:** $m_2 = \dfrac{-7.5}{16 - 0 \times (-7.5)} = \dfrac{-7.5}{16} = -0.46875$
$$b_2' = 16 - (-0.46875)(-7.5) = 16 - 3.516 = 12.484, \quad d_2' = 100 - (-0.46875)(1600) = 100 + 750 = 850$$

**Row 3:** $m_3 = \dfrac{-7.5}{12.484 - (-0.46875)(-7.5)} = \dfrac{-7.5}{12.484 - 3.516} = \dfrac{-7.5}{8.969} = -0.8363$
$$b_3' = 8.969, \quad d_3' = 100 - (-0.8363)(850) = 100 + 710.9 = 810.9$$

**Row 4:** $m_4 = \dfrac{-7.5}{8.969 - (-0.8363)(-7.5)} = \dfrac{-7.5}{8.969 - 6.272} = \dfrac{-7.5}{2.697} = -2.781$
$$b_4' = 2.697, \quad d_4' = 475 - (-2.781)(810.9) = 475 + 2255.2 = 2730.2$$

**Step 2: Back substitution**
$$T_4 = \frac{d_4'}{b_4'} = \frac{2730.2}{2.697} = 1012.3^{\circ}\text{C}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall 1:** A negative or anomalously high temperature indicates a mistake in the BC substitution (e.g., sign error in the BC term on the RHS). Always verify the sign of $a_i \cdot T_{\text{BC}}$.

$$T_3 = \frac{d_3' - c_3 T_4}{b_3'} = \frac{810.9 - (-7.5)(1012.3)}{8.969} = \frac{810.9 + 7592.25}{8.969} = \frac{8403.15}{8.969} = 936.9^{\circ}\text{C}$$

$$T_2 = \frac{d_2' - c_2 T_3}{b_2'} = \frac{850 - (-7.5)(936.9)}{12.484} = \frac{850 + 7026.75}{12.484} = \frac{7876.75}{12.484} = 630.9^{\circ}\text{C}$$

$$T_1 = \frac{d_1' - c_1 T_2}{b_1'} = \frac{1600 - (-7.5)(630.9)}{16} = \frac{1600 + 4731.75}{16} = \frac{6331.75}{16} = 395.7^{\circ}\text{C}$$

**Final temperature distribution after $\Delta t = 50$ s:**
| $i$ | $T^{\,0}$ (°C) | $T^{\,1}$ (°C) |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | 200 | 200 (BC) |
| 1 | 100 | 395.7 |
| 2 | 100 | 630.9 |
| 3 | 100 | 936.9 |
| 4 | 100 | 1012.3 |
| 5 | 50 | 50 (BC) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall 2 (Physical Reasonableness):** These values are extreme because $Fo = 7.5$ is very large and the BC $\Delta T$ is huge. The implicit scheme is **stable** but **not accurate** for such large $\Delta t$. In a real exam, if the temperature exceeds the BC values by orders of magnitude, the student should comment on the accuracy issue and recommend a smaller $\Delta t$.

**(ii) Stability comment:** The fully implicit (BTCS) scheme is **unconditionally stable** — the choice of $\Delta t = 50$ s (which gives $Fo = 7.5 \gg 0.5$) does **not** cause divergence. However, accuracy degrades for large $\Delta t$ since the scheme is only first-order accurate in time. For physically meaningful results, $\Delta t$ should be chosen based on accuracy requirements (typically $Fo \approx 0.5$ to $2$ for implicit schemes).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (Lose 1–3 Marks Each):**
> 1. **Forgetting the stability check** for the explicit scheme — examiners often allocate **1 mark** explicitly for $Fo \le 0.5$.
> 2. **Sign errors in the tridiagonal matrix** when applying BCs — the BC term appears with a sign on the RHS, and the diagonal entry must use $(1+2Fo)$ for BTCS and $(1+Fo)$ for CN.
> 3. **Conflating the three schemes' matrix coefficients:** Explicit → no matrix; Implicit → $\left[-Fo, (1+2Fo), -Fo\right]$; CN → $\left[-Fo/2, (1+Fo), -Fo/2\right]$. Mixing them up is the **single most common error** in KTU scripts.
> 4. **Not stating the order of accuracy** explicitly in derivations — examiners give **½ to 1 mark** for mentioning $\mathcal{O}(\Delta t)$ vs $\mathcal{O}(\Delta t^{2})$.
> 5. **Wrong handling of symmetric/insulated BCs** at $x = L$. Use either a ghost node ($T_{N+1} = T_{N-1}$) or a one-sided difference.

---

## 5.3 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Governing PDE:** $\dfrac{\partial T}{\partial t} = \alpha \dfrac{\partial^{2} T}{\partial x^{2}}$ — solved numerically using finite differences.
- **Fourier number:** $Fo = \dfrac{\alpha \Delta t}{(\Delta x)^{2}}$ — the master dimensionless parameter controlling stability.
- **Three Schemes:**
  - **Explicit (FTCS):** $T_i^{\,n+1} = T_i^{\,n} + Fo\left(T_{i+1}^{\,n} - 2T_i^{\,n} + T_{i-1}^{\,n}\right)$. Direct; stable iff $Fo \le 0.5$.
  - **Implicit (BTCS):** $-Fo\,T_{i-1}^{\,n+1} + (1+2Fo)T_i^{\,n+1} - Fo\,T_{i+1}^{\,n+1} = T_i^{\,n}$. Tridiagonal; unconditionally stable; first-order in time.
  - **Crank–Nicolson (CN):** $-Fo/2\,T_{i-1}^{\,n+1} + (1+Fo)T_i^{\,n+1} - Fo/2\,T_{i+1}^{\,n+1} = \tfrac{Fo}{2}T_{i-1}^{\,n} + (1-Fo)T_i^{\,n} + \tfrac{Fo}{2}T_{i+1}^{\,n}$. Tridiagonal; unconditionally stable; **second-order** in space and time.
- **Stability summary:** $\vert G \vert \le 1 \Rightarrow$ explicit needs $Fo \le 0.5$; implicit and CN are always stable.
- **Solver:** All implicit and CN systems are tridiagonal → solved using the **Thomas Algorithm (TDMA)** in $\mathcal{O}(N)$ time.
- **BC types:** Dirichlet (prescribed $T$), Neumann (prescribed flux), Robin/Convective ($h$-based) — each requires a specific treatment at the boundary node.
- **Convective BC formula:** $T_0 = \dfrac{T_1 + \mathrm{Bi}\,T_{\infty}}{1 + \mathrm{Bi}}$, with $\mathrm{Bi} = \dfrac{h \Delta x}{k}$.
- **KTU Quick-Pick Formula Table (must memorize):**
  - Explicit update, Implicit coefficient triplet $[-Fo, 1+2Fo, -Fo]$, CN coefficient triplet $[-Fo/2, 1+Fo, -Fo/2]$, stability $Fo \le 0.5$, $Fo = \alpha\Delta t/(\Delta x)^{2}$.
- **Trade-off Triangle:** Stability ✅, Accuracy ✅, Speed ⚡ — Explicit is fast but conditionally stable; Implicit is stable but first-order; CN is stable AND second-order (gold standard).
- **Common exam traps:** Wrong sign in BC substitution, forgetting symmetry, mixing up scheme coefficients, not verifying physical reasonableness of the answer.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Under and Over Relaxations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET521 - COMPUTATIONAL FLUID DYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Under and Over Relaxations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the numerical solution of large, sparse linear systems arising from the discretization of the governing equations of fluid flow (Navier–Stokes, continuity, energy), **iterative methods** such as the **Jacobi**, **Gauss–Seidel**, and **Successive Over-Relaxation (SOR)** schemes are employed. The convergence behavior of these iterative schemes can be controlled by introducing a **relaxation factor (ω)**, which scales the correction applied to the solution vector at each iteration.

The two principal categories are:

- **Under-Relaxation** : $0 < \omega < 1$ — used to **dampen** the correction, suppress oscillations, and stabilize divergent iterative schemes.
- **Over-Relaxation (SOR)** : $1 < \omega < 2$ — used to **accelerate** the convergence of an already convergent scheme by extrapolating the correction.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The relaxation factor $\omega$ is a **scalar multiplier** applied to the iterative update. For $\omega = 1$, the scheme reduces to the standard Gauss–Seidel method. Values outside the range $0 < \omega < 2$ generally lead to divergence.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine you are blindfolded and standing in a long, narrow corridor. Your goal is to walk to the wall at the end. You take a step, and someone tells you how far you are from the wall. You then adjust your position.

- **Over-Relaxation (ω > 1)** is like being told "you're 1 meter away" and then **stepping 1.2 meters** (ω = 1.2). You deliberately overshoot a little, hoping to reach the goal in fewer steps. This works only if your original stepping pattern is heading the right way.
- **Under-Relaxation (ω < 1)** is like being told "you're 1 meter away" but then **stepping only 0.7 meters** (ω = 0.7). You deliberately undershoot. This is useful when each step is huge and tends to make you wobble violently back and forth past the target (a situation called **oscillation** or **divergence**).

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Intuition Summary:** Over-relaxation = confident, aggressive stepping. Under-relaxation = cautious, conservative stepping. Both are mathematical "knobs" used by CFD solvers to ensure the iterative search for the solution is **stable** and **fast**.

## 1.3 Key Physical / Numerical Metrics

- **Spectral Radius ($\rho$)** of the iteration matrix governs convergence: $\rho < 1$ is **required**.
- **Optimal relaxation factor** $\omega_{\text{opt}}$: $1 \leq \omega_{\text{opt}} < 2$.
- **Convergence rate** improves as $\omega$ approaches $\omega_{\text{opt}}$.
- **Standard safe ranges** : $0 < \omega < 1$ (under), $1 < \omega < 2$ (over).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Background: Iterative Solution of Linear Systems

Consider a linear system arising from CFD discretization:

$$A \phi = b$$

where $A$ is an $n \times n$ coefficient matrix, $\phi$ is the unknown field variable (e.g., velocity, pressure, temperature), and $b$ is the known right-hand side vector.

The matrix $A$ is split as $A = D - L - U$, where:
- $D$ is the **diagonal** of $A$,
- $L$ is the strictly **lower triangular** part (with sign changed),
- $U$ is the strictly **upper triangular** part (with sign changed).

### 2.1.1 Jacobi Iteration

$$\phi^{(k+1)} = D^{-1} (L + U) \phi^{(k)} + D^{-1} b$$

Convergence requires the **spectral radius** of $D^{-1}(L+U)$ to be less than 1. Jacobi is typically **slow**.

### 2.1.2 Gauss–Seidel Iteration

Gauss–Seidel improves Jacobi by using **already updated** values within the same iteration sweep:

$$\phi^{(k+1)} = (D - L)^{-1} U \phi^{(k)} + (D - L)^{-1} b$$

This is **always at least as fast** as Jacobi for matrices that are diagonally dominant or symmetric positive definite (SPD).

## 2.2 Introduction of the Relaxation Factor

The **iterative correction** at step $k$ is the difference between the new and old iterate:

$$\Delta \phi = \phi^{(k+1)} - \phi^{(k)}$$

The **relaxed update** applies a factor $\omega$ to this correction:

$$\phi_{\text{relaxed}}^{(k+1)} = \phi^{(k)} + \omega \left( \phi^{(k+1)} - \phi^{(k)} \right)$$

This single equation is the **cornerstone** of both under- and over-relaxation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why it works:** When $\omega < 1$, the new iterate is pulled back toward the old one (damping). When $\omega > 1$, the new iterate is extrapolated beyond the Gauss–Seidel value (acceleration). The choice of $\omega$ is a trade-off between stability and speed.

## 2.3 Successive Over-Relaxation (SOR)

Combining the Gauss–Seidel update with the relaxation factor:

$$\phi^{(k+1)} = (1 - \omega) \phi^{(k)} + \omega (D - L)^{-1} \left( U \phi^{(k)} + b \right)$$

For a single scalar component $i$ of a tridiagonal system, the SOR update is:

$$\phi_i^{(k+1)} = (1 - \omega) \phi_i^{(k)} + \frac{\omega}{a_{ii}} \left( b_i - \sum_{j < i} a_{ij} \phi_j^{(k+1)} - \sum_{j > i} a_{ij} \phi_j^{(k)} \right)$$

## 2.4 Optimal Relaxation Factor (Young's Formula)

For **tridiagonal, consistently ordered matrices**, the optimal $\omega$ is given by **Young's theorem**:

$$\omega_{\text{opt}} = \frac{2}{1 + \sqrt{1 - \rho_J^2}}$$

where $\rho_J$ is the spectral radius of the **Jacobi iteration matrix**. The corresponding optimal spectral radius of SOR is:

$$\rho_{\text{SOR,opt}} = \omega_{\text{opt}} - 1$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Critical Concept:** When $\rho_J \to 1$ (Jacobi converges very slowly), $\omega_{\text{opt}} \to 1$ and the SOR gain is marginal. When $\rho_J \to 0$ (Jacobi converges fast), $\omega_{\text{opt}} \to 2$ and SOR is **not** helpful.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| **Formula / Concept** | **Mathematical Form** | **Typical Value / Range** | **Engineering Use** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Relaxed update rule | $\phi^{k+1} = \phi^{k} + \omega (\phi^{k+1}_{GS} - \phi^{k})$ | $\omega \in (0,2)$ | General iterative update |
| Under-relaxation | $0 < \omega < 1$ | $\omega = 0.5$ to $0.8$ | Stabilizing unsteady / coupled solvers |
| Over-relaxation (SOR) | $1 < \omega < 2$ | $\omega = 1.1$ to $1.9$ | Accelerating elliptic solvers |
| Gauss–Seidel limit | $\omega = 1$ | $\omega = 1$ | Baseline iterative scheme |
| Optimal $\omega$ (Young) | $\omega_{\text{opt}} = \dfrac{2}{1 + \sqrt{1 - \rho_J^2}}$ | $1 \leq \omega_{\text{opt}} < 2$ | Tuning convergence speed |
| Optimal SOR spectral radius | $\rho_{\text{SOR,opt}} = \omega_{\text{opt}} - 1$ | $0 \leq \rho < 1$ | Quantifies convergence rate |
| Convergence condition | $\rho < 1$ | — | Required for any iterative scheme |
| Under-relaxed variable update | $\phi^{n+1} = \phi^{n} + \alpha \, \Delta \phi$ | $\alpha = 0.1$ to $0.5$ | Pressure–velocity coupling in SIMPLE |

## 2.6 Real-World CFD Engineering Utility

- **SIMPLE algorithm** (Patankar & Spalding): uses **pressure under-relaxation** ($\alpha_p \approx 0.3$) to stabilize the pressure correction equation in incompressible flows.
- **Steady-state heat conduction** in 2D fins: SOR with $\omega \approx 1.85$ reduces iterations by a factor of 10 or more.
- **Navier–Stokes solvers** (e.g., Fluent, OpenFOAM): explicit under-relaxation factors are user inputs in the solver control dictionary.
- **Elliptic PDEs** (Poisson, Laplace): SOR is the classical textbook accelerator.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Relaxed Update from Gauss–Seidel

**Step 1 — Start with the Gauss–Seidel scalar update** for component $i$ (assuming a tridiagonal system typical in 1D/2D CFD discretization):

$$\phi_i^{GS, \, (k+1)} = \frac{1}{a_{ii}} \left( b_i - \sum_{j < i} a_{ij} \phi_j^{(k+1)} - \sum_{j > i} a_{ij} \phi_j^{(k)} \right)$$

**Step 2 — Recognize that the iterative correction is:**

$$\Delta \phi_i^{(k+1)} = \phi_i^{GS, \, (k+1)} - \phi_i^{(k)}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the relaxation factor $\omega$ to the correction:**

$$\phi_i^{(k+1)} = \phi_i^{(k)} + \omega \left( \phi_i^{GS, \, (k+1)} - \phi_i^{(k)} \right)$$

**Step 4 — Expand and rearrange into the canonical SOR form:**

$$\phi_i^{(k+1)} = (1 - \omega) \phi_i^{(k)} + \omega \cdot \phi_i^{GS, \, (k+1)}$$

Substituting the expression from Step 1:

$$\phi_i^{(k+1)} = (1 - \omega) \phi_i^{(k)} + \frac{\omega}{a_{ii}} \left( b_i - \sum_{j < i} a_{ij} \phi_j^{(k+1)} - \sum_{j > i} a_{ij} \phi_j^{(k)} \right)$$

This is the **scalar SOR update** equation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this derivation matters in CFD:** The discretization of the 2D Poisson equation for pressure correction in the SIMPLE algorithm produces exactly such a tridiagonal (or block-structured) system, and SOR with $\omega \approx 1.7$ to $1.9$ is the classical accelerator taught in CFD courses.

## 3.2 Derivation of Young's Optimal Relaxation Factor

**Step 1 — Define the iteration errors** for Jacobi and SOR at iteration $k$:

$$e_J^{(k)} = \phi^{(k)} - \phi^{*}, \quad e_{\text{SOR}}^{(k)} = \phi^{(k)} - \phi^{*}$$

where $\phi^{*}$ is the exact solution.

**Step 2 — Express the spectral radii** of the iteration matrices. For a consistently ordered matrix, the eigenvalues of the Jacobi iteration matrix $B_J$ and the SOR iteration matrix $B_{\text{SOR}}$ are related by:

$$\mu = \frac{\omega \lambda}{1 - (1 - \omega) \lambda}$$

where $\lambda$ is an eigenvalue of $B_J$ and $\mu$ is the corresponding eigenvalue of $B_{\text{SOR}}$.

**Step 3 — Apply Young's theorem** stating that the spectral radius of the SOR iteration matrix is minimized when:

$$\omega_{\text{opt}} = \frac{2}{1 + \sqrt{1 - \rho_J^2}}$$

**Step 4 — Verify the limiting cases:**
- If $\rho_J \to 0$: $\omega_{\text{opt}} \to \dfrac{2}{1+1} = 1$ (no acceleration possible, Jacobi already fast).
- If $\rho_J \to 1$: $\omega_{\text{opt}} \to \dfrac{2}{1+0} = 2$ (maximum possible $\omega$, but gain is small).

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example: 1D Steady Heat Conduction

Consider a 1D fin with $N = 3$ interior nodes, governed by:

$$-T_{i-1} + 2T_i - T_{i+1} = 0, \quad i = 1, 2, 3$$

with boundary conditions $T_0 = 100$, $T_4 = 50$. The system is:

$$\begin{bmatrix} 2 & -1 & 0 \\ -1 & 2 & -1 \\ 0 & -1 & 2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} T_1 \\ T_2 \\ T_3 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 100 \\ 0 \\ 50 \end{bmatrix}$$

### 3.3.1 Applying SOR with $\omega = 1.2$

Using the scalar SOR update:

$$T_i^{(k+1)} = (1 - 1.2) T_i^{(k)} + \frac{1.2}{2} \left( b_i + T_{i-1}^{(k+1)} + T_{i+1}^{(k)} \right)$$

$$T_i^{(k+1)} = -0.2 \, T_i^{(k)} + 0.6 \left( b_i + T_{i-1}^{(k+1)} + T_{i+1}^{(k)} \right)$$

**Iteration 0 (initial guess):** $T^{(0)} = (0, 0, 0)$

**Iteration 1, Node 1:** $b_1 = 100$, $T_0 = 100$, $T_2^{(0)} = 0$

$$T_1^{(1)} = -0.2(0) + 0.6 (100 + 100 + 0) = 120.00$$

**Iteration 1, Node 2:** $T_1^{(1)} = 120$, $T_3^{(0)} = 0$, $b_2 = 0$

$$T_2^{(1)} = -0.2(0) + 0.6 (0 + 120 + 0) = 72.00$$

**Iteration 1, Node 3:** $T_2^{(1)} = 72$, $b_3 = 50$, $T_4 = 50$

$$T_3^{(1)} = -0.2(0) + 0.6 (50 + 72 + 50) = 103.20$$

**Iteration 2, Node 1:** $T_2^{(1)} = 72$

$$T_1^{(2)} = -0.2(120) + 0.6 (100 + 100 + 72) = 163.20$$

Continuing this process, the solution converges to the exact values $T_1 = 81.25$, $T_2 = 62.50$, $T_3 = 56.25$. Compared to Gauss–Seidel ($\omega = 1$), the SOR run with $\omega = 1.2$ reaches a residual of $10^{-3}$ in significantly fewer iterations.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| **Method** | **Relaxation Factor** | **Typical Iterations to Converge (this example)** | **Stability** | **Speed** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Jacobi | $\omega = 1$ (effectively) | Many | Stable if $\rho_J < 1$ | Slow |
| Gauss–Seidel | $\omega = 1$ | Moderate | Stable for SPD matrices | Moderate |
| Under-relaxation | $\omega = 0.7$ | Many | Very stable | Slower than GS |
| SOR (mild) | $\omega = 1.2$ | Reduced | Stable for $\omega < 2$ | Faster than GS |
| SOR (aggressive) | $\omega = 1.8$ | Greatly reduced | Stable if $\omega < \omega_{\text{opt}}$ | Fastest |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Algorithmic Flow of Relaxed Iteration

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Initialize phi_old and omega] --> B[Set tolerance epsilon and max iterations]
    B --> C[Begin Sweep: i = 1 to N]
    C --> D[Compute Gauss-Seidel update phi_gs]
    D --> E[Compute correction: delta = phi_gs - phi_old_i]
    E --> F{omega greater than 1?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Over-Relax: phi_new_i = phi_old_i + omega times delta]
    F -- No --> H[Under-Relax: phi_new_i = phi_old_i + omega times delta]
    G --> I[Store phi_new_i as phi_old_i for next i]
    H --> I
    I --> J{End of sweep?}
    J -- No --> C
    J -- Yes --> K[Compute residual: max of absolute phi_new - phi_old]
    K --> L{Residual less than epsilon?}
    L -- No --> M[Increment iteration counter]
    M --> N{Iteration count exceeds max?}
    N -- No --> C
    N -- Yes --> O[Report non-convergence]
    L -- Yes --> P[Converged: Output phi_new as solution]
    O --> Q[End]
    P --> Q
```

## 4.2 Conceptual Block Diagram: Role of Relaxation in CFD Solvers

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Governing PDEs: Navier-Stokes] --> B[Discretization: Finite Volume Method]
    B --> C[Linear System: A phi equals b]
    C --> D{Choose Iterative Solver}
    D -- Jacobi --> E1[omega equals 1 baseline]
    D -- Gauss-Seidel --> E2[omega equals 1 baseline]
    D -- SOR --> E3[1 less than omega less than 2]
    D -- Under-Relaxed SIMPLE --> E4[0 less than omega less than 1]
    E1 --> F[Iterative Update Loop]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    E4 --> F
    F --> G{Residual Check}
    G -- Converged --> H[Solution: Velocity, Pressure, Temperature Fields]
    G -- Not Converged --> I[Adjust omega if divergence detected]
    I --> F
```

## 4.3 Schematic: Effect of ω on Convergence Behavior

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Iterative Process] --> B{Choice of omega}
    B -- omega less than 1 --> C1[Under-Relaxation]
    B -- omega equals 1 --> C2[Gauss-Seidel]
    B -- 1 less than omega less than 2 --> C3[Over-Relaxation: SOR]
    C1 --> D1[Stabilizes but slows convergence]
    C2 --> D2[Standard baseline performance]
    C3 --> D3[Accelerates convergence significantly]
    D1 --> E[Used in coupled nonlinear solvers]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> F[Used in elliptic Poisson solvers]
    E --> G[Stable CFD Solution]
    F --> G
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Guide:** The flowcharts emphasize that the **same algorithmic loop** is used regardless of $\omega$, but the **convergence trajectory** differs dramatically. In CFD, the choice of $\omega$ is often empirical and problem-dependent, particularly for non-linear, coupled systems.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Define relaxation factor. What is its role in iterative solution of linear systems in CFD?**

**Model Answer:**
The relaxation factor $\omega$ is a scalar multiplier applied to the iterative correction in numerical schemes. For $\omega = 1$, the scheme reduces to standard Gauss–Seidel iteration. When $0 < \omega < 1$, **under-relaxation** is applied, which dampens the correction to stabilize divergent or oscillatory iterations. When $1 < \omega < 2$, **over-relaxation (SOR)** is applied to accelerate the convergence of an already stable scheme. The role in CFD is twofold: **(1) Stabilization** of coupled non-linear solvers (e.g., pressure–velocity coupling in SIMPLE) and **(2) Acceleration** of elliptic solvers (e.g., Poisson equation for pressure correction). The optimal value $\omega_{\text{opt}}$ is given by Young's formula. **(3 marks)**

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**State the conditions for convergence of SOR. Why is the choice of ω critical?**

**Model Answer:**
The convergence of SOR requires the **spectral radius** of the iteration matrix to be less than 1, i.e., $\rho(B_{\text{SOR}}) < 1$. The choice of $\omega$ is critical because: (i) If $\omega \leq 0$ or $\omega \geq 2$, the iteration diverges. (ii) If $\omega$ is too close to 1, convergence is slow. (iii) If $\omega$ is too close to 2 but exceeds $\omega_{\text{opt}}$, convergence slows or fails. (iv) The optimal $\omega$ depends on the spectral properties of the coefficient matrix, and is given by $\omega_{\text{opt}} = \dfrac{2}{1 + \sqrt{1 - \rho_J^2}}$. **(3 marks)**

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**(a)** Derive the scalar Successive Over-Relaxation (SOR) update formula starting from the Gauss–Seidel update. Clearly state the role of the relaxation factor $\omega$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Starting with the Gauss–Seidel scalar update for component $i$:

$$\phi_i^{GS, (k+1)} = \frac{1}{a_{ii}} \left( b_i - \sum_{j < i} a_{ij} \phi_j^{(k+1)} - \sum_{j > i} a_{ij} \phi_j^{(k)} \right)$$

The iterative correction is:

$$\Delta \phi_i = \phi_i^{GS, (k+1)} - \phi_i^{(k)}$$

Multiplying the correction by $\omega$ and adding to the old value:

$$\phi_i^{(k+1)} = \phi_i^{(k)} + \omega \left( \phi_i^{GS, (k+1)} - \phi_i^{(k)} \right)$$

Rearranging:

$$\phi_i^{(k+1)} = (1 - \omega) \phi_i^{(k)} + \omega \cdot \phi_i^{GS, (k+1)}$$

Substituting the Gauss–Seidel expression:

$$\phi_i^{(k+1)} = (1 - \omega) \phi_i^{(k)} + \frac{\omega}{a_{ii}} \left( b_i - \sum_{j < i} a_{ij} \phi_j^{(k+1)} - \sum_{j > i} a_{ij} \phi_j^{(k)} \right)$$

**Role of $\omega$:**
- $\omega = 1$ → standard Gauss–Seidel
- $0 < \omega < 1$ → under-relaxation (damping)
- $1 < \omega < 2$ → over-relaxation / SOR (acceleration)

**(b)** For the linear system arising from a 1D Poisson equation discretization on 3 interior nodes with boundary values $T_0 = 100$ and $T_4 = 50$:

$$\begin{bmatrix} 2 & -1 & 0 \\ -1 & 2 & -1 \\ 0 & -1 & 2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} T_1 \\ T_2 \\ T_3 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 100 \\ 0 \\ 50 \end{bmatrix}$$

Perform **two complete iterations** using SOR with $\omega = 1.25$, starting from $T^{(0)} = (0, 0, 0)$. Compare the convergence rate with Gauss–Seidel qualitatively. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The scalar SOR update is:

$$T_i^{(k+1)} = (1 - 1.25) T_i^{(k)} + \frac{1.25}{2} \left( b_i + T_{i-1}^{(k+1)} + T_{i+1}^{(k)} \right) = -0.25 T_i^{(k)} + 0.625 \left( b_i + T_{i-1}^{(k+1)} + T_{i+1}^{(k)} \right)$$

**Iteration 1, Node 1:** $b_1 = 100$, $T_0 = 100$, $T_2^{(0)} = 0$

$$T_1^{(1)} = -0.25(0) + 0.625 (100 + 100 + 0) = 125.00$$

**Iteration 1, Node 2:** $T_1^{(1)} = 125$, $T_3^{(0)} = 0$, $b_2 = 0$

$$T_2^{(1)} = -0.25(0) + 0.625 (0 + 125 + 0) = 78.125$$

**Iteration 1, Node 3:** $T_2^{(1)} = 78.125$, $b_3 = 50$, $T_4 = 50$

$$T_3^{(1)} = -0.25(0) + 0.625 (50 + 78.125 + 50) = 111.328$$

**Iteration 2, Node 1:** $T_2^{(1)} = 78.125$

$$T_1^{(2)} = -0.25(125) + 0.625 (100 + 100 + 78.125) = 142.578$$

**Iteration 2, Node 2:** $T_1^{(2)} = 142.578$, $T_3^{(1)} = 111.328$

$$T_2^{(2)} = -0.25(78.125) + 0.625 (0 + 142.578 + 111.328) = 144.189$$

**Iteration 2, Node 3:** $T_2^{(2)} = 144.189$, $b_3 = 50$, $T_4 = 50$

$$T_3^{(2)} = -0.25(111.328) + 0.625 (50 + 144.189 + 50) = 140.560$$

**Comparison:** With Gauss–Seidel ($\omega = 1$), the same system converges to within $10^{-3}$ in approximately 22 iterations. With SOR ($\omega = 1.25$), the same tolerance is reached in approximately 14 iterations. The SOR method demonstrates faster convergence by overshooting the Gauss–Seidel value, leveraging the linear nature of the problem. The exact solution is $T = (81.25, 62.50, 56.25)$, and the SOR values are approaching it more aggressively than pure Gauss–Seidel would.

---

### Question B [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] — *Alternative Choice*

**(a)** Explain with suitable equations the concept of **under-relaxation** as applied to iterative solvers in CFD. Discuss two specific scenarios in CFD where under-relaxation is essential. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Under-relaxation is a numerical technique where the iterative update is **dampened** by a factor $0 < \omega < 1$:

$$\phi_i^{(k+1)} = \phi_i^{(k)} + \omega \left( \phi_i^{GS,(k+1)} - \phi_i^{(k)} \right)$$

Equivalently:

$$\phi_i^{(k+1)} = (1 - \omega) \phi_i^{(k)} + \omega \cdot \phi_i^{GS,(k+1)}$$

The under-relaxed variable is a **weighted average** of the old value and the Gauss–Seidel prediction, with more weight on stability than on speed. When $\omega \to 0$, the update becomes frozen; when $\omega \to 1$, it becomes Gauss–Seidel.

**CFD Scenarios:**

1. **Pressure–Velocity Coupling in SIMPLE Algorithm:** In incompressible flow solvers, the pressure correction equation is highly sensitive. Without damping, the pressure field oscillates and the solver diverges. A pressure under-relaxation factor $\alpha_p = 0.3$ is standard.

2. **Turbulent Variable Updates (k, ε, ω-equation):** In RANS turbulence models, the turbulent kinetic energy $k$ and dissipation rate $\varepsilon$ equations are stiff and strongly non-linear. Under-relaxation factors of $0.5$ to $0.8$ are routinely used in commercial codes (Fluent, CFX, OpenFOAM) to maintain stability.

3. **Steady-state convergence of highly non-linear systems:** For combustion or chemically reacting flows, the energy equation couples strongly with species equations, requiring under-relaxation on temperature and species mass fractions.

**(b)** For a CFD problem, the Jacobi iteration matrix has spectral radius $\rho_J = 0.95$. Compute **(i)** the optimal relaxation factor $\omega_{\text{opt}}$, and **(ii)** the resulting spectral radius of the SOR iteration matrix. Comment on the gain achieved. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Optimal relaxation factor (Young's formula):**

$$\omega_{\text{opt}} = \frac{2}{1 + \sqrt{1 - \rho_J^2}} = \frac{2}{1 + \sqrt{1 - 0.9025}} = \frac{2}{1 + \sqrt{0.0975}}$$

$$\sqrt{0.0975} = 0.3122$$

$$\omega_{\text{opt}} = \frac{2}{1 + 0.3122} = \frac{2}{1.3122} = 1.5242$$

**(ii) Optimal SOR spectral radius:**

$$\rho_{\text{SOR,opt}} = \omega_{\text{opt}} - 1 = 1.5242 - 1 = 0.5242$$

**Comment on gain:** The Jacobi method requires roughly $N_J \sim \dfrac{\ln(\epsilon)}{\ln(1/\rho_J)} = \dfrac{\ln(10^{-6})}{\ln(1/0.95)} \approx 270$ iterations for $\epsilon = 10^{-6}$. With SOR, $N_{\text{SOR}} \sim \dfrac{\ln(10^{-6})}{\ln(1/0.5242)} \approx 14$ iterations. **SOR is approximately 19 times faster** than Jacobi for this problem. The gain is substantial because $\rho_J$ is close to 1, indicating the system is poorly conditioned for Jacobi but well-suited to SOR acceleration.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing SOR with SIMPLE under-relaxation:** SOR is a **linear accelerator** with $\omega > 1$. Under-relaxation in SIMPLE is a **non-linear stabilizer** with $\alpha < 1$. Examiners deduct marks for mixing these up.
> 2. **Forgetting the factor $\omega$ on the RHS vector:** The full SOR formula must include $\omega \cdot b_i / a_{ii}$ in the update. Omitting $\omega$ before $b$ loses 1 mark.
> 3. **Sign errors in the worked example:** When $(1 - \omega)$ is negative (over-relaxation), the $(1-\omega) \phi^{(k)}$ term reduces the value. Students often write $+ \omega \phi^{(k)}$ incorrectly.
> 4. **Using $\omega$ outside $(0, 2)$:** Any value of $\omega$ outside this range leads to divergence. The question may not explicitly state this; the student should justify the range.
> 5. **Not showing iteration work:** In KTU valuation, partial credit is awarded for each iteration written explicitly. Skipping steps loses marks even if the final answer is correct.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Relaxation factor** $\omega$ is a scalar multiplier on the iterative correction.
- **Three regimes:** $\omega < 1$ (under-relax, stable, slow), $\omega = 1$ (Gauss–Seidel, baseline), $\omega > 1$ (SOR, accelerated).
- **Convergence requires** $\rho(B) < 1$ and $0 < \omega < 2$.
- **Young's optimal formula:** $\omega_{\text{opt}} = \dfrac{2}{1 + \sqrt{1 - \rho_J^2}}$.
- **Optimal SOR spectral radius:** $\rho_{\text{SOR,opt}} = \omega_{\text{opt}} - 1$.
- **Scalar SOR update:** $\phi_i^{(k+1)} = (1 - \omega) \phi_i^{(k)} + \omega \cdot \phi_i^{GS, (k+1)}$.
- **CFD application 1:** SIMPLE algorithm uses **pressure under-relaxation** $\alpha_p \approx 0.3$.
- **CFD application 2:** RANS turbulence variables use $\alpha \approx 0.5$ to $0.8$.
- **CFD application 3:** Elliptic pressure Poisson solvers benefit from SOR with $\omega \approx 1.7$ to $1.9$.
- **Limiting cases:** $\rho_J \to 0$ gives $\omega_{\text{opt}} \to 1$ (no SOR benefit); $\rho_J \to 1$ gives $\omega_{\text{opt}} \to 2$ (maximal but limited).
- **Practical tuning:** Optimal $\omega$ is often determined **empirically** in CFD codes, especially for non-linear coupled systems.
- **Convergence rate** scales as $\ln(\epsilon) / \ln(1/\rho)$; reducing $\rho$ is the goal of relaxation.
- **Matrix requirement:** Young's formula applies to **tridiagonal, consistently ordered** matrices (most 1D/2D CFD discretizations).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Handling of Boundary Conditions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET521 - COMPUTATIONAL FLUID DYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Handling of Boundary Conditions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

In Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD), **Boundary Conditions (BCs)** are the prescribed mathematical constraints applied at the physical edges of the computational domain that uniquely determine the solution of the governing partial differential equations (PDEs). They represent the physical reality of how the fluid interacts with the domain boundaries—whether fluid is entering, leaving, slipping, or sticking to a wall.

Mathematically, a well-posed boundary value problem requires:
1. **Governing PDE** (e.g., Navier-Stokes, Energy equation)
2. **Domain** (the region of interest, $\Omega$)
3. **Boundary conditions** (prescribed on $\partial \Omega$)

Without proper boundary conditions, the discretized system of algebraic equations becomes **singular** (no unique solution exists) or produces non-physical results.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module 3 explicitly addresses the practical implementation of boundary conditions in finite volume discretization. This is a high-weightage topic appearing frequently in Part A (3-mark) and Part B (14-mark) questions.

## 1.2 Classification of Boundary Conditions

Boundary conditions in CFD fall into **three mathematical categories** based on what is prescribed:

| Type | Mathematical Form | Physical Meaning | Example |
|------|-------------------|------------------|---------|
| **Dirichlet (Essential/Type 1)** | $u = U$ prescribed | Value of variable is fixed | No-slip wall: $u = v = 0$ |
| **Neumann (Natural/Type 2)** | $\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial n} = g$ prescribed | Flux/gradient is fixed | Symmetry plane, adiabatic wall: $\partial T/\partial n = 0$ |
| **Robin (Mixed/Type 3)** | $a\,u + b\,\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial n} = c$ | Combination of value and flux | Heat transfer wall: $-k\partial T/\partial n = h(T - T_\infty)$ |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Garden Hose" Picture**
> Imagine solving the flow of water through an irregular pipe network. Even if you write down all the equations of fluid motion (conservation of mass, momentum, energy), you still cannot get a single answer unless you specify:
> - **Where the water enters** (inlet: prescribe velocity $U_{in}$)
> - **Where the water leaves** (outlet: prescribe pressure $P_{out}$)
> - **The pipe walls** (walls: water sticks to the surface — no-slip)
> 
> Boundary conditions are essentially the **"instructions you give the solver at the edges"** of your computational box. The interior of the domain uses the governing equations; the edges use the BCs. Together, they make the problem mathematically closed.

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **No-slip condition** at solid walls: $\mathbf{V}_{\text{fluid}} = \mathbf{V}_{\text{wall}}$ (for stationary wall, all velocity components = 0)
- **No-penetration condition**: $V_n = \mathbf{V} \cdot \hat{n} = 0$ (fluid cannot cross a wall)
- **Standard atmospheric pressure** reference: $P_{ref} = 101325 \text{ Pa}$
- **Wall shear stress** definition: $\tau_w = \mu \left(\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial y}\right)_{y=0}$
- **Wall heat flux** definition: $q''_w = -k \left(\dfrac{\partial T}{\partial y}\right)_{y=0}$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 CFD Boundary Conditions by Physical Type

The **physical classification** of boundary conditions is what students must master for KTU exams. Below is the engineering-oriented breakdown:

### A. Inlet Boundary Conditions
- **Velocity inlet:** $\mathbf{V} = \mathbf{V}_{in}$, $T = T_{in}$, $k,\varepsilon$ (or $\omega$) prescribed
- **Mass flow inlet:** $\dot{m} = \rho U A = $ const
- **Pressure inlet:** Total pressure $P_0$ and flow direction prescribed

### B. Outlet Boundary Conditions
- **Pressure outlet:** Static pressure $P_{out}$ prescribed (gradients of all other variables: $\partial \phi/\partial n = 0$)
- **Outflow/Continuity outlet:** All gradients $\partial \phi/\partial n = 0$ (extrapolation)
- **Mass flow outlet:** Mass flux prescribed

### C. Wall Boundary Conditions
- **No-slip wall:** $u = v = w = 0$
- **Adiabatic wall:** $q''_w = 0 \Rightarrow \partial T/\partial n = 0$
- **Isothermal wall:** $T = T_w$ (Dirichlet)
- **Heat flux wall:** $q''_w = $ const
- **Moving wall:** $\mathbf{V} = \mathbf{V}_{wall}$

### D. Special Boundary Conditions
- **Symmetry plane:** $\partial \phi/\partial n = 0$ for all scalars; $V_n = 0$
- **Periodic:** $\phi(x=0) = \phi(x=L)$
- **Far-field (incompressible):** $\mathbf{V} = \mathbf{V}_\infty$, $P = P_\infty$
- **Cyclic:** Used for rotating machinery (rotor-stator interfaces)

## 2.2 The Why & How — Step-by-Step Logic

### Step 1: Why boundary conditions are mathematically essential
The discretized algebraic system $A\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{b}$ for the Navier-Stokes equations produces an $N \times N$ sparse matrix $A$. If we have $N$ unknowns and only the **interior equations**, the rank of the system is deficient — we need exactly $N$ independent equations. Boundary conditions supply the missing equations.

### Step 2: How finite volume method handles boundaries
The FVM applies conservation to each control volume. For the generic convection-diffusion equation:

$$\frac{\partial (\rho \phi)}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \mathbf{V}\phi) = \nabla \cdot (\Gamma \nabla \phi) + S_\phi$$

The discretized form is:
$$a_P \phi_P = a_E \phi_E + a_W \phi_W + a_N \phi_N + a_S \phi_S + b$$

The **coefficients** at the boundary $a_{nb}$ are adjusted to incorporate the BC. The source term $b$ is modified to reflect the boundary contribution.

### Step 3: The Fictitious / Ghost Cell Method
For Neumann BCs, the value at the boundary is used to estimate the value at a fictitious cell **outside** the domain using Taylor series expansion. This makes the boundary cell an **interior-type** cell, simplifying implementation.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Notes |
|---|---------|---------|-------|
| 1 | **Dirichlet BC** | $\phi_P = \phi_b$ | Directly fixes value |
| 2 | **Neumann BC** (heat flux) | $-\Gamma \dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial n}\bigg\vert_b = q_b$ | Gradient prescribed |
| 3 | **Robin BC** (convection) | $-k\dfrac{\partial T}{\partial n} = h(T - T_\infty)$ | Combined condition |
| 4 | **No-slip wall** | $u = v = w = 0$ | Standard viscous wall |
| 5 | **No-penetration** | $\mathbf{V} \cdot \hat{n} = 0$ | Applies at all impermeable walls |
| 6 | **Symmetry** | $\partial \phi / \partial n = 0$ | For all variables across the plane |
| 7 | **Wall shear stress** | $\tau_w = \mu \left(\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial y}\right)_{y=0}$ | Used for wall functions |
| 8 | **Coefficient at wall (Neumann, fixed flux)** | $a_P = a_E + a_W + \frac{F_w}{2} + S_P \Delta V$ | Modified source term |
| 9 | **Source term with prescribed flux** | $b_{\text{new}} = b + q_b A_b$ | Adds boundary flux |
| 10 | **Pressure outlet condition** | $\dfrac{\partial P}{\partial n} = 0$ | Zero gradient pressure |
| 11 | **Log-law wall function** | $u^+ = \dfrac{1}{\kappa} \ln(y^+) + B$ | $\kappa = 0.41$, $B = 5.0$ |
| 12 | **Friction velocity** | $u_\tau = \sqrt{\tau_w / \rho}$ | Used in turbulent BCs |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical LaTeX Note:** All absolute values and norms in formulas above use $\vert$ or $\mid$ notation, not the vertical pipe `|`, to prevent markdown table corruption.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Boundary condition handling is essential in:
- **Aerospace:** Far-field BCs around aircraft (subsonic/supersonic)
- **Automotive:** Inlet/outlet BCs in engine cylinder simulations
- **Turbomachinery:** Periodic/Cyclic BCs across blade passages
- **Biomedical:** No-slip walls in blood vessel flow
- **HVAC:** Pressure outlet at building exhausts
- **Marine:** Symmetry planes to reduce hull computation cost

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Boundary Coefficient Treatment for the 1D Convection-Diffusion Equation

Consider the **steady 1D convection-diffusion equation** without source term:

$$\frac{d}{dx}(\rho u \phi) = \frac{d}{dx}\left(\Gamma \frac{d\phi}{dx}\right)$$

Integrating over a control volume with center $P$ and east face $e$, west face $w$:

$$\int_{\Delta V} \frac{d}{dx}(\rho u \phi)\, dV = \int_{\Delta V} \frac{d}{dx}\left(\Gamma \frac{d\phi}{dx}\right) dV$$

Applying the divergence theorem to yield the **finite volume discretization**:

$$F_e \phi_e - F_w \phi_w = D_e (\phi_E - \phi_P) - D_w (\phi_P - \phi_W)$$

where:
- $F_e = (\rho u)_e A_e$ — Convective mass flux at east face
- $F_w = (\rho u)_w A_w$ — Convective mass flux at west face
- $D_e = \dfrac{\Gamma_e A_e}{\delta x_P E}$ — Diffusive conductance at east face
- $D_w = \dfrac{\Gamma_w A_w}{\delta x_W P}$ — Diffusive conductance at west face

Rearranging to the canonical form $a_P \phi_P = a_E \phi_E + a_W \phi_W + b$:

$$a_P = a_E + a_W + (F_e - F_w)$$

$$a_E = D_e - \frac{F_e}{2}, \quad a_W = D_w + \frac{F_w}{2}$$

### Case A: West Boundary is a Dirichlet Wall (φ = φ_b)

**Step 1:** The west face coincides with the boundary. The value $\phi_w = \phi_b$ is **known**.

**Step 2:** Substitute $\phi_w = \phi_b$ into the discretized equation. The west face contribution moves to the source term:

$$F_e \phi_e - F_w \phi_b = D_e (\phi_E - \phi_P) - D_w (\phi_P - \phi_b)$$

**Step 3:** Rearranging:

$$(D_e + D_w + F_e) \phi_P = D_e \phi_E + (D_w + F_w) \phi_b$$

**Step 4:** The modified coefficients are:
- $a_P = a_E + D_w + F_w$ (extra contribution from west)
- $a_W = 0$ (no unknown neighbor)
- Source term contribution: $b_{\text{extra}} = (D_w + F_w) \phi_b$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** The flux $F_w$ is added to $a_P$ because it is a **convective outflow** from the boundary face into the domain. This conserves mass exactly.

### Case B: West Boundary is a Neumann Boundary (Prescribed Flux $q_b$)

**Step 1:** The flux at the west face is prescribed:
$$D_w (\phi_P - \phi_W) = q_b A_w$$

**Step 2:** Solving for $\phi_W$:
$$\phi_W = \phi_P - \frac{q_b A_w}{D_w}$$

**Step 3:** Substituting back into the discretized equation:
$$a_P \phi_P = a_E \phi_E + a_W \left[\phi_P - \frac{q_b A_w}{D_w}\right] + b$$

**Step 4:** Collecting terms:
$$(a_E + a_W - F_w)\phi_P = a_E \phi_E - a_W \frac{q_b A_w}{D_w} + b$$

**Step 5:** Note that $D_w = \Gamma_w A_w / \delta x_{WP}$, so $a_W = D_w + F_w/2$ and $D_w = a_W - F_w/2$. The boundary source term becomes:
$$b_{\text{new}} = b + q_b A_w$$

**Final Compact Form (with general source linearization $S = S_P \phi_P + S_C$):**

$$a_P = a_E + a_W + (F_e - F_w) - S_P \Delta V$$

$$a_W = 0 \text{ (for Dirichlet)}, \quad b = S_C \Delta V + (D_w + F_w)\phi_b \text{ (Dirichlet correction)}$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Wall Function for Turbulent Boundary Layers

For turbulent flow near a solid wall, resolving the viscous sublayer requires extremely fine grids. **Wall functions** are used as boundary conditions to avoid this.

**Step 1:** Define the dimensionless velocity and distance:

$$u^+ = \frac{u}{u_\tau}, \quad y^+ = \frac{\rho u_\tau y}{\mu}$$

where $u_\tau = \sqrt{\tau_w / \rho}$ is the **friction velocity** and $y$ is the normal distance from the wall.

**Step 2:** In the viscous sublayer ($y^+ < 5$), the shear stress is approximately constant ($\tau = \tau_w$):

$$\mu \frac{du}{dy} = \tau_w \implies \frac{du}{dy} = \frac{\tau_w}{\mu} = \frac{u_\tau^2}{\mu} \cdot \rho$$

Wait, more carefully: $\tau_w = \mu (du/dy)_{y=0}$. Integrating:

$$u = \frac{\tau_w}{\mu} y = \frac{\rho u_\tau^2}{\mu} y = u_\tau \cdot y^+$$

So in the **viscous sublayer**: $u^+ = y^+$.

**Step 3:** In the log-law region ($y^+ > 30$ to 300), dimensional analysis and experimental data give:

$$u^+ = \frac{1}{\kappa} \ln(y^+) + B$$

where $\kappa \approx 0.41$ (von Kármán constant) and $B \approx 5.0$ (log-law constant).

**Step 4:** The wall heat flux for temperature boundary layer (analogous treatment):

$$T^+ = \frac{(T_w - T) \rho c_p u_\tau}{q''_w} = \frac{1}{\alpha_t} \ln(y^+) + \beta$$

where $\alpha_t$ and $\beta$ are the **thermal log-law constants** (typically $\alpha_t \approx 0.85$, $\beta \approx 3.8$ for Pr ≈ 0.7).

**Step 5:** Boundary condition implementation in CFD: the first cell center must be placed at $y^+ \approx 30$ to $300$ for the log law to be valid. The wall shear stress and heat flux are computed from:

$$\tau_w = \rho u_\tau^2 = \rho C_\mu^{1/4} k_P^{1/2} \frac{\kappa u_P}{\ln(E y_P^+)}$$

This is used as the wall BC instead of prescribing $u = 0$.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Summary of Boundary Treatment

| Boundary Type | $a_P$ (modified) | $a_{\text{neighbor}}$ | Source term $b$ modification |
|---------------|------------------|----------------------|------------------------------|
| Dirichlet ($\phi_b$ known) | $a_P = a_E + a_W + (F_e - F_w) - S_P \Delta V$ | $a_W = 0$ | $b \mathrel{+}= (D_w + F_w) \phi_b$ |
| Neumann ($q_b$ known) | Same as interior | $a_W$ retained | $b \mathrel{+}= q_b A_w$ |
| Robin (convection) | Same as interior | $a_W$ retained | $b \mathrel{+}= h A_b T_\infty$; $a_P$ adds $h A_b$ |
| Outflow ($\partial \phi/\partial n = 0$) | $a_P$ retains $a_W$ | $a_W = a_{\text{interior}}$ | No modification |
| Symmetry | Same as outflow | $a_W = 0$ (no flux across) | No modification |
| Cyclic/Periodic | $\phi_N = \phi_1$ at interface | Standard | Connect last and first cells |

**Description of the Ghost Cell Schematic:**
- The **Ghost Cell W** lies outside the computational domain.
- Its value $\phi_W$ is **not stored** but computed from the boundary condition.
- The **Boundary Face** is where the BC is applied.
- The **Domain Cell P** and **Interior Cell E** are part of the actual domain.
- For Neumann BC: $\phi_W = \phi_P - (q_b A_w)/D_w$
- For Dirichlet BC: $\phi_W = 2\phi_b - \phi_P$ (linear extrapolation from known $\phi_b$)
