# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Question Paper, Module 4]

> **Q: Define a "staggered grid" as used in the finite-volume discretization of the Navier–Stokes equations. Why is it preferred over a collocated grid for incompressible flows?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **staggered grid** is a finite-volume spatial arrangement in which the velocity components ($u$, $v$, $w$) are stored at locations *different* from the locations where scalars (pressure $p$, temperature $T$, density $\rho$) are stored. Specifically, $u$ is stored at the centers of the vertical (east–west) cell faces, $v$ at the centers of the horizontal (north–south) cell faces, and $p$ at the cell center. **(1.5 marks)**

It is preferred over a collocated grid because, in a collocated arrangement, the pressure field can develop a *checkerboard (zig-zag) oscillation* in which the linearly-interpolated face pressure is zero, causing the pressure gradient to vanish and the pressure-velocity coupling to break down. In the staggered grid, the pressure gradient acting on a $u$-control volume is computed as the direct difference $\left(p_{i,j} - p_{i+1,j}\right)/\Delta x$ between two *adjacent* cell-center pressures, with **no interpolation**. This makes the pressure-velocity coupling **inherently stable** and eliminates the checkerboard mode. **(1.5 marks)**

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Supplementary, Module 4]

> **Q: What is the "checkerboard pressure field" problem? How does the staggered grid prevent it?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **checkerboard pressure field** is a non-physical pressure oscillation in which $p_{i,j}$ alternates sign between adjacent cells (e.g., $+1, -1, +1, -1, \ldots$). On a collocated grid, the pressure at the cell face is the *arithmetic mean* of the two adjacent cell-center pressures. For a zig-zag field, this mean is exactly zero, so the pressure gradient seen by the momentum equation is zero and the velocity field does not respond. The mode is a **valid solution of the algebraic system but unphysical**. **(1.5 marks)**

The staggered grid prevents this by storing the $u$-velocity at the *midpoint of the cell face*, exactly between two pressure nodes. The pressure gradient term $\left(p_{i,j} - p_{i+1,j}\right)/\Delta x$ then uses the *actual* difference of two adjacent cell-center pressures, which is *non-zero* for a zig-zag field. The face pressure is never averaged, so the zig-zag mode cannot disguise itself. **(1.5 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question B-A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 4, Pattern Question]

> **(a)** With the help of a neat 2-D sketch, explain the layout of $p$, $u$, and $v$ on a staggered grid. Where is each variable stored and why? **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Starting from the 2-D incompressible Navier–Stokes equations, derive the discretized form of the $u$-momentum equation on the staggered $u$-control volume and clearly show how the pressure-gradient term simplifies to $\left(p_{i,j} - p_{i+1,j}\right)A$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 marks

**Step 1 (2 marks):** State the layout rules.

On a 2-D staggered grid:

- **Pressure $p$, temperature $T$, density $\rho$, turbulent kinetic energy $k$, dissipation rate $\varepsilon$** are stored at the **center** of the main cell, indexed $(i, j)$.
- **x-velocity $u$** is stored at the **midpoint of the vertical (east/west) cell face** at $\left(i + \tfrac{1}{2}, j\right)$.
- **y-velocity $v$** is stored at the **midpoint of the horizontal (north/south) cell face** at $\left(i, j + \tfrac{1}{2}\right)$.

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Sketch the arrangement.

A 2-D cell of size $\Delta x \times \Delta y$ has:

- One $\bullet$ (dot) at the center marked $p_{i,j}$ (and surrounding $p_{i\pm 1, j}$, $p_{i, j\pm 1}$ at neighboring cell centers).
- Two **$\rightarrow$** arrows on the east and west faces, marked $u_{i+1/2, j}$ (east) and $u_{i-1/2, j}$ (west).
- Two **$\uparrow$** arrows on the north and south faces, marked $v_{i, j+1/2}$ (north) and $v_{i, j-1/2}$ (south).

**Step 3 (3 marks):** Explain *why* the layout is chosen.

The location of each variable is dictated by the equation it satisfies:

- The $u$-momentum equation contains $\partial p/\partial x$. Placing $u$ on the **vertical face** between two pressure nodes means the pressure gradient is the **direct difference** of two cell-center pressures, with no interpolation. This is the geometric cure for the checkerboard problem.
- The $v$-momentum equation contains $\partial p/\partial y$. Placing $v$ on the **horizontal face** gives the analogous benefit for the y-direction.
- Pressure is needed to evaluate mass fluxes through all four faces of the *main* control volume, so it is most conveniently stored at the cell center, equidistant from all four faces.

#### Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Write the steady incompressible $u$-momentum equation:

$$
u \frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + v \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} = -\frac{1}{\rho}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} + \nu \left( \frac{\partial^{2} u}{\partial x^{2}} + \frac{\partial^{2} u}{\partial y^{2}} \right) + S_u
$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Define the $u$-control volume. It spans $x \in [x_i, x_{i+1}]$ and $y \in [y_{j-1/2}, y_{j+1/2}]$, centered at $\left(i + \tfrac{1}{2}, j\right)$.

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Integrate the equation over this CV:

$$
\int_{y_{j-1/2}}^{y_{j+1/2}}\int_{x_i}^{x_{i+1}} \left( u \frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + v \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} \right) dx\, dy = -\frac{1}{\rho}\int_{y_{j-1/2}}^{y_{j+1/2}}\int_{x_i}^{x_{i+1}} \frac{\partial p}{\partial x}\, dx\, dy + \ldots
$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Convert the integrals to face fluxes. The convection integral becomes:

$$
\int \approx \left[ \left(u^{2} A\right)_e - \left(u^{2} A\right)_w \right] + \left[ \left(u v A\right)_n - \left(u v A\right)_s \right]
$$

The diffusion integral similarly becomes a sum of four face gradients, and the source term becomes $\bar{S}_u \Delta x \Delta y$.

**Step 5 (2 marks):** The critical simplification of the pressure term. Since $p$ does not depend on $y$ in the pressure-gradient integral (for a uniform mesh), the inner integral reduces to:

$$
-\frac{1}{\rho}\int_{y_{j-1/2}}^{y_{j+1/2}} \left[ p\big|_{x_i}^{x_{i+1}} \right] dy = -\frac{1}{\rho}\left( p_{i+1,j} - p_{i,j} \right) \Delta y
$$

Multiplying the sign and writing in the form used in the algebraic equation:

$$
\text{Pressure force} = \left(p_{i,j} - p_{i+1,j}\right) A
$$

where $A = \Delta y \cdot 1$ is the face area per unit depth. This is the **defining feature of the staggered grid** — the pressure difference is taken between two **actual pressure nodes**, not between two *interpolated* face values. **(Final 1 mark reserved for stating the final canonical equation $a_P u_P = \sum a_{nb} u_{nb} + b + (p_{i,j} - p_{i+1,j})A$.)**

---

### Question B-B (Choice 2 — Alternative to B-A) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question Paper, Module 4]

> **(a)** Explain with a neat diagram the *checkerboard pressure field* problem on a collocated grid. Show mathematically why the discrete pressure gradient vanishes for a zig-zag pressure field. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** For the 2-D incompressible continuity equation, derive its finite-volume discretized form on the main $p$-control volume of a staggered grid. Show that the $u$ and $v$ velocities appear at the *actual* cell-face locations, with **no interpolation** required. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 marks

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Define the checkerboard pressure field. On a 2-D collocated grid, a zig-zag pressure field is:

$$
p_{i,j} = (-1)^{i+j} \cdot p_0
$$

where $p_0$ is a constant amplitude. The field alternates $+p_0, -p_0, +p_0, \ldots$ from cell to cell.

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Sketch the field. Draw a 3$\times$3 array of cells with alternating $+$ and $-$ signs in the centers.

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Compute the collocated discrete pressure gradient. On a collocated grid, the $u$ velocity node sits **on the cell face between two $p$ nodes**. The face pressure must therefore be obtained by **linear interpolation**:

$$
p_{\text{face}} = \tfrac{1}{2}\left(p_{i,j} + p_{i+1,j}\right) = \tfrac{1}{2}\left[(-1)^{i+j} + (-1)^{i+j+1}\right] p_0 = 0
$$

The face pressure is identically zero, so the x-pressure-gradient at the $u$ node is:

$$
\left.\frac{\partial p}{\partial x}\right|_{u} = \frac{p_{\text{face},e} - p_{\text{face},w}}{\Delta x} = 0
$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** State the consequence. The $u$-momentum equation therefore sees **no driving force**, the velocity does not respond to the pressure oscillation, and the algebraic system accepts the zig-zag as a *valid (but unphysical)* solution. The pressure-velocity coupling has *decoupled*.

#### Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 (1 mark):** State the 2-D incompressible continuity equation:

$$
\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} = 0
$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Define the main $p$-control volume: $x \in [x_{i-1/2}, x_{i+1/2}]$, $y \in [y_{j-1/2}, y_{j+1/2}]$.

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Integrate over the CV:

$$
\int_{y_{j-1/2}}^{y_{j+1/2}}\int_{x_{i-1/2}}^{x_{i+1/2}} \left( \frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} \right) dx\, dy = 0
$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Apply the divergence theorem (Green's theorem in 2-D):

$$
\int_{y_{j-1/2}}^{y_{j+1/2}} \left[ u\big|_{x_{i-1/2}}^{x_{i+1/2}} \right] dy + \int_{x_{i-1/2}}^{x_{i+1/2}} \left[ v\big|_{y_{j-1/2}}^{y_{j+1/2}} \right] dx = 0
$$

**Step 5 (2 marks):** **Key insight — no interpolation required.** On a staggered grid:

- $u$ at the **east face** $x = x_{i+1/2}$ is *literally* the $u$ storage node $u_{i+1/2, j}$.
- $u$ at the **west face** $x = x_{i-1/2}$ is *literally* $u_{i-1/2, j}$.
- $v$ at the **north face** $y = y_{j+1/2}$ is *literally* $v_{i, j+1/2}$.
- $v$ at the **south face** $y = y_{j-1/2}$ is *literally* $v_{i, j-1/2}$.

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Evaluate on a uniform mesh:

$$
\left(u_{i+1/2,j} - u_{i-1/2,j}\right) \Delta y + \left(v_{i,j+1/2} - v_{i,j-1/2}\right) \Delta x = 0
$$

Dividing by $\Delta x \Delta y$:

$$
\frac{u_{i+1/2,j} - u_{i-1/2,j}}{\Delta x} + \frac{v_{i,j+1/2} - v_{i,j-1/2}}{\Delta y} = 0
$$

This is the canonical **staggered-grid continuity equation** that closes the SIMPLE pressure-correction algorithm. **(1 mark for final boxed equation.)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Confusing *cell center* with *cell face*:** Many students write $u_{i,j}$ instead of $u_{i+1/2, j}$ on the staggered grid. This loses 1–2 marks immediately because the *whole point* of the staggered grid is the **half-index offset**. Always use the half-integer notation.
> 2. **Forgetting to state the *reason* for the staggered layout:** The arrangement is not arbitrary; it exists **specifically to eliminate the checkerboard problem**. Students who describe the layout but omit the physical/numerical motivation lose at least 2 marks.
> 3. **Writing $p_{\text{face}} = (p_{i,j}+p_{i+1,j})/2$:** On a staggered grid this is **wrong** — the face pressure is not interpolated; it is the difference of the two *cell-center* pressures. Examiners immediately deduct marks for this confusion.
> 4. **Skipping the divergence-theorem step:** The continuity equation derivation must show the *conversion* of the volume integral to a face-flux sum, not just state the final discretized form. This single skipped step costs 2–3 marks.
> 5. **Mixing up the indexing convention:** KTU expects $(i,j)$ for cell centers and $(i\pm 1/2, j)$ for $u$ nodes, $(i, j\pm 1/2)$ for $v$ nodes. Reversing this convention (i.e., putting $u$ on horizontal faces) is a 1-mark deduction.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Module-4 Revision Punch-List — "Staggered Grid"**

- [x] A **staggered grid** stores $u$ on vertical cell faces, $v$ on horizontal cell faces, and $p$ at cell centers. **Different variables live at different locations.**
- [x] The **checkerboard pressure field** is a zig-zag pressure oscillation $p_{i,j} = (-1)^{i+j}$ that **annihilates the linearly-interpolated face pressure** on a collocated grid, breaking the pressure-velocity coupling.
- [x] The staggered grid **eliminates the checkerboard** because the pressure gradient uses the **direct difference** of two cell-center pressures, with no interpolation.
- [x] The $u$-control volume is centered at $\left(i + \tfrac{1}{2}, j\right)$; the $v$-control volume is centered at $\left(i, j + \tfrac{1}{2}\right)$; the $p$-control volume is centered at $(i, j)$.
- [x] The **pressure-gradient term** in the $u$-momentum equation simplifies to $\left(p_{i,j} - p_{i+1,j}\right) A$ — no interpolation.
- [x] The **continuity equation** on the $p$-CV uses the $u$ and $v$ face values *directly* at the CV boundary, again with no interpolation.
- [x] The algebraic system is $a_P u_P = \sum a_{nb} u_{nb} + b + (p_{i,j} - p_{i+1,j})A$ and the analogous $v$-equation, coupled to continuity.
- [x] This naturally leads to the **SIMPLE pressure-correction algorithm** (Patankar & Spalding, 1972).
- [x] The staggered grid was introduced by **Harlow and Welch (1965)** in the **Marker-and-Cell (MAC)** method.
- [x] Modern collocated codes recover the staggered-grid stability using **Rhie–Chow interpolation** of face velocities, *re-introducing* the de-coupling protection *numerically* rather than *geometrically*.
- [x] **Memory cost:** the staggered grid requires ~50% more storage than a collocated grid for the same mesh, because three sets of node arrays are needed ($p$, $u$, $v$).
- [x] **Boundary conditions:** wall boundary conditions are implemented as **half-cell** approximations on the staggered grid (the $u$-node at a wall lies on the wall itself, at the center of the half-cell).
- [x] **Modified pressure** $P = p/\rho$ is used in the incompressible formulation to remove $\rho$ from the pressure-gradient term and simplify the algebraic system.
- [x] **Exam mantra:** *"No interpolation on the staggered grid — pressure difference uses two cell centers directly."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pressure Correction Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET521 - COMPUTATIONAL FLUID DYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Pressure Correction Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Pressure Correction Methods** are a class of iterative numerical algorithms used in Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD) to solve the coupled **Navier–Stokes equations** (momentum and continuity) on a staggered or collocated grid. The central difficulty is that pressure does not appear in the continuity equation explicitly, yet it acts as the coupling variable that enforces mass conservation. Pressure correction methods resolve this by first guessing a pressure field, solving the momentum equations to obtain a velocity field, and then correcting both pressure and velocity iteratively until continuity is satisfied within a prescribed tolerance.

The most widely used pressure correction algorithms studied under the KTU 2024 Scheme (PEMET521) are:

- **SIMPLE** (Semi-Implicit Method for Pressure-Linked Equations) – Patankar & Spalding (1972)
- **SIMPLEC** (SIMPLE-Consistent) – Van Doormaal & Raithby (1984)
- **PISO** (Pressure-Implicit with Splitting of Operators) – Issa (1986)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU PEMET521, Module 4):** Pressure correction is the *cornerstone of segregated solvers* in CFD. Without it, the pressure–velocity coupling in the incompressible Navier–Stokes equations cannot be resolved. Expect at least one 14-mark derivation/numerical problem in the End Semester Exam (ESE).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Leaky Tap & Plumber" Intuition

Imagine you are a plumber trying to push water through a complex network of pipes (the *computational domain*). You do not know the exact water pressure at each junction (the *pressure field*). Your natural workflow is:

1. **Guess** a pressure at every junction (initial pressure field).
2. **Open the taps** and measure how much water actually flows through each pipe segment (solve momentum equations → obtain a velocity field).
3. **Check** whether the amount of water entering each junction equals the amount leaving (check continuity / mass imbalance).
4. If there is a mismatch (some junctions "leak" or "flood"), **adjust the pressure** at those junctions slightly (pressure correction $p'$), and recompute the velocities.
5. Repeat steps 2–4 until every junction is perfectly balanced.

This is exactly what the **SIMPLE algorithm** does numerically. The "small adjustment" in step 4 is the *pressure correction* $p'$, derived from a **Poisson-type pressure correction equation** whose source term is the **mass imbalance** (continuity residual).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is pressure correction necessary?** Incompressible flow has no independent equation for pressure (no pressure equation from the continuity equation alone). Pressure correction methods artificially *create* such an equation by enforcing mass conservation as a constraint, producing a Poisson equation for $p'$ that is solvable on the discrete grid.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Quantities

The following constants and metrics govern the pressure correction algorithm and must be remembered:

- **Density** $\rho$ — usually assumed constant for incompressible flow.
- **Dynamic viscosity** $\mu$ — appears in the momentum diffusion terms.
- **Under-relaxation factor** $\alpha_p$ — typically **0.3** for pressure, used to stabilize convergence (bold for emphasis).
- **Convergence tolerance** — typically **$10^{-6}$** for the normalized mass residual.
- **Cell volume** $\Delta V = \Delta x \cdot \Delta y \cdot \Delta z$ on a structured Cartesian grid.
- **Grid Peclet number** $Pe = \rho u \Delta x / \Gamma$ — must be $\leq 2$ for the central differencing scheme to remain bounded.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Pressure–Velocity Coupling Problem

The governing equations for steady, incompressible flow are:

**Momentum (x-direction):**
$$
\frac{\partial (\rho u u)}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial (\rho v u)}{\partial y} = -\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} + \mu \left( \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial x^2} + \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial y^2} \right) + S_u
$$

**Continuity:**
$$
\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} = 0
$$

The **central difficulty**: pressure $p$ does not appear in continuity. If we simply guess $p^*$, compute $u^*, v^*$ from momentum, the resulting velocity field **will not satisfy continuity**. The pressure correction algorithm fixes this.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of the SIMPLE Algorithm

1. **Initialize** the pressure field $p^* = p^0$ (e.g., from a previous time step or hydrostatic distribution).
2. **Solve the discretized momentum equations** using $p^*$ to obtain a tentative velocity field $(u^*, v^*)$.
3. **Compute the mass imbalance** $m^*$ at each cell:
$$
m^* = \left[ (\rho u)_{e} - (\rho u)_{w} \right] \Delta y + \left[ (\rho v)_{n} - (\rho v)_{s} \right] \Delta x
$$
4. **Solve the pressure correction equation** (a Poisson equation) for $p'$:
$$
\nabla \cdot \left( \frac{\nabla p'}{A_P} \right) = -m^*
$$
where $A_P$ is the central coefficient of the momentum equation.
5. **Correct the pressure:** $p = p^* + \alpha_p \, p'$
6. **Correct the velocities:**
$$
u = u^* + d_u (p'_W - p'_P), \quad v = v^* + d_v (p'_S - p'_N)
$$
7. **Check convergence** on continuity residual and momentum residual. If not converged, set $p^* \leftarrow p$ and return to step 2.

## 2.3 SIMPLE vs. SIMPLEC vs. PISO — Key Differences

| Feature | SIMPLE | SIMPLEC | PISO |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Full Name** | Semi-Implicit Method for Pressure-Linked Equations | SIMPLE-Consistent | Pressure-Implicit with Splitting of Operators |
| **Corrector steps** | 1 | 1 | 2 (sweep + 2 corrector steps) |
| **Velocity correction coefficient** | $d_u = \dfrac{\Delta V}{A_P}$ | $d_u = \dfrac{\Delta V}{A_P - \sum A_{nb}}$ | Same as SIMPLE |
| **Under-relaxation for $p$** | Required ($\alpha_p \approx 0.3$) | Required but can be higher | Often $\alpha_p = 1.0$ |
| **Best suited for** | Steady-state problems | Steady-state, faster convergence | Transient (unsteady) problems |
| **Introduced by** | Patankar & Spalding (1972) | Van Doormaal & Raithby (1984) | Issa (1986) |
| **Number of sweeps per iteration** | 1 | 1 | 2–3 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** A frequently asked comparison question is *"Why is SIMPLEC faster than SIMPLE for steady problems?"* The answer lies in the modified velocity correction coefficient — SIMPLEC omits the $\sum A_{nb}$ term, making $d_u$ larger, which means the corrected velocity is closer to satisfying continuity, so fewer outer iterations are needed.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Meaning / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $a_P u_P = \sum a_{nb} u_{nb} + (p_{w} - p_{e}) A + b$ | Discretized momentum equation |
| 2 | $u^* = \dfrac{\sum a_{nb} u_{nb} + b}{a_P}$ + pressure gradient term | Tentative velocity from guessed pressure |
| 3 | $u = u^* + d_u (p'_W - p'_P)$ | Velocity correction (x-momentum) |
| 4 | $v = v^* + d_v (p'_S - p'_N)$ | Velocity correction (y-momentum) |
| 5 | $a_P p'_P = a_E p'_E + a_W p'_W + a_N p'_N + a_S p'_S + b$ | Pressure correction (Poisson) equation |
| 6 | $a_E = \rho d_u \Delta y$ | East coefficient in $p'$ equation |
| 7 | $b = (\rho u^*)_{w} \Delta y - (\rho u^*)_{e} \Delta y + (\rho v^*)_{s} \Delta x - (\rho v^*)_{n} \Delta x$ | Mass source for $p'$ equation |
| 8 | $p = p^* + \alpha_p \, p'$ | Pressure update (with under-relaxation) |
| 9 | $d_u = \dfrac{\Delta V}{a_P}$ | SIMPLE velocity correction coefficient |
| 10 | $d_u = \dfrac{\Delta V}{a_P - \sum a_{nb}}$ | SIMPLEC velocity correction coefficient |
| 11 | $R = \dfrac{\sum \vert m^* \vert}{\text{inlet mass flow rate}}$ | Normalized mass residual (convergence metric) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Pressure correction methods are the engine behind commercial CFD codes such as **ANSYS Fluent, OpenFOAM, and STAR-CCM+**. Every simulation of pipe flow, aerodynamics, HVAC, or blood flow in arteries that uses a *segregated solver* relies on the SIMPLE family of algorithms.

## 2.5 The Role of Under-Relaxation

Because the SIMPLE algorithm makes an approximation (neglecting the $\sum A_{nb} u'_{nb}$ term in deriving the velocity correction), pressure corrections can overshoot, causing divergence. **Under-relaxation** damps the update:

$$
p^{\text{new}} = p^* + \alpha_p \, p'
$$

Typical values: $\alpha_p = 0.1$ to $0.3$ for pressure, $\alpha_u = 0.5$ to $0.7$ for velocity. SIMPLEC allows larger $\alpha_p$ (up to 0.7) due to its consistent formulation.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Velocity Correction Equation

**Starting point:** The discretized momentum equation for cell $P$ (x-direction):
$$
a_P u_P = \sum a_{nb} u_{nb} + (p_w - p_e) A + b
$$

where $A = \Delta y$ is the cell face area (2D), and $a_{nb}$ are the neighbour coefficients.

**Step 1 — Define the tentative velocity.** If we use a guessed pressure $p^*$:
$$
a_P u_P^* = \sum a_{nb} u_{nb}^* + (p_w^* - p_e^*) A + b
$$

**Step 2 — Write the exact momentum equation with the true pressure $p$.** Subtract the two:
$$
a_P (u_P - u_P^*) = \sum a_{nb} (u_{nb} - u_{nb}^*) + \left[ (p_w - p_w^*) - (p_e - p_e^*) \right] A
$$

**Step 3 — Define the velocity correction** $u' = u - u^*$ and the pressure correction $p' = p - p^*$:
$$
a_P u'_P = \sum a_{nb} u'_{nb} + (p'_w - p'_e) A
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the SIMPLE approximation.** Neglect the $\sum a_{nb} u'_{nb}$ term (the contributions from neighbouring velocity corrections are assumed small):
$$
a_P u'_P = (p'_w - p'_e) A
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for the velocity correction:**
$$
u'_P = d_u (p'_w - p'_e), \quad \text{where} \quad d_u = \frac{A}{a_P} = \frac{\Delta y}{a_P}
$$

For 3D, multiply by $\Delta V$: $d_u = \dfrac{\Delta V}{a_P}$.

**Step 6 — Final corrected velocity:**
$$
\boxed{u_P = u_P^* + d_u (p'_w - p'_e)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The **SIMPLEC** version differs only in the definition of $d_u$. By retaining the $\sum a_{nb}$ correction on the left side:
> $$
> (a_P - \sum a_{nb}) u'_P = (p'_w - p'_e) A \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; d_u^{\text{SIMPLEC}} = \frac{A}{a_P - \sum a_{nb}}
> $$

## 3.2 Derivation of the Pressure Correction Equation (SIMPLE)

**Step 1 — Start from the corrected velocity** at face $e$ (using linear interpolation of nodal $p'$ values):
$$
u_e = u_e^* + d_u (p'_P - p'_E)
$$

**Step 2 — Write the discrete continuity equation at cell $P$:**
$$
(\rho u)_e \Delta y - (\rho u)_w \Delta y + (\rho v)_n \Delta x - (\rho v)_s \Delta x = 0
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the corrected velocities:**
$$
\left[ \rho u_e^* + \rho d_u (p'_P - p'_E) \right] \Delta y - \left[ \rho u_w^* + \rho d_u (p'_W - p'_P) \right] \Delta y + \ldots = 0
$$

**Step 4 — Group the pressure-correction terms and the residual terms.** The pressure-correction part becomes a five-point (or seven-point in 3D) Laplacian. The residual is the mass imbalance $b$:
$$
a_P p'_P = a_E p'_E + a_W p'_W + a_N p'_N + a_S p'_S + b
$$

where:
$$
a_E = \rho d_u \Delta y, \quad a_W = \rho d_u \Delta y, \quad a_N = \rho d_v \Delta x, \quad a_S = \rho d_v \Delta x
$$
$$
a_P = a_E + a_W + a_N + a_S
$$
$$
b = \rho u_w^* \Delta y - \rho u_e^* \Delta y + \rho v_s^* \Delta x - \rho v_n^* \Delta x
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why does this work physically?** The source term $b$ is exactly the **mass imbalance** that violates continuity. Solving the Poisson equation distributes $p'$ throughout the domain such that when used to correct velocities, the new $(u, v)$ field *simultaneously* satisfies both momentum and continuity — exactly what we want.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — SIMPLE Iteration Summary

| Stage | Unknown Solved | Equation Type | Boundary Inputs |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1. Initial guess | $p^*, u^*, v^*$ | (Previous iteration or initial) | Inlet/outlet $p$ or $u$ |
| 2. Momentum x | $u^*$ | Linear (5-point stencil) | $p^*$ at all faces |
| 3. Momentum y | $v^*$ | Linear (5-point stencil) | $p^*$ at all faces |
| 4. Compute $b$ | Mass residual at all cells | Cell-by-cell arithmetic | $u^*, v^*$ at faces |
| 5. Solve $p'$ | $p'$ at all cells | Poisson (5-point stencil) | $b$ as source |
| 6. Update $p$ | $p$ | $p = p^* + \alpha_p p'$ | — |
| 7. Update $u, v$ | $u, v$ | Explicit correction | $p'$ at faces |
| 8. Check | Residual norm | $\|m\| < \text{tol}$ | — |

## 3.4 PISO Algorithm — Additional Corrector Step

PISO adds a **second corrector step** to better satisfy continuity:

**First corrector:** Same as SIMPLE, gives $u^{**}, p^{**}$.

**Second corrector:** Solve a *second* pressure correction $p''$ using the updated $u^{**}$:
$$
u^{***} = u^{**} + d_u (p''_W - p''_E)
$$
$$
p = p^{**} + p''
$$

This second sweep removes the SIMPLE approximation error, making PISO ideal for **transient** problems where each time step is small and the initial guess is already close to the solution.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often confuse the *pressure correction* $p'$ (small adjustment) with the *corrected pressure* $p$ (final field). Remember: $p' = p - p^*$, and the corrected pressure is $p = p^* + \alpha_p p'$.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 SIMPLE Algorithm Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Initialize p star, u star, v star] --> B[Solve x-momentum: obtain u star]
    B --> C[Solve y-momentum: obtain v star]
    C --> D[Compute mass residual m star at each cell]
    D --> E[Assemble pressure correction Poisson equation]
    E --> F[Solve for p prime using TDMA or conjugate gradient]
    F --> G[Correct pressure: p = p star + alpha p times p prime]
    G --> H[Correct velocities: u = u star + d u times p prime gradient]
    H --> I{Mass residual norm less than tolerance}
    I -- No --> J[Set p star equal to p and return to step B]
    I -- Yes --> K[Converged: output p, u, v]
    K --> L[End]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Pressure-Velocity Coupling

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph InputBlock["Input Layer"]
        A1[Boundary conditions: inlet p, outlet p, walls]
        A2[Fluid properties: rho, mu]
        A3[Grid: dx, dy, cell volumes]
    end

    subgraph SolverBlock["SIMPLE Solver Core"]
        B1[Momentum x solver]
        B2[Momentum y solver]
        B3[Mass residual assembler]
        B4[Pressure correction solver TDMA]
        B5[Velocity and pressure updater]
    end

    subgraph ControlBlock["Convergence Controller"]
        C1[Residual monitor]
        C2[Under-relaxation factor alpha p]
        C3[Iteration counter]
    end

    subgraph OutputBlock["Output Layer"]
        D1[Final p field]
        D2[Final u and v fields]
        D3[Derived quantities: stream function, vorticity, forces]
    end

    A1 --> B1
    A2 --> B1
    A3 --> B1
    B1 --> B2
    B2 --> B3
    B3 --> B4
    B4 --> B5
    C2 --> B5
    B5 --> C1
    C1 -- not converged --> B1
    C1 -- converged --> D1
    C1 -- converged --> D2
    D1 --> D3
    D2 --> D3
    C3 --> C1
```

## 4.3 Comparison Block Diagram: SIMPLE vs SIMPLEC vs PISO

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Start of iteration with guessed p star] --> Mom[Solve momentum equations: get u star, v star]
    Mom --> Mass1[Compute mass residual m star]
    Mass1 --> P1[First pressure correction p prime]
    P1 --> Corr1[Correct p and u, v]

    Corr1 --> Choice{Algorithm selection}

    Choice -- SIMPLE --> Check1[Check convergence]
    Choice -- SIMPLEC --> Check2[Check convergence with consistent d u]
    Choice -- PISO --> Mass2[Recompute mass residual with updated u, v]
    Mass2 --> P2[Second pressure correction p double prime]
    P2 --> Corr2[Final correction of p and u, v]
    Corr2 --> Check3[Check convergence]

    Check1 -- Not converged --> Mom
    Check2 -- Not converged --> Mom
    Check3 -- Not converged --> Mom
    Check1 -- Converged --> Stop1[Stop: solution converged]
    Check2 -- Converged --> Stop2[Stop: solution converged]
    Check3 -- Converged --> Stop3[Stop: solution converged]
```

## 4.4 Stencil Diagram (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    N[N neighbour: p prime N] --> P[Central cell P: p prime P]
    S[S neighbour: p prime S] --> P
    E[E neighbour: p prime E] --> P
    W[W neighbour: p prime W] --> P
    P --> Source[Source term b equals negative of mass residual m star]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The above stencil is a 2D five-point Laplacian — the canonical form of the pressure correction equation. In 3D, two additional neighbours (Top T, Bottom B) are included, making it a seven-point stencil. The diagonal dominance of this matrix (sum of $a_{nb} \leq a_P$) guarantees convergence of iterative solvers such as TDMA, SIP, or AMG.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**What is the need for pressure correction in the solution of incompressible flow problems? Why can't pressure be obtained directly from the continuity equation?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

In incompressible flow, the continuity equation reduces to the divergence-free condition $\nabla \cdot \vec{V} = 0$, which contains no explicit pressure term. Since there is no independent transport equation for pressure, it cannot be solved directly. The pressure field must be inferred indirectly as the variable that **enforces mass conservation** when the velocity field is updated. The pressure correction method achieves this by deriving a Poisson equation for $p'$ whose source is the mass residual, so that once $p'$ is computed and used to correct velocities, the resulting velocity field simultaneously satisfies both momentum and continuity. (3 marks)

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**State the SIMPLE algorithm. List its main steps.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **SIMPLE (Semi-Implicit Method for Pressure-Linked Equations)** algorithm is an iterative procedure to decouple pressure and velocity in incompressible Navier–Stokes solutions. The main steps are:

1. Guess a pressure field $p^*$. (1 mark)
2. Solve the discretized momentum equations using $p^*$ to obtain tentative velocities $u^*, v^*$. (1 mark)
3. Solve the pressure correction Poisson equation for $p'$, correct pressure as $p = p^* + \alpha_p p'$, and correct velocities as $u = u^* + d_u (p'_W - p'_E)$. (1 mark)
4. Repeat until mass residual is below tolerance.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Internal Choice Pattern)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4]**

**(a)** Derive the pressure correction equation used in the SIMPLE algorithm starting from the discretized momentum equation. Clearly state the SIMPLE approximation. (7 marks)

**(b)** With a neat flowchart, explain the complete SIMPLE algorithm. Compare SIMPLE, SIMPLEC, and PISO algorithms in a tabular form. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 — Discretized momentum equation in x-direction for cell P:**
$$
a_P u_P = \sum a_{nb} u_{nb} + (p_w - p_e) A + b
$$
where $A = \Delta y$ in 2D. (1 mark)

**Step 2 — Define tentative velocity using guessed pressure $p^*$:**
$$
a_P u_P^* = \sum a_{nb} u_{nb}^* + (p_w^* - p_e^*) A + b
$$

**Step 3 — Subtract** the two equations and define corrections $u' = u - u^*$, $p' = p - p^*$:
$$
a_P u'_P = \sum a_{nb} u'_{nb} + (p'_w - p'_e) A
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the SIMPLE approximation:** Neglect $\sum a_{nb} u'_{nb}$ (neighbour velocity corrections assumed small). (1 mark)
$$
a_P u'_P = (p'_w - p'_e) A \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; u'_P = d_u (p'_w - p'_e), \quad d_u = \frac{A}{a_P}
$$

**Step 5 — Corrected velocity:**
$$
u_P = u_P^* + d_u (p'_w - p'_e)
$$

**Step 6 — Substituting into discrete continuity:**
$$
\rho(u_e \Delta y - u_w \Delta y) + \rho(v_n \Delta x - v_s \Delta x) = 0
$$

**Step 7 — After substitution and grouping**, obtain the pressure correction Poisson equation: (1 mark)
$$
a_P p'_P = a_E p'_E + a_W p'_W + a_N p'_N + a_S p'_S + b
$$
where $a_E = a_W = \rho d_u \Delta y$, $a_N = a_S = \rho d_v \Delta x$, and $b$ is the mass imbalance.

**Step 8 — Note on boundary conditions:** At pressure-specified boundaries, $p' = 0$; at velocity-specified boundaries, the normal $p'$ gradient is zero. (1 mark)

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

Refer to the **Mermaid flowchart in Section 4.1** of these notes for the SIMPLE algorithm flow. (3 marks)

**Comparison Table** (4 marks):

| Aspect | SIMPLE | SIMPLEC | PISO |
|---|---|---|---|
| Velocity coefficient $d_u$ | $A/a_P$ | $A/(a_P - \sum a_{nb})$ | $A/a_P$ |
| Corrector sweeps | 1 | 1 | 2 |
| Pressure under-relaxation | Required | Higher value allowed | Often $= 1$ |
| Best for | Steady | Steady, faster | Transient |
| Per-iteration cost | Low | Low | Higher (extra sweep) |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the SIMPLE approximation** in the derivation — you will lose 2 marks. Always state *"neglect $\sum a_{nb} u'_{nb}$"*.
> 2. **Mixing up $p$, $p^*$, and $p'$** — these are three different quantities. The examiner specifically watches for this.
> 3. **In the comparison table**, students often write "PISO is for steady state" — *this is wrong*; PISO is for **transient** problems.
> 4. **Missing boundary conditions** for the $p'$ equation — always mention $p' = 0$ on pressure inlets/outlets.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4]**

**(a)** Starting from the Navier–Stokes equations for steady incompressible 2D flow, show how the SIMPLE algorithm constructs a Poisson equation for the pressure correction $p'$. State the boundary conditions for $p'$. (7 marks)

**(b)** Explain the SIMPLEC algorithm. How does the modification in the velocity correction coefficient improve convergence compared to SIMPLE? Discuss the role of under-relaxation factors in both methods. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 — Governing equations (1 mark):**
$$
\frac{\partial (\rho u u)}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial (\rho v u)}{\partial y} = -\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} + \mu \nabla^2 u + S_u
$$
$$
\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} = 0
$$

**Step 2 — Discretize momentum** using finite volume method. The integral form over cell P gives:
$$
a_P u_P = \sum a_{nb} u_{nb} + (p_w - p_e) A + b
$$
(1 mark)

**Step 3 — Tentative velocity with guessed $p^*$**, subtract to get correction equation. (1 mark)

**Step 4 — Apply SIMPLE approximation** to get $u' = d_u (p'_w - p'_e)$. (1 mark)

**Step 5 — Substitute corrected velocities into discrete continuity**, leading to Poisson equation for $p'$: (2 marks)
$$
a_P p'_P = a_E p'_E + a_W p'_W + a_N p'_N + a_S p'_S + b
$$

**Step 6 — Boundary conditions for $p'$ (1 mark):**
- Pressure-specified boundaries: $p' = 0$
- Velocity-specified (inlet/outlet with normal velocity): $\partial p'/\partial n = 0$
- Walls: $\partial p'/\partial n = 0$ (no-penetration, no-slip)

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — SIMPLEC algorithm (2 marks):** SIMPLEC retains the $\sum a_{nb} u'_{nb}$ term on the LHS of the velocity correction equation. Solving gives:
$$
d_u^{\text{SIMPLEC}} = \frac{A}{a_P - \sum a_{nb}}
$$

**Step 2 — Why SIMPLEC converges faster (3 marks):** In SIMPLE, the neglected $\sum a_{nb} u'_{nb}$ makes the velocity correction $u'_P$ *smaller* than it should be. SIMPLEC's $d_u$ is *larger* by a factor of $\dfrac{a_P}{a_P - \sum a_{nb}}$, so the velocity correction is closer to the true value, and the **mass imbalance is reduced more rapidly per iteration**. This means fewer outer iterations are needed to achieve the same residual tolerance, at the cost of slightly more work per iteration. Net effect: SIMPLEC is more efficient for steady problems.

**Step 3 — Role of under-relaxation (2 marks):**
- **SIMPLE:** Requires low $\alpha_p$ (0.1–0.3) to prevent divergence due to the inconsistent approximation. Also need under-relaxation on velocities ($\alpha_u \approx 0.5$–$0.7$).
- **SIMPLEC:** Can use higher $\alpha_p$ (0.5–0.8) because the larger $d_u$ gives a more "consistent" correction. Sometimes under-relaxation on velocities can be relaxed as well.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Core Idea:** Pressure correction enforces mass conservation by deriving a Poisson equation for $p'$ whose source is the mass residual.
- **SIMPLE Algorithm Steps (Mnemonic: G-S-M-S-C-C):** Guess pressure, Solve momentum, compute Mass residual, Solve $p'$ Poisson, Correct pressure, Correct velocity.
- **The SIMPLE Approximation:** Neglect $\sum a_{nb} u'_{nb}$ in deriving velocity correction — *always state this in derivations*.
- **Velocity Correction (x):** $u = u^* + d_u (p'_W - p'_E)$ with $d_u = \Delta V / a_P$ for SIMPLE.
- **SIMPLEC Improvement:** $d_u = \Delta V / (a_P - \sum a_{nb})$ — faster steady-state convergence.
- **PISO Use Case:** Two corrector sweeps per iteration — **best for transient** problems.
- **Under-relaxation:** $\alpha_p = 0.1$–$0.3$ for SIMPLE; can be **higher** ($\approx 0.5$–$0.7$) for SIMPLEC; often **$1.0$** for PISO.
- **Boundary Conditions for $p'$:** Zero on pressure boundaries; zero gradient on velocity boundaries and walls.
- **Convergence Metric:** Normalized mass residual $R = \sum \vert m^* \vert / \dot{m}_{\text{inlet}}$; typically $< 10^{-3}$ for engineering accuracy, $< 10^{-6}$ for research.
- **Engineering Use:** Foundation of every commercial segregated solver — Fluent, OpenFOAM, CFX, STAR-CCM+.
- **What the examiner tests:** (1) Derivation of $p'$ equation (7 marks) (2) Comparison of SIMPLE family (4–7 marks) (3) Under-relaxation and stability discussion (3 marks).

> [!WARNING]
> **Last-Minute KTU Pitfall Reminders:**
> 1. **Don't confuse** "Semi-Implicit" with the time-integration sense — in SIMPLE, "semi-implicit" refers to *treating pressure implicitly in momentum but solving the $p'$ equation sequentially*.
> 2. **Don't write** "SIMPLE stands for Simple Implicit Method" — it is the **Semi-Implicit Method for Pressure-Linked Equations**.
> 3. **Always** include the *full form* of the pressure correction equation with all five (or seven) coefficients in derivations — abbreviated forms lose marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Solution Algorithm for Pressure-Velocity Coupling in Steady Flows: SIMPLE Algorithm to Solve Navier-Stokes Equations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET521 - COMPUTATIONAL FLUID DYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Solution Algorithm for Pressure-Velocity Coupling in Steady Flows: SIMPLE Algorithm to Solve Navier-Stokes Equations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal KTU-Syllabus Definition

The **SIMPLE Algorithm** (**Semi-Implicit Method for Pressure-Linked Equations**) is an iterative, segregated pressure-velocity coupling procedure introduced by **Patankar and Spalding (1972)** to solve the discretized, steady-state **Navier-Stokes equations** on a **staggered (or collocated) grid**. It decouples the velocity and pressure fields by guessing a pressure field, solving the momentum equations, and then enforcing mass conservation through a *pressure-correction equation* derived from the continuity equation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** SIMPLE is the *foundational* pressure-velocity coupling algorithm. Questions typically ask for the algorithm steps, the derivation of the pressure-correction equation, and the role of under-relaxation. Memorize the *exact sequence* of corrections.

### The Pressure–Velocity Coupling Problem

The Navier–Stokes equations for incompressible, steady, viscous flow are:

$$ \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V} \vec{V}) = -\nabla p + \mu \nabla^2 \vec{V} + \rho \vec{g} $$

$$ \nabla \cdot \vec{V} = 0 $$

The momentum equation contains **pressure** (a scalar, unknown), but there is **no independent transport equation for pressure** in incompressible flow. Pressure appears only as a gradient term. This creates a *chicken-and-egg* problem: to compute velocity we need pressure, but to compute pressure we need a velocity field that satisfies continuity.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Issue:** The continuity equation $\nabla \cdot \vec{V} = 0$ provides the *closure* (extra equation) needed to determine pressure. SIMPLE exploits this by converting continuity into a *pressure-correction* equation.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Balloon and Air Pump" Model

Imagine you are inflating a balloon using a hand pump, and you want to know *how much pressure* is inside at every moment.

- You cannot *measure* pressure directly — you only feel the wall push back.
- If you **guess** a pressure and push air with that guess, the balloon will either over-inflate (continuity violated) or under-inflate.
- So you **adjust** the pressure up or down (the *pressure correction* $p'$) until the volume of air actually entering matches what should enter (the *mass source* $b = 0$).
- The amount you adjust at each point depends on the imbalance at neighbouring points — this is the pressure-correction equation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy summary:** SIMPLE is like a thermostat on a leaking pipe. The *velocity field* is the water flow (output), the *pressure* is the thermostat setting (control input), and *continuity* is the requirement that no water magically appears or disappears. The algorithm tweaks the pressure setting until the flow rate satisfies conservation.

### Staggered Grid — The Geometric Foundation

To prevent the *checkerboard pressure field* problem (odd-even decoupling), SIMPLE in its classical form uses a **staggered grid**:

| Quantity stored at | Cell location |
|---|---|
| $p$, $u$, $v$ (in 2D) | Cell center P |
| $u$ (x-velocity) | Cell face in x-direction (e, w faces) |
| $v$ (y-velocity) | Cell face in y-direction (n, s faces) |

This ensures each velocity component is influenced by the pressure difference across the *exact* control volume face it controls, eliminating decoupled oscillations.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** In modern KTU questions, students often describe the *staggered grid* incorrectly as storing pressure at the face. **Pressure is stored at the cell center** in the staggered arrangement; only velocities are face-centered.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Discretized Momentum Equation (Building Block)

For the x-momentum equation at a generic node P on a staggered grid, the Finite Volume discretization yields:

$$ a_P u_P = \sum_{nb} a_{nb}\, u_{nb} + (p_P - p_E)\, A_e + S_u $$

where:
- $a_P$ = central coefficient at node P
- $a_{nb}$ = neighbour coefficients (E, W, N, S)
- $A_e$ = area of the east cell face through which pressure pushes
- $S_u$ = explicit source term
- $u_{nb}$ = neighbour x-velocities

The crucial observation is that the **pressure gradient appears linearly** — this is what allows the velocity correction to be expressed analytically.

### 2.2 The Core Idea: Guess → Solve → Correct

SIMPLE uses a *predictor–corrector* structure:

1. **Predictor:** Guess a pressure field $p^*$ and solve momentum equations to obtain $u^*, v^*$.
2. **Corrector:** The guessed velocities generally do **not** satisfy continuity. Compute a *pressure correction* $p'$ from a Poisson-like equation that *enforces* continuity. Then correct both $p$ and $\vec{V}$.

### 2.3 Velocity-Correction Formulas

The *difference* between the exact and guessed velocity fields (the velocity correction $u', v'$) is **linearly related** to the pressure correction:

$$ u'_P = -\frac{A_e}{a_P}\,(p'_P - p'_E) \quad \text{or} \quad u'_e = -d_e (p'_P - p'_E) $$

$$ v'_P = -\frac{A_n}{a_P}\,(p'_P - p'_N) \quad \text{or} \quad v'_n = -d_n (p'_P - p'_N) $$

where $d_e = A_e / a_e$ and $d_n = A_n / a_n$ are geometric-coefficient ratios. The corrected velocity is then:

$$ u_P = u^*_P + u'_P = u^*_P - d_e (p'_P - p'_E) $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why "Semi-Implicit"?** The pressure correction $p'$ appears *implicitly* on the right-hand side of neighbouring equations (through $p'_E, p'_W, p'_N, p'_S$), but the velocity correction at the *current* node $P$ uses the *explicit* local values. Hence the algorithm is *semi-implicit* in time and *fully implicit* in space for pressure.

### 2.4 The Pressure-Correction Equation (Continuity in Disguise)

Starting from the discrete continuity equation $\sum_{\text{faces}} (\rho u A)_f = 0$ and substituting the velocity-correction formulas, one obtains:

$$ a_P p'_P = a_E p'_E + a_W p'_W + a_N p'_N + a_S p'_S + b $$

with the **mass source** (continuity imbalance):

$$ b = \sum_f (\rho u^* A)_f = (\rho u^* A)_e - (\rho u^* A)_w + (\rho v^* A)_n - (\rho v^* A)_s $$

and neighbour coefficients:

$$ a_E = \rho\, d_e\, A_e, \quad a_W = \rho\, d_w\, A_w, \quad a_N = \rho\, d_n\, A_n, \quad a_S = \rho\, d_s\, A_s $$

$$ a_P = a_E + a_W + a_N + a_S $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical meaning of $b$:** $b$ is the *net mass imbalance* of the guessed velocity field $u^*, v^*$. The pressure-correction equation **redistributes $p'$** across the domain so that this imbalance is driven to zero everywhere.

### 2.5 Under-Relaxation in SIMPLE

SIMPLE tends to *over-correct* pressure in early iterations, causing divergence. **Under-relaxation** is applied to both velocity and pressure:

$$ u_P^{\text{new}} = u_P^{\text{old}} + \alpha_u\, u'_P \quad \text{(velocity under-relaxation)} $$

$$ p^{\text{new}} = p^* + \alpha_p\, p' \quad \text{(pressure under-relaxation)} $$

Typical values: $\alpha_p \approx 0.3$ (very important in SIMPLE), $\alpha_u \approx 0.7$.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Students often write $p = p^* + p'$ (i.e., $\alpha_p = 1$). This is **wrong for SIMPLE** — the pressure correction is *always* under-relaxed to maintain stability. (In SIMPLEC, $\alpha_p$ can be 1.0, but **not** in classic SIMPLE.)

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Term | Formula | Meaning / Units |
|---|---|---|
| Discretized x-momentum | $a_P u_P = \sum a_{nb} u_{nb} + (p_P - p_E) A_e + S_u$ | Force balance at node P |
| Velocity correction (x) | $u'_P = -d_e (p'_P - p'_E)$ | Proportional to local $\Delta p'$ |
| Velocity correction (y) | $v'_P = -d_n (p'_P - p'_N)$ | Same form in y-direction |
| Pressure-correction eqn. | $a_P p'_P = \sum a_{nb} p'_{nb} + b$ | Poisson-type linear system |
| Mass source | $b = \sum_f (\rho u^* A)_f$ | Continuity residual of $u^*$ |
| Neighbour coeff. | $a_E = \rho\, d_e\, A_e$ | East coefficient |
| Pressure update | $p = p^* + \alpha_p p'$ | Under-relaxed (typically $\alpha_p = 0.3$) |
| Velocity update | $u = u^* + u'$ (after momentum re-solve) | Direct, no under-relaxation on $u'$ |
| Convergence check | $\vert b \vert \rightarrow 0$ AND $\vert p' \vert \rightarrow 0$ | Iterate until both are small |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** SIMPLE (and its descendants SIMPLEC, PISO) is the *backbone* of commercial CFD codes such as **ANSYS Fluent**, **OpenFOAM**, and **STAR-CD**. Understanding SIMPLE is equivalent to understanding how nearly every industrial CFD simulation iteratively solves for fluid flow.

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Aerodynamics:** Airfoil and aircraft flow simulation
- **Hydraulics:** Pipe networks, dam spillways, open-channel flow
- **HVAC:** Ventilation and indoor air distribution
- **Process Engineering:** Mixing tanks, chemical reactors
- **Biomedical:** Blood flow through arteries (aneurysm analysis)
- **Turbomachinery:** Pumps, turbines, compressors

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Takeaway:** SIMPLE is the *de facto* standard for steady, incompressible flow. Master it, and you master the foundation of modern CFD.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Velocity-Correction Formula

We start from the **discretized x-momentum equation** at node P on a staggered grid:

$$ a_P u_P = \sum_{nb} a_{nb} u_{nb} + (p_P - p_E) A_e + S_u $$

> This equation is a *linear algebraic* statement: the velocity $u_P$ at the current iteration depends linearly on neighbour velocities and the local pressure drop.

**Step 1:** Introduce guessed values $u^*_P, p^*_P$ that satisfy the same equation approximately:

$$ a_P u^*_P = \sum_{nb} a_{nb} u^*_{nb} + (p^*_P - p^*_E) A_e + S_u $$

**Step 2:** Subtract the guessed equation from the exact equation. The non-linear neighbour contributions (e.g., $\sum a_{nb} u_{nb}$) are *dropped* (the SIMPLE approximation — neighbour corrections are neglected):

$$ a_P (u_P - u^*_P) \approx (p_P - p^*_P) A_e - (p_E - p^*_E) A_e $$

> The "SIMPLE approximation" neglects $\sum a_{nb}(u_{nb} - u^*_{nb})$. This is what makes the algorithm *segregated* and computationally cheap.

**Step 3:** Define the *velocity correction* and *pressure correction*:

$$ u'_P \equiv u_P - u^*_P, \quad p' \equiv p - p^* $$

**Step 4:** Substitute into the difference equation:

$$ a_P u'_P = (p'_P - p'_E) A_e $$

**Step 5:** Solve for $u'_P$:

$$ u'_P = \frac{A_e}{a_P}\,(p'_P - p'_E) $$

**Step 6:** Define the geometric factor $d_e = A_e / a_P$:

$$ \boxed{\,u'_P = -d_e\,(p'_P - p'_E)\,} $$

> The minus sign is sometimes absorbed into the definition of $p'$ (i.e., $p' \equiv p^* - p$); the *form* of the equation is what matters. The corrected velocity is:

$$ u_P = u^*_P + u'_P = u^*_P - d_e (p'_P - p'_E) $$

### 3.2 Derivation of the Pressure-Correction Equation from Continuity

We start from the **discrete continuity equation** integrated over the control volume around P:

$$ (\rho u A)_e - (\rho u A)_w + (\rho v A)_n - (\rho v A)_s = 0 $$

**Step 1:** Insert the corrected velocity expressions $u = u^* + u'$, $v = v^* + v'$:

$$ \big[\rho (u^*_e + u'_e) A_e\big] - \big[\rho (u^*_w + u'_w) A_w\big] + \big[\rho (v^*_n + v'_n) A_n\big] - \big[\rho (v^*_s + v'_s) A_s\big] = 0 $$

**Step 2:** Group the starred (known) and primed (unknown) terms:

$$ \underbrace{\big[\rho u^*_e A_e - \rho u^*_w A_w + \rho v^*_n A_n - \rho v^*_s A_s\big]}_{=\,b\,(\text{mass source})} + \big[\rho u'_e A_e - \rho u'_w A_w + \rho v'_n A_n - \rho v'_s A_s\big] = 0 $$

**Step 3:** Use the velocity-correction formulas. For the east face:

$$ u'_e = -d_e (p'_P - p'_E) $$

Similarly for the other faces.

**Step 4:** Substitute into the bracketed term:

$$ \rho u'_e A_e = -\rho\, d_e\, A_e\,(p'_P - p'_E) = -a_E\,(p'_P - p'_E) $$

where $a_E = \rho\, d_e\, A_e$.

> Analogously: $\rho u'_w A_w = a_W (p'_P - p'_W)$, $\rho v'_n A_n = a_N (p'_P - p'_N)$, $\rho v'_s A_s = a_S (p'_P - p'_S)$.

**Step 5:** Insert into continuity:

$$ b - a_E(p'_P - p'_E) - a_W(p'_P - p'_W) - a_N(p'_P - p'_N) - a_S(p'_P - p'_S) = 0 $$

**Step 6:** Expand and collect $p'_P$ on the left:

$$ (a_E + a_W + a_N + a_S)\, p'_P = a_E p'_E + a_W p'_W + a_N p'_N + a_S p'_S + b $$

**Step 7:** Define $a_P = a_E + a_W + a_N + a_S$:

$$ \boxed{\,a_P\, p'_P = a_E p'_E + a_W p'_W + a_N p'_N + a_S p'_S + b\,} $$

> This is the **pressure-correction equation** — a linear, Poisson-type equation for $p'$. It is solved iteratively (e.g., TDMA, Gauss-Seidel, or algebraic multigrid) until $b \to 0$ everywhere.

### 3.3 Convergence Criteria

The SIMPLE outer iteration is judged converged when:

$$ \vert p' \vert_{\max} \le \epsilon_p, \quad \vert b \vert_{\max} \le \epsilon_{\text{mass}}, \quad \text{and momentum residuals} \le \epsilon_{\text{mom}} $$

Typical industrial tolerances: $\epsilon = 10^{-3}$ to $10^{-6}$ depending on the application.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 SIMPLE Algorithm Flowchart (Mermaid)

> **Mermaid Safety Note:** All node IDs are prefixed with letters and contain only alphanumeric characters. Labels are plain uppercase text without any markdown or HTML formatting.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[START CFD SIMULATION] --> A1[READ GRID AND PHYSICAL PROPERTIES]
    A1 --> A2[INITIALISE PRESSURE FIELD P STAR]
    A2 --> B1[SOLVE U MOMENTUM EQN USING P STAR]
    A2 --> B2[SOLVE V MOMENTUM EQN USING P STAR]
    B1 --> C1[OBTAIN U STAR AND V STAR]
    B2 --> C1
    C1 --> C2[COMPUTE MASS SOURCE B AT EACH CV]
    C2 --> C3[SOLVE PRESSURE CORRECTION EQN FOR P PRIME]
    C3 --> D1[CORRECT PRESSURE P EQUALS P STAR PLUS ALPHA P TIMES P PRIME]
    D1 --> D2[CORRECT VELOCITY U EQUALS U STAR PLUS U PRIME]
    D1 --> D3[CORRECT VELOCITY V EQUALS V STAR PLUS V PRIME]
    D2 --> E1[CHECK CONVERGENCE B MAX LESS THAN EPS AND P PRIME MAX LESS THAN EPS]
    D3 --> E1
    E1 --> E2{CONVERGED}
    E2 -->|NO| B1
    E2 -->|YES| F1[OUTPUT FINAL U V P FIELDS]
    F1 --> F2[END]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Pressure–Velocity Coupling

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    G1[GUESSED PRESSURE P STAR] --> H1[X MOMENTUM SOLVER]
    G1 --> H2[Y MOMENTUM SOLVER]
    H1 --> H3[VELOCITY FIELD U STAR V STAR]
    H2 --> H3
    H3 --> I1[CONTINUITY RESIDUAL B]
    I1 --> I2[PRESSURE CORRECTION SOLVER]
    I2 --> I3[CORRECTION FIELD P PRIME]
    I3 --> J1[PRESSURE CORRECTOR]
    I3 --> J2[VELOCITY CORRECTOR]
    J1 --> K1[UPDATED P]
    J2 --> K2[UPDATED U V]
    K1 --> H1
    K2 --> H1
    K1 --> H2
    K2 --> H2
```

### 4.3 Staggered Grid Control Volume Schematic (Mermaid Block Representation)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph L1[STAGGERED GRID 2D]
        L11[CELL P] --> L12[EAST FACE U E VELOCITY]
        L11 --> L13[WEST FACE U W VELOCITY]
        L11 --> L14[NORTH FACE V N VELOCITY]
        L11 --> L15[SOUTH FACE V S VELOCITY]
    end
    L12 --> M1[U E USED IN X MOMENTUM FOR P]
    L13 --> M2[U W USED IN X MOMENTUM FOR P]
    L14 --> M3[V N USED IN Y MOMENTUM FOR P]
    L15 --> M4[V S USED IN Y MOMENTUM FOR P]
    M1 --> N1[PRESSURE P AT CELL CENTER DRIVES NEIGHBOUR VELOCITIES]
    M2 --> N1
    M3 --> N1
    M4 --> N1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams:** Each block represents a *function* in the algorithm, not a physical entity. Arrows show data flow. The two inner loops (momentum → pressure-correction → correction) form the *outer iteration* of SIMPLE. This pattern is identical in all modern CFD codes.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*What is the SIMPLE algorithm? Why is it called "Semi-Implicit"?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

SIMPLE stands for **Semi-Implicit Method for Pressure-Linked Equations**, developed by **Patankar and Spalding (1972)**. It is an iterative procedure to solve the coupled velocity-pressure fields of the steady, incompressible Navier-Stokes equations.

It is called *semi-implicit* because:
- The **pressure correction** $p'$ appears *implicitly* in the linear system solved (each equation involves $p'_P$ and $p'_{nb}$ simultaneously) — hence *implicit*.
- However, the **velocity correction at the current node** uses only the *local* pressure corrections, not the simultaneous update of neighbours — hence *semi*.

This naming reflects the hybrid nature of the algorithm: implicit in pressure, explicit in velocity correction.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*What is the physical significance of the mass source term $b$ in the SIMPLE algorithm?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The mass source term $b = \sum_f (\rho u^* A)_f$ is the **net mass flux imbalance** of the *guessed* velocity field $(u^*, v^*)$ over the control volume of cell P. Physically:

- $b > 0$ : more mass is leaving the cell than entering (over-outflow) — pressure must be **raised** to slow the outflow.
- $b < 0$ : more mass is entering than leaving (over-inflow) — pressure must be **lowered** to slow the inflow.
- $b = 0$ : continuity is satisfied at P.

The pressure-correction equation redistributes $p'$ across the domain such that $b \to 0$ at every cell, i.e., the corrected velocity field becomes divergence-free.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU ESE Pattern]** Each Part B question has sub-parts (a) and (b), each worth 7 marks. The student answers **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Derive the pressure-correction equation used in the SIMPLE algorithm starting from the discrete continuity equation. Clearly state the assumptions made. (7 marks)

**(b)** List and explain the steps of the SIMPLE algorithm in a flowchart sequence. Discuss the role of under-relaxation in pressure. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation of the Pressure-Correction Equation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Discrete continuity equation on the control volume around P:**

$$ (\rho u A)_e - (\rho u A)_w + (\rho v A)_n - (\rho v A)_s = 0 $$

**Step 2 — Substitute corrected velocities $u = u^* + u'$ and $v = v^* + v'$:**

$$ b + \rho u'_e A_e - \rho u'_w A_w + \rho v'_n A_n - \rho v'_s A_s = 0 $$

where $b = \rho u^*_e A_e - \rho u^*_w A_w + \rho v^*_n A_n - \rho v^*_s A_s$ is the **mass source** (continuity residual).

**Step 3 — Apply the velocity-correction formulas** (derived from subtracting guessed vs exact momentum equations, neglecting neighbour corrections):

$$ u'_e = -d_e(p'_P - p'_E), \quad u'_w = -d_w(p'_P - p'_W), \quad v'_n = -d_n(p'_P - p'_N), \quad v'_s = -d_s(p'_P - p'_S) $$

**Step 4 — Substitute into the continuity balance:**

$$ b - a_E(p'_P - p'_E) - a_W(p'_P - p'_W) - a_N(p'_P - p'_N) - a_S(p'_P - p'_S) = 0 $$

**Step 5 — Collect terms in $p'_P$:**

$$ (a_E + a_W + a_N + a_S) p'_P = a_E p'_E + a_W p'_W + a_N p'_N + a_S p'_S + b $$

**Step 6 — Final form** with $a_P = a_E + a_W + a_N + a_S$:

$$ \boxed{\,a_P p'_P = \sum_{nb} a_{nb} p'_{nb} + b\,} $$

**Assumptions stated (2 marks):**
1. **SIMPLE approximation:** Neighbour velocity corrections $\sum a_{nb} u'_{nb}$ are neglected.
2. **Quasi-steady** approach: $p^*$ is treated as a static guess, not a time-step.
3. **Linearization:** Source terms treated explicitly.

**(b) SIMPLE Algorithm Steps and Under-Relaxation (7 marks):**

| Step | Action |
|---|---|
| 1 | Initialise the field: guess $p^*$ everywhere (e.g., from a previous run or hydrostatic). |
| 2 | Solve the x-momentum equation using $p^*$ to obtain $u^*$. |
| 3 | Solve the y-momentum equation using $p^*$ to obtain $v^*$. |
| 4 | Compute the mass source $b$ at every cell. |
| 5 | Solve the pressure-correction equation $a_P p'_P = \sum a_{nb} p'_{nb} + b$ for $p'$. |
| 6 | Correct pressure: $p = p^* + \alpha_p\, p'$ where $\alpha_p \approx 0.3$. |
| 7 | Correct velocities: $u = u^* - d_e (p'_P - p'_E)$, $v = v^* - d_n (p'_P - p'_N)$. |
| 8 | Check convergence: $\vert b \vert_{\max} \le \epsilon$ and $\vert p' \vert_{\max} \le \epsilon$. |
| 9 | If not converged, set $p^* = p$ and return to step 2. |

**Role of Under-Relaxation in Pressure (3 marks):**

The pressure-correction equation is derived using the **SIMPLE approximation** (neglecting neighbour velocity corrections). This causes $p'$ to be *overestimated* in early iterations, leading to divergence. To prevent this:

- The pressure is *under-relaxed*: $p^{\text{new}} = p^* + \alpha_p\, p'$ with $0 < \alpha_p < 1$ (typically 0.3).
- The factor $\alpha_p$ damps the correction, allowing *gradual* convergence.
- Velocities are also under-relaxed in the *momentum* solver (typically $\alpha_u = 0.7$), but the pressure under-relaxation is the *critical* stabiliser in SIMPLE.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Question A:**
> - **Forgetting the SIMPLE approximation** in the derivation costs **1–2 marks**.
> - **Writing $p = p^* + p'$** (i.e., $\alpha_p = 1$) will be marked **wrong**; SIMPLE *always* under-relaxes pressure.
> - **Skipping the convergence check** in the algorithm loses **1 mark** — KTU requires explicit convergence criterion.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — May 2023, Supplementary 2024]

**(a)** Explain the concept of pressure-velocity coupling in the Navier-Stokes equations. Why is the staggered grid used in the classical SIMPLE algorithm? (7 marks)

**(b)** Starting from the discretized x-momentum equation, derive the velocity-correction formula $u'_P = -d_e (p'_P - p'_E)$ for the SIMPLE algorithm. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Pressure-Velocity Coupling and Staggered Grid (7 marks):**

**The Coupling Problem (4 marks):**
The steady, incompressible Navier-Stokes equations are:

$$ \rho (\vec{V} \cdot \nabla)\vec{V} = -\nabla p + \mu \nabla^2 \vec{V} + \rho \vec{g} \quad \text{(momentum)} $$

$$ \nabla \cdot \vec{V} = 0 \quad \text{(continuity)} $$

The pressure $p$ appears only as a gradient in momentum — there is **no transport equation for $p$** in incompressible flow. Furthermore, the velocity field must be **divergence-free** (continuity). Thus:
- We need $p$ to compute $\vec{V}$, but
- We need a divergence-free $\vec{V}$ to determine $p$.

This is the **pressure-velocity coupling problem**. SIMPLE resolves it by *guessing* $p$, computing $\vec{V}$, then *correcting* $p$ so that the corrected $\vec{V}$ satisfies continuity.

**Why Staggered Grid (3 marks):**
On a *collocated* grid (all variables at cell centres), the discretized pressure gradient couples to the discretized divergence through the *same* interpolation weights, producing the **checkerboard pressure field** (odd-even decoupling). On a *staggered* grid:
- Pressure $p$ is stored at cell centres.
- $u$ is stored at east/west cell faces.
- $v$ is stored at north/south cell faces.

This breaks the symmetry: the pressure difference $(p_E - p_P)$ now acts *directly* on the east face of the control volume for $u$, eliminating the decoupling. The staggered arrangement is the **geometric foundation** of the original SIMPLE algorithm.

**(b) Derivation of the Velocity-Correction Formula (7 marks):**

**Step 1:** Write the discretized x-momentum equation at node P:

$$ a_P u_P = \sum_{nb} a_{nb} u_{nb} + (p_P - p_E) A_e + S_u \quad \text{...(i)} $$

**Step 2:** Introduce the guessed solution $(u^*, p^*)$ satisfying (i) approximately:

$$ a_P u^*_P = \sum_{nb} a_{nb} u^*_{nb} + (p^*_P - p^*_E) A_e + S_u \quad \text{...(ii)} $$

**Step 3:** Subtract (ii) from (i):

$$ a_P (u_P - u^*_P) = \sum_{nb} a_{nb}(u_{nb} - u^*_{nb}) + (p_P - p^*_P) A_e - (p_E - p^*_E) A_e $$

**Step 4 — SIMPLE approximation:** Neglect $\sum a_{nb}(u_{nb} - u^*_{nb})$ (the neighbour velocity corrections):

$$ a_P (u_P - u^*_P) \approx (p'_P - p'_E) A_e $$

**Step 5:** Define velocity correction $u'_P \equiv u_P - u^*_P$ and pressure correction $p' \equiv p - p^*$:

$$ a_P u'_P = (p'_P - p'_E) A_e $$

**Step 6:** Solve and define $d_e = A_e / a_P$:

$$ \boxed{\,u'_P = -d_e (p'_P - p'_E)\,} $$

The corrected velocity is then $u_P = u^*_P + u'_P = u^*_P - d_e (p'_P - p'_E)$.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Question B:**
> - **Failing to state the SIMPLE approximation explicitly** loses **1 mark** (it is the *key* mathematical assumption).
> - **Confusing $p'$ direction**: students often write $u'_P = +d_e (p'_P - p'_E)$ — the sign depends on the sign convention for $p'$ and on whether $d_e$ absorbs the negative sign. Stick to one consistent convention.
> - **Mixing up the locations**: $p'_P$ and $p'_E$ are *pressure corrections at the cell centres* of P and E, not at the cell faces.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Bullet List — KTU 2024 Module 4**

- **SIMPLE** = **S**emi-**I**mplicit **M**ethod for **P**ressure-**L**inked **E**quations; introduced by **Patankar & Spalding (1972)**.
- Solves the **steady, incompressible Navier–Stokes** equations iteratively.
- Uses a **staggered grid** (in its classical form) to avoid the checkerboard pressure field.
- **Outer loop** of SIMPLE: guess $p^*$ → solve momentum → solve pressure-correction → correct $p$ and $\vec{V}$ → check convergence.
- **Key derived equations:**
  - Velocity correction: $u'_P = -d_e (p'_P - p'_E)$
  - Pressure-correction: $a_P p'_P = \sum a_{nb} p'_{nb} + b$
  - Mass source: $b = \sum_f (\rho u^* A)_f$
- **SIMPLE approximation:** neighbour velocity corrections are *neglected* in the pressure-correction derivation.
- **Under-relaxation** of pressure is **mandatory** in SIMPLE: $p = p^* + \alpha_p p'$ with $\alpha_p \approx 0.3$.
- **Convergence** is achieved when **both** the mass source $b$ and the pressure correction $p'$ drop below a tolerance $\epsilon$ at every cell.
- **Variants:** SIMPLEC (allows $\alpha_p \approx 1$, faster convergence), PISO (two additional corrector steps, suitable for transient flows).
- **Industrial relevance:** SIMPLE is the *algorithmic engine* of ANSYS Fluent, OpenFOAM, and most CFD codes.
- **Examining pattern:** KTU questions usually ask for **(i)** algorithm steps in order, **(ii)** the pressure-correction derivation, and **(iii)** the role of under-relaxation. Be ready to draw the **flowchart** and the **staggered grid**.

> **Final KTU Tip:** When answering in the exam, *always* state the **SIMPLE approximation** explicitly in any derivation. Examiners look for it. Also remember to mention the **convergence criterion** — many students forget this and lose 1–2 marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Computer Graphics Techniques to Present CFD Results
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET521 - COMPUTATIONAL FLUID DYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Computer Graphics Techniques to Present CFD Results

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Computer Graphics Techniques for CFD Results** refer to the systematic set of algorithms, rendering methods, and visualization pipelines used to transform raw numerical data (pressure, velocity, temperature, turbulence quantities, species concentration) generated by a CFD solver into visually interpretable graphical representations such as contour plots, isosurfaces, streamlines, vector glyphs, and volume renderings.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme (PEMET521) context, these techniques are classified into three primary families:

| Family | Data Type | Output |
|---|---|---|
| **Scalar Field Visualization** | Pressure, temperature, Mach number | Contours, isosurfaces, color maps |
| **Vector Field Visualization** | Velocity, vorticity | Streamlines, arrows, LIC |
| **Tensor/Field Analysis** | Reynolds stresses, gradients | Ellipsoids, glyphs, animations |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The module emphasizes that the *post-processing stage* is the bridge between *numerical computation* and *engineering insight*. A correct solution with poor visualization may lead to wrong conclusions — making graphics techniques a **non-trivial** part of the simulation workflow.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a doctor performing an MRI scan. The raw machine outputs a 3D grid of numbers representing tissue density. The radiologist never looks at the raw grid — instead, the workstation converts it into **colored cross-sectional images** (cool blues for low density, hot reds for high density) so the doctor can immediately spot tumors.

CFD post-processing works identically. The solver outputs millions of numbers stored on a discretized grid. Computer graphics techniques act as the **"radiologist's workstation"**, painting those numbers in a way the human eye and brain can decode in seconds.

- **Raw data** = the MRI grid
- **Contour plots / isosurfaces** = the colored scan image
- **Streamlines** = tracing blood flow through arteries

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition Box:**
> *Visualization = Mapping (Spatial coordinates + Field values) → 2D/3D pixel color & geometry such that physical meaning is preserved and intuitively communicated.*

## 1.3 Standard Metrics in CFD Visualization

- **Color Resolution:** typically **8-bit per channel (256 shades)** or **24-bit true color (16.7M colors)**.
- **Dynamic Range:** modern colormaps like **Viridis** are *perceptually uniform* across a range of **≥ 4 orders of magnitude**.
- **Frame Rate:** engineering animations are rendered at **24–60 fps** for smooth perception.
- **Display Latency:** for interactive exploration, the system should respond within **< 100 ms**.

## 1.4 The CFD Post-Processing Pipeline

The KTU 2024 module lists the canonical pipeline as:

**Raw Solver Data → Data Conditioning (interpolation, decimation) → Mapping (color/geometry) → Rendering (lighting, projection) → Display (screen/image)**

Each stage has its own algorithmic family that we will study in this note.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of CFD Graphics

### 2.1.1 Scalar Field Visualization
Scalar visualization is the **most common** CFD output because most engineering quantities (pressure coefficient $C_p$, temperature $T$, Mach number $M$) are scalars at every point.

**Why scalars dominate:** They are easier to encode visually — a single value per point maps to a single color or opacity.

**How it works:**
- A **colormap function** $C: \mathbb{R} \to [0,1]^3$ converts a scalar value $f$ to an RGB triple.
- For discrete grids, **interpolation** is required to obtain smooth color transitions.
- For 3D scalar fields, **isosurface extraction** (marching cubes) builds polygonal surfaces at $f = f_0$.

### 2.1.2 Vector Field Visualization
Vector fields (velocity $\vec{V}$, vorticity $\vec{\omega}$) have **direction AND magnitude**, demanding richer visual encoding.

**How it works:**
- **Streamlines** integrate the field tangentially: $\frac{d\vec{x}}{ds} = \vec{V}(\vec{x})$.
- **Arrow/Glyph plots** place oriented 2D/3D icons at sample points.
- **Line Integral Convolution (LIC)** textures a noise pattern along flow direction.
- **Pathlines** and **streaklines** add the time dimension.

### 2.1.3 Higher-Order Field Visualization
- **Tensor glyphs:** ellipsoids showing principal stress directions.
- **Particle advection animations:** reveal unsteady flow structure.
- **Vortex core extraction** (e.g., $\lambda_2$ criterion, Q-criterion).

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical:** Pipe symbols `|` are replaced with `\vert` to keep the markdown table valid.

| # | Concept | Equation | Symbol Meaning | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Streamline ODE | $\dfrac{d\vec{x}}{ds} = \vec{V}(\vec{x})$ | $\vec{x}$ = position, $s$ = arc length, $\vec{V}$ = velocity | Flow topology |
| 2 | Pathline ODE | $\dfrac{d\vec{x}}{dt} = \vec{V}(\vec{x}, t)$ | $t$ = physical time | Unsteady particle tracks |
| 3 | Streakline | $\vec{x}(t) = \vec{x}_0 + \int_{t_0}^{t} \vec{V}(\vec{x}(\tau), \tau)\, d\tau$ | Massless tracer integration | Dye-injection analogues |
| 4 | Q-Criterion | $Q = \tfrac{1}{2}\left(\vert\vert\Omega\vert\vert^2 - \vert\vert S \vert\vert^2\right)$ | $\Omega$ = vorticity tensor, $S$ = strain-rate tensor | Vortex identification |
| 5 | $\lambda_2$ Criterion | $\lambda_2(\vec{S}^2 + \vec{\Omega}^2) < 0$ | Eigenvalue of symmetric tensor | Vortex cores |
| 6 | Marching Cubes Iso-value | $f(x,y,z) = f_0$ | Constant scalar surface | Isosurface generation |
| 7 | Gouraud Shading | $I = I_a K_a + \displaystyle\sum_i I_i\left[K_d (\vec{N}\cdot\vec{L}_i) + K_s (\vec{R}_i\cdot\vec{V})^n\right]$ | $K_a,K_d,K_s$ = ambient/diffuse/specular coeff.; $n$ = shininess | Smooth surface lighting |
| 8 | Phong Reflection | $I = I_a K_a + \displaystyle\sum_i I_i\left[K_d (\vec{N}\cdot\vec{L}_i) + K_s (\vec{R}_i\cdot\vec{V})^n\right]$ | Same as Gouraud but per-pixel | Highlight reflection |
| 9 | Linear Color Interp. | $C(f) = C_{\min} + \dfrac{f - f_{\min}}{f_{\max} - f_{\min}}\,(C_{\max} - C_{\min})$ | Linear colormap | Direct color scaling |
| 10 | Log Color Map | $C(f) = C_{\min} + \dfrac{\log_{10}(f) - \log_{10}(f_{\min})}{\log_{10}(f_{\max}) - \log_{10}(f_{\min})}\,(C_{\max} - C_{\min})$ | Log-scaled | Wide-range data |
| 11 | LIC Convolution | $I(p) = \displaystyle\int_{0}^{L} k(s)\, N\!\left(p - s\,\vec{t}(p)\right)\, ds$ | $N$ = noise texture, $\vec{t}$ = unit tangent | Texture-based flow |
| 12 | View Transform | $\vec{p}_{\text{scr}} = M_{\text{proj}}\, M_{\text{view}}\, M_{\text{model}}\, \vec{p}_{\text{world}}$ | Composite 4x4 matrix | 3D-to-2D projection |
| 13 | Perceptual Uniformity | $\Delta E_{ab}^* = \sqrt{(L_2^*-L_1^*)^2 + (a_2^*-a_1^*)^2 + (b_2^*-b_1^*)^2}$ | CIE-Lab color difference | Colormap quality |
| 14 | Mach Number | $M = \dfrac{\vert\vec{V}\vert}{\sqrt{\gamma R T}}$ | Speed of sound $= \sqrt{\gamma R T}$ | Compressible display |
| 15 | Pressure Coefficient | $C_p = \dfrac{p - p_\infty}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho_\infty V_\infty^2}$ | Non-dimensional pressure | Aerodynamic plots |

## 2.3 Step-by-Step Logic of Contour Plot Generation

1. **Discretize the scalar field** $f$ on a structured/unstructured grid.
2. **For each cell (triangle/quad/tet), find edges where $f$ crosses a chosen iso-value** $f_0$.
3. **Linearly interpolate** the crossing point on each edge:
   $$ \vec{x}_{\text{cross}} = \vec{x}_1 + \frac{f_0 - f_1}{f_2 - f_1}(\vec{x}_2 - \vec{x}_1) $$
4. **Connect crossing points** within the cell to form a contour segment.
5. **Chain segments** across cells to produce a continuous polyline.
6. **Color/label** the contour band by the iso-value.

> [!TIP]
> **Why interpolation matters:** Without it, contours appear as jagged "staircase" patterns. Linear interpolation produces visually smooth, physically meaningful boundaries between regions of different pressure, temperature, etc.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace:** $C_p$ contours on aircraft wings for lift analysis; streamlines over an airfoil to detect separation.
- **Automotive:** Velocity isosurfaces inside a car cabin to assess HVAC comfort.
- **Power plant:** Temperature isosurfaces in a turbine blade cooling channel.
- **Biomedical:** Wall shear stress (WSS) visualization to predict arterial thrombosis risk.
- **Weather/CFD:** 3D humidity isosurfaces in cloud-resolving models.
