# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Streamline Equation from Particle Motion

A streamline is a curve that is **everywhere tangent to the instantaneous velocity field**. For a particle moving with the fluid:

At every point on the streamline, the local tangent $\dfrac{d\vec{x}}{ds}$ must equal the unit direction of velocity $\vec{V}/\vert\vec{V}\vert$.

**Step 1 — Tangent definition:**
For a smooth space curve parametrized by $s$ (arc length), the tangent vector is:

$$
\frac{d\vec{x}}{ds} = \frac{d\vec{x}/dt}{\vert d\vec{x}/dt\vert}
$$

**Step 2 — Recognize that for a fluid particle $\dfrac{d\vec{x}}{dt} = \vec{V}$:**

Substitute into the tangent expression:

$$
\frac{d\vec{x}}{ds} = \frac{\vec{V}(\vec{x},t)}{\vert\vec{V}(\vec{x},t)\vert}
$$

**Step 3 — Eliminate the denominator by allowing $s$ to be any monotonic parameter:**

If we let the parameter be $\sigma$ such that $d\sigma = \vert\vec{V}\vert\, ds$, then:

$$
\frac{d\vec{x}}{d\sigma} = \vec{V}(\vec{x},t)
$$

**Step 4 — Write the symmetric (Kármán-Trefftz) form for 3 components:**

$$
\frac{dx}{u} = \frac{dy}{v} = \frac{dz}{w} = d\sigma
$$

where $u, v, w$ are the components of $\vec{V}$. This is the **canonical streamline equation** you will find in every CFD textbook and is highly testable on KTU papers.

> [!NOTE]
> **Important distinction (often asked in KTU):** For *steady* flow, streamlines = pathlines = streaklines. For *unsteady* flow, they are different.

## 3.2 Derivation: Q-Criterion for Vortex Identification

The Q-criterion identifies a vortex as a region where **rotation strength dominates strain**.

**Step 1 — Decompose the velocity gradient tensor $\nabla \vec{V}$ into symmetric and skew-symmetric parts:**

$$
\nabla \vec{V} = S + \Omega
$$

where $S = \tfrac{1}{2}(\nabla \vec{V} + \nabla \vec{V}^T)$ (strain-rate tensor) and $\Omega = \tfrac{1}{2}(\nabla \vec{V} - \nabla \vec{V}^T)$ (rotation tensor).

**Step 2 — Compute the Frobenius norms (squared):**

$$
\vert\vert S \vert\vert^2 = \sum_{i,j} S_{ij}^2, \qquad \vert\vert \Omega \vert\vert^2 = \sum_{i,j} \Omega_{ij}^2
$$

**Step 3 — Define Q as the second invariant difference:**

$$
Q = \tfrac{1}{2}\left(\vert\vert\Omega\vert\vert^2 - \vert\vert S \vert\vert^2\right)
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation:**

- $Q > 0$ ⇒ rotation dominates ⇒ **vortex core**.
- $Q < 0$ ⇒ strain dominates ⇒ shear layer.
- $Q = 0$ ⇒ equipartition.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Some students write $Q = \tfrac{1}{2}(\vert\vert S \vert\vert^2 - \vert\vert\Omega\vert\vert^2)$ — flipped sign. Always remember: **Q > 0 means vortex**.

## 3.3 Derivation: Marching Cubes Iso-Surface Interpolation

The marching cubes algorithm extracts an isosurface $f(x,y,z) = f_0$ from a scalar volume.

**Step 1 — Discretize space into cubic cells** with 8 corner samples $f_1, f_2, \ldots, f_8$.

**Step 2 — Compute a 8-bit cube index** by testing each corner against $f_0$:

$$
b_i = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } f_i \geq f_0 \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

**Step 3 — Look up the index in a 256-entry table** to determine the triangle topology inside the cube (the *cube cases*).

**Step 4 — For each edge crossing an iso-contour, find the intersection point** by linear interpolation:

$$
\vec{x}_{\text{edge}} = \vec{x}_A + \frac{f_0 - f_A}{f_B - f_A}\,(\vec{x}_B - \vec{x}_A)
$$

**Step 5 — Emit triangles** connecting the intersection points and proceed to the next cube.

The result is a watertight (usually) triangulated surface representing the locus of all points with $f = f_0$.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering value:** Iso-surfaces let engineers see the *shape* of a 3D scalar field at a glance — e.g., the boundary of a recirculation bubble (where $u = 0$), the surface of a flame (where $T = T_{\text{adiabatic}}$), or a pollutant plume (where $Y_{\text{CO}} = $ threshold).

## 3.4 Derivation: Gouraud vs. Phong Shading

Lighting in CFD surfaces (e.g., a car body colored by surface pressure) follows the Phong illumination model.

**Step 1 — Ambient contribution** (constant background glow):
$$
I_a = K_a I_{a,\text{global}}
$$

**Step 2 — Diffuse contribution** (Lambertian):
$$
I_d = \sum_i K_d\, I_i\, (\vec{N} \cdot \vec{L}_i)
$$

where $\vec{N}$ is the surface normal and $\vec{L}_i$ the direction to light $i$.

**Step 3 — Specular contribution** (highlight):
$$
I_s = \sum_i K_s\, I_i\, (\vec{R}_i \cdot \vec{V})^n
$$

where $\vec{R}_i$ is the reflected light direction and $n$ is the shininess exponent (often 8–128).

**Step 4 — Total intensity** (Phong equation):
$$
I = I_a + I_d + I_s
$$

**Step 5 — Difference:**

- **Gouraud shading:** Compute $I$ at every vertex, then **interpolate $I$ across the polygon face**.
- **Phong shading:** Interpolate the **normal vector $\vec{N}$** across the face, then recompute $I$ at every pixel.

Phong is more accurate for highlights; Gouraud is faster. CFD post-processors like **ParaView** and **Tecplot** typically offer both.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Technique

| Problem Type | Recommended Visualization | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| 2D steady pressure on a wing | Filled contour plot | Reveals shock location, suction peak |
| 3D vortex breakdown | Q-isosurface | Extracts vortex core cleanly |
| Wall-bounded turbulent flow | LIC + streamlines | Shows near-wall streaky structure |
| Unsteady wake behind a cylinder | Animated pathlines (time-resolved) | Captures vortex shedding (Strouhal) |
| Wide-range temperature (0–10⁵ K) | Logarithmic colormap | Linear maps wash out low-T detail |
| Compressible nozzle flow | Adaptive colormap with shock highlighting | Shock = discontinuity needs explicit marking |
| Multi-field comparison | Side-by-side plots with shared scale | Avoids perceptual bias from rescaling |
| Publication-quality figure | Vector PDF with Viridis colormap | Color-blind safe, perceptually uniform |

## 3.6 Choosing a Colormap: Mathematical Justification

A colormap $C: \mathbb{R} \to [0,1]^3$ is **perceptually uniform** if equal increments in data produce equal perceptual differences:

$$
\Delta E_{ab}^* = \text{const} \quad \text{whenever} \quad \Delta f = \text{const}
$$

The legacy **"Jet"** colormap fails this test: it has a perceptual flat region in cyan and a sharp yellow band. The **"Viridis"** colormap (matplotlib) is designed to be monotonically increasing in luminance, making it print-safe and color-blind friendly.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 CFD Visualization Pipeline (Block-Level Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Solver Output: Grid plus Field Data] --> B[Data Conditioning]
    B --> B1[Interpolation across cells]
    B --> B2[Decimation or resampling]
    B --> B3[Unit conversion to SI or non-dim]
    B1 --> C[Mapping Engine]
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    C --> C1[Scalar to color via colormap]
    C --> C2[Vector to glyphs or streamlines]
    C --> C3[Scalar to isosurface via marching cubes]
    C1 --> D[Rendering Engine]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    D --> D1[Lighting: Phong or Gouraud]
    D --> D2[Projection: orthographic or perspective]
    D --> D3[Compositing and transparency]
    D1 --> E[Display Buffer]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    E --> F[2D Image or 3D Scene on Screen]
    E --> G[Animation Frames for Unsteady Flow]
    E --> H[Publication Quality Vector Output]
```

## 4.2 Colormap Classification Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Colormap Families] --> B[Sequential]
    A --> C[Diverging]
    A --> D[Qualitative]
    A --> E[Cyclic]
    B --> B1[Viridis]
    B --> B2[Plasma]
    B --> B3[Grays]
    C --> C1[Coolwarm]
    C --> C2[BrBG]
    C --> C3[RedBlue]
    D --> D1[Category10]
    D --> D2[Set1]
    E --> E1[HSV circular]
    E --> E2[Twilight]
    B1 --> F1[Use for ordered scalar data]
    C1 --> F2[Use for signed data with zero mid-point]
    D1 --> F3[Use for discrete categories]
    E1 --> F4[Use for angular or periodic data]
```

## 4.3 Marching Cubes Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Cube with 8 corner samples] --> B[Compute 8-bit case index]
    B --> C{Look up 256-entry table}
    C --> D[Case 0: All inside or all outside]
    C --> E[Cases 1 to 254: Triangle topology]
    C --> F[Case 255: Cube entirely on one side]
    D --> G[Skip cube]
    E --> H[Compute edge intersections by linear interp]
    F --> G
    H --> I[Emit triangles]
    I --> J[Move to next cube]
    J --> B
```

## 4.4 Streamline Integration Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Seed point x0] --> B[Evaluate velocity V at x]
    B --> C[Compute step ds = h or adaptive RK]
    C --> D[Update position x = x + ds times V normalized]
    D --> E{Exit domain or velocity too low?}
    E -->|No| B
    E -->|Yes| F[Stop and render polyline]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define streamlines, pathlines, and streaklines. When do they coincide?**

**Model Answer:**
- **Streamline:** A curve everywhere tangent to the *instantaneous* velocity field. Defined by $\dfrac{dx}{u} = \dfrac{dy}{v} = \dfrac{dz}{w}$.
- **Pathline:** The actual trajectory of a fluid particle over time. Defined by $\dfrac{d\vec{x}}{dt} = \vec{V}(\vec{x}, t)$.
- **Streakline:** The locus of all particles that have previously passed through a fixed point.
- **Coincidence:** All three coincide **only for steady flow** ($\partial \vec{V}/\partial t = 0$).

**Key terms for full marks:** Tangent, instantaneous, integration in time, steady-state equivalence.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**What is the marching cubes algorithm? State two limitations.**

**Model Answer:**
- **Definition:** Marching cubes is an algorithm that extracts a polygonal **isosurface** $f(\vec{x}) = f_0$ from a discrete 3D scalar field by processing each cubic cell, classifying corner values as inside/outside the iso-value, and triangulating intersections.
- **Limitations (any two):**
  1. Produces ambiguous face/topology on cells with corner values exactly equal to $f_0$ (resolved by *marching tetrahedra*).
  2. Resolution is limited to grid cell size; small features can be missed.
  3. Computationally expensive for very large volumes.
  4. No topological guarantee (holes possible) without post-processing.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
>
> **(a)** With neat derivations, explain the **streamline equation** and the **Q-criterion** for vortex identification. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Describe the **marching cubes algorithm** for isosurface extraction. List its applications in CFD. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**1. Streamline Derivation (4 marks):**

A streamline is everywhere tangent to the velocity vector. If $\vec{x}(s)$ is a point on the streamline parametrized by arc length $s$, then:

$$
\frac{d\vec{x}}{ds} = \frac{\vec{V}}{\vert\vec{V}\vert}
$$

Letting $d\sigma = \vert\vec{V}\vert\, ds$ be the integration parameter:

$$
\frac{d\vec{x}}{d\sigma} = \vec{V}(\vec{x}, t)
$$

Component-wise in 3D:

$$
\frac{dx}{u} = \frac{dy}{v} = \frac{dz}{w} = d\sigma
$$

This is the **canonical streamline ODE** solved by Euler or RK4 integration from a seed point. For steady flow, $t$ drops out and streamlines are time-invariant.

**2. Q-Criterion Derivation (3 marks):**

Decompose $\nabla\vec{V} = S + \Omega$ where $S$ is symmetric (strain), $\Omega$ skew-symmetric (rotation).

$$
Q = \tfrac{1}{2}\left(\vert\vert\Omega\vert\vert^2 - \vert\vert S \vert\vert^2\right)
$$

**Interpretation:** $Q > 0$ → rotation dominates → **vortex core**. Used to extract vortex isosurfaces in flows around wings, turbomachinery, etc.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Marching Cubes Algorithm (5 marks):**

1. Divide the 3D domain into a regular grid of cubes.
2. For each cube, sample the scalar at 8 corners.
3. Form an 8-bit index where bit $i = 1$ if corner $i$ has $f_i \geq f_0$.
4. Use the index to look up one of 256 pre-computed triangle configurations.
5. For each intersected edge, compute the crossing point by **linear interpolation**:
   $$ \vec{x}_{\text{edge}} = \vec{x}_A + \frac{f_0 - f_A}{f_B - f_A}(\vec{x}_B - \vec{x}_A) $$
6. Output triangles; advance to the next cube.

**Applications in CFD (2 marks — list any two):**

- Pressure isosurfaces on aircraft fuselage.
- Flame surface extraction (where $T = T_{\text{adiabatic}}$).
- Pollutant plume visualization (where species mass fraction = threshold).
- Vortex core surfaces (where $Q = Q_0$).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
>
> **(a)** Discuss **scalar field visualization techniques** in CFD. Compare linear and logarithmic colormaps with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Explain **Gouraud and Phong shading** models. Why is Phong preferred for engineering visualization? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Scalar Visualization Techniques (4 marks):**

1. **Contour plots (2D):** Lines or filled bands of constant scalar value.
2. **Isosurfaces (3D):** Surfaces of constant $f$ extracted by marching cubes.
3. **Volume rendering:** Direct color-and-transparency mapping through 3D scalar volume (ray casting).
4. **Color mapping on surfaces:** Painting a polygonal surface (e.g., car body) with the local $f$ value.
5. **Cut planes / slicing:** A planar slice through the 3D field, colored by $f$.

**Linear vs. Logarithmic Colormaps (3 marks):**

- **Linear:** $C(f) = C_{\min} + \dfrac{f - f_{\min}}{f_{\max} - f_{\min}}(C_{\max} - C_{\min})$ — good when data spans ≤ 2 orders of magnitude (e.g., $C_p$ on an airfoil).
- **Logarithmic:** $C(f) = C_{\min} + \dfrac{\log f - \log f_{\min}}{\log f_{\max} - \log f_{\min}}(C_{\max} - C_{\min})$ — needed for wide dynamic range (e.g., turbulence kinetic energy from 10⁻⁶ to 10² m²/s²).
- Linear maps wash out low-end detail; log maps compress high-end detail.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Gouraud Shading (3 marks):**

- Compute Phong illumination **at every vertex** of the polygon.
- Interpolate the resulting intensity **across the face** using screen-space bilinear interpolation.
- **Pros:** Fast; **Cons:** Misses specular highlights smaller than a triangle (Mach band artifact).

**Phong Shading (3 marks):**

- Interpolate the **surface normal $\vec{N}$** across the face.
- Recompute the Phong reflection at every pixel.
- **Pros:** Accurate specular highlights, smooth gradients; **Cons:** More expensive.

**Why Phong is preferred in engineering viz (1 mark):**
Engineering surfaces (turbine blades, car bodies) demand accurate specular response to convey curvature. Phong delivers this without polygon-tessellation overhead, making it the standard in CFD post-processors like Tecplot and ParaView.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common ways students LOSE marks on this topic:**
> 1. **Sign error in Q-criterion** — writing $Q = \tfrac{1}{2}(\vert\vert S \vert\vert^2 - \vert\vert\Omega\vert\vert^2)$. Mark loss: **1–2 marks**.
> 2. **Confusing streamlines and pathlines** — forgetting that streamlines are *instantaneous* and pathlines are *time-integrated*. Examiner deducts **up to 3 marks** if interchange is used in derivation.
> 3. **Skipping the interpolation step in marching cubes** — writing only the lookup step without the edge-intersection equation loses **2 marks**.
> 4. **Calling colormaps "colour schemes" without justification** — examiners want mathematical definition $C: \mathbb{R} \to [0,1]^3$ or a perceptual-uniformity statement.
> 5. **Omitting lighting equations** in shading questions — Phong equation must be reproduced; a hand-wave "uses ambient + diffuse + specular" gets only **1 of 3 marks**.
> 6. **Not distinguishing Gouraud (vertex) vs. Phong (per-pixel)** — the *location* of computation is what differs; do not write only the formula.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Streamline ODE:** $\dfrac{dx}{u} = \dfrac{dy}{v} = \dfrac{dz}{w}$ — derived from $\dfrac{d\vec{x}}{ds} = \dfrac{\vec{V}}{\vert\vec{V}\vert}$.
- **Q-Criterion:** $Q = \tfrac{1}{2}(\vert\vert\Omega\vert\vert^2 - \vert\vert S \vert\vert^2)$; **positive Q = vortex**.
- **$\lambda_2$ Criterion:** negative eigenvalues of $S^2 + \Omega^2$ indicate vortex cores.
- **Marching Cubes:** 8-corner lookup + linear edge interpolation = isosurface triangles.
- **Phong Equation:** $I = I_a K_a + \sum_i I_i \left[ K_d (\vec{N}\cdot\vec{L}_i) + K_s (\vec{R}_i\cdot\vec{V})^n \right]$.
- **Gouraud:** compute $I$ at vertices, then interpolate. **Phong:** interpolate $\vec{N}$, compute $I$ per pixel.
- **Linear colormap** for narrow data range; **logarithmic colormap** for wide dynamic range.
- **Perceptually uniform** colormaps (Viridis) are preferred over legacy "Jet" for publication.
- **LIC** textures noise along flow direction; **streamlines** integrate tangentially; **pathlines** follow particles in time; **streaklines** connect particles emitted from one point.
- **Vortex core extraction** uses Q or $\lambda_2$, not just $|\vec{\omega}|$ (which is high in shear too).
- **Volume rendering** uses ray casting through a 3D scalar field with transfer functions for color and opacity.
- **KTU exam focus:** derivations of streamline ODE and Q-criterion; definitions of marching cubes; comparison of shading models; colormap selection logic.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Scheme Reminder:** Always state the *governing equation* before plugging in values. Examiners reward methodological rigor over rote memorization. In particular, the difference between "streamline" and "pathline" is among the most frequently asked distinctions in Module 4 of PEMET521.
